question: Bellman–Ford relaxes every edge V−1 times. Why exactly V−1?

choices.1: Relaxing fewer than V−1 times risks integer overflow when summing negative weights
choices.2: The algorithm must traverse all V−1 spanning-tree edges exactly once to guarantee correctness
choices.3: Each edge can only be safely relaxed once per vertex, and there are V−1 vertices other than the source
choices.4: A shortest path in a graph of V vertices uses at most V−1 edges, so V−1 passes give every shortest path the chance to fully form

question: After the V−1 relaxation passes, Bellman–Ford runs one additional pass. What does it mean if some distance still decreases during this extra pass?

choices.1: The graph is disconnected, so some vertices remain unreachable
choices.2: The earlier passes terminated early and simply need to be repeated
choices.3: The source vertex was chosen incorrectly and the algorithm must restart
choices.4: A negative cycle reachable from the source exists, so no well-defined shortest path exists for the affected vertices

question: What distinguishes Bellman–Ford from Dijkstra's algorithm?

choices.1: It correctly handles negative edge weights and can detect reachable negative cycles
choices.2: It computes all-pairs shortest paths instead of single-source
choices.3: It runs faster on dense graphs because it avoids a priority queue
choices.4: It still finds an optimal path even when edge weights change during execution

question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: O((V + E) log V)
choices.2: O(V²)
choices.3: O(V · E)
choices.4: O(E log V)

question: The implementation tracks an `updated` flag and breaks out early when a full pass makes no changes. What does this optimization accomplish?

choices.1: It lowers the worst-case complexity to O(E log V)
choices.2: It is required for correctness; without it, negative cycles could not be detected
choices.3: It guarantees the algorithm finishes in at most O(V) passes regardless of graph shape
choices.4: It lets typical graphs settle and terminate early, but the worst case stays O(V · E)

question: Relaxation is the algorithm's only primitive. For a directed edge u → v of weight w, when is dist[v] lowered?

choices.1: When dist[u] is not infinite and dist[u] + w < dist[v]
choices.2: When dist[v] + w < dist[u], regardless of whether dist[u] is finite
choices.3: Whenever w is negative, since a negative edge always shortens a path
choices.4: When v has not yet been touched during the current pass

question: What invariant holds after the i-th relaxation pass over all edges?

choices.1: The i vertices closest to the source are finalized, as in Dijkstra
choices.2: Every shortest path that uses at most i edges has its correct distance recorded
choices.3: All paths of length exactly i are discovered, but shorter ones may still be wrong
choices.4: Exactly i vertices have reached their final shortest-path distance

question: Given the edges 0→1 (5), 1→2 (−2), and 0→2 (10) with source 0, what is the final shortest distance to vertex 2?

choices.1: Infinity, because the negative edge forms a cycle
choices.2: 5, since that is the cost to first reach vertex 1
choices.3: 10, via the direct edge 0→2
choices.4: 3, via the path 0→1→2 (5 + (−2))

question: Bellman–Ford's negative-cycle detection makes it the standard tool for which of the following?

choices.1: Computing minimum spanning trees in graphs with negative weights
choices.2: Finding strongly connected components in a directed graph
choices.3: Detecting currency-arbitrage opportunities and serving as the per-vertex subroutine inside Johnson's all-pairs shortest-path algorithm
choices.4: Maximizing flow through a network with capacity constraints

question: How does the SPFA variant relate to standard Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: It only queues the vertices whose distance just changed — often faster in practice but with the same O(V · E) worst case
choices.2: It improves the worst-case bound to O(E + V log V) by using a Fibonacci heap
choices.3: It processes edges in sorted weight order, like Kruskal's algorithm
choices.4: It drops negative-cycle detection in exchange for guaranteed linear time

question: What is the space complexity of Bellman–Ford, and what does it store?

choices.1: O(V), one distance value per vertex
choices.2: O(V²), an adjacency matrix of distances
choices.3: O(E), one entry per edge
choices.4: O(V · E), one distance per edge per pass

question: Binary lifting precomputes only Θ(log n) different jump lengths per node, yet can answer a jump of any length k. Which property of integers makes that sufficient?

choices.1: Every tree has at most log n distinct depths, so only log n jump lengths can ever occur
choices.2: Tree paths always have a power-of-two length, so non-power-of-two jumps are never requested
choices.3: Applying the single parent pointer log n times can reach any ancestor
choices.4: Any k can be decomposed into the set bits of its binary representation, so composing the precomputed 2^i jumps reproduces a jump of length k

question: Let up[k][v] be the 2^k-th ancestor of v. Which recurrence correctly builds row k from the row below it?

choices.1: up[k][v] = up[k-1][v] + up[k-1][v]

question: Why does the doubling recurrence up[k][v] = up[k-1][up[k-1][v]] give the 2^k-th ancestor?

choices.1: It averages two adjacent rows, which doubles the implied depth
choices.2: It multiplies the parent index by k, scaling the jump to 2^k
choices.3: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then 1 more, giving 2^(k-1)+1 levels
choices.4: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then 2^(k-1) more, and 2^(k-1)+2^(k-1) = 2^k

question: What are the time and space costs of building the binary-lifting table for an n-node tree?

choices.1: O(n^2) time and O(n^2) space
choices.2: O(n log n) time and O(n log n) space
choices.3: O(n log n) time but only O(n) space
choices.4: O(n) time and O(n) space

question: How many rows (power-of-two levels) does the table need for an n-node tree?

choices.1: ceil(log2 n) + 1 rows
choices.2: n / log2 n rows
choices.3: Exactly 2 rows, the parent and grandparent
choices.4: Exactly n rows

question: kthAncestor loops over bit positions i and does `if (k & (1<<i)) v = up[i][v];`. Why is a single k-th-ancestor query O(log n)?

choices.1: Each jump halves the remaining distance, like a binary search on edge count
choices.2: k < n has at most ceil(log2 n) bits, so at most that many power-of-two jumps are taken across the log n rows
choices.3: The loop always runs exactly n / log n iterations
choices.4: Each up[i][v] lookup internally recurses log n times

question: In LCA, phase 1 lifts the deeper node up by k = |depth(u) - depth(v)| levels. What is its purpose, and what does it mean if the two nodes coincide afterward?

choices.1: It moves both nodes to the root; coinciding means the tree is a single path
choices.2: It finds the midpoint of the path; coinciding means they were the same node to begin with
choices.3: It makes the two nodes siblings; coinciding means the tree is malformed
choices.4: It equalises their depths; coinciding means the shallower node was an ancestor of the deeper one and is itself the LCA

question: In phase 2 of the LCA algorithm, iterating k from the highest power down, when are BOTH u and v jumped up by 2^k?

choices.1: Only when up[k][u] != up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors still differ
choices.2: Whenever 2^k is less than or equal to the remaining depth difference
choices.3: On every iteration unconditionally, until k reaches 0
choices.4: Only when up[k][u] == up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors already match

question: After phase 2 of LCA finishes, why is the returned answer up[0][u] (u's parent) rather than u itself?

choices.1: u and v are now equal, and up[0] retrieves the deepest distinct ancestor
choices.2: u has been lifted exactly one level too high, and up[0] corrects that off-by-one
choices.3: The greedy lifting keeps u and v strictly below the LCA, stopping when they are distinct children of it, so their shared parent up[0][u] is the LCA
choices.4: up[0][u] is always the root, the only guaranteed common ancestor

question: The rows up[k][v] for k >= 1 are filled inside the DFS, before recursing into v's children. Why is that ordering correct?

choices.1: Because the recurrence reads only v's own column, which never depends on any other node
choices.2: In a pre-order traversal every ancestor of v is visited before v, so the lower-row entries that up[k][v] composes are already complete
choices.3: Because v's children are visited first, so their columns are ready to compose
choices.4: Because DFS visits nodes in increasing index order, so dependencies are always met

question: The build sets the root's parent to itself (up[0][root] = root). What does this sentinel accomplish?

choices.1: It forces the root's depth to be computed as 1 instead of 0
choices.2: It lets kthAncestor return -1 when k exceeds the node's depth
choices.3: It marks the root so the LCA routine can detect when both nodes reach the top
choices.4: Any jump that would go above the root harmlessly stays at the root, so over-jumps never read an invalid node

question: Per the comparison of LCA methods, when is Euler tour + sparse-table RMQ the better choice than binary lifting?

choices.1: When preprocessing must be O(n) rather than O(n log n)
choices.2: When all queries are known up front and can be answered offline
choices.3: When you specifically need O(1) queries and can accept more code for an LCA-only structure
choices.4: When you also need k-th-ancestor and level-ancestor queries from the same structure

question: Why is binary lifting described as an 'online' LCA method while Tarjan's Union-Find approach is 'offline'?

choices.1: Tarjan's is online because Union-Find processes unions incrementally; binary lifting must batch its queries
choices.2: Binary lifting runs over a live network connection while Tarjan's runs locally
choices.3: Binary lifting answers queries as they arrive one at a time, whereas Tarjan's requires all queries to be known in advance
choices.4: Binary lifting mutates the tree during each query while Tarjan's leaves it unchanged

question: Binary lifting generalises to any functional graph (each node has a single succ(v) pointer). With edge data stored beside the 2^k jumps, which extra query can the same power-of-two decomposition answer?

choices.1: The exact number of distinct cycles reachable from a node

choices.3: Aggregates like the min/max/sum of edge weights along a path, combined over the same 2^k segments being jumped
choices.4: The shortest path between any two nodes in an arbitrary weighted graph

question: Dijkstra is greedy: it always finalises the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance. Why is a vertex's distance final the moment it is popped from the min-heap?

choices.1: Because each vertex is pushed to the heap exactly once, so the first pop is the only pop
choices.2: Because edges are non-negative, so any later path to it can only add non-negative weight and can never beat the distance it was popped with
choices.3: Because the heap guarantees no other vertex can ever later be pushed with a smaller key
choices.4: Because relaxation always processes a vertex's edges in ascending weight order

question: Why does Dijkstra's correctness require non-negative edge weights?

choices.1: Negative weights force the algorithm into an infinite loop re-relaxing the source
choices.2: A negative edge could make a cheaper route to an already-settled vertex appear later, breaking the greedy finality invariant and silently producing wrong answers
choices.3: Negative weights make the distance array hold negative values, which a min-heap cannot order
choices.4: Negative weights cause the min-heap to extract vertices in the wrong order because of integer overflow

question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra implemented with a binary min-heap?

choices.2: O(E + V log V)

choices.4: O((V + E) log V)

question: Python's heapq has no decrease-key operation. How does the implementation handle a vertex whose tentative distance improves during relaxation?

choices.1: It pushes a new, smaller (distance, node) entry and leaves the old one, so a vertex may appear several times (lazy deletion)
choices.2: It removes the old entry from the middle of the heap and reinserts the smaller one

question: What precisely is a strongly connected component (SCC) of a directed graph?

question: How does a polynomial (rolling) hash represent a string?

choices.1: As the XOR of its character codes

choices.3: As a bit array with one bit per character

question: What is the headline benefit of precomputing prefix hashes and base powers?

choices.1: It sorts the string in O(n)

choices.3: After O(n) preprocessing, the hash of any substring can be read in O(1)
choices.4: It removes all collisions

question: What is the formula for the hash of substring s[l:r] from prefix hashes?

choices.1: pref[r] - pref[l]
choices.2: pref[r] + pref[l] * B^(r-l)
choices.3: (pref[r] * pref[l]) mod M
choices.4: (pref[r] - pref[l] * B^(r-l)) mod M

question: Why is the pref[l] term scaled by B^(r-l) in the substring formula?

choices.1: To convert the hash to a different modulus
choices.2: Because B^(r-l) is the number of characters in the prefix
choices.3: To avoid negative numbers after the modulo
choices.4: Because the s[:l] portion sits (r-l) digits to the left of s[l:r], so its contribution is scaled by B^(r-l)

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: How is the basic polynomial hash computed iteratively (Horner's method)?

choices.1: h = (h + code * base) mod M, summing independently
choices.2: h = max(h, code) over all characters
choices.3: h = h XOR (code << base)
choices.4: h = (h * base + code) mod M for each character

question: Since a hash maps infinitely many strings into M values, what must correctness-critical matching do on a hash hit?

choices.1: Verify the hit with a real character-by-character comparison
choices.2: Trust the hash, since collisions are impossible with a prime modulus

question: What does using two independent (base, mod) pairs (double hashing) achieve?

choices.1: It makes substring hashing O(log n) instead of O(1)

question: Why map the alphabet starting from 1 (e.g. ord(ch) - ord('a') + 1) rather than 0?

choices.1: To fit the codes in a single byte

question: How does Rabin-Karp use rolling hashes to search, and where does it shine?

choices.1: It builds an LPS array from the hashes; best for repetitive text
choices.2: It sorts the text by hash; best for single-pattern search

question: Why randomise the base at startup for untrusted input?

choices.1: To speed up the modulo operation

choices.3: To guarantee zero collisions
choices.4: Because fixed public constants can be attacked to force collisions; randomising defends against adversaries

question: What algorithm does std::sort implement, and what worst-case guarantee does that give?

choices.1: Heapsort only, O(N log N) but cache-unfriendly
choices.2: Pure quicksort, with O(N^2) worst case
choices.3: Merge sort, O(N log N) but always stable

question: When does introsort (std::sort) switch from quicksort to heapsort?

question: How does std::stable_sort differ from std::sort?

question: What does std::nth_element do, and at what average cost?

question: What is the difference between std::lower_bound and std::upper_bound on a sorted range?

question: What is the key difference between std::accumulate and std::reduce, and why does it matter for floating point?

question: What does std::partial_sort cost and produce?

choices.3: Because unsigned types are larger
choices.4: Because signed types can't use bitwise operators

question: How do you test whether bit n of x is set, yielding 0 or 1?

question: What are the four key stages of building C++ code, in order?

choices.1: Preprocessing, compilation, assembly, linking
choices.2: Compilation, linking, assembly, preprocessing
choices.3: Parsing, optimization, linking, loading
choices.4: Tokenizing, compiling, interpreting, running

choices.4: A single function within a source file

question: What does the One Definition Rule (ODR) require?

choices.3: Automatic resolution of the ODR
choices.4: A smaller final executable

question: What does the inline keyword on a function actually mean?

question: Which of these has INTERNAL linkage by default?

choices.4: A non-static class member

choices.3: Define it in a header
choices.4: Mark it extern

question: What belongs in headers versus source files to keep ODR happy?

question: What does declaring a static data member `static inline constexpr int size2 = 4;` (C++17) avoid?

question: What is copy elision?

question: Why is `return std::move(local);` a pessimization?

question: Why can a factory function return an immovable type (move ctor deleted) by value in C++17?

question: What is a key difference between copy initialization (T obj = value) and direct initialization (T obj(value))?

question: What silently suppresses the compiler-generated move operations for a class?

question: What is the Rule of Zero, the modern best practice?

question: What is the core hazard-pointer protocol for safely reading a shared node?

choices.1: Lock the node, read it, unlock it
choices.2: Increment a reference count and decrement after
choices.3: Copy the whole structure before reading
choices.4: Read the shared pointer, publish it in your hazard slot, re-read to confirm it didn't change, dereference, then clear the slot when done

question: Why is the re-read (validation) step `while (head.load() != p)` necessary after publishing p?

choices.1: To confirm p is still reachable; if it changed between the first read and the publish, the thread retries so it never protects/dereferences a stale pointer
choices.2: To free the old node
choices.3: To make the load atomic
choices.4: To increment a version counter

question: How do hazard pointers separate logical removal from physical deletion?

choices.1: They use a garbage collector to free nodes
choices.2: They never delete nodes at all
choices.3: A removed node is retired (added to a per-thread retired list), not deleted; it is freed later only after a scan confirms no thread has it in a hazard slot
choices.4: They delete the node immediately on removal

question: During a reclamation scan, when is a retired node actually freed?

choices.1: Always immediately
choices.2: Only when the retired list is full
choices.3: After a fixed time delay
choices.4: Only when it does not appear in any thread's hazard pointer slots

question: In the Michael-Scott lock-free queue dequeue, what does a thread do with the old head after a successful CAS moves head forward?

choices.1: delete head immediately
choices.2: retire(head) - because another thread may still be reading it via a hazard pointer
choices.3: Leave head leaked
choices.4: Push head back onto the queue

question: Which property describes hazard pointers' protection granularity?

choices.1: They protect whole epochs of allocations at once
choices.2: They protect only the most recently allocated node
choices.3: They protect the entire data structure with one flag
choices.4: They are precise: they protect specific nodes, not whole epochs

question: With T threads and H hazard slots each, what is the cost of a reclamation scan?

choices.1: It scans the entire heap
choices.2: It must stop all threads first
choices.3: It is O(1) regardless of thread count
choices.4: It inspects up to T*H hazard pointers

question: Which problem do hazard pointers NOT automatically solve?

choices.1: Publishing a pointer before dereferencing
choices.2: Use-after-free
choices.3: Retiring removed nodes
choices.4: The ABA problem (CAS fooled when memory is reused at the same address) and reference cycles

question: What are the three progress guarantees for nonblocking data structures, from weakest to strongest?

choices.1: Obstruction-free, lock-free, wait-free
choices.2: Blocking, lock-free, wait-free
choices.3: Wait-free, lock-free, obstruction-free
choices.4: Lock-free, wait-free, obstruction-free

question: What does lock-free guarantee (as opposed to wait-free)?

choices.1: Every thread completes in a bounded number of steps
choices.2: At least one (some) thread always makes progress system-wide, though an individual thread may starve
choices.3: Progress only when a thread runs alone without interference
choices.4: No thread ever retries an operation

question: What does wait-free guarantee?

choices.1: Some thread eventually finishes
choices.2: Progress only without interference
choices.3: Threads never use atomics
choices.4: Every thread completes its operation in a bounded number of steps, regardless of other threads - no starvation

question: What does obstruction-free mean?

choices.1: Threads block on a mutex
choices.2: At least one thread always progresses
choices.3: A thread makes progress only if it eventually runs alone (without interference); the weakest guarantee
choices.4: Every thread finishes in bounded time

question: What is 'helping' in wait-free algorithms?

choices.1: Threads back off randomly on contention
choices.2: The runtime garbage-collects nodes
choices.3: One thread completes another thread's pending operation
choices.4: A thread waits for another to finish

question: What is the most common practical implementation family for lock-free data structures?

choices.1: CAS (compare-and-swap)-based
choices.2: Mutex-based
choices.3: Descriptor-based
choices.4: Garbage-collection-based

question: What does linearizability mean?

choices.1: Threads never contend
choices.2: Each operation appears to occur atomically at one instant
choices.3: Operations run in numeric order
choices.4: The structure is implemented as a linear array

question: What is the ABA problem?

choices.1: Two threads alternate on a lock forever
choices.2: A thread aborts before another begins
choices.3: A memory location changes from A to B and back to A, fooling a CAS into thinking nothing changed
choices.4: An allocator runs out of memory

question: Which of these is a memory-reclamation strategy for nonblocking structures?

choices.1: Priority inheritance
choices.2: Double-checked locking
choices.3: Hazard pointers, epoch-based reclamation, reference counting, GC, or RCU
choices.4: Spinlocks

question: Why do lock-free queues often use a manual reference-counting scheme instead of std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<Node>>?

choices.1: shared_ptr cannot point to nodes
choices.2: shared_ptr is too small
choices.3: If std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<Node>> is not lock-free on the platform, using it defeats the purpose of the lock-free structure
choices.4: Manual counting is always simpler

question: In the counted-pointer scheme, what is the purpose of the EXTERNAL count?

choices.1: It counts how many times the node was deleted
choices.2: It protects a node while a thread is acquiring it from a shared atomic pointer (head/tail/next)
choices.3: It stores the node's data size
choices.4: It tracks references already acquired by threads, stored in the node

question: Why can't you simply put one atomic refcount inside the node and increment it?

choices.1: Incrementing a refcount requires a lock
choices.2: Atomics can't live inside nodes
choices.3: Chicken-and-egg: to increment node->refcount you must first dereference the node, but it might already be deleted - so the first reference must be acquired atomically through the shared pointer
choices.4: The node has no address until constructed

question: How does a thread safely acquire a node from head before dereferencing it?

choices.1: It deletes and re-creates the node
choices.2: It locks a mutex around head
choices.3: It CASes head to a copy with external_count incremented; the increment and pointer check happen in one atomic CAS, retrying if head changed
choices.4: It copies the whole node first

question: When is a node actually deleted in this scheme?

choices.1: Immediately when removed from the queue
choices.2: Only when no external pointer references it AND no internal (thread-held) reference remains
choices.3: After a fixed timeout
choices.4: When the external count reaches 1

question: When a shared atomic pointer (e.g. head) stops referencing a node, what happens to its external count?

choices.1: It is reset to zero
choices.2: It is discarded
choices.3: It is folded/transferred into the node's internal count (the acquired references become internal references)
choices.4: It is copied into tail

question: How does the counted pointer help mitigate the ABA problem?

choices.1: It uses a mutex on every CAS
choices.2: It never reuses addresses
choices.3: The CAS compares both the pointer AND the count, so even if a raw address is reused, the counted value is much less likely to look identical
choices.4: It prevents memory from ever being reused

question: What is the main platform dependency / downside of the counted-pointer scheme?

choices.1: It only works single-threaded
choices.2: std::atomic<counted_node_ptr> (pointer + counter, often 16 bytes) needs lock-free double-width CAS support, or it won't be lock-free
choices.3: It cannot store any data in nodes
choices.4: It requires garbage collection

question: Why does an SPSC ring buffer need no CAS at all?

choices.1: Exactly one thread owns the write index and one owns the read index, so no two threads ever modify the same index - only atomic loads/stores with the right ordering are needed
choices.2: Because the buffer is single-threaded
choices.3: Because reads and writes never overlap in time
choices.4: Because it uses a mutex internally

question: Why must the capacity be a power of two?

choices.1: To avoid false sharing
choices.2: To make the buffer fit in a cache line
choices.3: To allow CAS on the indices
choices.4: So wrapping can use a single AND mask (& (Capacity-1)) instead of the costlier % operation

question: Using free-running counters (head_, tail_) that only increment, how are empty and full detected?

choices.1: Empty when both are zero; full when both equal Capacity
choices.2: Empty when head_ == tail_; full when head_ - tail_ == Capacity
choices.3: Empty when head_ - tail_ == Capacity; full when head_ == tail_
choices.4: Empty when head_ < 0; full when tail_ overflows

question: What is the single memory-ordering rule for the SPSC buffer?

choices.1: Relaxed everywhere, since one thread owns each index
choices.2: Acquire on your own index, release on the other's
choices.3: Relaxed on your own index, acquire when loading the other thread's index, release when publishing (after the payload write or slot free)
choices.4: Use seq_cst everywhere for correctness

question: In push, why is head_.store(head+1) done with memory_order_release?

choices.1: Because release is faster than relaxed
choices.2: To prevent the producer from reading tail_
choices.3: To make the store atomic
choices.4: So the payload write buf_[...] = item happens-before the consumer's acquire-load of head_ sees the new index - the release publishes the data, not just the index

question: What is the classic bug if you use relaxed ordering on the CROSS-thread loads/stores?

choices.1: The consumer could see the incremented head_ before the payload write lands, reading garbage
choices.2: The producer overwrites tail_
choices.3: The capacity must double
choices.4: The buffer deadlocks

question: Why are head_ and tail_ each given alignas(64)?

choices.1: To make them atomic
choices.2: To put each index on its own cache line, avoiding false sharing where one thread's write invalidates the other's cached line (often a 2-3x throughput difference)
choices.3: To make the struct exactly 64 bytes
choices.4: To align them for SIMD

question: What does the 'cached indices' optimization achieve?

choices.1: It makes the queue support multiple producers
choices.2: Each side caches the other's last-seen index and only re-reads the real atomic when the cache says full/empty, keeping the common path cache-local
choices.3: It removes the need for a payload buffer
choices.4: It replaces acquire/release with seq_cst

question: What progress guarantee do push and pop provide, and what is the hard usage constraint?

choices.1: Lock-free; any number of producers and consumers
choices.2: Wait-free (bounded steps, no retries); strictly one producer and one consumer - two producers racing on head_ corrupts it
choices.3: Blocking; multi-producer safe
choices.4: Obstruction-free; single-threaded only

question: What is the problem with a single shared task queue in a threadpool?

choices.1: It can only hold one task
choices.2: It cannot store tasks
choices.3: All workers contend for the same lock, which scales poorly
choices.4: It requires lock-free programming

question: What is the usual high-performance alternative to one central queue?

choices.1: One thread per task
choices.2: A global lock-free stack only
choices.3: One local queue per worker plus work stealing - each worker mostly uses its own queue and only steals when out of work
choices.4: A single bigger queue

question: In work stealing, why do the owner and thieves take from opposite ends of the deque?

choices.1: The owner pops from the back and thieves steal from the front, so they usually touch different ends and contend less
choices.2: To guarantee wait-freedom
choices.3: To preserve FIFO order
choices.4: Because deques only allow one end

question: What is the single easiest, highest-impact rule (level 1) for a threadpool?

choices.1: Create a thread per task
choices.2: Use lock-free queues
choices.3: Always notify_all
choices.4: Do not hold the queue lock while running a task - move the task out under the lock, then run it outside

question: Why prefer cv.notify_one (or a counting semaphore) over cv.notify_all when adding a task?

choices.1: notify_all is deprecated
choices.2: Waking too many workers causes contention; notify_one or a semaphore reduces pointless wakeups
choices.3: notify_one wakes more threads
choices.4: Semaphores cannot wake workers

question: How does batching pops from the shared queue help?

choices.1: It forces single-threaded execution
choices.2: It takes several tasks per lock acquisition, turning many lock acquisitions into fewer - especially helpful when tasks are small
choices.3: It runs tasks while holding the lock
choices.4: It eliminates the queue entirely

question: Why push a worker-generated (internal) child task to that same worker's LOCAL queue?

choices.1: To force the task onto a different core
choices.2: To guarantee FIFO ordering globally
choices.3: Because the global queue is read-only
choices.4: For locality and to reduce global contention - useful for divide-and-conquer workloads (parallel_sort, tree_search)

question: What does the note advise about starting with lock-free queues?

choices.1: Always start lock-free for best performance
choices.2: Lock-free queues remove the need for work stealing
choices.3: Don't start with lock-free; start with good queue topology - a std::mutex + std::deque often beats a complex lock-free queue unless task granularity is tiny
choices.4: Lock-free queues are never useful

question: Which C++20 facility gives a condition-variable wait that wakes on either work availability OR cancellation?

choices.1: std::condition_variable with a timeout
choices.2: std::binary_semaphore
choices.3: std::atomic<bool> with busy-waiting
choices.4: std::condition_variable_any combined with a std::stop_token (cv.wait(lock, stop, pred))

question: Why must you use std::condition_variable_any rather than std::condition_variable for stop-token waits?

choices.1: std::condition_variable does not directly support std::stop_token; condition_variable_any does
choices.2: condition_variable_any is faster
choices.3: condition_variable_any avoids needing a mutex
choices.4: condition_variable cannot take a predicate

question: What is the main rule for the wait predicate in an interruptible worker?

choices.1: Always include cancellation in the predicate (e.g. stop.stop_requested() || hasWork) - the real condition is your predicate, not the cv itself
choices.2: Wait on the condition variable alone, with no predicate
choices.3: Only check tasks.empty()
choices.4: Never use a predicate with stop tokens

question: What is the difference between the two shutdown modes shown?

choices.1: 'Stop immediately' returns even with tasks queued (checks stop_requested); 'drain first' stops accepting new work but keeps processing while the queue is non-empty
choices.2: One uses a mutex, the other does not
choices.3: One is for C++17, the other for C++20
choices.4: They are identical

question: Why call cv.notify_all() during shutdown even though condition_variable_any is stop-aware?

choices.1: notify_all is the only way to wake any worker
choices.2: It ensures every sleeping worker promptly reevaluates the predicate
choices.3: It is required or the program crashes
choices.4: It cancels the stop request

question: How do you achieve interruptible waiting before C++20 (no stop_token)?

choices.1: Busy-spin on tasks.empty()
choices.2: Use a std::atomic<bool> stopping flag in the predicate plus cv.notify_all() on shutdown
choices.3: Use std::condition_variable_any anyway
choices.4: Use pthread_cancel

question: What is the modern, clean way to interrupt a thread in C++?

choices.1: Cooperative cancellation: std::jthread + std::stop_token, where the function checks stop.stop_requested() at safe points
choices.2: Set the thread priority to zero
choices.3: Throw an exception into the thread
choices.4: Forcefully kill it with TerminateThread / pthread_cancel

question: What does a std::jthread do automatically when it is destroyed?

choices.1: It detaches the thread
choices.2: Nothing; you must join manually
choices.3: It requests stop and then joins
choices.4: It throws if the thread is still running

question: Why is a task like doOneHugeBlockingOperation() not meaningfully interruptible?

choices.1: Because cooperative cancellation only works if the code periodically checks the stop token; code that blocks for 30s without checking can't be cancelled until it returns
choices.2: Because stop tokens only work with condition variables
choices.3: Because blocking operations ignore mutexes
choices.4: Because jthread can't pass a stop token to it

question: How do you make a long sleep interruptible?

choices.1: Lower the thread priority
choices.2: Use std::this_thread::sleep_for once with the full duration
choices.3: Use pthread_cancel during the sleep
choices.4: Break it into smaller waits that check stop.stop_requested(), or use a stop-aware cv.wait_for

question: Since std::future::get() is not stop-token-aware, how do you wait on another task interruptibly?

choices.1: Detach the producing thread
choices.2: Call get() and hope it returns
choices.3: Poll with future.wait_for(small_timeout) in a loop, checking stop.stop_requested() between polls
choices.4: Use pthread_cancel on the future

question: Why avoid forcibly killing threads (TerminateThread, pthread_cancel)?

choices.1: They are not available on Linux
choices.2: They can leave mutexes locked, memory half-mutated, files half-written, and invariants broken
choices.3: They require C++20
choices.4: They are slower than cooperative cancellation

question: What task signature does the note recommend for clean, composable interruptible threadpool tasks?

choices.1: using Task = void*
choices.2: using Task = std::packaged_task<void()>
choices.3: using Task = std::function<void()>
choices.4: using Task = std::function<void(std::stop_token)>, with workers running task(stop)

question: What is the simple way to make task B depend on task A in a threadpool?

choices.1: Submit A, then move A's std::future into B's lambda and call a.get() inside B
choices.2: Use a global mutex shared by A and B
choices.3: Submit B before A
choices.4: Run both on the same thread sequentially

question: How can naively blocking on dependent tasks deadlock the pool?

choices.1: If two tasks share a mutex
choices.2: If all worker threads are blocked waiting for other tasks that are queued but not yet running (e.g. a 1-thread pool where B occupies the only worker and waits for A)
choices.3: If get() is called from the main thread
choices.4: If the future is moved twice

question: For a simple dependency chain, what ordering avoids the deadlock?

choices.1: Submit dependent tasks before their dependencies
choices.2: Submit the dependencies first, then the dependent tasks
choices.3: Use a single-thread pool
choices.4: Submit everything from inside a single worker task

question: Why are then()-style continuations a better design than blocking inside a worker?

choices.1: They guarantee FIFO ordering
choices.2: They remove the need for futures
choices.3: Dependent tasks don't block (and waste) worker threads while waiting; the continuation runs only after the dependency completes
choices.4: They run faster on a single core

question: How do you handle a task that depends on MULTIPLE prior tasks?

choices.1: Merge the tasks into one before submitting
choices.2: Only one dependency is allowed per task
choices.3: Capture multiple futures in the dependent task and call get() on each (e.g. a.get() and b.get() before combine)
choices.4: Use a single shared future for all of them

question: What is the best general solution for complex dependencies in a robust threadpool?

choices.1: Call get() from the destructor
choices.2: Dependency-aware scheduling: represent dependencies explicitly and only enqueue a task after its dependencies finish (e.g. submit_after), so workers aren't wasted waiting
choices.3: Block inside workers but add more threads
choices.4: Always use a single worker thread

question: The simple move-the-future approach is fine under which conditions?

choices.1: Dependencies submitted before dependents, a simple dependency graph, enough worker threads, and tasks that don't recursively submit-and-wait on more pool tasks
choices.2: Only when tasks return void
choices.3: Any conditions; it never deadlocks
choices.4: Only with exactly one worker thread

question: How does a threadpool make a submitted task waitable?

choices.1: By blocking submit() until the task finishes
choices.2: By writing the result to a global variable
choices.3: By returning the thread id
choices.4: By wrapping the task in a std::packaged_task, storing it in the queue, and returning the task's std::future to the caller

question: What does std::packaged_task provide?

choices.1: It connects a callable to a std::future: the worker runs the packaged task while the submitter keeps the future
choices.2: A lock-free queue
choices.3: Automatic thread creation
choices.4: A stop token for cancellation

question: What is a key restriction on calling get() on a std::future?

choices.1: It never blocks
choices.2: It can only be called once on a given future
choices.3: It must be called before the task starts
choices.4: It can only be called from the main thread

question: How are exceptions from a submitted task handled via std::future?

choices.1: They are only logged
choices.2: They are swallowed silently
choices.3: They propagate automatically and are rethrown when you call future.get()
choices.4: They terminate the program

question: How do you wait for multiple submitted tasks?

choices.1: Call pool.join()
choices.2: Collect their futures (e.g. in a vector) and call get() on each
choices.3: Use a single future for all
choices.4: Submit them as one combined task

question: For a task with no return value, how is waiting handled?

choices.1: Use std::future<void>; get() (or wait()) blocks until done and rethrows any exception
choices.2: Poll a global flag
choices.3: Return a dummy int instead
choices.4: You cannot wait on void tasks

question: If you only need to wait for a BATCH to finish (no return values), what C++20 primitive is suggested?

choices.1: std::mutex
choices.2: std::future<void> for the whole batch
choices.3: std::atomic<int> with busy-waiting
choices.4: std::latch - initialize with the count, count_down() in each task, and wait()

question: What guarantee do std::atomic types provide?

choices.1: They make all surrounding code thread-safe automatically
choices.2: They let multiple threads access the same object without a data race, as long as all concurrent accesses to it are atomic; an atomic op is indivisible w.r.t. other atomic ops on that object
choices.3: They replace the need for any memory ordering
choices.4: They guarantee numerical reproducibility

question: Which statement about lock-freedom of std::atomic is correct?

choices.1: Every std::atomic<T> is guaranteed lock-free
choices.2: Most std::atomic<T> are NOT guaranteed lock-free (check is_lock_free() / is_always_lock_free); std::atomic_flag is the exception whose operations are required to be lock-free
choices.3: No atomic type is ever lock-free
choices.4: Only std::atomic<bool> is lock-free

question: What is the default memory order for most atomic operations?

choices.1: memory_order_relaxed
choices.2: memory_order_acquire
choices.3: memory_order_seq_cst
choices.4: memory_order_release

question: What is special about std::atomic_flag?

choices.1: It supports load(), store(), and arithmetic
choices.2: It has two states (set/clear) with test_and_set/clear, is required to be lock-free, and is meant for low-level test-and-set (e.g. spinlocks)
choices.3: It is the only atomic with fetch_add
choices.4: It is never lock-free

question: Why is compare_exchange_weak typically used inside a loop?

choices.1: Because it locks the atomic on each call
choices.2: Because it may fail spuriously even when the value matched expected, so the loop retries (and expected is updated with the current value on failure)
choices.3: Because it is slower than a single call
choices.4: Because it cannot update expected

question: Which operations does std::atomic<bool> NOT support?

choices.1: load() and store()
choices.2: exchange() and compare_exchange_*
choices.3: fetch_add/fetch_sub and ++/-- (it uses the primary template, not the integral specialization)
choices.4: wait()/notify_* in C++20

question: Which extra operations do atomic INTEGRAL specializations add over the primary template?

choices.1: None; they are identical
choices.2: Arithmetic RMW (fetch_add/fetch_sub) and bitwise RMW (fetch_and/fetch_or/fetch_xor), plus operators like ++, +=, &=
choices.3: Only pointer arithmetic
choices.4: Only floating-point min/max

question: What does std::atomic_ref<T> provide?

choices.1: A new atomic object that owns its value
choices.2: An atomic VIEW over an existing (non-atomic-typed) object; you must avoid mixing ordinary non-atomic access to that same object concurrently
choices.3: A reference-counted atomic pointer
choices.4: A lock-free guarantee for any T

question: What is the mental model for choosing atomics vs a mutex?

choices.1: Always use atomics; mutexes are obsolete
choices.2: Use atomics for a single small naturally-atomic value (counter, flag, pointer); use a mutex when you must protect multiple values together as one invariant
choices.3: Use a mutex for counters and atomics for vectors
choices.4: They are interchangeable in all cases

question: What is the difference between compare_exchange_strong and compare_exchange_weak?

choices.1: Strong fails only if the value doesn't match expected; weak may ALSO fail spuriously even when the value did match
choices.2: Weak compares two values; strong compares three
choices.3: Strong may fail spuriously; weak never fails
choices.4: They are identical in all respects

question: Why does the weak form exist at all?

choices.1: To save memory
choices.2: To map efficiently onto load-linked/store-conditional (LL/SC) architectures, where a reservation can be lost for reasons other than a value change
choices.3: Because strong CAS is not implementable
choices.4: To avoid using the expected parameter

question: On native CAS hardware like x86 (CMPXCHG), how do weak and strong compare?

choices.1: Weak is always much faster
choices.2: They often compile to essentially the same instruction, since there's no natural reason for a single CMPXCHG to fail when the value matched
choices.3: Strong is unavailable on x86
choices.4: Weak requires two instructions

question: Why can an LL/SC store-conditional fail even when the value still matched expected?

choices.1: Because LL/SC always fails the first time
choices.2: Because the reservation can be lost for non-value reasons: another core writing the reservation set, interrupts, context switches, reservation granularity, or microarchitectural limits
choices.3: Because store-conditional ignores the value entirely
choices.4: Because expected is never compared

question: On LL/SC hardware, why can compare_exchange_strong be more expensive than weak?

choices.1: Strong allocates memory
choices.2: Strong cannot expose a spurious SC failure as failure, so it may need an internal retry loop - wasted work when the caller already has an outer loop
choices.3: Strong uses two reservations
choices.4: Strong disables the cache

question: Why is weak the natural choice inside a typical CAS loop?

choices.1: Because weak never fails inside a loop
choices.2: The algorithm's outer loop already retries (another thread may legitimately win the race), so a spurious weak failure just causes a harmless retry
choices.3: Because weak skips the comparison
choices.4: Because strong cannot be used in loops

question: When is compare_exchange_strong the better choice?

choices.1: Always, since it's simpler
choices.2: For a one-shot decision where a spurious failure would change the meaning of the code (e.g. acquiring a lock with a single compare_exchange_strong)
choices.3: Only inside tight retry loops
choices.4: Never; weak is always preferable

question: What happens to the 'expected' argument when a compare-exchange fails?

choices.1: It is left unchanged
choices.2: It is updated with the current value of the atomic
choices.3: It is set to the desired value
choices.4: It is set to zero

question: What does an atomic fence (memory barrier) do?

choices.1: It atomically reads and writes a shared variable
choices.2: It controls memory ordering - it tells the compiler/CPU not to move certain memory operations across that point - without itself reading/writing a shared variable
choices.3: It provides mutual exclusion like a lock
choices.4: It makes ordinary variables atomic

question: What does a release fence do?

choices.1: It prevents later memory operations from being reordered before the fence
choices.2: It prevents earlier memory operations from being reordered after the fence (so writes before it are visible before a subsequent signal)
choices.3: It locks the atomic variable
choices.4: It makes all operations sequentially consistent

question: What does an acquire fence do?

choices.1: It prevents earlier operations from moving after the fence
choices.2: It prevents later memory operations from being reordered before the fence (so once you observe a signal, later reads stay after the synchronization point)
choices.3: It flushes the cache
choices.4: It atomically increments a counter

question: Does adding a fence make ordinary (non-atomic) shared variables safe to race on?

choices.1: Yes, a fence makes any nearby variable atomic
choices.2: No - a fence only constrains ordering; shared data still needs atomics, locks, or another synchronization mechanism to avoid a data race
choices.3: Yes, but only on x86
choices.4: Only if the fence is seq_cst

question: What does the note recommend over standalone fences for most code?

choices.1: Always use seq_cst fences
choices.2: Attach the ordering directly to atomic operations (store with memory_order_release, load with memory_order_acquire); reserve explicit fences for low-level lock-free code
choices.3: Use volatile variables instead
choices.4: Avoid atomics entirely

question: How does the same C++ fence differ across architectures like x86 vs ARM/POWER?

choices.1: It is illegal on ARM
choices.2: x86 has relatively strong ordering so many fences are cheap or compile-only; ARM/POWER are weakly ordered so fences often require real CPU barrier instructions
choices.3: ARM fences are always cheaper than x86
choices.4: Architecture has no effect on fences

question: Is a fence about mutual exclusion or ordering?

choices.1: Mutual exclusion - it locks a critical section
choices.2: Ordering - it creates an ordering boundary and does not lock anything
choices.3: Both equally
choices.4: Neither; it only affects the cache

question: For fences to enable inter-thread communication, what must they generally pair with?

choices.1: A mutex
choices.2: Matching atomic operations, so one thread's ordering is actually observed by another
choices.3: A condition variable
choices.4: A volatile read

question: When does an evaluation A happen-before B?

choices.1: Only when A and B are on the same thread
choices.2: When A is sequenced-before B (same thread), or A synchronizes-with B, or the relation follows transitively through other evaluations
choices.3: Only when both are seq_cst
choices.4: Whenever A executes earlier in wall-clock time

question: How does a release store create a happens-before edge with an acquire load?

choices.1: It never does
choices.2: If the acquire load reads the value written by the release store, the release store synchronizes-with the acquire load, creating a happens-before edge
choices.3: Only if both use relaxed ordering
choices.4: Only if they are on the same thread

question: How is 'strongly happens-before' stricter than 'happens-before' for the synchronizes-with case?

choices.1: It requires the operations to be on the same thread
choices.2: For the synchronizes-with case it requires BOTH operations to be memory_order_seq_cst
choices.3: It requires relaxed ordering
choices.4: It only applies to non-atomic operations

question: For an acquire/release pair where the acquire reads the release (not seq_cst), which holds?

choices.1: Both happens-before and strongly happens-before hold
choices.2: happens-before holds, but NOT necessarily strongly happens-before (since they aren't both seq_cst)
choices.3: Neither holds
choices.4: Only strongly happens-before holds

question: Why does 'strongly happens-before' exist?

choices.1: To make relaxed atomics synchronize
choices.2: To constrain the single global total order S over all memory_order_seq_cst operations (which must respect strongly happens-before, but not every ordinary happens-before)
choices.3: To replace happens-before entirely
choices.4: To order non-atomic writes

question: What do relaxed atomic operations contribute to happens-before?

choices.1: They create the same happens-before edges as acquire/release
choices.2: They are still atomic/indivisible, but by themselves create no synchronization or happens-before relationship
choices.3: They prevent all happens-before relationships
choices.4: They force a seq_cst total order

question: What is the simplest mental model contrasting the two relations?

choices.1: They are identical
choices.2: happens-before is the visibility relation (which writes a load can observe); strongly happens-before is the part of ordering strong enough to constrain sequential consistency
choices.3: happens-before constrains seq_cst; strongly happens-before is about visibility
choices.4: Both only matter for relaxed atomics

question: How does happens-before affect which value an atomic load may observe?

choices.1: It has no effect on observed values
choices.2: If an atomic write happens-before a later load, the load must see that write or a later write in the object's modification order
choices.3: The load always sees the initial value
choices.4: The load may see any write regardless of ordering

question: When you write x.fetch_add(1) on a std::atomic<int>, what does the compiler actually do?

choices.1: It wraps the int in a hidden mutex for every access
choices.2: It makes the int a special compiler-only type with no runtime cost
choices.3: It always asserts a physical bus lock for the whole operation
choices.4: It emits machine instruction(s) the target CPU guarantees are atomic (e.g. an atomic RMW instruction or an LL/SC loop)

question: What does the C++ standard actually specify about an atomic operation, leaving the rest to the implementation?

choices.1: That every atomic is lock-free
choices.2: That all atomics use sequential consistency
choices.3: That the access is indivisible relative to other atomic accesses to that object, and that memory ordering matches the requested std::memory_order
choices.4: The exact machine instructions to emit

question: Although a std::atomic<int> may be only 4 bytes, at what granularity does the cache-coherency system typically arbitrate ownership?

choices.1: Per individual byte
choices.2: Per virtual memory page
choices.3: Per cache line (commonly 64 bytes), which contains the atomic plus surrounding bytes
choices.4: Per 4-byte word matching the atomic's size

question: In the MESI protocol, what does the Modified (M) state mean for a cache line?

choices.1: Multiple cores hold clean copies
choices.2: This cache's copy is invalid and must be refetched
choices.3: This core has the only valid, dirty copy; main memory may be stale
choices.4: This core has the only valid, clean copy

question: With write-back caching, after x.store(42, release) succeeds in a core's cache, what is true of main memory (DRAM)?

choices.1: The value is written only to DRAM, never cached
choices.2: The store is lost unless flushed explicitly
choices.3: DRAM is immediately updated to 42 before the store returns
choices.4: DRAM may still hold the stale value; the new value can live in the core's cache in Modified state, and coherency supplies it to other readers later

question: On modern x86, what is the common-case behaviour of a `lock xadd`/`lock cmpxchg` on cached write-back memory within one cache line?

choices.1: It disables interrupts on all cores
choices.2: It always asserts a physical bus lock (LOCK#) for the whole system
choices.3: It uses 'cache locking': the core gains exclusive ownership of the cache line and coherency prevents other cores from modifying it, often without asserting the bus lock
choices.4: It falls back to a software mutex

question: Which situation still forces a real x86 bus lock rather than cheap cache locking?

choices.1: A relaxed-ordered load
choices.2: Any fetch_add regardless of alignment
choices.3: A naturally aligned atomic on write-back memory
choices.4: A split lock where the access crosses multiple cache lines, or access to non-write-back memory (UC/WC/WP/WT)

question: On classic ARM/RISC hardware without single-instruction atomics, how is a fetch_add typically implemented?

choices.1: A single locked add instruction like x86
choices.2: A load-linked/store-conditional (exclusive load/store) retry loop: ldaxr, add, stlxr, branch-back-on-failure
choices.3: By disabling the cache
choices.4: By calling into the OS kernel

question: What do ARM's Large System Extensions (LSE) add?

choices.1: Hardware transactional memory only
choices.2: Single-instruction atomics (e.g. CAS, atomic add) so the compiler can emit one-instruction RMWs instead of an LL/SC loop
choices.3: A larger cache line size
choices.4: They remove the need for cache coherency

question: What is the difference between cache coherency and memory ordering?

choices.1: Coherency is a compiler concept; ordering is a hardware concept
choices.2: They are two names for the same mechanism
choices.3: Coherency only matters for relaxed atomics; ordering only for seq_cst
choices.4: Coherency ensures cores eventually agree on the latest value of ONE location/cache line; memory ordering governs the order in which operations to DIFFERENT locations can be observed

question: Does memory_order_relaxed avoid the cost of obtaining exclusive cache-line ownership for an RMW under contention?

choices.1: Yes, relaxed makes the operation non-atomic and therefore free
choices.2: Yes, relaxed RMWs skip coherency entirely
choices.3: No - relaxed removes ordering constraints, not the need for exclusive ownership; a contended relaxed RMW still causes cache-line ping-pong
choices.4: No, but only because relaxed forces a bus lock

question: Why can two atomics on the same cache line slow each other down even when different threads touch different variables (false sharing)?

choices.1: Coherency tracks the whole cache line, so a write to one variable invalidates the other core's copy of the line; fix with alignas(64) / cache-line padding
choices.2: The two atomics share the same memory address
choices.3: Atomics are globally serialized regardless of address
choices.4: The compiler merges the two atomics into one

question: Why does a spinlock built on atomic_flag often spin on a relaxed test() before attempting test_and_set()?

choices.1: To avoid needing acquire/release semantics anywhere
choices.2: Because test() is more accurate than test_and_set()
choices.3: Because test_and_set cannot be used in a loop
choices.4: Plain reads can share the line in Shared state, while test_and_set (an RMW) needs exclusive ownership; read-spinning first reduces failed RMWs and coherency traffic

question: How do 'lock-free' and 'wait-free' differ at the hardware level per the note?

choices.1: They are identical guarantees
choices.2: Wait-free allows unbounded retries; lock-free does not
choices.3: Lock-free means the operation has zero cost
choices.4: Lock-free means the system as a whole makes progress (an LL/SC store-conditional may fail and retry many times under contention); wait-free means each thread completes in a bounded number of steps

question: According to the C++ memory model, when do two accesses to the same non-atomic object form a data race (undefined behaviour)?

choices.1: Whenever any object is shared between threads, even with synchronization
choices.2: Only when both accesses are writes through different pointers
choices.3: Whenever two threads read the same object simultaneously
choices.4: When the accesses are potentially concurrent, at least one is a write, at least one is non-atomic, and neither happens-before the other

question: What is a 'modification order' in the C++ atomics model?

choices.1: The order in which threads were created
choices.2: The per-atomic-object total order of all writes to that one object
choices.3: A single global total order across all atomic objects in the program
choices.4: The compiler's instruction-scheduling order

question: With two separate relaxed atomics x and y (both starting 0), thread 1 stores x=1 then loads y, thread 2 stores y=1 then loads x. Is the outcome r1==0 && r2==0 allowed?

choices.1: Yes - x and y each have their own modification order; there is no single global order relaxed ops must agree on
choices.2: No - the stores must be visible before the loads on any hardware
choices.3: No - relaxed atomics still impose one global total order across all objects
choices.4: Only if the two atomics share a cache line

question: Even with memory_order_relaxed, what 'per-object coherence' guarantee still holds for a single atomic? (Suppose x's modification order is 0->1->2.)

choices.1: If one relaxed load reads 2, a later relaxed load in the same thread cannot then read the older value 1
choices.2: None; relaxed means any read can return any prior value in any order
choices.3: Relaxed loads always return the initial value 0
choices.4: All threads must observe x change at the same wall-clock instant

question: Why are relaxed atomic operations described as NOT being synchronization operations?

choices.1: Because they are not actually atomic
choices.2: Because they provide atomicity and per-object modification order but create no happens-before/synchronizes-with edge between threads
choices.3: Because the compiler ignores them entirely
choices.4: Because they require a mutex internally

question: Can a compiler reorder two relaxed stores to the SAME atomic object x that are sequenced in one thread (x=1 then x=2)?

choices.1: No - per-object coherence requires x's modification order to be 0->1->2, so they can't be reordered to 0->2->1
choices.2: Yes, relaxed lets the compiler do anything
choices.3: Yes, because they target the same address
choices.4: Only on weakly-ordered hardware

question: How is the happens-before relation built in the C++ model?

choices.1: From the modification order of every object combined
choices.2: From sequenced-before (single-thread order), synchronizes-with (e.g. release/acquire), and transitivity
choices.3: Purely from wall-clock timestamps
choices.4: Only from mutex lock/unlock pairs

question: In what restricted situation does the standard guarantee sequential consistency in the simple, intuitive sense?

choices.1: Only for single-threaded programs
choices.2: For programs that use only relaxed atomics
choices.3: For any program that uses atomics at all
choices.4: For data-race-free programs that use exclusively memory_order_seq_cst atomic operations

question: What are the three logical steps of an atomic read-modify-write (RMW) operation, performed indivisibly?

choices.1: Read the current value, compute a new value from it, write the new value back
choices.2: Allocate, copy, free
choices.3: Load, fence, store
choices.4: Lock, compute, unlock

question: Why is `x.store(x.load() + 1)` wrong for a shared counter where fetch_add(1) is right?

choices.1: store and load are not atomic individually
choices.2: The load and store are each atomic but the pair is not one indivisible operation, so two threads can read the same old value and lose an increment
choices.3: fetch_add is slower but always correct
choices.4: load() returns the new value, causing double counting

question: What does fetch_add return?

choices.1: The old value, i.e. the value immediately before the operation
choices.2: A boolean success flag
choices.3: The new value after the addition
choices.4: The amount added

question: For compare_exchange, how do success and failure differ in what kind of atomic operation occurs?

choices.1: A successful compare_exchange is an atomic RMW; a failed one is only an atomic load (and updates 'expected' with the observed value)
choices.2: Both are plain non-atomic operations
choices.3: Both are always full RMWs
choices.4: Success is a load; failure is a store

question: Why must the FAILURE memory order of compare_exchange not be release or acq_rel?

choices.1: Because release is slower on failure
choices.2: Because failure is undefined behaviour otherwise
choices.3: Because failure performs only a load, and release/acq_rel imply a store
choices.4: Because the failure order must always be seq_cst

question: How do atomic RMW operations relate to a release sequence?

choices.1: They have no relationship to release sequences
choices.2: Only seq_cst RMWs can be in a release sequence
choices.3: They terminate any release sequence immediately
choices.4: Atomic RMW operations on the same object can EXTEND a release sequence begun by a release store, so a later acquire reading from them still synchronizes with the original release

question: In a lock-free push using compare_exchange_weak in a loop, why does the loop work correctly on spurious or contended failure?

choices.1: Because on failure compare_exchange updates 'expected' (old_head) with the current value, so the loop retries against the latest head
choices.2: Because failure switches to compare_exchange_strong automatically
choices.3: Because weak CAS never fails
choices.4: Because the node is freed and reallocated each time

question: What is the role of the synchronizes-with relationship?

choices.1: It is a direct cross-thread edge that, combined with sequenced-before and transitivity, builds happens-before so one thread's work becomes visible to another
choices.2: It guarantees two threads run at the same time
choices.3: It replaces the need for atomics
choices.4: It orders operations within a single thread

question: For a release store to synchronize-with an acquire load on the same atomic, what must be true?

choices.1: The acquire load must take its value from the release operation or its release sequence
choices.2: The store must happen later in wall-clock time
choices.3: They must be on the same thread
choices.4: Both must use relaxed ordering

question: A release store publishes data, but the consumer's acquire load reads the OLD (pre-store) value. Is there a synchronizes-with edge?

choices.1: Yes, but only on x86
choices.2: Yes, the acquire always synchronizes regardless of value read
choices.3: Only if the data is also atomic
choices.4: No - because the acquire load did not read the value from the release store, no synchronizes-with edge is established

question: Does a release store synchronize-with a RELAXED load that reads its value?

choices.1: Only if they are on the same thread
choices.2: No - the load must be an acquire (or seq_cst) operation; a relaxed load does not establish synchronizes-with
choices.3: Yes, reading the value is sufficient
choices.4: Yes, because the store was release

question: A release store to x is followed by a relaxed fetch_add on x (making it 2); a third thread's acquire load reads 2. Can it synchronize with the original release?

choices.1: No, release sequences require seq_cst
choices.2: Only if the third thread also writes x
choices.3: No, because the RMW used relaxed ordering
choices.4: Yes - the relaxed RMW continues the release sequence, so an acquire reading from it synchronizes with the original release store

question: When does synchronizes-with also contribute to 'strongly happens-before'?

choices.1: Only when both synchronized operations are sequentially consistent atomic operations
choices.2: Always, whenever it contributes to happens-before
choices.3: Only for relaxed operations
choices.4: Never; strongly happens-before ignores synchronizes-with

question: What does a release operation guarantee about earlier memory operations?

choices.1: It allows them to be reordered after it for speed
choices.2: It prevents earlier memory operations from being reordered after it, so prior writes are published before the atomic value is observed
choices.3: It discards them
choices.4: It makes them atomic

question: What does an acquire operation guarantee about later memory operations?

choices.1: It prevents later memory operations from being reordered before it, so after observing the released value the thread sees the prior writes
choices.2: It allows later reads to move before the load
choices.3: It flushes the cache to DRAM
choices.4: It makes later writes atomic

question: What is the central rule of acquire-release synchronization?

choices.1: Any two atomic ops synchronize regardless of value
choices.2: Release and acquire must use the same thread
choices.3: Acquire-release only works with seq_cst
choices.4: If an acquire operation observes a value written by a release operation, then everything before the release happens-before everything after the acquire

question: Why must the acquire and release operations act on the SAME atomic object?

choices.1: For performance only
choices.2: The atomic object is the synchronization channel; an acquire on one atomic does not acquire releases made to a different, unrelated atomic
choices.3: They don't have to; any two atomics synchronize
choices.4: Because different atomics share a modification order

question: How does acquire-release ordering chain across three threads (A releases, B acquires then releases, C acquires)?

choices.1: By transitivity of happens-before: A's writes -> B observes -> B publishes -> C observes, so C sees A's earlier writes too
choices.2: Only if all three use the same atomic variable
choices.3: Only with seq_cst on every operation
choices.4: It doesn't chain; each pair is isolated

question: In a spinlock built on atomic_flag, why does lock() use acquire and unlock() use release?

choices.1: For symmetry only, with no semantic effect
choices.2: lock should use release and unlock acquire instead
choices.3: unlock's release publishes the critical section's writes; lock's acquire ensures the new owner sees the previous owner's writes
choices.4: Both should be relaxed for speed

question: How does acquire-release differ from sequential consistency?

choices.1: seq_cst is weaker than acquire-release
choices.2: Acquire-release provides a single global order; seq_cst does not
choices.3: Acquire-release orders only through matching synchronization edges; seq_cst additionally puts all seq_cst operations into a single global total order (stronger, sometimes more expensive)
choices.4: They are identical

question: For a read-modify-write that must both consume prior state and publish new state, which order is appropriate?

choices.1: memory_order_acq_rel (acquire on the read, release on the write)
choices.2: memory_order_release only
choices.3: memory_order_acquire only
choices.4: memory_order_relaxed

question: What is the key difference between memory_order_consume and memory_order_acquire on a load?

choices.1: consume is for stores; acquire is for loads
choices.2: They are identical in every respect
choices.3: consume orders ALL later operations; acquire orders only dependent ones
choices.4: acquire orders all later operations in the consumer thread; consume orders only operations that carry a dependency on the loaded value

question: After p = published.load(consume), which access is guaranteed ordered, and which is not?

choices.1: Neither is ordered
choices.2: p->payload (depends on p) is ordered; an unrelated side_counter.load(relaxed) (no dependency on p) is not generally protected
choices.3: Both p->payload and an unrelated side_counter load are guaranteed
choices.4: Only the unrelated side_counter load is ordered

question: What makes p->payload 'dependent' on a consume-loaded pointer p?

choices.1: It is written before the load
choices.2: It is an atomic access
choices.3: The address of p->payload cannot be computed without the value of p, so it carries a dependency
choices.4: It uses the same memory order

question: What does the note recommend for portable real-world C++ regarding consume?

choices.1: Usually use memory_order_acquire instead; consume is hard to specify/implement and current compilers essentially map it to acquire
choices.2: Always use consume for pointer-based publication
choices.3: Use seq_cst for all loads to avoid consume
choices.4: Use relaxed instead of consume

question: What is the status of memory_order_consume per the WG21 direction noted?

choices.1: It is the recommended default for loads
choices.2: Its current definition is widely considered not useful; there is a proposal to make it behave the same as acquire
choices.3: It was removed in C++20
choices.4: It is faster than acquire on all hardware

question: What does memory_order_relaxed guarantee?

choices.1: Atomicity, no data race on that object, and a per-object modification order - but no ordering/visibility for other memory
choices.2: Full ordering of all surrounding memory operations
choices.3: A single global order across all atomics
choices.4: Nothing; it is non-atomic

question: Why is using a relaxed store as a 'ready' flag for published data unsafe?

choices.1: Relaxed flags deadlock
choices.2: A consumer seeing ready==true via a relaxed load is not guaranteed to see the prior non-atomic writes to data (no publication)
choices.3: Relaxed stores are not atomic
choices.4: Relaxed stores always tear

question: Which is a GOOD use case for relaxed atomics?

choices.1: Implementing a lock's acquire/release
choices.2: Signaling that initialization is complete
choices.3: Publishing a fully-initialized object to other threads
choices.4: A statistics/event counter or unique-ID generator where only the atomic value itself matters

question: Why is 'relaxed means random' a misconception?

choices.1: Because per-object coherence still holds: for one atomic, if a reader sees value 2 it can't subsequently see the older value 1
choices.2: Because relaxed forbids any reordering at all
choices.3: Because relaxed acts as a full memory barrier
choices.4: Because relaxed is actually the strongest order

question: What is the rule of thumb for when relaxed is appropriate?

choices.1: Whenever you want maximum performance, regardless of use
choices.2: Only when the atomic is NOT used to communicate ownership, readiness, initialization, or protection of other data
choices.3: Only inside a mutex
choices.4: Only for boolean flags

question: What does memory_order_seq_cst provide?

choices.1: The behavior as if all seq_cst operations from all threads are placed in one single global order that every thread observes consistently
choices.2: Ordering only for the single atomic touched
choices.3: Atomicity only, like relaxed
choices.4: A per-object order but no cross-object order

question: In the classic two-flag test (T1: store x=1 then load y; T2: store y=1 then load x), all seq_cst - is r1==0 && r2==0 possible?

choices.1: No - seq_cst forbids it, because allowing both zero loads would create an impossible cycle in the single global order
choices.2: Only if x and y share a cache line
choices.3: Yes, on weakly-ordered hardware
choices.4: Yes, seq_cst doesn't constrain different objects

question: How can seq_cst be summarized relative to acquire/release?

choices.1: It only adds atomicity
choices.2: Roughly acquire/release behavior PLUS participation in one globally consistent total order of all seq_cst operations
choices.3: It is weaker than acquire/release
choices.4: It is identical to relaxed plus a fence

question: Does seq_cst make ordinary non-atomic concurrent access safe?

choices.1: Yes, but only for integers
choices.2: Yes, if at least one seq_cst atomic exists in the program
choices.3: Yes, seq_cst protects all nearby data
choices.4: No - it only orders atomic operations; unsynchronized non-atomic concurrent access (e.g. two threads doing data++) is still a data race

question: When does the note recommend using seq_cst?

choices.1: When correctness matters more than peak performance, or while still designing/debugging the synchronization - it gives the simplest mental model
choices.2: Never; acquire/release is always better
choices.3: Always, even in the hottest loops
choices.4: Only for relaxed counters

question: What is the difference between a mutex and a lock in modern C++?

choices.1: A mutex is the synchronization object; a lock is usually an RAII wrapper that locks/unlocks the mutex for you
choices.2: A lock is the primitive; a mutex is the wrapper
choices.3: A mutex is only for reading; a lock is for writing
choices.4: They are the same thing

question: Why prefer std::lock_guard over manual m.lock()/m.unlock()?

choices.1: It is RAII and therefore exception-safe: the mutex is released automatically at scope exit even if the body throws or returns early
choices.2: It supports timed locking
choices.3: It is faster at runtime
choices.4: It allows recursive locking

question: When should you choose std::unique_lock over std::lock_guard?

choices.1: Only when locking multiple mutexes
choices.2: For every lock, since it's strictly better
choices.3: Only for shared/read locks
choices.4: When you need flexibility: deferred locking, early unlock/relock, timed locking, movability, or condition-variable compatibility

question: What is the main advantage of std::scoped_lock when locking multiple mutexes?

choices.1: It supports early unlock
choices.2: It locks all the mutexes using a deadlock-avoidance algorithm (like std::lock), avoiding lock-ordering deadlocks
choices.3: It is movable
choices.4: It allows shared/read access

question: What does std::shared_mutex provide?

choices.1: Recursive locking for one thread
choices.2: Timed locking only
choices.3: Only exclusive locking, like std::mutex
choices.4: Two modes: shared ownership (multiple concurrent readers via shared_lock) and exclusive ownership (one writer via unique_lock)

question: Why must you re-check the condition after releasing a shared_lock and taking a unique_lock on a std::shared_mutex?

choices.1: Standard shared mutexes don't support direct shared-to-exclusive upgrade, so in the gap between unlocking shared and locking exclusive, another thread may have changed the data
choices.2: Because shared_lock is not RAII
choices.3: Because unique_lock is slower
choices.4: Because the mutex becomes recursive

question: What is a common downside of std::recursive_mutex?

choices.1: It cannot be locked at all
choices.2: It can hide design problems, making it harder to reason about which function protects the object's invariant; redesign is often cleaner
choices.3: It only works with shared_lock
choices.4: It is not exception-safe

question: What does the std::defer_lock tag do when constructing a unique_lock?

choices.1: Locks immediately and adopts ownership
choices.2: Tries to lock without blocking
choices.3: Constructs the lock object WITHOUT locking the mutex yet; you lock later with lock()
choices.4: Assumes the mutex is already locked

question: What does std::try_lock(a, b) return?

choices.1: true if all locked, false otherwise
choices.2: The number of mutexes locked
choices.3: A pointer to the failed mutex
choices.4: -1 if all locks were acquired; otherwise the zero-based index of the object it failed to lock (releasing any it already took)

question: Why is `std::lock_guard<std::mutex>{m};` (an unnamed temporary) a bug?

choices.1: It fails to compile
choices.2: The temporary is destroyed immediately, so the mutex is unlocked right away instead of being held for the scope
choices.3: It locks the mutex twice
choices.4: It makes the mutex recursive

question: Per the note, can std::mutex::try_lock fail even when the mutex is currently free?

choices.1: Yes - try_lock is allowed to fail spuriously, so a failure should be treated only as 'not acquired', not proof another thread owns it
choices.2: No, only timed mutexes can fail
choices.3: Yes, but only on recursive mutexes
choices.4: No, try_lock is always exact

question: What does a semaphore's counter represent?

choices.1: Whether the resource is dirty
choices.2: The owning thread's ID
choices.3: The number of threads currently blocked
choices.4: How many permits (units of a resource) are available

question: What do acquire() and release() do on a semaphore?

choices.1: Both increment the counter
choices.2: acquire decrements the counter (waiting if it is 0); release increments it and wakes a waiter
choices.3: acquire increments and waits; release decrements
choices.4: acquire locks a mutex; release unlocks it

question: What is a key ownership difference between a semaphore and a mutex?

choices.1: A semaphore must be released by the same thread that acquired it
choices.2: A mutex has no owner; a semaphore does
choices.3: A semaphore has no strict ownership - one thread can acquire and a different thread can release, which makes it useful for signaling
choices.4: Neither has any concept of ownership

question: In the producer-consumer example, what is the division of labour between the semaphore and the mutex?

choices.1: The semaphore signals that an item is available; the mutex protects the queue's internal state
choices.2: The semaphore protects the queue; the mutex signals availability
choices.3: The semaphore replaces the queue entirely
choices.4: Both protect the queue redundantly

question: What is the rule of thumb for choosing a semaphore vs a mutex?

choices.1: Use a semaphore for protecting a single variable; a mutex for counting
