choices.2: When ownership must be obvious, you need the results multiple times (views may recompute on repeated iteration), or debugging simplicity matters
choices.3: Only when the data is sorted
choices.4: Whenever you filter or transform data

question: How do you materialize a view into an owning container?

choices.1: Views cannot be materialized; you must rebuild from the source
choices.2: Assign the view directly to a vector with operator=
choices.3: Call r.to_owning() which every view provides
choices.4: Construct a container from the view's iterators, e.g. std::vector<int> out(std::ranges::begin(r), std::ranges::end(r))

question: Technically, what is the difference between a 'view' and an 'adaptor'?

choices.1: A view owns data while an adaptor is always non-owning storage
choices.2: The view is the resulting range object; the adaptor is the transformation (filter, transform, take) you apply to a range
choices.3: The view is the transformation; the adaptor is the resulting object
choices.4: They are exactly the same thing with two names

question: What two things does a std::shared_ptr<T> store?

choices.1: Just a single pointer to the managed object
choices.2: A pointer (what get()/operator-> expose) and a pointer to a control block holding the ownership metadata
choices.3: The object inline plus a reference count
choices.4: A pointer to the object and an inline copy of the deleter

question: When is the managed object destroyed?

choices.1: When the control block is freed
choices.2: When the weak count reaches 0
choices.3: When the strong reference count transitions from 1 to 0 (no shared_ptr owns it anymore)
choices.4: As soon as any one shared_ptr to it is destroyed

question: When is the control block itself destroyed?

choices.1: Immediately after the managed object's destructor runs, regardless of weak_ptrs
choices.2: When both the strong count and the weak count have reached 0
choices.3: When the first weak_ptr is created
choices.4: As soon as the strong count reaches 0

question: Why is constructing two shared_ptrs directly from the same raw pointer (shared_ptr<Foo> p1(raw); p2(raw);) a bug?

choices.1: It compiles but raw is leaked because neither deletes it
choices.2: It shares one control block but the strong count is double-counted
choices.3: Each constructor creates an independent control block, so both think they own raw and both delete it — a double free
choices.4: It is fine; the two control blocks coordinate automatically

question: What does the aliasing constructor (e.g. shared_ptr<int>(wrapper, &wrapper->value)) demonstrate?

choices.1: It copies the int out of the Wrapper into a new allocation
choices.2: It converts a shared_ptr<Wrapper> into a shared_ptr<int> by reinterpret_cast
choices.3: The pointer get() exposes can differ from the object the control block owns/deletes — the int is observed while the Wrapper is kept alive
choices.4: It makes two control blocks for the same object

question: Where is a shared_ptr's custom deleter stored, and why does that matter for sizeof?

choices.1: In a global registry keyed by pointer value
choices.2: It is not stored; the deleter type must be known at the delete site
choices.3: Inline in every shared_ptr copy, so sizeof grows with the deleter
choices.4: In the control block, not in each shared_ptr — so sizeof(shared_ptr) stays ~two pointers even with a large deleter (unlike unique_ptr, which stores the deleter in the object)

question: What problem does std::enable_shared_from_this solve?

choices.1: It makes the object's members thread-safe
choices.2: It automatically breaks reference cycles
choices.3: It removes the need for a control block entirely
choices.4: It lets an object hand out a shared_ptr to itself (shared_from_this) that reuses the existing control block, instead of shared_ptr<T>(this) which would create a separate control block

question: Why can std::shared_ptr not reclaim a cycle of nodes that reference each other?

choices.1: Because cycles overflow the atomic counter
choices.2: Because the control block is shared, deletion is ambiguous
choices.3: Reference counting never drops the strong counts to 0 because each node keeps the other alive; use weak_ptr for the non-owning/back link
choices.4: It can — shared_ptr has a built-in cycle collector

question: What is true about the thread-safety of shared_ptr's reference count versus the managed object?

choices.1: Neither the count nor the object is thread-safe
choices.2: The object is thread-safe but the count is not
choices.3: The reference-count machinery is atomic and thread-safe (separate shared_ptr objects sharing one control block are safe to copy/destroy concurrently), but the pointed-to object is not automatically thread-safe
choices.4: Both the count and the object are fully thread-safe

question: How does moving a shared_ptr differ from copying it?

choices.1: A move transfers ownership (stealing ptr/ctrl and nulling the source) with no atomic count change, while a copy atomically increments the strong count
choices.2: A move atomically increments while a copy does not
choices.3: Both increment the strong count
choices.4: A move increments the weak count instead of the strong count

question: What is the key allocation difference between std::make_shared<T>(args) and std::shared_ptr<T>(new T(args))?

choices.1: Both always perform exactly one allocation
choices.2: make_shared usually allocates the control block and the T object together in one allocation; the new T form does two separate allocations
choices.3: make_shared does two allocations while new T does one
choices.4: make_shared never allocates; it uses the stack

question: Which is NOT a benefit of make_shared's single combined allocation?

choices.1: Cleaner exception safety during construction
choices.2: It lets you supply a custom deleter that make_shared otherwise cannot
choices.3: Fewer allocations (one instead of two)
choices.4: Better cache locality between the control block and object

question: With make_shared, what is the lifetime consequence of a lingering weak_ptr after the last shared_ptr dies?

choices.1: The object stays fully alive until the weak_ptr is gone
choices.2: The weak_ptr is automatically converted to a shared_ptr to keep T alive
choices.3: The combined allocation is freed immediately, invalidating the weak_ptr's control block
choices.4: The object's destructor runs, but the combined allocation (including the storage for T) cannot be freed until all weak_ptrs are gone — so a large T's memory stays occupied

question: Why can't std::make_shared take a custom deleter?

choices.1: It constructs T inside the combined control block and uses its own destruction path, so there is no overload to pass a deleter — use shared_ptr<T>(p, deleter) or move from a unique_ptr instead
choices.2: It can, via make_shared<T>(args..., deleter)
choices.3: Because the deleter would have to be stored in every shared_ptr copy
choices.4: Custom deleters are incompatible with reference counting

question: What is std::allocate_shared?

choices.1: A version of make_shared that takes a custom deleter
choices.2: An overload that always uses two allocations
choices.3: The allocator-aware sibling of make_shared: it allocates the combined control block/object using a provided allocator
choices.4: A function that shares an allocator across all shared_ptrs

question: If type X defines a class-specific operator new/delete, how do shared_ptr<X>(new X) and make_shared<X>() differ?

choices.1: shared_ptr<X>(new X) uses X::operator new, but make_shared<X> allocates one internal block and placement-constructs X, so it does not use X's class-specific allocation
choices.2: Both always use X::operator new
choices.3: Neither uses X::operator new; both call malloc directly
choices.4: make_shared uses X::operator new twice

question: Why can std::make_shared<Widget>(...) fail to compile when Widget's constructor is private (even inside a Widget static method)?

choices.1: Because make_shared requires a public destructor, not constructor
choices.2: Private constructors are always inaccessible to any factory
choices.3: The actual constructor call happens inside the library's make_shared, which is not a friend of Widget; shared_ptr<Widget>(new Widget(...)) works because the new-expression is in the member function
choices.4: make_shared cannot allocate private types at all

question: What does std::make_shared_for_overwrite<T>() (C++20) provide?

choices.1: Same combined-allocation family as make_shared, but with default-initialization semantics that skip value-initialization when you intend to overwrite the object immediately
choices.2: A thread-safe overwrite of an existing shared_ptr
choices.3: A way to overwrite the control block's deleter at runtime
choices.4: A make_shared that accepts a custom deleter

question: When should you prefer something other than make_shared?

choices.1: Always — shared_ptr<T>(new T) is the modern default
choices.2: Only when the type is trivially copyable
choices.3: When you need a custom deleter, class-specific operator new/delete, private-constructor access, the object's memory freed as soon as the last strong owner dies, ownership of an existing object, or aliasing
choices.4: Only in single-threaded programs

question: What is std::make_unique<Foo>(1,2,3) roughly equivalent to?

choices.1: std::unique_ptr<Foo>(new Foo[3])
choices.2: std::unique_ptr<Foo>(new Foo(1,2,3)) — it allocates, constructs Foo with forwarded args, and wraps the pointer
choices.3: std::shared_ptr<Foo>(new Foo(1,2,3))
choices.4: A stack-allocated Foo with automatic storage

question: What exception-safety problem does make_unique avoid in f(std::unique_ptr<Foo>(new Foo), g())?

choices.1: Pre-C++17, g() could be evaluated after new Foo succeeds but before the unique_ptr wraps it; if g() throws, the raw Foo leaks. make_unique never exposes a raw owning pointer between new and the wrapper
choices.2: It avoids a double free if g() succeeds
choices.3: It avoids constructing Foo twice
choices.4: It guarantees g() is evaluated first

question: How can std::make_unique<X>(1,2) and std::unique_ptr<X>(new X{1,2}) call different constructors?

choices.1: make_unique uses parentheses (new X(1,2)), while the brace form new X{1,2} prefers an initializer_list constructor — so they can select different overloads
choices.2: make_unique always uses braces, the other always uses parentheses
choices.3: They always call the same constructor; the syntax is cosmetic
choices.4: make_unique calls the move constructor, the other the copy constructor

question: How do std::make_unique<int[]>(10) and std::unique_ptr<int[]>(new int[10]) differ in initialization?

choices.1: make_unique leaves them uninitialized while new int[10] zeroes them
choices.2: make_unique<int[]>(10) value-initializes the elements (zeroes ints), while new int[10] default-initializes them, leaving fundamental types with indeterminate values
choices.3: Both leave the elements uninitialized
choices.4: Both zero-initialize the elements

question: What happens with std::make_unique<int[10]>() (a known-bound array)?

choices.1: It value-initializes 10 ints like the unbounded form
choices.2: It is deleted (ill-formed); use make_unique<int[]>(10) or std::vector<int>(10) instead
choices.3: It allocates a unique_ptr<int[10]> normally
choices.4: It is identical to make_unique<int[]>(10)

question: Can std::make_unique specify a custom deleter?

choices.1: Yes, via make_unique<T, MyDeleter>()
choices.2: Yes, by passing the deleter as the last argument
choices.3: Only for array types
choices.4: No — it always uses std::default_delete<T>; for a custom deleter you construct the unique_ptr yourself

question: What does std::make_unique_for_overwrite<T>() (C++20) do?

choices.1: Makes the unique_ptr copyable for overwriting
choices.2: Allocates the object together with a control block
choices.3: Avoids value-initialization when you intend to overwrite the object/array immediately — useful for large buffers
choices.4: Adds a custom deleter to make_unique

question: Unlike make_shared, what does make_unique NOT do?

choices.1: It does not combine the object with any control block — unique_ptr has no reference-counting control block, so make_unique just allocates the object
choices.2: It does not call the object's constructor
choices.3: It does not return by value
choices.4: It does not forward constructor arguments

question: Why does std::unique_ptr<Foo> p = new Foo(42); fail to compile?

choices.1: Because unique_ptr has no constructor taking a pointer
choices.2: Because Foo must be trivially copyable
choices.3: Because new Foo(42) returns a Foo, not a Foo*
choices.4: The unique_ptr constructor from a raw pointer is explicit, so implicit conversion from Foo* is forbidden; use unique_ptr<Foo> p(new Foo(42)) or make_unique

question: What ownership model does std::unique_ptr represent?

choices.1: Borrowed ownership that must be returned
choices.2: Shared ownership via reference counting
choices.3: Non-owning observation of a shared object
choices.4: Exclusive ownership — exactly one unique_ptr owns the object, and it destroys the object when the unique_ptr dies

question: Why is unique_ptr's copy constructor deleted while its move constructor is allowed?

choices.1: Both are deleted; you must use release() to transfer
choices.2: Copying would make two owners that both delete the object (double free); moving transfers ownership and leaves the source null, preserving exclusivity
choices.3: Copying is allowed; only assignment is deleted
choices.4: Moving is deleted because it would leave a dangling source

question: Why is sizeof(std::unique_ptr<T>) with the default deleter usually just sizeof(T*)?

choices.1: The deleter is stored in a global table, not the object
choices.2: std::default_delete is an empty, stateless type, so implementations use empty-base optimization / [[no_unique_address]] to store it in zero bytes
choices.3: unique_ptr drops the pointer and stores only the deleter
choices.4: The standard guarantees unique_ptr is exactly one pointer

question: What does unique_ptr::release() do?

choices.1: Returns the raw pointer while keeping ownership
choices.2: Deletes the managed object and returns nullptr
choices.3: Resets the pointer to a new object
choices.4: Returns the raw pointer and gives up ownership without deleting the object — the caller becomes responsible for it

question: Where is a unique_ptr's deleter stored, and how does that contrast with shared_ptr?

choices.1: Inside the unique_ptr object itself, so a stateful or function-pointer deleter increases sizeof; shared_ptr stores its deleter in the control block instead
choices.2: Nowhere — unique_ptr cannot have a custom deleter
choices.3: In a global registry shared by all unique_ptrs
choices.4: In the control block, exactly like shared_ptr

question: How can a unique_ptr manage something that is not a normal object pointer, like an OS HANDLE?

choices.1: By storing the HANDLE in the control block
choices.2: It cannot; unique_ptr only manages T*
choices.3: A custom deleter can define a member type 'pointer' (e.g. HANDLE), and unique_ptr uses Deleter::pointer if present instead of T*
choices.4: By reinterpret_cast-ing the HANDLE to T* internally

question: Why does p.reset(p.get()) constitute a bug?

choices.1: It leaks the object instead of deleting it
choices.2: reset deletes the currently owned object and then stores the passed pointer — which is the same now-dangling pointer, so it may be deleted again later
choices.3: It is fine and is the idiomatic way to refresh a unique_ptr
choices.4: reset cannot take any argument

question: Why must a class using unique_ptr<Impl> with an incomplete Impl (PImpl) define its destructor in the .cpp?

choices.1: default_delete needs the complete type of Impl to destroy it, and that completeness is only available where Impl is defined (the .cpp)
choices.2: Because incomplete types cannot be members at all
choices.3: Because the constructor must also be in the .cpp
choices.4: Because unique_ptr is not movable with incomplete types

question: What is required for std::unique_ptr<Base> b = std::move(derivedPtr); to be safe?

choices.1: Derived must define a custom deleter
choices.2: Base and Derived must be the same size
choices.3: The conversion is never safe and should be avoided
choices.4: Base should have a virtual destructor, so deleting the Derived through the Base pointer runs the derived destructor

question: What is std::weak_ptr<T>?

choices.1: A unique_ptr that allows multiple observers
choices.2: A non-owning observer of a shared_ptr-managed object that does not increase the strong count, so it does not keep the object alive
choices.3: A shared_ptr that owns the object but cannot dereference it
choices.4: A raw pointer with automatic deletion

question: What does weak_ptr::lock() do?

choices.1: Permanently converts the weak_ptr into a shared_ptr
choices.2: Locks a mutex protecting the object
choices.3: Atomically attempts to promote the weak reference into a shared_ptr, returning a valid one if the object is still alive or an empty shared_ptr if it has expired
choices.4: Returns the raw pointer without any liveness check

question: Why must lock() use an atomic compare-and-swap rather than 'if (strong>0) ++strong'?

choices.1: Because ++ on an atomic is not allowed in C++
choices.2: Because incrementing must use acquire ordering, which only CAS provides
choices.3: Because the weak count and strong count must be updated together
choices.4: Between the check and the increment another thread could destroy the last shared_ptr and the object; CAS increments only if the count is still nonzero, forbidding the 0->1 transition that would resurrect a dead object

question: What is the classic reason weak_ptr exists?

choices.1: To provide thread-safe access to the managed object
choices.2: To make shared_ptr copies cheaper
choices.3: To break shared_ptr reference cycles by making a back/non-owning link a weak_ptr so strong counts can reach zero
choices.4: To allow a shared_ptr to be stored in a vector

question: A weak_ptr keeps what alive, and under what condition?

choices.1: Nothing — weak_ptrs hold no references at all
choices.2: The object — it stays alive while any weak_ptr exists
choices.3: Both the object and control block until the last weak_ptr dies
choices.4: The control block — which survives while weak_count > 0; the object itself is gone once strong_count reaches 0

question: What is the role of the 'implicit weak reference' in the control block?

choices.1: While strong_count > 0, the control block holds one implicit weak reference, so it survives even when there are no explicit weak_ptrs
choices.2: It is a debug-only field with no runtime effect
choices.3: It counts the number of lock() calls in flight
choices.4: It is an extra strong reference that prevents the object from being destroyed

question: Why is weak_ptr::expired() only advisory in multithreaded code?

choices.1: expired() blocks until the object is destroyed
choices.2: It is fully reliable; lock() is the advisory one
choices.3: Another thread can destroy the last shared_ptr between expired() returning false and your next use; the correct pattern is if (auto sp = wp.lock()) which atomically checks and acquires
choices.4: expired() returns a random value under contention

question: Why does weak_ptr not provide operator* or operator->?

choices.1: Because weak_ptr stores no pointer to the object
choices.2: After the object is destroyed the stored raw pointer is invalid to dereference, so you must go through lock() and use the resulting shared_ptr only if it is non-empty
choices.3: Because operator-> is reserved for unique_ptr
choices.4: Because dereferencing a weak_ptr would increment the strong count

question: When is the object destroyed versus the control block, in shared/weak terms?

choices.1: The control block is destroyed first, then the object
choices.2: The object is destroyed when strong_count reaches 0; the control block is destroyed when weak_count reaches 0
choices.3: The object is destroyed when weak_count reaches 0
choices.4: Both are destroyed when strong_count reaches 0

question: What is the Small String Optimization (SSO)?

choices.1: Storing all strings in a shared global pool
choices.2: Short strings are stored in an inline buffer inside the string object itself, with no heap allocation (~15 chars on libstdc++/libc++)
choices.3: A cache that deduplicates identical string literals
choices.4: Compression of long strings to fit in fewer bytes

question: Why does SSO matter for latency?

choices.1: Allocation is expensive (allocator call, possible lock, cache-cold memory); SSO makes the common short-string case allocation-free and cache-local — ideal for keys/symbols
choices.2: It guarantees strings are always null-terminated
choices.3: It allows strings to be shared between threads without locks
choices.4: It makes long strings grow faster

question: What is a trade-off of SSO?

choices.1: It forces every string to allocate at least once
choices.2: sizeof(std::string) is large (~32 bytes), and moving a small (inline) string copies the buffer rather than stealing a pointer — the cheap pointer-steal move only applies to heap-allocated strings
choices.3: It removes the null terminator from short strings
choices.4: Short strings become slower to access than long ones

question: What happens once a std::string exceeds its inline SSO capacity?

choices.1: It silently truncates to the inline capacity
choices.2: It switches to a reference-counted shared buffer
choices.3: It throws std::length_error
choices.4: It allocates on the heap and behaves like a vector<char> — geometric growth, reallocation on overflow, reserve/capacity/shrink_to_fit

question: How do c_str()/data() differ from std::string_view regarding termination?

choices.1: Neither is null-terminated
choices.2: string_view is always null-terminated while std::string is not
choices.3: Both guarantee null termination identically
choices.4: std::string keeps a null terminator one past the end so c_str()/data() return a null-terminated buffer for C APIs; string_view carries no null-termination guarantee

question: Why can a pointer from c_str() dangle after appending to the string?

choices.1: c_str() pointers are only valid in the same statement
choices.2: c_str() always returns a copy that is freed immediately
choices.3: Appending zeroes out the previous buffer for security
choices.4: Appending may reallocate (or cross the SSO->heap boundary), which invalidates pointers, references, and iterators including c_str()/data()

question: Why prefer std::string_view::substr over std::string::substr in a hot parsing loop?

choices.1: std::string::substr copies into a new allocated string, while string_view::substr just adjusts pointers with no allocation
choices.2: They are identical; the choice is purely stylistic
choices.3: string::substr is undefined behaviour on long strings
choices.4: string_view::substr null-terminates the result

question: Why can std::string r = a + b + c + d; be inefficient, and what is the fix?

choices.1: The fix is to use substr instead of +
choices.2: It always allocates exactly once, so there is nothing to fix
choices.3: operator+ is undefined for more than two operands
choices.4: Each operator+ creates temporaries/allocations; reserve the total size once and use += repeatedly to avoid them

question: The small-buffer idea behind SSO also appears where?

choices.1: In std::shared_ptr's control block
choices.2: Small buffer optimization (SBO) appears in std::function (small callables stored inline) and small-vector types like boost::small_vector / llvm::SmallVector
choices.3: Only in std::string; no other type uses it
choices.4: In std::map's red-black tree nodes

question: What is a std::string_view?

choices.1: A non-owning {const char*, size_t} view — no allocation, ~16 bytes, cheap to copy, viewing a string/literal/char buffer
choices.2: A null-terminated mutable character buffer
choices.3: A heap-allocated immutable string with shared ownership
choices.4: An owning copy of a string with reference counting

question: Why is string_view a better read-only string parameter than const std::string&?

choices.1: const std::string& cannot bind to a temporary at all
choices.2: string_view automatically null-terminates its argument
choices.3: string_view deep-copies the argument for safety
choices.4: Passing a literal or char* to const std::string& constructs a temporary std::string (an allocation); string_view accepts strings, literals, and buffers with zero copies

question: Why is slicing a string_view (substr / remove_prefix) cheap?

choices.1: It caches previously computed substrings
choices.2: It returns another view with an adjusted pointer/length and copies nothing, unlike std::string::substr which allocates a fresh string each call
choices.3: It allocates once and reuses the buffer for all slices
choices.4: It mutates the original buffer in place

question: What is the dangling trap with string_view?

choices.1: It throws std::bad_alloc when the buffer is destroyed
choices.2: It does not extend the lifetime of what it points at, so returning a view to a local string, or storing a view of a temporary, leaves it dangling
choices.3: It copies the buffer lazily, so dangling only happens on mutation
choices.4: It reference-counts the buffer, so it can never dangle

question: Why must you not pass string_view::data() directly to a C API like std::fopen?

choices.1: C APIs require a wide-character pointer that data() does not provide
choices.2: string_view is a pointer+length with no null-terminator guarantee, so data() may point into the middle of a larger buffer; materialize a std::string first for a null-terminated string
choices.3: data() is always nullptr for views of literals
choices.4: data() returns a non-const pointer that the C API may corrupt

question: How does std::span relate to std::string_view?

choices.1: span is null-terminated while string_view is not
choices.2: string_view is essentially std::span<const char> with string methods; std::span<T> (C++20) is the same non-owning {pointer,length} view over any contiguous range, with the same lifetime caveat
choices.3: span only works over std::string, not other containers
choices.4: span owns its data while string_view does not

question: What does auto&& deduce to depending on its initializer?

choices.1: An lvalue initializer makes it an lvalue reference (T&); an rvalue initializer makes it an rvalue reference (T&&) — via template-like deduction plus reference collapsing
choices.2: An lvalue reference for rvalues and an rvalue reference for lvalues
choices.3: Always a plain value copy
choices.4: Always an rvalue reference (T&&), regardless of the initializer

question: What is the most important use of auto&&?

choices.1: As a forwarding reference that preserves value category for later forwarding (e.g. std::forward<decltype(x)>(x))
choices.2: To convert any rvalue into an lvalue
choices.3: To force a copy of the initializer
choices.4: To make a variable const automatically

question: Why is auto&& a strong default in a range-based for loop?

choices.1: It adapts to whatever the range yields — mutable/const containers, ranges returning references or proxy objects, and temporary ranges
choices.2: It always copies each element for safety
choices.3: It forces every element to be const
choices.4: It only binds lvalue elements, avoiding proxies

question: Why is it a mistake to read auto&& as 'always an rvalue reference'?

choices.1: Because auto&& can only bind to const objects
choices.2: Because && in auto&& is a logical-and operator
choices.3: With deduction, auto&& is a forwarding reference meaning 'deduce and bind appropriately' — auto&& x = a; gives int& for an lvalue a, and int&& for an rvalue
choices.4: Because auto&& is actually always a value copy

question: Which comparison of binding forms is correct?

choices.1: auto and auto&& are identical in all cases
choices.2: auto copies; auto& binds lvalues only; const auto& binds almost anything read-only; auto&& is forwarding-style and preserves value category
choices.3: auto& binds rvalues only; auto&& binds lvalues only
choices.4: const auto& copies; auto&& is read-only

question: When should you prefer auto, auto&, or const auto& over auto&&?

choices.1: Whenever the initializer is a temporary
choices.2: When you just want a simple local copy or read-only access and do not need forwarding behaviour
choices.3: Whenever you call a template function
choices.4: Whenever you iterate a container

question: What is the difference between decltype(x) and decltype((x)) for a variable int x?

choices.1: Both are int
choices.2: decltype((x)) is int&& because parentheses make it an xvalue
choices.3: decltype(x) is int& and decltype((x)) is int
choices.4: decltype(x) is the declared type int; decltype((x)) is the expression type int& (a parenthesized name is an lvalue expression)

question: For an expression that is not a plain name, how does decltype choose the type?

choices.1: It depends on whether the expression is constexpr
choices.2: By value category: lvalue -> T&, xvalue -> T&&, prvalue -> T
choices.3: It always yields T, dropping all references
choices.4: It always yields T&, adding a reference

question: How does decltype differ from auto when deducing from int& rx?

choices.1: auto deduces int& and decltype deduces int
choices.2: Both deduce int
choices.3: auto a = rx; deduces int (drops the reference), while decltype(rx) is int& (preserves it)
choices.4: Both deduce int&

question: What does decltype(auto) do, e.g. decltype(auto) a = get_ref(); where int& get_ref()?

choices.1: It is ill-formed; decltype(auto) is not valid syntax
choices.2: It is identical to auto and deduces int
choices.3: It always deduces a const-qualified type
choices.4: It deduces using decltype rules, so a is int& (it preserves references), unlike auto which would deduce int

question: Given int f(); and int& g();, what are decltype(f()) and decltype(g())?

choices.1: decltype(f()) is int (prvalue call) and decltype(g()) is int& (the call is an lvalue returning int&)
choices.2: decltype(f()) is int& and decltype(g()) is int
choices.3: Both are int&
choices.4: Both are int

question: What is std::declval<T>() used for?

choices.1: To allocate a T on the heap and return a pointer
choices.2: To default-construct a T at runtime cheaply
choices.3: To convert a T into its base class
choices.4: In unevaluated contexts, to pretend you have a value of type T without constructing one — for decltype/sizeof/noexcept/SFINAE/requires checks

question: Why does declval exist when you could write decltype(T{}.foo())?

choices.1: declval is faster at runtime than constructing T{}
choices.2: declval constructs the object lazily, deferring the cost
choices.3: decltype(T{}.foo()) requires T{} to be valid; declval lets you inspect a member's type even when T has no default constructor, is abstract, incomplete, or expensive to instantiate
choices.4: T{} is illegal in all template contexts

question: What type does std::declval<T>() act as for a non-reference T, and how do you get an lvalue instead?

choices.1: It acts as T by value; there is no way to get a reference
choices.2: It acts as const T&; use declval<T&&>() for an rvalue
choices.3: It acts as T&& (add_rvalue_reference_t<T>); ask for declval<T&>() to act as an lvalue T&
choices.4: It acts as T* and you dereference it for a value

question: What is the rule about evaluating declval?

choices.1: It may only appear in unevaluated contexts; auto x = std::declval<T>(); is ill-formed because declval has no usable runtime definition (a compile-time fiction)
choices.2: It can be called at runtime if T is default-constructible
choices.3: It is evaluated normally but throws if used
choices.4: It returns a valid object that must be deleted

question: Why does the choice of declval<X&>() vs declval<X>() matter for a ref-qualified member f() & / f() &&?

choices.1: declval<X&>().f() selects the lvalue-qualified f() &, while declval<X>().f() selects the rvalue-qualified f() && — so T vs T& vs const T& changes which overload is modeled
choices.2: Only declval<X&&>() can call any member function
choices.3: declval<X>() always calls the const overload
choices.4: It makes no difference; ref-qualifiers are ignored in unevaluated contexts

question: What are the reference-collapsing rules?

choices.1: All combinations collapse to a plain non-reference type
choices.3: Any combination involving && collapses to &&
choices.4: & + & -> &, & + && -> &, && + & -> &, && + && -> && — i.e. if either side is an lvalue reference the result is an lvalue reference; only && + && stays &&

question: Which parameter is a forwarding reference?

choices.1: A T&& member of struct S<T>, because T appears in the signature
choices.2: template<typename T> void f(T&& x); where T is deduced at the call — but NOT const T&&, and NOT a T&& member parameter whose T is fixed by the enclosing class template
choices.3: const T&& x, because const makes deduction more flexible
choices.4: Any parameter of the form T&& in any context

question: For template<typename T> void f(T&& x), how is T deduced for an lvalue vs an rvalue argument?

choices.1: An lvalue deduces T = int and an rvalue deduces T = int&
choices.2: Both deduce T = int; only the parameter type differs
choices.3: T is always deduced as int&& regardless of the argument
choices.4: An lvalue deduces T = int& so the parameter collapses to int&; an rvalue deduces T = int so the parameter is int&&

question: Inside void g(int&& x), why is the expression x an lvalue?

choices.1: Because x has a name; named rvalue references are lvalues, so you must write std::move(x) to pass it on as an rvalue
choices.2: Because int&& decays to int& at function entry
choices.3: Because rvalue references can only hold lvalues
choices.4: It is actually an rvalue; the note is mistaken

question: In a forwarding wrapper, why is target(arg) wrong, and why is target(std::move(arg)) also wrong?

choices.1: Both are correct and equivalent to std::forward
choices.2: std::move(arg) is correct; the problem is only with target(arg)
choices.3: target(arg) is correct; std::move(arg) is the only mistake
choices.4: target(arg) always passes an lvalue (arg is named), losing rvalue-ness; target(std::move(arg)) forces an rvalue even for lvalue arguments — std::forward<T>(arg) preserves the original category

question: What does std::forward<T>(arg) yield based on how T was deduced?

choices.1: It always yields an lvalue reference
choices.2: It yields a copy of arg regardless of T
choices.3: If T was deduced as U it yields U&& (rvalue); if T was deduced as U& it yields U& (lvalue) — preserving the caller's value category
choices.4: It always yields an rvalue, like std::move

question: What is the difference between std::move and std::forward?

choices.1: They are interchangeable; both cast to rvalue
choices.2: std::move unconditionally casts to an rvalue (use it to consume a known object); std::forward<T> conditionally preserves the deduced value category (use it to relay a forwarding reference)
choices.3: std::move preserves value category; std::forward always casts to rvalue
choices.4: std::forward copies while std::move references

question: How do forwarding references combine with variadic templates?

choices.1: Variadic packs cannot use forwarding references
choices.2: All pack elements are forced to share one deduced category
choices.3: std::forward cannot be applied to a parameter pack
choices.4: template<typename... Args> void wrapper(Args&&... args) { target(std::forward<Args>(args)...); } deduces each Args separately, preserving a mix of lvalues, const lvalues, and rvalues in one call

question: How does the compile-time Factorial<N> template compute its value?

choices.1: It loops at runtime from N down to 1
choices.2: It relies on a recursive constructor call
choices.3: Factorial<N>::value = N * Factorial<N-1>::value, recursively instantiating templates until the specialized base case Factorial<0>::value = 1
choices.4: It uses constexpr std::accumulate over a range

question: What role does the explicit specialization Factorial<0> play?

choices.1: It overrides Factorial<5> at runtime
choices.2: It provides the recursive step while the primary template is the base case
choices.3: It is an optimization that can be omitted safely
choices.4: It is the base case that terminates the recursion; without it the template would instantiate indefinitely

question: How does recursion over a parameter pack work in print_all(T first, Rest... rest)?

choices.1: It peels off one argument at a time and calls print_all(rest...), until a less-general base overload (e.g. print_all(T x)) handles the final argument
choices.2: It expands the entire pack into a single function call with no recursion
choices.3: It recurses by adding one argument at a time until the pack is full
choices.4: It requires the pack to be sorted first

question: What two ingredients does template recursion generally require?

choices.1: A loop and a counter
choices.2: A general/recursive case plus a base case (a specialization, or a less-general overload, that terminates)
choices.3: A virtual function and an override
choices.4: A mutex and a condition variable

question: What are type traits?

choices.1: Macros that expand into type names
choices.2: Compile-time templates (in <type_traits>) that ask questions about types, transform types, or steer template behaviour — with no runtime cost
choices.3: Runtime reflection objects that describe a type's members
choices.4: Virtual functions that report a type's category at runtime

question: What are the three big categories of type traits?

choices.1: Input, output, and forward traits
choices.2: Compile-time, link-time, and run-time traits
choices.3: Property traits (yes/no, e.g. is_integral), transformation traits (produce a ::type, e.g. remove_reference), and helper/meta traits (enable_if, void_t, integral_constant)
choices.4: Static, dynamic, and thread-local traits

question: What do std::true_type and std::false_type provide, and what are they?

choices.1: They are integral_constant<bool, true/false> specializations giving a ::value, a type identity, and an implicit conversion to bool
choices.2: They are runtime boolean wrappers used in if statements
choices.3: They are enum values of a Boolean enum class
choices.4: They are macros for true and false

question: What do the _v and _t shorthands mean (e.g. is_integral_v<T>, remove_reference_t<T>)?

choices.1: _v means 'volatile' and _t means 'thread-local'
choices.2: They are identical and interchangeable
choices.3: _v returns a type and _t returns a value
choices.4: _v is a variable-template shorthand for the trait's ::value; _t is an alias for the trait's nested ::type

question: Why does typename std::remove_reference<T>::type need typename, and how does _t help?

choices.1: Trait<T>::type is a dependent name, so typename is required to tell the compiler it names a type; the _t alias (remove_reference_t<T>) removes that burden
choices.2: _t adds typename internally but only works for value traits
choices.3: typename is needed because ::type is private
choices.4: typename is only needed in C++98; _t is purely cosmetic

question: What is the standard pattern for defining a custom boolean trait like is_std_vector<T>?

choices.1: A single template returning a runtime bool from a member function
choices.2: A primary template inheriting std::false_type, a partial specialization for the matching pattern inheriting std::true_type, plus an inline constexpr bool _v shorthand
choices.3: An enum with one entry per supported type
choices.4: A macro that checks the type name string at compile time

question: Why is if constexpr (std::is_integral<T>) a mistake?

choices.1: is_integral<T> is only valid at runtime
choices.2: It needs typename in front of is_integral<T>
choices.3: std::is_integral<T> is a type, not a bool; you need std::is_integral_v<T> (or ::value) to get the compile-time boolean
choices.4: if constexpr cannot take any trait

question: A trait specialized for std::vector<T> returns false for const std::vector<int>&. What is the fix?

choices.1: Remove the partial specialization and use std::is_same instead
choices.2: Add a runtime check for the reference
choices.3: Normalize the type first, e.g. test is_std_vector<std::decay_t<T>> (or remove_cvref_t<T>), so cv-qualifiers and references don't block the match
choices.4: Make the trait a virtual function

question: In class template A<T>, what does 'friend class B<T>;' grant?

choices.1: B<T> becomes a base class of A<T>
choices.2: Nothing — you cannot friend a class template specialization
choices.3: Only the matching specialization is a friend: B<int> can access A<int>, B<double> can access A<double>, with no cross-specialization access
choices.4: Every B<U> can access every A<T>

question: What does 'template <class U> friend class B;' inside A<T> grant?

choices.1: Only B<T> can access A<T>
choices.2: It makes B a member class of A
choices.3: Only B<int> is a friend
choices.4: Every specialization B<U> can access every specialization A<T>

question: How does 'friend void f<T>(const A<T>&);' differ from 'friend void f(const A<T>&);' inside A<T>?

choices.1: The first friends all overloads of f while the second friends none
choices.2: The first is illegal; only the second compiles
choices.3: The first friends the existing function-template specialization f<T>; the second declares a non-template (hidden) friend per specialization — they are different functions
choices.4: They are identical; the <T> is optional syntax

question: What is true of a hidden friend defined inside a class template, e.g. 'friend void print(const A& a){...}'?

choices.1: It is a function template that can be called as print<int>(a)
choices.2: It must be declared outside the class before use
choices.3: It can access every other specialization of A
choices.4: It is a separate non-template friend for each specialization, found only by ADL; print<int>(a) fails because it is not a function template

question: Why does 'friend void f<T>(const A<T>&);' fail without a prior declaration of f?

choices.1: f<T> names a specialization of a function template, so f must already be declared as a template before the class; otherwise f<T> refers to nothing
choices.2: Because friend declarations must come after the class body
choices.3: Because the compiler instantiates f<T> twice
choices.4: Because f<T> is only valid for class templates, not functions

question: Why can't you partially specialize a function template (a common point of confusion)?

choices.1: Function templates may be overloaded and explicitly (fully) specialized, but partial specialization is not allowed for them
choices.2: Because function templates cannot be specialized at all
choices.3: Because partial specialization only works inside namespaces
choices.4: Because functions have no template parameters

question: What are templates in C++?

choices.1: A way to share code via dynamic libraries at link time
choices.2: A runtime polymorphism feature based on virtual dispatch
choices.3: C++'s mechanism for generic programming: you write code once using placeholders (types/values/templates) and the compiler generates concrete code for the arguments you use
choices.4: A preprocessor macro system for code substitution

question: Why are template-based abstractions often described as zero/low-overhead?

choices.1: They compile down to code as efficient as handwritten specialized code — inlining, compile-time dispatch, and no virtual calls unless you opt into runtime polymorphism
choices.2: They always produce smaller binaries than non-generic code
choices.3: They eliminate compile-time type checking for speed
choices.4: They defer all work to runtime where a JIT optimizes it

question: What is 'template bloat'?

choices.1: Runtime overhead from template argument deduction
choices.2: Instantiating many versions of the same template for different arguments can increase binary size (alongside longer compile times)
choices.3: Templates leaking memory at runtime
choices.4: Excessive virtual table entries from template inheritance

question: What is a downside of templates regarding ABI / library interfaces?

choices.1: Templates always break binary compatibility on any code change, even non-template code
choices.2: Template-heavy libraries are often header-only and do not provide a stable ABI the way a non-templated shared-library interface can; changes can force downstream recompilation
choices.3: Templates cannot be used across translation units
choices.4: Templates require a separate runtime library to be linked

question: What is partial specialization, and which templates support it?

choices.1: A version for exactly one set of arguments; allowed only for functions
choices.2: A more specific version of a template for a pattern of arguments (e.g. Box<T*>); allowed for class and variable templates, but not function templates (overload instead)
choices.3: Specialization that works for every template kind including functions
choices.4: A way to instantiate a template in another translation unit

question: What is full (explicit) specialization?

choices.1: Defining a version for a pattern of arguments like Box<T*>
choices.2: Defining a version for exactly one set of template arguments (e.g. template<> struct Box<int>); it works for function, class, and variable templates
choices.3: Forcing instantiation of a template in a specific .cpp
choices.4: Letting the compiler pick the most specialized partial specialization

question: When several partial specializations match, which does the compiler pick?

choices.1: The most specialized matching partial specialization
choices.2: It is ambiguous and always an error
choices.3: The primary template, ignoring the specializations
choices.4: The first one declared in the file

question: What is implicit instantiation?

choices.1: You explicitly write 'template int add<int>(int,int);' to emit code
choices.2: The compiler automatically generates a template instantiation when you use it (e.g. add(1,2) instantiates add<int>); class members are instantiated when odr-used
choices.3: The linker generates instantiations after compilation
choices.4: You declare 'extern template' to suppress instantiation

question: What does an explicit instantiation definition like 'template int add<int>(int, int);' do?

choices.1: It partially specializes add for int
choices.2: It declares that add<int> is instantiated elsewhere, suppressing local codegen
choices.3: It calls add<int> at namespace scope
choices.4: It forces the compiler to emit code for add<int> in that translation unit — useful to control where code is generated and reduce redundant instantiation

question: What does 'extern template int add<int>(int, int);' (explicit instantiation declaration) achieve?

choices.1: It tells the compiler not to instantiate add<int> in this translation unit because it is instantiated elsewhere — cutting redundant instantiations and compile time
choices.2: It makes add<int> have external linkage where it otherwise wouldn't
choices.3: It fully specializes add for int
choices.4: It forces add<int> to be emitted in every translation unit

question: What does the C++20 explicit template parameter list on a lambda enable, e.g. []<typename T>(T x){...}?

choices.1: It forces the lambda to be evaluated at compile time
choices.2: It makes the lambda a class template you can specialize
choices.3: It lets a lambda declare named template parameters directly, giving tighter control than plain auto parameters
choices.4: It allows the lambda to capture template parameters by reference

question: What does []<typename T>(T a, T b){ return a == b; } require of its arguments?

choices.1: Both arguments must deduce to the same type T
choices.2: Exactly one argument must be a pointer
choices.3: The arguments may be any two types
choices.4: Both arguments must be the same value

question: Why can't you partially specialize a lambda, and what do you use instead?

choices.1: You can partially specialize a lambda with template<> syntax
choices.2: You must inherit from the lambda to specialize it
choices.3: A lambda has an unnamed type, so it cannot be partially specialized like a class template; use if constexpr, concepts, or overloads — the lambda's operator() can be templated, but the lambda itself is not specializable
choices.4: Lambdas cannot be templated at all, so the question does not arise

question: Which is a complete list of template kinds described in the note?

choices.1: Only function templates and class templates
choices.2: Header templates, source templates, and link templates
choices.3: Function templates, class templates, variable templates (C++14), alias templates, and concept templates (C++20)
choices.4: Macro templates, inline templates, and virtual templates

question: What is a variable template (C++14)?

choices.1: A non-type template parameter
choices.2: A template that generates a type alias
choices.3: A variable whose type changes at runtime
choices.4: A template that produces a variable, e.g. template<class T> inline constexpr bool is_small_v = (sizeof(T) <= 8);

question: What is an alias template?

choices.1: A fully specialized class template
choices.2: A template that produces a type alias, e.g. template<class T> using Vec = std::vector<T>;
choices.3: A template that produces a variable
choices.4: A template parameter that is itself a template

question: What is a non-type template parameter (NTTP)?

choices.1: A parameter that must be a class type only
choices.2: A runtime argument passed to a template function
choices.3: A compile-time value parameter, e.g. template<std::size_t N> struct Buffer; C++20 expanded NTTPs to allow more structural types beyond integers/pointers
choices.4: A parameter that is itself a template

question: What is a template template parameter?

choices.1: A template parameter that is itself a template, e.g. template<template<class...> class Container, class T> struct Holder { Container<T> c; };
choices.2: A parameter pack of types
choices.3: An alias template used as an argument
choices.4: A non-type parameter deduced with auto

question: What does an auto template parameter (C++17) provide, e.g. template<auto N>?

choices.1: A way to deduce a type parameter from a function argument
choices.2: A type template parameter with a default of auto
choices.3: A shorthand for a non-type template parameter whose type is deduced from the argument
choices.4: A variadic pack of values

question: What are the four kinds of requirements inside a C++20 requires expression?

choices.1: Simple, type, compound, and nested requirements
choices.2: Public, protected, private, and friend requirements
choices.3: Static, dynamic, virtual, and final requirements
choices.4: Input, output, forward, and random-access requirements

question: What does a simple requirement like 'x.size();' check?

choices.1: That x.size() returns something convertible to size_t
choices.2: Only that the expression is well-formed (valid) — not its return type, value, or noexcept-ness
choices.3: That x.size() is noexcept
choices.4: That T has a nested type named size

question: What does a type requirement like 'typename T::value_type;' check?

choices.1: That the type name T::value_type exists and is a valid type — not anything about what that type is
choices.2: That T has a member function value_type()
choices.3: That an object of type T::value_type can be constructed
choices.4: That T::value_type is exactly int

question: What can a compound requirement '{ expr } noexcept -> constraint;' check?

choices.1: Only that a named type exists
choices.2: That the expression is valid, optionally that it is noexcept, and optionally that its result type satisfies a constraint
choices.3: That the expression evaluates to true at runtime
choices.4: Only that the expression is valid, identical to a simple requirement

question: What does a nested requirement like 'requires sizeof(T) == 4;' express?

choices.1: That T has a member named requires
choices.2: An additional boolean compile-time constraint that must hold, beyond expression/type validity
choices.3: A runtime assertion checked when the function is called
choices.4: That the expression sizeof(T) is merely well-formed

question: How does '{ x.size() } -> std::convertible_to<std::size_t>;' differ from the simple requirement 'x.size();'?

choices.1: The simple form is stronger because it also checks the value
choices.2: The compound form additionally constrains the result type (convertible to size_t), whereas the simple form only checks that x.size() is valid
choices.3: The compound form only checks noexcept, not the return type
choices.4: They are identical; the braces are cosmetic

question: What is a concept in C++20?

choices.1: A runtime type-checking object
choices.2: A base class that types inherit to gain an interface
choices.3: A macro that expands to a type trait
choices.4: A named compile-time predicate on types — a reusable constraint answering questions like 'is this type integral / sortable / addable?'

question: What are the three main syntactic ways to apply a concept?

choices.1: Only via std::enable_if, decltype, and void_t
choices.2: Via macros, typedefs, and inheritance
choices.3: As a base class, a member function, and a friend declaration
choices.4: As a template-parameter constraint (template<std::integral T>), in a requires clause, or as a constrained auto parameter (std::integral auto x)

question: How do concepts differ philosophically from SFINAE?

choices.1: SFINAE is proactive while concepts are reactive
choices.2: They are identical; concepts are just a macro over SFINAE
choices.3: Concepts run at runtime while SFINAE runs at compile time
choices.4: Concepts are proactive ('only consider templates that satisfy this requirement'); SFINAE is reactive ('try the template, and if it breaks, ignore it'). Concepts also give better diagnostics

question: What does the compiler do when a template's concept constraint is not satisfied?

choices.1: It silently picks a default implementation
choices.2: It instantiates the template anyway and asserts at runtime
choices.3: It removes the template from the overload set without a hard error, similar to SFINAE
choices.4: It emits an immediate hard error and stops compilation

question: With void print(auto) and void print(std::integral auto), what does print(3) call, and why?

choices.1: It is ambiguous and fails to compile
choices.2: The std::integral overload — the more-constrained candidate wins overload resolution
choices.3: The unconstrained auto overload, since it is more general
choices.4: Both overloads run in sequence

question: How do you define a concept that requires a type to support addition?

choices.1: concept Addable { virtual T operator+(T) = 0; };
choices.2: template<typename T> concept Addable = requires(T a, T b) { a + b; };
choices.3: #define Addable(T) (sizeof(T) > 0)
choices.4: template<typename T> concept Addable = std::enable_if_t<has_plus<T>>;

question: Can a non-template member function be constrained, and how?

choices.1: No — only function templates can be constrained
choices.2: Yes — with a requires clause that depends on the enclosing class template's parameter, e.g. T get() const requires std::integral<T>;
choices.3: Yes, but only by making the whole class abstract
choices.4: Yes, by adding std::enable_if to the return type of every member

question: How can you provide two mutually exclusive non-template member functions selected by type?

choices.1: Use a virtual function and override it per type
choices.2: Give them the same signature but different requires clauses, e.g. void print() const requires std::integral<T> and another requires std::floating_point<T>
choices.3: It is impossible without a separate class template
choices.4: Overload on return type alone

question: How do you constrain a class template to integral types?

choices.1: struct IntBox { static_assert(integral); };
choices.2: template<std::integral T> struct IntBox { T value; }; (or the longer template<typename T> requires std::integral<T> form)
choices.3: template<typename T> struct IntBox : std::integral {};
choices.4: You cannot constrain class templates, only functions

question: What does void f(std::integral auto x) declare?

choices.1: An abbreviated (constrained) function template — conceptually equivalent to template<std::integral T> void f(T x)
choices.2: A function whose parameter is deduced but unconstrained
choices.3: A non-template function taking a concrete integral type
choices.4: A lambda with a templated operator()

question: A member function with a constrained auto parameter (e.g. void print_sum(std::integral auto a, ...)) is which of the following?

choices.1: Illegal syntax inside a struct
choices.2: An abstract virtual function
choices.3: A function template, because the auto parameters make it templated — unlike the earlier non-template constrained members
choices.4: A non-template member function

question: What does 'std::integral auto square(std::integral auto x) { return x*x; }' constrain?

choices.1: Both the parameter and the deduced return type — each must satisfy std::integral
choices.2: Nothing; constrained auto is invalid in return position
choices.3: Only the parameter; the return type is unconstrained
choices.4: Only the return type; the parameter is unconstrained

question: How does std::enable_if work?

choices.1: It evaluates the condition at runtime and branches
choices.2: It conditionally provides a nested ::type: when the condition is true ::type exists, when false it does not, and that missing type triggers SFINAE to remove the candidate
choices.3: It always provides ::type but sets it to void when false
choices.4: It throws a compile error when the condition is false

question: What is std::enable_if_t<Cond, T> shorthand for?

choices.1: std::conditional_t<Cond, T, void>
choices.2: typename std::enable_if<Cond, T>::type
choices.3: std::enable_if<Cond, T>::value
choices.4: static_cast<T>(Cond)

question: What are the three common places to apply enable_if?

choices.1: As a template parameter with a default argument, as a function parameter with a default argument, and as the function's return type
choices.2: In the class body, the constructor, and the destructor only
choices.3: In a requires clause, an if constexpr, and a static_assert
choices.4: As a base class, a member, and a friend

question: Why does writing two overloads as 'template<typename T, typename = std::enable_if_t<condA>>' and '...condB' fail?

choices.1: Because the two conditions can both be true simultaneously
choices.2: Because enable_if_t cannot appear in a template parameter list
choices.3: After substitution the two templates have the same signature (a redeclaration), so they conflict; use the non-type form 'std::enable_if_t<cond, int> = 0' instead
choices.4: Because default template arguments are not allowed at all

question: Why is the non-type parameter form 'template<typename T, std::enable_if_t<cond, int> = 0>' often preferred for overloads?

choices.1: It is shorter to type
choices.2: It avoids the same-signature redeclaration problem that the 'typename = enable_if_t<...>' form causes when writing multiple overloads
choices.3: It works at runtime instead of compile time
choices.4: It allows the condition to be mutable

question: What is a downside of the return-type enable_if form?

choices.1: It cannot be used for constructors or destructors (they have no return type), and it is often harder to read
choices.2: It evaluates the condition at link time
choices.3: It is the only form that works for member functions
choices.4: It forces the function to be virtual

question: What does SFINAE stand for and mean?

choices.1: Single Function Interface, No Abstract Errors: an interface design rule
choices.2: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error: if substituting template arguments fails in the immediate context, the candidate is removed from overload resolution instead of causing a hard error
choices.3: Static Functions In Non-Abstract Entities: a rule about static dispatch
choices.4: Substitution For Inline Non-Abstract Expressions: an inlining optimization

question: In the integral/non-integral overload pair using enable_if, what selects the right one for print_type(3.14)?

choices.1: Runtime type information picks the overload
choices.2: It is a hard error because two overloads exist
choices.3: Both overloads are viable and the first is chosen arbitrarily
choices.4: The is_integral overload fails substitution and is removed, so the !is_integral overload is chosen — no compile error

question: In the classic has_foo detection trait, how is the result determined?

choices.1: It uses dynamic_cast to check for the member
choices.2: It reads the class's type_info for a member named foo
choices.3: It calls foo() at runtime and catches an exception
choices.4: test(int) returns decltype(declval<U>().foo(), true_type()) and is selected only if foo() is valid; otherwise the variadic test(...) returning false_type is used

question: Why is 'template<typename T> void f(){ T::nonexistent(); }' NOT an example of SFINAE?

choices.1: Because SFINAE applies only to class templates
choices.2: The error occurs after substitution, inside the function body during instantiation — SFINAE only applies to failures in the immediate context of substituting template arguments
choices.3: Because f has no template parameters
choices.4: Because T::nonexistent() always compiles

question: What is the modern (C++17) alternative to many SFINAE branches, and how does it differ?

choices.1: virtual functions, which dispatch at runtime
choices.2: static_assert, which removes overloads like SFINAE
choices.3: dynamic_cast, which checks types at runtime
choices.4: if constexpr branches inside a single template body — it keeps one overload and discards a branch, rather than removing overloads from resolution

question: Does constexpr by itself create SFINAE?

choices.1: Yes — any constexpr expression that is false removes the enclosing template
choices.2: No — constexpr computes compile-time values; SFINAE mechanisms like enable_if then use those values to include or exclude templates
choices.3: Yes — constexpr is the only way to trigger SFINAE
choices.4: No — constexpr has nothing to do with compile-time evaluation

question: In 'template<typename T, std::enable_if_t<is_small_v<T>, int> = 0>', what is the division of labour between constexpr and SFINAE?

choices.1: constexpr removes the overload; enable_if just documents intent
choices.2: Both do the same thing redundantly
choices.3: The constexpr variable is_small_v supplies the boolean; enable_if_t is what actually produces the substitution failure (the SFINAE)
choices.4: Neither is involved in SFINAE; it is pure overloading

question: Why is 'if constexpr (std::is_integral_v<T>)' inside a function body NOT SFINAE?

choices.1: Because if constexpr runs at runtime
choices.2: Because is_integral_v is not a constexpr value
choices.3: The function template remains a valid candidate for any T; if constexpr only selects which branch of the body is compiled — it does not remove the overload
choices.4: Because if constexpr removes the overload exactly like enable_if

question: How does a failing static_assert differ from SFINAE?

choices.1: static_assert failure removes the overload while SFINAE hard-errors
choices.2: static_assert only works at runtime
choices.3: A static_assert failure is a hard error on a template that was selected and instantiated; SFINAE instead makes the candidate non-viable so another overload is chosen
choices.4: They are identical — both silently remove the overload

question: What is std::void_t, essentially?

choices.1: A function that erases a type to void*
choices.2: A concept that matches only void
choices.3: A trait that returns true if a type is void
choices.4: template<typename...> using void_t = void; — it aliases to void for any arguments, but only if all of those types/expressions are well-formed

question: What is the standard void_t detection pattern?

choices.1: A virtual method that reports capability at runtime
choices.2: A single function returning a runtime bool
choices.3: An enable_if in the return type of a member function
choices.4: A primary template inheriting std::false_type, plus a partial specialization '<T, std::void_t<dependent type/expression>>' inheriting std::true_type

question: Why is the void_t detection idiom a form of SFINAE?

choices.1: Because void_t triggers a static_assert on failure
choices.2: Because void_t uses dynamic_cast internally
choices.3: Because void_t evaluates the expression at runtime and catches errors
choices.4: If the dependent type/expression inside void_t is ill-formed during substitution, that partial specialization is discarded and the compiler falls back to the primary template — substitution failure, not a hard error

question: How do you detect whether T has a callable size() member with void_t?

choices.1: Specialize on std::void_t<decltype(std::declval<T&>().size())> — the expression is valid only if T has a callable size(); void_t's pack lets you test several expressions at once
choices.2: Use a virtual size() in a base class
choices.3: Use sizeof(T) to check for the member
choices.4: Use std::is_same_v<T, std::vector<int>>

question: How does void_t typically differ in role from enable_if?

choices.1: void_t gates overloads while enable_if detects members
choices.2: void_t is usually for the detection idiom (choose a specialization when types/expressions are well-formed); enable_if is usually for gating overloads on a boolean condition
choices.3: They are identical and interchangeable in every case
choices.4: void_t works at runtime while enable_if works at compile time

question: Is std::void_t synonymous with C++20 concepts?

choices.1: Yes — concepts are just a nicer spelling of void_t
choices.2: No — void_t is newer than concepts and supersedes them
choices.3: No — void_t is the older SFINAE-based library technique for detection; concepts are a clearer core language feature (not implemented via void_t) that express requirements directly with better diagnostics
choices.4: Yes — concepts are implemented internally with void_t

question: What is the core CRTP mechanism used here?

choices.1: Two classes that mutually inherit from each other
choices.2: A base class with pure virtual functions that the derived overrides at runtime
choices.3: A base template parameterized on the derived type that calls the derived's methods via static_cast<Derived*>(this), giving compile-time (static) dispatch with no virtual functions
choices.4: A macro that generates a class hierarchy

source: C++/C++/Templates/Patterns & Idioms/CRTP In Composite Design Pattern.md
question: How does CRTP apply to the Composite pattern?

choices.1: It forces every node to be heap-allocated through a shared pointer
choices.2: Common interface and recursive tree logic go in a CRTP base, and derived node types provide the type-specific behavior statically — sharing code without virtual dispatch
choices.3: It replaces the tree with a flat array
choices.4: It requires every node to inherit std::enable_shared_from_this

source: C++/C++/Templates/Patterns & Idioms/CRTP In Composite Design Pattern.md
question: What is CRTP's big limitation for the classic Composite pattern?

choices.1: CRTP forces all nodes to have the same size
choices.2: CRTP cannot define recursive traversal functions
choices.3: CRTP objects cannot be stored in a std::vector at all
choices.4: CRTP alone cannot give one heterogeneous container of different node types — Component<Leaf> and Component<Group> are unrelated concrete types, so there is no single erased base to store them through

source: C++/C++/Templates/Patterns & Idioms/CRTP In Composite Design Pattern.md
question: What is the practical hybrid pattern combining CRTP with a virtual interface?

choices.1: Replacing the virtual base with a std::function table
choices.2: A virtual base (struct INode{ virtual void draw()=0; }) plus template<class D> struct NodeCRTP : INode { void draw() final { static_cast<D*>(this)->drawImpl(); } } — CRTP writes the override once while the virtual base enables heterogeneous storage
choices.3: Making INode a CRTP base of itself
choices.4: Two separate vtables merged at link time

source: C++/C++/Templates/Patterns & Idioms/CRTP In Composite Design Pattern.md
question: How can CRTP be combined with std::variant for a heterogeneous Composite?

choices.1: Store each node in a separate std::any and cast at runtime
choices.2: Use std::variant as the CRTP base class
choices.3: variant cannot be combined with CRTP
choices.4: Store nodes as std::vector<std::variant<Leaf, Group>> and dispatch via visitation — heterogeneous storage without virtuals, shifting complexity to variant visitation

source: C++/C++/Templates/Patterns & Idioms/CRTP In Composite Design Pattern.md
question: When is CRTP a poor fit for Composite, favouring classic virtual polymorphism instead?

choices.1: When you want to factor repetitive interface code
choices.2: When you want zero-overhead abstractions
choices.3: When performance matters and types are known at compile time
choices.4: When you need arbitrary heterogeneous runtime trees, plugin-style extensibility, separate compilation boundaries, or an ABI-stable polymorphic API

question: What does CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern) consist of?

choices.1: A template function that calls itself recursively at compile time
choices.2: Two classes that each declare the other as a friend
choices.3: A class inherits from a template instantiated with itself, e.g. class Derived : public Base<Derived>, so the base is parameterized on the derived type
choices.4: A class contains a member of its own type, forming recursion

question: How does the CRTP base call into the derived class?

choices.1: By storing a function pointer to the derived method
choices.2: Via static_cast<Derived*>(this)->impl() — the derived type is known at compile time, giving static dispatch with no vtable
choices.3: Through a virtual function resolved at runtime
choices.4: Using dynamic_cast to recover the derived type

question: What is the main benefit of CRTP over virtual functions?

choices.1: Compile-time (static) dispatch with no vtable lookup and easy inlining — zero runtime overhead
choices.2: A stable binary interface (ABI) across versions
choices.3: Reduced compile times
choices.4: The ability to store unrelated types in one heterogeneous container

question: How can CRTP synthesize operator!= from a user-provided operator==?

choices.1: It cannot — operators can't be added via CRTP
choices.2: It uses std::rel_ops to inject the operator at runtime
choices.3: A base like EqualityComparable<Derived> defines operator!= as !static_cast<const Derived*>(this)->operator==(other), so the derived type only writes ==
choices.4: It overrides operator!= virtually in each derived class

question: Which standard-library facility is a real-world example of CRTP?

choices.1: std::vector<T>
choices.2: std::function<void()>
choices.3: std::enable_shared_from_this<T>, used as class MyClass : public std::enable_shared_from_this<MyClass>
choices.4: std::variant<Ts...>

question: What is a trade-off of CRTP compared to virtual functions?

choices.1: It incurs a vtable lookup on every call
choices.2: It always requires heap allocation
choices.3: It is less flexible and template-based rather than offering a stable runtime/binary interface — the type relationships are fixed at compile time
choices.4: It cannot be inlined by the compiler

question: What is a mixin in C++ template metaprogramming?

choices.1: A macro that injects methods into a class body
choices.2: A runtime plugin loaded via dlopen
choices.3: A small reusable (usually template) class meant to be inherited from to add reusable behavior or state to another class, composably
choices.4: A built-in C++ keyword for multiple inheritance

question: In the pattern template<typename Base> class WithID : public Base { int id; };, what does WithID<Person> express?

choices.1: A specialization of WithID only for Person
choices.2: Take Person and add the id feature on top of it — composing behavior/state by inheriting from the Base parameter
choices.3: Person inherits from WithID
choices.4: Two unrelated classes merged by the linker

question: How do you stack multiple mixins, e.g. Printable<Named<Empty>>?

choices.1: You must merge them manually with a union
choices.2: You list them with commas inside one template argument
choices.3: Stacking is impossible; only one mixin can apply at a time
choices.4: Each mixin inherits from its Base parameter, so the final type accumulates all the features (name from Named, print() from Printable)

question: How does a CRTP-style mixin like Comparable<Point> differ from a plain Base-parameter mixin?

choices.1: It must be the most-derived class in the hierarchy
choices.2: It uses virtual dispatch instead of static_cast
choices.3: It is parameterized on the derived type (the mixin refers to Derived), so it can call the derived class's methods — e.g. implement != in terms of the derived ==
choices.4: It cannot add any new members

question: How does policy-based design relate to mixins?

choices.1: Policy-based design forbids inheritance
choices.2: Policies are runtime-only and unrelated to mixins
choices.3: Policies are the same as virtual base classes
choices.4: A policy class supplies behavior chosen at compile time (e.g. Processor<ConsoleOutput> inherits the output policy); it is related to and often discussed alongside mixins

question: What are common caveats of overusing mixins?

choices.1: Name clashes between mixins, deep inheritance stacks, confusing template errors, accidental dependence on base internals, and awkward constructors when stacking many
choices.2: They require runtime type information
choices.3: They prevent the class from being copied
choices.4: They force every method to be virtual

question: What is type erasure?

choices.1: Deleting unused template instantiations from the binary
choices.2: Casting every object to void* and back
choices.3: Removing type information at runtime to save memory
choices.4: A technique that hides a concrete type behind a uniform interface, so unrelated types can be stored/passed/used through one wrapper type
choices.5: A compiler pass that strips debug type symbols

question: Why is std::function<void()> a canonical type-erasure example?

choices.1: It is a compile-time-only abstraction with no runtime cost
choices.2: It stores the callable's type_info and requires a cast to call it
choices.3: It only accepts function pointers, not lambdas
choices.4: It erases the exact callable type (lambda, functor, function pointer) and presents one uniform call interface behind a runtime operation table

question: What does a type-erased object typically contain internally?

choices.1: A std::type_info object and nothing else
choices.2: Only a void* with no operation information
choices.3: Storage (a pointer or inline buffer) for the real object plus a table of operations (destroy, copy/move, invoke, etc.) — similar in spirit to a hand-built vtable
choices.4: A full copy of the class's source code

question: How does type erasure differ from classical inheritance?

choices.1: They are identical mechanisms
choices.2: Inheritance hides the concrete type while type erasure exposes it
choices.3: Type erasure does not require the wrapped types to share a base class — you can adapt unrelated existing types without modifying them
choices.4: Type erasure requires all types to inherit from a common base

question: How does type erasure differ from template (compile-time) polymorphism?

choices.1: Templates keep the concrete type known at compile time (a separate instantiation per type); type erasure is runtime polymorphism over unrelated types through one concrete wrapper type
choices.2: Templates store heterogeneous objects in one container; type erasure cannot
choices.3: They produce identical machine code
choices.4: Type erasure is faster and fully inlined like templates

question: What are the typical trade-offs of type erasure?

choices.1: Zero overhead and full inlining with no downsides
choices.2: It requires every type to be trivially copyable
choices.3: One extra indirection, possible heap allocation, loss of exact type information, and weaker optimization than pure templates — in exchange for flexible runtime polymorphism without inheritance
choices.4: It eliminates all virtual dispatch and allocation

question: What is a typelist?

choices.1: A list of values whose types vary
choices.2: An enum of type identifiers
choices.3: A compile-time list of types (e.g. type_list<int, double, char>) — a type representing a sequence of types, not a container of values
choices.4: A runtime vector of std::type_info objects

question: How is a minimal typelist defined?

choices.1: template <typename T> struct type_list { T value; };
choices.2: template <typename... Ts> struct type_list {}; — a variadic class template holding the types in its parameter pack
choices.3: using type_list = std::vector<std::type_info>;
choices.4: #define type_list(...) struct {}

question: Why can push_front/push_back on a typelist be written as alias templates, while pop_back and at need helper structs?

choices.1: Alias templates cannot express any pack operation
choices.2: Helper structs are needed for all operations equally
choices.3: Simple front/back pack transformations can be expressed directly, but pop_back and at require recursion / pattern-matching on head and tail, which needs helper struct specializations
choices.4: pop_back is impossible at compile time

question: Why is pop_back harder to implement than pop_front for a typelist?

choices.1: pop_front needs recursion while pop_back does not
choices.2: Packs cannot be modified at the front at all
choices.3: pop_back requires runtime iteration
choices.4: Parameter packs are naturally peeled from the front, not the back, so removing the last element requires recursion (rebuilding the list while dropping the tail)

question: How does 'at<List, I>' retrieve the I-th type via recursion?

choices.1: It uses sizeof... to index directly with no recursion
choices.2: If the index is 0 it returns the head; otherwise it recurses into the tail with index I-1 until it reaches 0
choices.3: It linearly scans a runtime array of types
choices.4: It hashes the index to find the type

question: In modern C++, what often replaces hand-rolled typelists in practice?

choices.1: std::vector<std::any>
choices.2: Preprocessor macros
choices.3: Virtual base classes
choices.4: Parameter packs used directly, std::tuple<Ts...>, and standard type traits

question: What does CTAD (class template argument deduction) let you do?

choices.1: Deduce a function template's arguments from its return type
choices.2: Let the compiler deduce a class template's arguments from the constructor call, e.g. std::pair p(1, 2.5) deduces std::pair<int, double>
choices.3: Automatically specialize a class template for every type
choices.4: Deduce the type of any expression like auto

question: What does CTAD actually use to deduce the class template arguments?

choices.1: The members' declared types alone
choices.2: Only the explicit template argument list
choices.3: The function parameter types of free functions
