question: How does eager reliable broadcast work, and what is its cost?

choices.1: It gossips to a few random peers, with O(log n) cost
choices.2: It sends each message only once from the sender, with O(n) cost
choices.3: It uses a central sequencer to forward messages, with O(1) cost
choices.4: Whenever a process receives a message for the first time it immediately re-broadcasts it to all processes (robust even if the original sender crashes), but this gives high overhead — about O(n^2) messages per broadcast

question: What three properties does reliable broadcast guarantee?

choices.1: Total order, FIFO order, and causal order
choices.2: Atomicity, Consistency, and Durability
choices.3: Validity (a correct process eventually delivers its own broadcast), Agreement (if one correct process delivers a message, all correct processes eventually do), and Integrity (a message is delivered at most once and only if actually broadcast)
choices.4: Liveness, fairness, and termination only

question: How do gossip (epidemic) protocols spread information?

choices.1: A leader sends every message directly to every process
choices.2: Every process broadcasts to all others on every event
choices.3: Messages travel along a fixed spanning tree only
choices.4: Each process sends information to a small number of randomly chosen peers, who forward it to more random peers, so it reaches everyone with high probability — scalable and fault-tolerant but probabilistic

question: How is FIFO broadcast typically implemented, and what does it order?

choices.1: It uses vector clocks to order all messages globally
choices.2: It uses a sequencer to assign one global order
choices.3: Each sender attaches a sequence number and each receiver delivers a sender's messages in sequence order — so it orders messages from the same sender, while messages from different senders may be delivered in different orders
choices.4: It orders messages by arrival time at each receiver

question: How does causal broadcast decide when a message can be delivered?

choices.1: It waits for acknowledgements from all processes
choices.2: It delivers strictly by a global sequence number
choices.3: It delivers messages immediately in arrival order
choices.4: Using vector clocks: a message from sender Pi with timestamp T is delivered only when T[i] = VC[i]+1 (it is the next from Pi) and T[k] <= VC[k] for all other k (all causally preceding messages have been delivered)

question: Which is a correct way to implement total order broadcast?

choices.1: A sequencer that assigns a global sequence number to every message (simple but a bottleneck/SPOF), consensus rounds that agree on the next batch, or Lamport timestamps delivering in (timestamp, process_id) order once all acks are received
choices.2: Vector clocks alone with no other mechanism
choices.3: Per-sender sequence numbers alone
choices.4: Randomly forwarding to peers like gossip

question: How do the ordered broadcasts relate to reliable broadcast as a building block?

choices.1: Reliable broadcast = FIFO + causal + total order combined
choices.2: FIFO broadcast = reliable broadcast + per-sender sequence numbers; causal broadcast = reliable broadcast + vector clocks; total order broadcast = reliable broadcast + a global ordering mechanism
choices.3: Total order broadcast is weaker than reliable broadcast
choices.4: They are all unrelated to reliable broadcast

question: What does FIFO broadcast guarantee about message ordering?

choices.1: Messages are delivered in arrival-time order
choices.2: Messages from the same sender are delivered in the order that sender broadcast them; messages from different senders may be delivered in different orders at different processes
choices.3: All processes deliver all messages in one global order
choices.4: Causally related messages are always ordered, regardless of sender

question: What does causal broadcast guarantee, and how does it relate to FIFO?

choices.1: It is weaker than FIFO and orders only same-sender messages
choices.2: It is identical to FIFO broadcast
choices.3: It guarantees a single global order for all messages
choices.4: It delivers messages respecting cause-and-effect (if m1 causally precedes m2, every process delivers m1 before m2), allowing concurrent messages in any order; it is stronger than FIFO — causal broadcast implies FIFO but not vice versa

question: When does message m1 causally precede m2 (m1 -> m2)?

choices.1: Whenever m1 arrives before m2 at some receiver
choices.2: Whenever m1 has a smaller message id than m2
choices.3: Only when m1 and m2 are from the same sender
choices.4: When the same process broadcasts m1 before m2, or a process delivers m1 and later broadcasts m2, or transitively via a chain of such relations

question: What does total order broadcast guarantee?

choices.1: Messages are delivered in increasing timestamp order at each node independently
choices.2: Each sender's messages are delivered in send order only
choices.3: All processes deliver all messages in the same relative order — if one process delivers m1 before m2, every process that delivers both does the same; this agreed order need not respect send time or causality unless the protocol also enforces them
choices.4: Causally related messages are ordered but concurrent ones are not

question: Which implication between broadcast orderings is correct?

choices.1: Causal broadcast implies total order broadcast
choices.2: FIFO broadcast implies causal broadcast
choices.3: Causal broadcast implies FIFO broadcast (same-sender messages are causally ordered), but FIFO does not imply causal, and total order does not automatically imply FIFO or causal order
choices.4: Total order broadcast always implies causal order

question: P1 broadcasts m1; P2 delivers m1 then broadcasts m2. Which protocol must deliver m1 before m2 everywhere?

choices.1: Causal broadcast must (m1 causally precedes m2); FIFO need not (they are from different senders), and total order need not unless it is also causal
choices.2: Total order must, but causal need not
choices.3: FIFO must, but causal need not
choices.4: None of them require m1 before m2

question: When do two operations commute, and why does it matter for replication?

choices.1: They commute if both are read-only
choices.2: They commute if they are sent by the same process
choices.3: They commute if A(B(s)) = B(A(s)) — applying them in either order gives the same final state — so replicas can receive them in different orders and still converge
choices.4: They commute only if delivered in total order

question: For naturally commutative operations (e.g. adding different items to a set), what broadcast strength suffices?

choices.1: No broadcast at all is needed
choices.2: Consensus on every operation is required
choices.3: Total-order (atomic) broadcast is always required
choices.4: A weaker broadcast such as reliable broadcast, causal broadcast, gossip/anti-entropy, or CRDT-style replication — no global total order is required

question: Why do operations like withdraw(80) and withdraw(30) on a balance of 100 require a shared order?

choices.1: They commute, so any order is fine
choices.2: They only need causal broadcast
choices.3: They do not commute: applying them in different orders yields different final balances (20 vs 70, with one withdrawal failing), so replicas must agree on one order via total-order broadcast or consensus
choices.4: They need FIFO broadcast from the same sender

question: When is causal broadcast the right fit (rather than total order)?

choices.1: When all operations must be globally ordered regardless of dependencies
choices.2: When operations are fully commutative
choices.3: Only for read-only operations
choices.4: When operations have cause-and-effect dependencies (e.g. comment after the post it replies to) but unrelated/concurrent operations don't need a shared global order

question: Why isn't total-order broadcast used for everything despite being general?

choices.1: It cannot handle non-commutative operations
choices.2: It provides weaker guarantees than reliable broadcast
choices.3: It works even for non-commuting operations but costs more coordination, higher latency, reduced availability during partitions, and less parallelism — so it's wasteful when operations commute or only need causal order
choices.4: It is only valid in single-node systems

question: Which operations can typically be designed to avoid coordination, and which usually need stronger ordering?

choices.1: Append-only events require consensus but withdrawals do not
choices.2: Lock acquisition commutes but counter increments do not
choices.3: Incrementing independent counters, adding to a set, and append-only events can commute/merge; check-then-withdraw, acquiring a lock, and reserving the last ticket usually need total order or consensus
choices.4: All operations need total order; none can avoid coordination

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: What is state machine replication (SMR)?

choices.1: Running different services on each replica for load balancing
choices.2: Sharding data across replicas so each holds a slice
choices.3: Running the same deterministic service on multiple replicas and ensuring every replica processes the same commands in the same order, so they all reach the same state
choices.4: Caching read results on multiple machines

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: Which broadcast abstraction is the key one for SMR, and why?

choices.1: FIFO broadcast, because per-sender order is enough
choices.2: Best-effort broadcast, because it is fastest
choices.3: Total-order (atomic) broadcast — it ensures every correct replica delivers the same messages in the same order, which is exactly what SMR needs
choices.4: Gossip, because it is the most scalable

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: What is the concise formula for state machine replication?

choices.1: Causal broadcast + caching
choices.2: Reliable broadcast + sharding
choices.3: Consensus + eventual consistency
choices.4: Deterministic state machine + total-order broadcast

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: What is the theoretical relationship between consensus and total-order broadcast?

choices.1: They are equivalent in many models — consensus agrees on one value while total-order broadcast agrees on a sequence of values — which is why Paxos, Raft, and Zab are consensus-based replicated-log protocols
choices.2: Consensus is strictly weaker than total-order broadcast
choices.3: Total-order broadcast cannot be built from consensus
choices.4: They are unrelated problems

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: How does the 'replicated log' perspective describe total-order broadcast in SMR?

choices.1: Each replica keeps its own independent log order
choices.2: Total-order broadcast decides which command goes at each log index; every replica applies the log entries in index order, so deterministic execution yields the same state
choices.3: Logs are applied in arrival-time order at each replica
choices.4: The log stores only the final state, not commands

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Broadcast/State Machine Replication via Broadcasting.md
question: Which assumption is essential for SMR to keep replicas consistent?

choices.1: Each replica must have a synchronized physical clock
choices.2: Deterministic execution — the same command applied in the same state must produce the same next state
choices.3: Replicas must run different code for redundancy
choices.4: Commands may be applied in any order per replica

question: What three notions of 'time' do distributed-system clocks address?

choices.1: UTC, GMT, and local time only
choices.2: Physical time (what time is it), logical time (which event happened before which / ordering), and hybrid time (physical + logical)
choices.3: CPU time, wall time, and disk time
choices.4: Past, present, and future

question: What is the difference between a wall (real-time) clock and a monotonic clock?

choices.1: The wall clock never changes while the monotonic clock jumps
choices.2: The wall clock shows current date/time but can jump forward/back (NTP corrections, admin changes, leap seconds); the monotonic clock always moves forward and is used for timeouts and measuring intervals
choices.3: The monotonic clock shows the date; the wall clock measures intervals
choices.4: They are identical; the names are interchangeable

question: Why are physical clocks across machines never perfectly aligned?

choices.1: Because monotonic clocks cannot be compared
choices.2: Because NTP guarantees identical clocks everywhere
choices.3: There is always an offset (difference in the current reading) and skew/drift (difference in the rate at which clocks advance)
choices.4: Because all machines share one hardware oscillator

question: What do Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC) combine, and what do they give you?

choices.1: Vector clocks with a wall-clock prefix, giving exact concurrency detection
choices.2: Only a logical counter with no physical component
choices.3: Two physical clocks averaged together
choices.4: A physical timestamp component plus a logical counter to break ties — yielding timestamps close to physical time that also respect happened-before order (good for 'latest write' queries plus causal safety)

question: How does a node typically derive its notion of time, in layers?

choices.1: Hardware oscillator (the root) -> OS clock discipline (ticks to readings, slewing/stepping) -> synchronization with external references (NTP/PTP/GPS) -> distributed-time semantics on top (quorums, leases, logical/hybrid timestamps)
choices.2: Only from NTP, with no hardware involvement
choices.3: From the application layer down to the hardware
choices.4: Purely from a single GPS receiver per node

question: Which clock should you use for which job?

choices.1: Vector clocks for measuring durations
choices.2: Wall clock for everything, since it is the most accurate
choices.3: Monotonic clock for user-facing dates and wall clock for timeouts
choices.4: Monotonic for timeouts/backoff/durations; wall/real time for logging and user-facing timestamps; logical/vector clocks for causal ordering; hybrid for 'latest write wins' plus causal safety

question: Why do distributed systems need logical time?

choices.1: To replace the need for any network communication
choices.2: To measure precise wall-clock durations
choices.3: There is no perfectly synchronized global clock and network delays are unpredictable, so logical time tracks the ordering (causality) of events rather than real-world seconds
choices.4: Because physical clocks are always perfectly synchronized

question: What are the rules of the happened-before relation (a -> b)?

choices.1: a -> b only if both events are on the same machine
choices.2: a -> b only if a has a smaller wall-clock timestamp
choices.3: Same-process order (an earlier event precedes a later one in the same process), message order (a send precedes its receive), and transitivity; if neither a -> b nor b -> a, the events are concurrent
choices.4: a -> b whenever a's process id is smaller than b's

question: What is the Lamport clock update rule on receiving a message with timestamp t?

choices.1: C = t (overwrite with the received value)
choices.2: C = min(C, t) - 1
choices.3: C = C + t (add the timestamps)
choices.4: C = max(C, t) + 1 — ensuring the receive event has a higher logical time than the send

question: What is the key property and limitation of Lamport clocks?

choices.1: If a -> b then C(a) < C(b), but the reverse does not hold: C(a) < C(b) does not imply a -> b (the events may be concurrent) — Lamport clocks cannot detect concurrency
choices.2: C(a) < C(b) if and only if a -> b, in both directions
choices.3: They require synchronized physical clocks to work
choices.4: They detect concurrency but not causality

question: How do you build a total order from Lamport timestamps?

choices.1: Use the raw timestamp alone, which is always unique
choices.2: Break ties using the pair (timestamp, processID) compared lexicographically, producing a consistent total order across all events
choices.3: Use vector-clock comparison instead
choices.4: Sort by wall-clock arrival time

question: How do vector clocks improve on Lamport clocks?

choices.1: They use a single integer but compare it differently
choices.2: They remove the need for the happened-before relation
choices.3: They reduce the space cost to O(1)
choices.4: Each process keeps a vector of counters (one per process), so a -> b iff V(a) < V(b) component-wise and incomparable vectors mean the events are concurrent — detecting concurrency exactly, at O(n) space versus O(1)

question: What is the basic idea of NTP (Network Time Protocol)?

choices.1: Broadcast the local clock to all machines and average them
choices.2: Ask a time server what time it is, measure how long the request/response took, estimate the true time assuming network delay splits roughly evenly, and adjust the clock gradually so it converges without breaking software
choices.3: Read GPS directly on every machine with no network
choices.4: Step the clock to the first server's reply with no measurement

question: What are the four key timestamps in an NTP exchange?

choices.1: T1 (client send), T2 (server receive), T3 (server send), T4 (client receive)
choices.2: Start, middle, end, and retry times
choices.3: Stratum 0, 1, 2, and 3 times
choices.4: Local, remote, offset, and delay

question: How does NTP compute round-trip delay and clock offset from the four timestamps?

choices.1: Delay = (T3 - T2); offset = T4 - T1
choices.2: Delay = T4 - T1; offset = T2 - T3
choices.4: Delay = (T2 - T1) + (T3 - T4); offset = T4 - T1

question: What is the difference between stepping and slewing the clock?

choices.1: Stepping is for LANs and slewing is for the internet
choices.2: Both jump the clock; they differ only in size
choices.3: Stepping jumps the clock once when it is far off (e.g. at startup); slewing gradually speeds up or slows down the clock for normal corrections, avoiding breaking logs, TLS, databases, and timers
choices.4: Stepping gradually adjusts the rate; slewing jumps the clock

question: What is the NTP stratum hierarchy?

choices.1: Stratum 0 is reference clocks (GPS/atomic, not directly on the network); stratum 1 servers connect directly to stratum 0; stratum 2/3/... sync from the level above — lower stratum is closer to the source
choices.2: Higher stratum numbers mean closer to the atomic source
choices.3: Strata describe network ports, not time sources
choices.4: Stratum 0 is the least accurate layer

question: How does NTP stay accurate despite a messy network?

choices.1: It disables the local clock and reads the server live each time
choices.2: It polls repeatedly and keeps history, filters out bad samples (spikes/jitter), selects from multiple servers rejecting 'falsetickers', and estimates clock drift to compensate continuously — often achieving millisecond accuracy on the internet (UDP port 123)
choices.3: It uses TCP to guarantee zero network delay
choices.4: It trusts a single server's reply without any filtering

question: What are the four levels of ordering guarantees described?

choices.1: Read, write, execute, and commit ordering
choices.2: No ordering (best-effort), FIFO ordering (per-sender), causal ordering (respecting happened-before), and total ordering (everyone delivers all events in the same order)
choices.3: Stratum 0, 1, 2, and 3 ordering
choices.4: Strong, eventual, weak, and none

question: How do Lamport and vector clocks differ in capturing causality?

choices.1: Lamport: a -> b implies L(a) < L(b), but L(a) < L(b) may still be concurrent (can't detect concurrency); vector: V(A) <= V(B) component-wise (and not equal) means A -> B, and incomparable vectors mean concurrent
choices.2: Neither can preserve causality
choices.3: Both can detect concurrency exactly
choices.4: Lamport detects concurrency; vector clocks only preserve causality

question: What does causal consistency guarantee, with a common example?

choices.1: All operations appear in a single global real-time order
choices.2: Each sender's messages are ordered but nothing else
choices.3: Replicas may diverge forever with no convergence
choices.4: Reads respect causality: if you read something and then write based on it, others won't observe your write without the thing it depended on — e.g. you shouldn't see a reply to a post without first seeing the post

question: What does strong consistency / linearizability require beyond causal ordering?

choices.1: Eventual convergence with conflict resolution
choices.2: Nothing extra; it is weaker than causal ordering
choices.3: All operations appear to occur in a single global order that matches real time, which is stronger than causal ordering and typically requires consensus/quorums
choices.4: Only per-sender FIFO ordering

question: Why does message reordering happen in practice even if each machine runs in program order?

choices.1: Because logical clocks force reordering
choices.2: Networks delay packets differently, retries duplicate messages, partitions cause late messages, and multi-leader replication creates concurrent writes
choices.3: Because TCP guarantees reordering of all streams
choices.4: Because CPUs execute instructions out of order

question: What is the practical rule of thumb for choosing an ordering guarantee?

choices.1: Use FIFO ordering for money movement
choices.2: Always use best-effort ordering for correctness
choices.3: Need everyone to agree exactly (money movement, metadata, leadership) -> total order via consensus; need cause-and-effect to make sense (feeds, collaboration) -> causal ordering; need maximum availability -> allow concurrency and resolve conflicts later
choices.4: Always use total order for every operation

question: What limitation of Lamport clocks do vector clocks solve?

choices.1: Lamport clocks preserve a -> b implies C(a) < C(b) but cannot tell whether two events are causally related or concurrent; vector clocks determine causality exactly in both directions
choices.2: Lamport clocks require synchronized physical time; vector clocks do not need any counter
choices.3: Lamport clocks use too much memory; vector clocks use less
choices.4: Lamport clocks cannot order same-process events

question: What is a vector clock?

choices.1: An array of logical clocks with one entry per process, where VC_i[k] represents process i's knowledge of process k's logical time
choices.2: A wall-clock timestamp with a logical suffix
choices.3: A list of every event's physical timestamp
choices.4: A single integer counter shared by all processes

question: What are the vector-clock update rules?

choices.1: Receive: overwrite your vector with the received one
choices.2: Send: attach only your own entry, not the full vector
choices.3: Internal event: increment every entry
choices.4: Internal event: increment your own entry; send: increment your own entry and attach the full vector; receive: take the component-wise max of your vector and the received one, then increment your own entry

question: When is one vector clock considered less than another (X < Y)?

choices.1: When the sum of X is less than the sum of Y
choices.2: When X and Y are incomparable
choices.3: When x_i <= y_i for all components i and at least one component is strictly less
choices.4: When x_0 < y_0 (the first component only)

question: How do vector clocks distinguish causality from concurrency?

choices.1: Incomparable vectors mean a -> b
choices.2: VC(a) < VC(b) means a -> b (causal, and it works in both directions unlike Lamport); if neither VC(a) < VC(b) nor VC(b) < VC(a), the events are concurrent
choices.3: Equal vectors mean the events are concurrent
choices.4: VC(a) < VC(b) means the events are concurrent

question: What is the main tradeoff of vector clocks, and where are they used?

choices.1: They cost O(n) space (the vector grows with the number of processes) and have a dynamic-membership problem, but give exact causality — used in Amazon Dynamo, Cassandra-style databases, Riak, and version control
choices.2: They cost O(1) space but lose causality detection
choices.3: They are only useful in single-process systems
choices.4: They require synchronized physical clocks

question: What does the Byzantine Generals Problem model?

choices.1: Achieving consensus in a distributed system where some participants may behave maliciously or arbitrarily, sending conflicting messages to different parties
choices.2: Electing a leader among honest nodes with synchronized clocks
choices.3: Compressing messages sent between honest generals
choices.4: Achieving coordination when the network may lose messages but all nodes are honest

question: What is the famous 3f+1 rule for Byzantine fault tolerance?

choices.1: To tolerate f Byzantine (malicious) nodes you need at least n = 3f+1 total nodes (e.g. 1 traitor needs 4, 2 traitors need 7)
choices.2: To tolerate f faults you need 2f+1 nodes
choices.3: To tolerate f faults you need f+1 nodes
choices.4: To tolerate f faults you need f^2 nodes

question: How does the Byzantine Generals Problem differ from the Two-Generals Problem?

choices.1: Both are about malicious nodes, differing only in network reliability
choices.2: Byzantine is about dishonest/malicious participants (messages are delivered fine); Two-Generals is about an unreliable network where messages can be lost
choices.3: Both are about lost messages, differing only in the number of parties
choices.4: Byzantine is about lost messages; Two-Generals is about malicious nodes

question: What is the difference between a crash fault and a Byzantine fault?

choices.1: A Byzantine fault only affects the network, not nodes
choices.2: A crash fault sends wrong data; a Byzantine fault stops responding
choices.3: They are the same; the terms are interchangeable
choices.4: A crash fault is a node that simply stops responding; a Byzantine fault is a node that sends inconsistent or malicious messages (or behaves arbitrarily)

question: Which real-world systems are built on Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus?

choices.1: Single-node relational databases
choices.2: DNS resolvers
choices.3: BFT protocols like PBFT, Tendermint, and HotStuff, used in blockchains, distributed ledgers, and cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum)
choices.4: Plain TCP/IP networking stacks

question: Why does tolerating one malicious node require 4 nodes rather than 3?

choices.1: Because the malicious node counts as two faults
choices.2: Because each node must store 3 copies of every message
choices.3: With only 3 nodes and 1 malicious, the two honest nodes cannot distinguish whether the other honest node is lying or the commander is lying; you need enough redundancy for an honest majority to outvote the inconsistent information
choices.4: Because 3 nodes cannot form a network at all

question: What is RPC (Remote Procedure Call)?

choices.1: A protocol for streaming video over UDP
choices.2: A communication protocol that lets a program execute a function on another machine as if it were a local function call, abstracting away low-level socket code
choices.3: A way to share memory directly between processes on one machine
choices.4: A database query language for remote tables

question: What is the RPC call flow (simplified)?

choices.1: The client calls a function (looks local), the client stub marshals the request, it's sent over the network, the server stub unmarshals it, the server executes the function, and the result is serialized back and unmarshalled by the client
choices.2: The client and server share memory and read each other's variables
choices.3: The client opens a raw socket and writes bytes the server interprets ad hoc
choices.4: The client polls the server with HTTP GET requests until done

question: Why is gRPC especially relevant for low-latency systems?

choices.1: It uses human-readable JSON over HTTP/1.1 for clarity
choices.2: It avoids serialization entirely by sharing memory
choices.3: It is the only RPC framework that supports retries
choices.4: It is a high-performance, HTTP/2-based RPC framework using Protocol Buffers, giving low latency, strong typing, and efficient binary serialization

question: What is the main pitfall of RPC's network transparency?

choices.1: It makes local calls look remote, adding unnecessary latency
choices.2: It prevents the client and server from sharing a schema
choices.3: It makes a remote call look like a local call, hiding that it can fail, has latency, can time out, and suffers partial failures — leading to retry storms, duplicate requests, and idempotency problems
choices.4: It forces all calls to be synchronous and blocking forever

question: How does RPC typically compare with REST?

choices.1: RPC is resource-oriented and human-readable; REST is function-call and binary
choices.2: Both use JSON over HTTP/1.1 and are equally fast
choices.3: RPC is function-call oriented, often over HTTP/2 with binary (protobuf) payloads, faster but more tightly coupled; REST is resource-oriented, over HTTP/1.1 with human-readable JSON, slower but more loosely coupled
choices.4: RPC is always slower than REST

question: When is RPC a good fit, and when should you avoid it?

choices.1: Good only for single-machine programs
choices.2: Good for internal microservices, low-latency distributed systems, strongly typed APIs, and polyglot environments; avoid for public APIs, easy debugging, and loosely coupled systems
choices.3: Good for public APIs and human debugging; avoid for internal services
choices.4: Good for everything; there are no downsides

question: What is the core impossibility result of the Two-Generals Problem?

choices.1: Coordination is always possible with exactly three messages
choices.2: It is impossible to achieve guaranteed coordination over an unreliable channel using only message acknowledgments — no finite number of confirmations creates absolute certainty
choices.3: Coordination is possible if both parties use synchronized clocks
choices.4: Coordination is impossible only if the parties are malicious

question: Why does the Two-Generals Problem lead to infinite regress?

choices.1: Because messengers travel infinitely slowly
choices.2: Because the generals keep changing the attack time
choices.3: To know the other party received your message you need a confirmation; to know that confirmation arrived you need another confirmation, and so on — it never terminates
choices.4: Because each message doubles in size

question: How does the Two-Generals Problem map onto real distributed systems?

choices.1: Messengers being captured maps to lost packets, no shared clock maps to no global time, and the need for a simultaneous attack maps to the need for a coordinated commit over an unreliable network
choices.2: It maps to disk failure recovery
choices.3: It maps to CPU cache coherence between cores
choices.4: It maps to malicious nodes sending false data

question: How do consensus protocols like Paxos and Raft relate to the Two-Generals impossibility?

choices.1: They fully solve it, achieving guaranteed coordination over a lossy channel
choices.2: They make it worse by requiring infinite messages
choices.3: They ignore it because they assume a perfect network
choices.4: They do NOT solve the Two-Generals problem; they weaken the goal (guaranteeing agreement, validity, and termination only under assumptions like a live majority and eventually reliable communication)

question: What practical delivery consequence follows from the Two-Generals impossibility?

choices.1: You can only ever send one message
choices.2: You can always guarantee exactly-once delivery with enough retries
choices.3: You cannot guarantee exactly-once delivery or perfect coordination; systems instead implement at-least-once or at-most-once delivery plus idempotency
choices.4: You must use a synchronized global clock

question: How does the Two-Generals Problem contrast with the Byzantine Generals Problem?

choices.1: Both are impossible in all cases
choices.2: Two-Generals is unreliable communication between 2 parties and is impossible under a lossy channel; Byzantine Generals is malicious participants among many parties and is solvable with at least 3f+1 nodes
choices.3: Two-Generals is about malicious nodes; Byzantine is about lost messages
choices.4: Both are solvable with 3f+1 nodes

question: What is consensus in distributed systems?

choices.1: The problem of compressing data sent between nodes
choices.2: Synchronizing every node's physical clock exactly
choices.3: Electing the fastest node to do all the work
choices.4: The problem of getting a group of distributed nodes to agree on one value despite delayed/lost messages, node failures, and no shared memory or global clock

question: What are the standard consensus properties?

choices.1: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
choices.2: Liveness, Safety, Fairness, Ordering only
choices.3: Availability, Partition tolerance, Latency, Throughput
choices.4: Agreement (no two correct nodes decide different values), Validity (the decided value relates to proposed values), Termination (every correct node eventually decides), and Integrity (a node decides at most once)

question: Why does consensus matter for fault tolerance?

choices.1: It makes individual nodes faster
choices.2: It guarantees the network never loses packets
choices.3: Failures create uncertainty (did the leader crash or is the network slow? was the transaction committed?), and consensus lets the system make one consistent decision despite failures — underpinning leader election, replicated state machines, distributed commit, and membership
choices.4: It removes the need for replication

question: What is the difference between single-value consensus and total order broadcast?

choices.1: Total order broadcast is weaker than single-value consensus
choices.2: Single-value consensus orders messages; total order broadcast picks one value
choices.3: Single-value consensus agrees on one value (one decision); total order broadcast agrees on one global order of messages (a sequence of decisions), as needed for replicated logs/state machines
choices.4: They are unrelated problems

question: How can total order broadcast be viewed in terms of consensus?

choices.1: As repeated consensus: consensus instance 1 decides the 1st message, instance 2 decides the 2nd, and so on, producing one agreed sequence every replica applies
choices.2: As a single consensus instance that decides all messages at once
choices.3: As consensus with no agreement property
choices.4: As a weaker problem unrelated to consensus

question: What is the relationship in power between consensus and total order broadcast?

choices.1: They are equivalent in many models: you can build total order broadcast from repeated consensus, and build consensus from total order broadcast (broadcast proposals and decide the first delivered)
choices.2: They are completely unrelated
choices.3: Consensus is strictly more powerful and cannot be built from total order broadcast
choices.4: Total order broadcast is strictly more powerful and cannot be built from consensus

question: What is Raft's job?

choices.1: To shard data across servers for write scaling
choices.2: To synchronize the physical clocks of all servers
choices.3: To make a group of servers agree on a single, ordered log of commands even if some servers crash or messages are delayed — enabling state machine replication
choices.4: To compress the replicated log for storage

question: What are the three Raft server roles, and how is time divided?

choices.1: Master, Worker, and Observer, divided by epochs of fixed length
choices.2: Proposer, Acceptor, and Learner, divided by rounds
choices.3: Primary, Secondary, and Arbiter, with no notion of terms
choices.4: Leader (handles client requests and replicates log entries), Follower (passive, responds to leaders/candidates), and Candidate (trying to become leader); Raft proceeds in numbered terms with at most one leader per term

question: How does Raft leader election work?

choices.1: The server with the lowest ID is always elected
choices.2: Followers have randomized election timeouts; if one hears no heartbeat in time it becomes a candidate, increments its term, votes for itself, and sends RequestVote messages — winning if it gets a majority; randomized timeouts reduce simultaneous (split-vote) elections
choices.3: A fixed server is always the leader and never changes
choices.4: Leaders are chosen by an external coordinator

question: Under what conditions does a Raft follower grant its vote, and why does the log check matter?

choices.1: Only if the candidate's log is shorter than the follower's
choices.2: Only if the candidate has the smallest server ID
choices.3: Whenever any candidate asks, regardless of term or log
choices.4: If the candidate's term is at least as new, the follower hasn't voted that term, and the candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as its own — the log check prevents a server with an incomplete log from becoming leader, preserving committed data

question: When is a Raft log entry considered committed?

choices.1: Once it is replicated on a majority of servers (and is safe to apply); the entry is then applied to the state machine, and quorum overlap between majorities preserves committed data across leader changes
choices.2: Once any single follower acknowledges it
choices.3: Only after every follower has acknowledged it
choices.4: As soon as the leader writes it to its own log

question: What is Raft's log matching property?

choices.1: Followers may keep entries that conflict with the leader's
choices.2: Two logs always agree on the most recent entry only
choices.3: If two logs contain an entry with the same index and term, then all previous entries in those logs are identical — enforced by the prevLogIndex/prevLogTerm consistency check in AppendEntries
choices.4: All logs must have identical lengths at all times

question: How does Raft prevent two valid leaders when an old leader reappears after a partition?

choices.1: Both leaders coexist and their logs are merged
choices.2: The old leader is permanently removed from the cluster
choices.3: The cluster pauses all writes until the old leader is rebooted
choices.4: Through terms: a returning old leader's messages carry a stale term and are rejected, and when it sees a higher term it steps down to follower — so at most one leader is valid per term

question: What are Raft's fault-tolerance characteristics and limits?

choices.1: It makes progress even when only a minority of nodes can communicate
choices.2: It tolerates any number of crash failures
choices.3: It tolerates Byzantine faults with 3f+1 servers
choices.4: A cluster of 2f+1 servers tolerates up to f crash failures and makes no progress without a majority; it does NOT tolerate Byzantine faults (it assumes nodes may crash but not lie maliciously)

question: What does two-phase commit (2PC) guarantee?

choices.1: Atomicity across multiple participants: a distributed transaction either commits everywhere or aborts everywhere
choices.2: That every participant always commits independently
choices.3: That the fastest participant's decision wins
choices.4: Eventual consistency across replicas

question: What is the role of the coordinator in 2PC?

choices.1: It picks one participant to decide for the rest
choices.2: It asks every participant whether it is prepared to commit, then tells everyone to commit only if all voted yes, otherwise to abort
choices.3: It executes the transaction alone and replicates the result
choices.4: It only logs the outcome after participants commit independently

question: What happens in Phase 1 (the prepare/voting phase)?

choices.1: The coordinator sends a prepare message; each participant validates constraints, acquires locks, and durably logs its work, then votes yes (and must not unilaterally abort afterward) or no
choices.2: The coordinator immediately tells everyone to commit
choices.3: Participants roll back any prior work
choices.4: Participants elect a new coordinator

question: Why is the prepare step what makes 2PC stronger than simply telling everyone to commit?

choices.1: Because the prepare step makes the transaction faster
choices.2: A yes vote is a durable promise — the participant writes a prepare record so that even if it crashes it can recover and still commit if told to — so no participant commits unless all are ready
choices.3: Because it lets participants abort independently after voting yes
choices.4: Because it removes the need for the coordinator

question: What is the painful failure case in 2PC?

choices.1: If the coordinator crashes after participants voted yes but before they receive the final decision, those participants are stuck — they cannot safely commit or abort on their own
choices.2: A participant crashing before voting, which is unrecoverable
choices.3: All participants voting no, which corrupts the log
choices.4: The coordinator committing before any participant votes

question: What is the main downside of 2PC?

choices.1: Blocking: if the coordinator fails at the wrong time, participants may be blocked holding locks until it recovers, and the coordinator is a bottleneck/single point of failure
choices.2: It cannot provide atomicity across participants
choices.3: It always returns stale data
choices.4: It requires Byzantine fault tolerance

question: What is the core problem conflict resolution solves in collaborative systems?

choices.1: Making multiple concurrent edits (possibly made while disconnected) converge into one consistent shared state when the changes meet
choices.2: Electing a leader to serialize all edits
choices.3: Encrypting edits between users
choices.4: Compressing edits to reduce bandwidth

question: What three properties does a good conflict-resolution system want?

choices.1: Convergence (all replicas eventually reach the same state), intention preservation (a user's edit still means what they intended after merging), and causality awareness (knowing which operations happened before/after/concurrently)
choices.2: Speed, compression, and encryption
choices.3: Atomicity, consistency, and durability
choices.4: Availability, partition tolerance, and latency

question: What makes a CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) converge automatically?

choices.1: It discards all but the most recent update
choices.2: It uses a global lock during every merge
choices.3: It routes all updates through a central coordinator
choices.4: Its merge function is commutative, associative, and idempotent, so replicas converge even if messages arrive late, out of order, or more than once — conflict resolution is built into the data structure

question: How does Operational Transformation (OT) resolve conflicts?

choices.1: It designs the data so any merge is safe, like a CRDT
choices.2: It locks the document while one user edits
choices.3: It picks the operation with the latest timestamp and discards the rest
choices.4: Instead of merging states, it transforms concurrent operations so they can be applied safely despite each other — e.g. shifting an insert's position to account for another user's earlier insert

question: What is the biggest conceptual difference between CRDTs and OT?

choices.1: CRDTs require a central server while OT is always peer-to-peer
choices.2: CRDTs make the data type inherently mergeable ('can we design data so merging always works?'); OT rewrites operations so they remain valid after concurrent edits ('can we transform this operation so it still makes sense?')
choices.3: CRDTs transform operations while OT merges states
choices.4: They are identical approaches with different names

question: When is each approach typically a better fit?

choices.1: CRDTs for decentralized, offline-first, peer-to-peer, eventually-consistent systems; OT for centralized, real-time collaborative text editing where a server orders operations
choices.2: CRDTs for centralized servers; OT for decentralized systems
choices.3: CRDTs for real-time text editing; OT for offline peer-to-peer
choices.4: Both are only suitable for single-user applications

question: What does eventual consistency guarantee?

choices.1: Updates propagate asynchronously, so replicas may temporarily disagree, but if no new updates are made all replicas eventually converge to the same value
choices.2: Every read always sees the most recent write immediately
choices.3: All replicas are updated synchronously on every write
choices.4: Writes are rejected until all replicas agree

question: Why do systems use eventual consistency?

choices.1: To avoid storing any replicas
choices.2: To eliminate the need for conflict resolution
choices.3: To stay fast, available, scalable, and fault-tolerant by accepting temporary disagreement rather than requiring synchronous coordination on every write
choices.4: To guarantee strict correctness for financial data

question: Which is a genuine downside of eventual consistency?

choices.1: Guaranteed lower availability than strong consistency
choices.2: It cannot scale beyond one node
choices.3: It always rejects writes during partitions
choices.4: Stale reads, conflicting writes, more complex application logic (retries, idempotency, conflict resolution), and confusing UX like a counter going backward

question: How does eventual consistency relate to the CAP theorem?

choices.1: It requires sacrificing partition tolerance
choices.2: It avoids the CAP tradeoff entirely
choices.3: It usually leans toward availability over immediate consistency, so during a network partition the system keeps serving requests even though replicas temporarily disagree
choices.4: It chooses consistency over availability during a partition

question: For which workload is eventual consistency a poor fit?

choices.1: Social media likes and view counts
choices.2: Notification badge counts
choices.3: Bank balances, selling the last ticket, granting/revoking permissions, password changes, and financial ledgers — where stale or conflicting data causes serious problems
choices.4: Search index updates

question: Which is a common conflict-resolution strategy for eventually-consistent systems?

choices.1: Last-write-wins (latest timestamp wins, but can overwrite valid data), version/vector clocks, merge functions, CRDTs, or manual resolution
choices.2: Two-phase commit on every write
choices.3: Rejecting all concurrent writes
choices.4: Synchronous global locking

question: What does Google Spanner uniquely combine?

choices.1: Eventual consistency with no transactions
choices.2: A single-region key-value store with no replication
choices.3: Pure availability with no consistency guarantees
choices.4: Horizontal scale and geo-replication together with strong transactional consistency, even across regions — a globally distributed relational database

question: How does Spanner store and replicate data?

choices.1: All data lives on a single leader with async backups
choices.2: Tables are partitioned into contiguous key-range 'splits', each replicated across machines via a Paxos group with a leader; a write must be durably recorded by a majority of replicas, not just one
choices.3: Each row is stored on exactly one machine with no replication
choices.4: Data is sharded but never replicated

question: How does Spanner handle a transaction that touches multiple splits?

choices.1: It uses eventual consistency across the splits
choices.2: It rejects any transaction spanning more than one split
choices.3: It uses two-phase commit, with the Paxos-group leaders of the affected splits acting as participants (Paxos within a shard, 2PC across shards)
choices.4: It picks one split's leader to apply everything alone

question: What consistency guarantee does Spanner provide, and what does it mean?

choices.1: Eventual consistency with conflict resolution
choices.2: External consistency (strict serializability): transactions behave as if run one at a time AND that serial order respects real-world commit order — if T1 commits before T2 starts committing, everyone observes T1 before T2
choices.3: Plain serializability with no real-time ordering guarantee
choices.4: Read-your-writes consistency only

question: What is TrueTime, and how is it different from ordinary clock sync?

choices.1: A logical (Lamport) clock with no physical component
choices.2: An API that returns a time interval [earliest, latest] guaranteed to contain the true current time (bounded uncertainty), rather than a single timestamp; implemented with GPS and atomic clocks
choices.3: A perfectly synchronized global clock readable as one exact value
choices.4: A counter incremented on each commit

question: How does 'commit wait' give Spanner external consistency?

choices.1: It uses a single global lock during commit
choices.2: It commits immediately and reorders later if needed
choices.3: It waits a fixed one second after every commit
choices.4: The coordinator picks a commit timestamp s at least TT.now().latest and waits until TT.after(s) is true (real time is definitely past s) before exposing the commit — so any later transaction necessarily gets a larger timestamp

question: How does Spanner behave during a network partition?

choices.1: It switches to eventual consistency automatically
choices.2: It stays fully available and serves stale data
choices.3: It silently drops the partition and continues
choices.4: It chooses consistency over availability: if a Paxos group can't maintain a majority quorum, transactions stall or time out, but they remain consistent (a CP system)

question: What does it mean for a system to be linearizable?

choices.1: Transactions run in some serial order not tied to real time
choices.2: Every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at a single point (its linearization point) between its invocation and return — as if there is one copy of the data and operations happen one at a time in real-time order
choices.3: Operations eventually converge but may be reordered
choices.4: Reads may return any previously written value

question: What real-time ordering does linearizability enforce?

choices.1: Writes are ordered but reads are unordered
choices.2: Reads always return the oldest committed value
choices.3: If operation A finishes before operation B starts, A must appear before B in the global order — so a read after a completed write sees that write or a later value, never an older one
choices.4: Operations may be observed in any order regardless of timing

question: What does linearizability achieve for clients?

choices.1: Automatic conflict-free merging of concurrent writes
choices.2: Strong consistency and the illusion of a single, up-to-date, non-replicated object, enabling safe coordination (e.g. two clients can't both hold the same distributed lock)
choices.3: The lowest possible latency
choices.4: Maximum availability during partitions

question: How is linearizability commonly achieved in distributed systems?

choices.1: By disabling replication entirely
choices.2: Leader-based replication (all writes through one leader), consensus protocols (Raft/Paxos), quorum reads/writes with R+W>N, and read barriers/leases to ensure fresh reads
choices.3: By letting every replica accept writes independently
choices.4: By using only eventual consistency

question: How does linearizability differ from serializability?

choices.1: Linearizability is weaker than serializability
choices.2: Serializability requires real-time ordering while linearizability does not
choices.3: They are the same property under different names
choices.4: Linearizability is about single-object operation ordering respecting real time; serializability is about transactions appearing in some serial order, not necessarily real-time — a system can be serializable without being linearizable

question: What is the cost of linearizability, and how does it relate to CAP?

choices.1: It removes the need for consensus
choices.2: It improves availability during partitions
choices.3: It requires coordination, so it brings higher latency and reduced availability during partitions — by CAP, a linearizable system must choose consistency over availability when a partition occurs
choices.4: It is free and always available

question: What is the core difference between SAGA and 2PC?

choices.1: SAGA uses a coordinator while 2PC uses events
choices.2: Both provide identical atomicity guarantees
choices.3: 2PC tries to make a distributed transaction behave like one atomic database transaction (strong consistency); SAGA breaks the workflow into local transactions and uses compensating actions to undo work on failure (eventual consistency)
choices.4: SAGA gives strong consistency; 2PC gives eventual consistency

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions/Distributed Transactions - SAGA vs 2PC.md
question: Why is a SAGA compensation not always the same as a rollback?

choices.1: Compensation requires holding locks until the saga ends
choices.2: Compensation always perfectly erases the committed data
choices.3: Once a local transaction has committed, the system cannot always pretend it never happened — so compensation runs a meaningful business-level undo (e.g. issue a refund, release inventory, send a correction) rather than erasing the record
choices.4: Compensation is identical to a database rollback

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions/Distributed Transactions - SAGA vs 2PC.md
question: What is the difference between choreography-based and orchestration-based SAGAs?

choices.1: They differ only in programming language
choices.2: Choreography uses a central orchestrator; orchestration uses events
choices.3: Choreography has services react to each other's events with no central controller (loose coupling, harder to trace); orchestration has a central orchestrator directing each step and compensation (easier to understand, but the orchestrator becomes a dependency)
choices.4: Both require a coordinator like 2PC

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions/Distributed Transactions - SAGA vs 2PC.md
question: Why is 2PC a poor fit for microservices?

choices.1: It only works with a single database
choices.2: It cannot provide atomicity
choices.3: It requires eventual consistency
choices.4: It can block, holds locks longer, and forces service-owned databases into a shared distributed transaction, creating tight coupling — plus the coordinator is a critical dependency

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions/Distributed Transactions - SAGA vs 2PC.md
question: When should you choose SAGA over 2PC?

choices.1: When participants support XA and blocking is acceptable
choices.2: For long-running microservice workflows where each service owns its database, you can tolerate eventual consistency, and business-level compensation is possible — favoring availability over immediate global consistency
choices.3: For short transactions needing strict atomicity
choices.4: When compensation is impossible to define

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions/Distributed Transactions - SAGA vs 2PC.md
question: What is the practical recommendation for modern microservice architectures?

choices.1: Use 2PC for event-driven systems
choices.2: Always use 2PC for every workflow
choices.3: SAGA is usually preferred because it avoids long locks, improves availability, and fits service-owned databases; use 2PC only when you truly need atomic consistency across resources and can accept the operational cost
choices.4: Avoid both and use a single shared database

question: What does the CAP theorem state?

choices.1: A distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee all three of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance
choices.2: A distributed system can always have all three of C, A, and P
choices.3: Consistency and availability are the same property
choices.4: Partition tolerance is optional in real systems

question: What do the three CAP properties mean?

choices.1: Consistency = eventual convergence; Availability = strong consistency; Partition tolerance = replication
choices.2: They are three names for fault tolerance
choices.3: Consistency = fast reads; Availability = no downtime ever; Partition tolerance = sharding
choices.4: Consistency = every read sees the most recent write (linearizability); Availability = every request gets a non-error response; Partition tolerance = the system keeps operating despite dropped/delayed messages between nodes

question: What is the correct interpretation of CAP (the 'interview gold')?

choices.1: Availability is impossible in any distributed system
choices.2: You can always avoid partitions and keep all three
choices.3: You freely pick any two of the three at design time
choices.4: It does NOT say 'pick two of three'; since network partitions are unavoidable, during a partition you must choose between consistency and availability — so real systems are CP or AP

question: How do CP and AP systems behave during a partition, with examples?

choices.1: CP systems serve stale data; AP systems reject requests
choices.2: Both reject all requests during a partition
choices.3: Both always return the latest data during a partition
choices.4: CP systems reject requests to preserve strong consistency (Spanner, ZooKeeper, etcd); AP systems keep serving requests, possibly returning stale data and resolving conflicts later (Dynamo, Cassandra, Riak)

question: Where do consensus protocols like Paxos and Raft sit on the CAP spectrum?

choices.1: They are CP: they sacrifice availability during a partition and require a majority quorum to make progress
choices.2: They are AP: they stay available and return stale data
choices.3: They avoid CAP because they are not distributed
choices.4: They guarantee all three of C, A, and P

question: What does the PACELC refinement add to CAP?

choices.1: It states that consistency is always the best choice
choices.2: It says you can always have all three properties
choices.3: 'If Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else (normal operation) choose Latency or Consistency' — capturing the latency/consistency tradeoff that exists even without partitions, which CAP ignores
choices.4: It replaces partition tolerance with performance

question: What is a quorum in distributed systems?

choices.1: The minimum number of nodes that must agree on an operation for it to be valid/committed — typically a majority
choices.2: The single fastest node that decides for the rest
choices.3: The number of nodes that have failed
choices.4: The total number of nodes in the cluster

question: What is the majority-quorum formula and how many failures does it tolerate?

choices.1: quorum = 2; it tolerates half the nodes failing
choices.2: quorum = N; it tolerates N-1 failures
choices.3: quorum = floor(N/2) + 1; e.g. 5 nodes need a quorum of 3 and tolerate 2 failures
choices.4: quorum = N/2; it tolerates N/2 failures

question: What is the key intersection property that makes quorums work?

choices.1: Any two quorums must overlap by at least one node, so two conflicting decisions cannot both succeed — preventing split-brain and keeping the system consistent
choices.2: Quorums must never share any nodes
choices.3: Quorums must be the same size as the cluster
choices.4: Every quorum must include the leader

question: For read/write quorums, what condition guarantees a read sees the latest write?

choices.1: R + W = N exactly
choices.2: R + W < N, to keep reads and writes independent
choices.3: R = W = N, requiring all nodes for every operation
choices.4: R + W > N, so every read quorum overlaps every completed write quorum

question: Why are odd cluster sizes preferred for quorums?

choices.1: Odd sizes allow ties to be broken by coin flip
choices.2: An even size adds cost without increasing fault tolerance — 4 nodes tolerate only 1 failure (same as 3), while 5 tolerate 2
choices.3: Odd sizes use less memory per node
choices.4: Even sizes cannot form a majority at all

question: What does a Dynamo-style flexible quorum like N=3, W=2, R=1 give you?

choices.1: Faster reads (only one replica needed) at the cost of weaker consistency, since R + W = 3 is not greater than N
choices.2: The same guarantees as a majority quorum
choices.3: Strong consistency with the fastest possible writes
choices.4: Guaranteed linearizability for every read

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: What does replication provide, and what problem does it introduce?

choices.1: It guarantees all replicas are always identical instantly
choices.2: It provides fault tolerance, availability, lower latency, and read scalability — but it introduces the problem that different replicas may temporarily disagree
choices.3: It removes the need for any consistency model
choices.4: It only helps with write throughput, not reads

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: What are the three main replication styles?

choices.1: Hash-based, range-based, and round-robin
choices.2: Synchronous, asynchronous, and semi-synchronous only
choices.3: Leader-based (writes go to a leader then followers, easing ordering), leaderless (clients read/write multiple replicas directly, Dynamo-style), and multi-leader (multiple replicas accept writes, increasing conflicts)
choices.4: Primary, secondary, and tertiary, all read-only

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: Why are retries dangerous for non-idempotent operations?

choices.1: Retries are only dangerous for read operations
choices.2: Retries cause the leader to crash
choices.3: If a success response is lost, the client retries; blindly re-applying an operation like 'deduct $10' charges the account twice — at-least-once delivery makes duplicate requests common
choices.4: Retries always corrupt the network connection

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: What does it mean for an operation to be idempotent?

choices.1: Doing it once has the same effect as doing it many times — e.g. 'set plan to Pro' is idempotent, while 'increment login_count by 1' is not
choices.2: It always returns the same value regardless of input
choices.3: It requires a lock to execute
choices.4: It can only be executed once ever

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: How does an idempotency key make retries safe?

choices.1: It blocks all other clients during the operation
choices.2: It makes the operation run faster on retry
choices.3: It encrypts the operation so it can't be duplicated
choices.4: Each operation carries a unique ID; the server records which IDs it has processed, so a retry with the same ID returns the prior result instead of re-applying the operation

source: C++/Distributed Systems/Replication & Fault Tolerance/Replication & State Inconsistency Resolution.md
question: Which strategies reconcile concurrent writes to replicas?

choices.1: Last-write-wins (latest timestamp, simple but can lose data and depends on clock accuracy), version/vector clocks (track causal history), application-level merge, CRDTs, and consensus (force a single order)
choices.2: Replaying both writes in arrival order with no conflict handling
choices.3: Randomly picking one write to keep
choices.4: Always rejecting the second write

question: What is function inlining?

choices.1: The compiler defers a function call to runtime
choices.2: The compiler moves a function to a separate translation unit
choices.3: The compiler replaces a function call with the function's body, avoiding the call/return overhead (stack manipulation, jumps, register save/restore)
choices.4: The compiler converts a function into a macro at preprocessing time

question: Why does inlining enable further optimizations beyond removing call overhead?

choices.1: With the body substituted, the compiler can constant-fold across the old boundary, eliminate dead code, propagate values/ranges, and reorder instructions — whereas a non-inlined call is a black box
choices.2: Inlining disables all other optimizations for safety
choices.3: Inlining only matters for recursive functions
choices.4: Inlining forces the function to run on a separate thread

question: What is the tradeoff of inlining?

choices.1: It slows down every function call
choices.2: It increases code size; small functions inlined help, but over-aggressive inlining causes instruction-cache misses (fewer calls vs code bloat)
choices.3: It removes the need for a stack entirely
choices.4: It always reduces binary size

question: What do the [[likely]] and [[unlikely]] attributes do?

choices.1: They force a branch to always be taken or not taken at runtime
choices.2: They make a branch execute on a separate core
choices.3: They mark a function for inlining
choices.4: They are branch-prediction hints telling the compiler which condition is common, so it can arrange the generated code for better CPU performance — without changing semantics

question: At the low level, what does marking a branch [[likely]] achieve?

choices.1: It removes the branch entirely
choices.2: It duplicates the branch to run both paths in parallel
choices.3: The compiler reorders basic blocks so the likely branch is fall-through (no jump) and places cold paths like error handling far away, improving I-cache/I-TLB locality and predictor accuracy
choices.4: It inserts a runtime check that aborts if the guess is wrong

question: Why do branch hints matter for performance?

choices.1: A mispredicted branch costs roughly 15-20 cycles from a pipeline flush, so steering code layout toward the dominant branch improves prediction accuracy and keeps hot paths compact
choices.2: Hints reduce the number of instructions executed to zero on the cold path
choices.3: Branches are free, so hints only affect readability
choices.4: Hints change which branch is semantically taken

question: What are vector (SIMD) intrinsics?

choices.1: Functions that call the operating system kernel directly
choices.2: Low-level functions giving direct access to CPU vector instructions (SSE/AVX) so a program can operate on multiple data points in parallel using registers like __m128 and __m256
choices.3: Macros that expand into inline assembly comments
choices.4: Compiler built-ins for string formatting

question: What are the register widths and float capacities of SSE, AVX, and AVX-512?

choices.1: All three are 128-bit, differing only in instruction names
choices.2: SSE is 256-bit, AVX is 128-bit, AVX-512 is 1024-bit
choices.3: SSE is 64-bit, AVX is 128-bit, AVX-512 is 256-bit
choices.4: SSE/SSE2 is 128-bit (__m128, 4 floats), AVX is 256-bit (__m256, 8 floats), and AVX-512 is 512-bit (__m512, 16 floats)

question: What does _mm_add_ps do?

choices.1: Packed single-precision float addition on a __m128 register — adding 4 floats element-wise at once
choices.2: Adds 8 floats in a 256-bit register
choices.3: Performs a horizontal sum of all elements in a vector
choices.4: Adds two scalar doubles

question: What are streaming (non-temporal) stores, and what are their requirements?

choices.1: Stores that automatically read back the value to verify it
choices.2: Stores that write directly to memory bypassing some cache levels to avoid polluting the cache with one-time-use/large data; they require aligned memory (e.g. 16-byte for _mm_stream_ps) and you must not read the region immediately after writing
choices.3: Stores that compress data before writing
choices.4: Stores that always go through L1 cache for speed

question: How do you enable Intel intrinsics in C++?

choices.1: Only by writing raw inline assembly
choices.2: No headers or flags are needed; they are always available
choices.3: By linking a special SIMD runtime library at load time
choices.4: Include the right header (e.g. xmmintrin.h for SSE, immintrin.h for AVX) and compile with flags like -msse4.2, -mavx, or -mavx2 (MSVC enables them by default on x64)

question: What do __m128, __m128d, and __m128i represent?

choices.1: They are 128-, 256-, and 512-bit float registers respectively
choices.2: They are identical; only the operations differ
choices.3: __m128 holds 4 single-precision floats, __m128d holds 2 doubles, and __m128i holds packed integers — all in a 128-bit register
choices.4: They are 1, 2, and 4 byte scalar types

question: What two things does modern HTTP separate, and why does it matter?

choices.1: Encryption from authentication
choices.2: Semantics (what a request means: method, status code, headers, caching) from transport/framing (how messages are encoded and carried) — so HTTP/1.1, /2, and /3 keep the same meaning while changing delivery efficiency
choices.3: Headers from cookies
choices.4: Client code from server code

question: What does it mean that HTTP is stateless?

choices.1: Each request stands on its own; login sessions are built on top of HTTP using cookies, tokens, or server-side sessions
choices.2: The server remembers every client's full history automatically
choices.3: Every request must reopen the TCP connection
choices.4: HTTP cannot send any data, only status codes

question: Which improvement did HTTP/1.1 add over HTTP/1.0?

choices.1: Binary framing and stream multiplexing
choices.2: Status codes and headers (those were HTTP/1.0)
choices.3: Persistent connections (reuse one TCP connection for multiple requests), the Host header for virtual hosting, chunked transfer encoding, better caching, and range requests
choices.4: Running over QUIC instead of TCP

question: What performance problem remained in HTTP/1.1?

choices.1: It required a new connection per byte
choices.2: It had no caching support
choices.3: Head-of-line blocking: responses on a connection generally had to come back in order, so a slow request blocked later ones (browsers worked around it by opening several TCP connections per origin)
choices.4: It could not send headers

question: What did HTTP/2 fix, and what problem did it leave?

choices.1: Binary framing plus multiplexing many independent streams over one connection (and header compression) fixed application-level head-of-line blocking, but HTTP/2 still runs over TCP, so a single lost TCP packet stalls all streams (TCP-level HOL blocking)
choices.2: It removed headers to save bandwidth, but lost caching
choices.3: It made connections non-persistent again
choices.4: It moved to QUIC, fully eliminating head-of-line blocking

question: How does HTTP/3 address HTTP/2's remaining problem?

choices.1: It runs over QUIC (on UDP) with independent streams, integrated TLS 1.3, and connection migration — so packet loss affecting one stream does not freeze every stream (avoiding TCP-level head-of-line blocking)
choices.2: It opens a separate TCP connection per stream
choices.3: It disables encryption to reduce latency
choices.4: It reverts to plain-text framing for simplicity

question: What is Server-Sent Events (SSE)?

choices.1: A standard where a server pushes a stream of updates to a browser over a single, long-lived HTTP connection — the client opens one GET and the server keeps the response open, writing events as they happen
choices.2: A peer-to-peer media protocol for audio/video
choices.3: A polling technique where the client repeatedly requests updates
choices.4: A full-duplex protocol upgraded from HTTP for two-way messaging

question: What is the defining directional trait of SSE?

choices.1: It is bidirectional but only for binary data
choices.2: It is full-duplex, sending data both ways over one channel
choices.3: It is one-directional: data flows server-to-client only, and anything the client wants to send goes back over a separate normal HTTP request
choices.4: It is client-to-server only

question: What does the SSE wire format look like?

choices.1: Length-prefixed protobuf messages
choices.2: A binary framing layer like HTTP/2
choices.3: JSON-RPC envelopes over a WebSocket
choices.4: A text/event-stream body with line prefixes data:, event:, id:, retry:, and : (a comment/heartbeat); each event is UTF-8 text terminated by a blank line

question: What is SSE's built-in reconnection feature?

choices.1: The client must manually detect drops and reissue the request
choices.2: Reconnection requires a separate WebSocket fallback
choices.3: There is no reconnection; the stream simply ends
choices.4: If the connection drops, the browser automatically reconnects after the retry: interval and sends the Last-Event-ID header, so the server can resume the stream from where it left off — with no client code required

question: Why does the HTTP version matter so much for SSE deployment?

choices.1: Over HTTP/2 SSE is disabled entirely
choices.2: HTTP version has no effect on SSE
choices.3: SSE only works over HTTP/1.0
choices.4: Over HTTP/1.1 each SSE stream holds one TCP connection and browsers cap ~6 connections per origin (so ~6 streams max); over HTTP/2 (and /3) streams are multiplexed over one connection, so that limit effectively disappears

question: How does SSE compare with WebSockets?

choices.1: SSE is server-to-client only, plain HTTP, text (UTF-8) with automatic reconnect; WebSockets are full-duplex over an upgraded protocol, support text and binary, but require manual reconnection
choices.2: SSE supports binary while WebSockets are text only
choices.3: They are identical except for the API name
choices.4: SSE is full-duplex and binary; WebSockets are server-to-client text only

question: What fundamentally distinguishes WebRTC from SSE and WebSockets?

choices.1: WebRTC always routes media through a central server
choices.2: WebRTC is server-to-client only, like SSE
choices.3: WebRTC only supports text messages
choices.4: WebRTC connects two peers directly (peer-to-peer) so media flows the shortest path, with the server only needed to help peers find each other; SSE/WebSockets route everything through a server

question: Why does WebRTC media use UDP rather than TCP?

choices.1: For live audio/video, arriving late is worse than not arriving at all — a dropped frame should be skipped, not retransmitted
choices.2: Because TCP cannot carry encrypted data
choices.3: Because UDP guarantees ordered, reliable delivery
choices.4: Because UDP is the only protocol browsers support

question: What is the role of the signaling channel in WebRTC?

choices.1: It encrypts the media stream
choices.2: It is a standardized protocol WebRTC provides out of the box
choices.3: It relays all audio/video between the peers
choices.4: It is something you build (often a WebSocket/HTTP server) to exchange SDP offer/answer and ICE candidates so peers can connect — but it only brokers the introduction; media does not flow through it

question: What is the difference between STUN and TURN servers?

choices.1: STUN tells a peer its public IP/port so peers can connect directly (cheap, still peer-to-peer); TURN relays the media when NATs are too strict for a direct path (no longer P2P, bandwidth-expensive fallback)
choices.2: Neither is needed if both peers are behind NAT
choices.3: STUN relays media; TURN discovers public addresses
choices.4: Both relay media; they differ only in port

question: What unique capability does the RTCDataChannel offer over WebSockets/SSE?

choices.1: It guarantees lower latency than any UDP transport
choices.2: It can only send binary data, never text
choices.3: It automatically reconnects, unlike WebSockets
choices.4: You can choose reliability and ordering per channel — ordered+reliable (like TCP) or unordered+lossy (like UDP, where newest data beats complete data) — whereas WebSockets/SSE are always reliable+ordered

question: Why doesn't pure peer-to-peer WebRTC scale to group calls, and what fixes it?

choices.1: Group calls are impossible with WebRTC
choices.2: P2P scales fine; the N^2 problem is a myth
choices.3: Adding more STUN servers makes mesh calls scale
choices.4: N peers in a full mesh need N^2 connections and each uplink sends its stream N-1 times; the fix is a media server — an SFU (forwards each stream to all) or MCU (mixes streams) — which gives up pure P2P

question: What is true about encryption in WebRTC?

choices.1: Encryption is optional and off by default
choices.2: Only the signaling is encrypted, not the media
choices.3: It is mandatory — media uses SRTP with DTLS to set up keys, so WebRTC is secure by default with no opt-in
choices.4: WebRTC uses plain RTP with no encryption

question: What does a WebSocket provide?

choices.1: A full-duplex, bidirectional communication channel over a single long-lived TCP connection, where both client and server can send messages at any time with low per-message overhead
choices.2: A peer-to-peer UDP channel for media
choices.3: A request/response protocol like HTTP
choices.4: A server-to-client-only stream over HTTP

question: How does a WebSocket connection begin?

choices.1: As an ordinary HTTP/1.1 GET with an Upgrade: websocket header; if the server supports it, it replies 101 Switching Protocols and the connection becomes a raw bidirectional channel
choices.2: With a UDP handshake on a dedicated port
choices.3: With a TLS-only handshake, never HTTP
choices.4: By the server pushing an unsolicited frame to the client

question: Why are WebSockets efficient for chatty, high-frequency traffic?

choices.1: Because they compress every message with gzip
choices.2: Data travels in lightweight frames with a tiny header (as little as 2 bytes), so a small message costs only a few bytes rather than the hundreds of bytes of full HTTP headers
choices.3: Because they batch all messages into one HTTP response
choices.4: Because each message reopens the connection to stay fresh

question: What is the difference between ws:// and wss://?

choices.1: ws:// is encrypted; wss:// is plaintext
choices.2: ws:// is WebSocket over plain TCP (unencrypted); wss:// is over TLS (encrypted) — wss is the production choice and also passes proxies/firewalls more reliably
choices.3: They are identical aliases
choices.4: ws:// is for browsers; wss:// is for servers

question: What is a key drawback of WebSockets compared with SSE?

choices.1: WebSockets are server-to-client only
choices.2: WebSockets have no automatic reconnection — you must detect drops and reconnect (and re-authenticate, re-subscribe, replay missed messages) — whereas SSE reconnects and resumes for free
choices.3: WebSockets cannot traverse proxies at all
choices.4: WebSockets cannot send binary data

question: Why are WebSockets harder to scale horizontally than HTTP?

choices.1: Because each message opens a new TCP connection
choices.2: Because they cannot use load balancers at all
choices.3: They are stateful: a client is pinned to one server for the connection's whole life, so you need sticky sessions and a pub/sub backplane (e.g. Redis/Kafka) for one server to reach clients connected to another
choices.4: Because they require a fixed number of servers

question: What is a transport-layer checksum, and what does it cover?

choices.1: A 32-bit CRC computed only over the payload
