choices.2: A field in the IP header only, covering routing data
choices.3: A cryptographic hash that prevents tampering
choices.4: A 16-bit one's-complement value in the TCP/UDP header summarizing the packet, computed over the transport header, the payload, and a pseudo-header (source/dest IP, protocol number, length)

question: Why is the pseudo-header included in the checksum even though it isn't transmitted?

choices.1: It is transmitted and used for routing
choices.2: It compresses the header to save space
choices.3: It is included in the calculation to catch errors such as delivery to the wrong IP endpoint (wrong source/dest/protocol/length)
choices.4: It encrypts the payload

question: What does a transport checksum tell the receiver, and what does it not?

choices.1: It identifies and repairs the corrupted bit automatically
choices.2: It tells whether the packet arrived exactly as checksummed (valid/invalid) but not which bit is wrong — it is for detection, not correction
choices.3: It reorders out-of-order packets
choices.4: It guarantees the packet was not tampered with by an attacker

question: What are the limitations of the transport checksum?

choices.1: It never produces false negatives
choices.2: It is not perfect (some bit changes cancel out; ~1/65536 chance random corruption goes undetected) and it is not a security feature, since an attacker can modify data and recompute it — use cryptographic auth (TLS MAC/AEAD) for tamper resistance
choices.3: It can detect and correct any number of bit errors
choices.4: It is cryptographically secure against tampering

question: How do UDP and TCP differ when a packet fails its checksum?

choices.1: Both forward the corrupted packet to the application
choices.2: Both automatically correct the corrupted bits
choices.3: UDP retransmits; TCP silently drops with no recovery
choices.4: UDP discards the datagram (the application may never know it existed); TCP discards the segment and repairs the stream by retransmission (no ACK -> sender resends)

question: How does UDP checksum behavior differ between IPv4 and IPv6?

choices.1: It is required in IPv4 and optional in IPv6
choices.2: It is required in both
choices.3: It is optional in both
choices.4: In IPv4 the UDP checksum is optional (though normally used); in IPv6 it is generally required

question: What is head-of-line (HOL) blocking?

choices.1: When a queue overflows and drops the newest items
choices.2: When one delayed or lost item at the front of a queue prevents later, already-available items from being delivered or processed
choices.3: When two senders compete for the same port
choices.4: When packets arrive faster than they can be sent

question: Why does TCP cause transport-level head-of-line blocking?

choices.1: TCP has no retransmission, so loss is permanent
choices.2: TCP delivers bytes out of order, confusing the application
choices.3: TCP exposes a single reliable, ordered byte stream, so if one segment is lost, later bytes cannot be delivered to the application until the missing segment is retransmitted
choices.4: TCP opens too many connections at once

question: How does HOL blocking still affect HTTP/2 despite its multiplexing?

choices.1: HTTP/2 fully eliminates HOL blocking at every layer
choices.2: HTTP/2 multiplexes many streams over one TCP connection (fixing application-level HOL blocking), but because it sits on TCP, a single lost TCP packet can block all HTTP/2 streams sharing that connection
choices.3: HTTP/2 makes HOL blocking worse than HTTP/1.1 at the app layer
choices.4: HTTP/2 uses UDP, so it has no HOL blocking

question: How does plain UDP relate to head-of-line blocking?

choices.1: UDP does not enforce ordering, so a lost datagram doesn't block later ones — but it provides no built-in reliability or ordering, so the application must add those (and may recreate HOL blocking if it builds a single ordered stream)
choices.2: UDP guarantees ordered delivery, causing HOL blocking
choices.3: UDP cannot deliver any packet out of order
choices.4: UDP retransmits lost packets, blocking later ones

question: How does QUIC address head-of-line blocking, and what does it not eliminate?

choices.1: QUIC runs over UDP with independent streams, so packet loss affecting one stream doesn't block others (solving connection-wide HOL blocking) — but HOL blocking can still occur within a single ordered stream
choices.2: QUIC eliminates all HOL blocking, even within a stream
choices.3: QUIC reintroduces TCP-style global ordering, so it has full HOL blocking
choices.4: QUIC has no streams, so the concept doesn't apply

question: Which row correctly summarizes HOL blocking behavior?

choices.1: TCP: none; UDP: full; QUIC: full
choices.2: TCP: per-stream only; UDP: connection-wide; QUIC: none
choices.3: TCP: yes, across the whole byte stream; UDP: not by itself (but no recovery either); QUIC: partially solved (loss blocks only the affected stream)
choices.4: All three behave identically

question: What are the seven OSI layers from top (7) to bottom (1)?

choices.1: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
choices.2: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application (this is bottom-to-top)
choices.3: HTTP, TLS, TCP, IP, Ethernet, MAC, Bits
choices.4: Application, Transport, Internet, Link (that is the TCP/IP model)

question: What does each of the Transport, Network, and Data Link layers do?

choices.1: Transport: end-to-end delivery between devices (TCP/UDP, ports); Network: routes packets between networks (IP, routers); Data Link: moves frames within the same local network (Ethernet/Wi-Fi, MAC addresses)
choices.2: Transport sends raw bits; Network moves frames; Data Link uses ports
choices.3: All three do the same routing job at different speeds
choices.4: Transport routes between networks; Network does end-to-end delivery; Data Link sends raw bits

question: What do the Presentation and Session layers handle?

choices.1: Both handle physical cabling
choices.2: Presentation manages sessions; Session encrypts data
choices.3: Presentation formats/encrypts/compresses data (TLS/SSL, JPEG, character encoding); Session starts, manages, and ends communication sessions (login sessions, RPC)
choices.4: Presentation routes packets; Session sends raw bits

question: How does the TCP/IP model map onto the OSI layers?

choices.1: Application (OSI 7/6/5), Transport (OSI 4), Internet (OSI 3), Network Access/Link (OSI 2/1)
choices.2: Application (OSI 7 only), Transport (OSI 6), Internet (OSI 5), Link (OSI 4)
choices.3: Application (OSI 4), Transport (OSI 3), Internet (OSI 2), Link (OSI 1)
choices.4: It has all seven OSI layers under different names

question: Which mnemonic captures the OSI layers from Application down to Physical?

choices.1: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away
choices.2: Every Good Boy Deserves Fudge
choices.3: All People Seem To Need Data Processing
choices.4: Richard Of York Gave Battle In Vain

question: What does TCP do when it does not receive an ACK before the retransmission timeout?

choices.1: It retransmits immediately at the same rate every time
choices.2: It permanently gives up after the first timeout
choices.3: It retransmits the segment but doubles the timeout before each subsequent retry (RTO <- 2 x RTO), e.g. 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s
choices.4: It halves the timeout on each retry to recover faster

question: Why does TCP back off at all on repeated timeouts?

choices.1: A timeout often signals network congestion; aggressive retransmission would inject more packets and worsen it, so backing off avoids congestion collapse (the network carrying mostly retransmissions)
choices.2: To synchronize all senders to retry together
choices.3: Because the OS limits how fast sockets can send
choices.4: To give the receiver time to reboot

question: Why is exponential backoff used rather than linear backoff?

choices.1: Linear backoff is illegal in the TCP spec
choices.2: Exponential backoff retries faster, improving throughput
choices.3: Congestion can get bad quickly; linear growth (1,2,3,...) is too gentle and keeps too much traffic in the network, whereas exponential growth (1,2,4,8,...) rapidly reduces pressure
choices.4: Linear backoff cannot be implemented in hardware

question: What is the distinction between retransmission-timeout backoff and congestion control?

choices.1: They are the same mechanism with two names
choices.2: Congestion control only applies to UDP
choices.3: RTO backoff controls how long TCP waits before retrying a lost segment; congestion control (the congestion window) controls how much data is allowed in flight — both reduce network load but are separate mechanisms
choices.4: RTO backoff controls how much data is in flight; congestion control controls the wait time

question: Why is exponential backoff especially valuable when many senders fail at once?

choices.1: It only matters for a single connection, not many
choices.2: It eliminates the need for congestion control
choices.3: It guarantees every sender retries at the exact same instant
choices.4: If a congested router drops packets from thousands of connections that all retry frequently/synchronized, they re-overload it; exponential backoff spreads retransmissions over time and reduces synchronized retries

question: How does exponential backoff balance recovery speed against network protection?

choices.1: The first retry is still reasonably quick (good if loss was minor), but if failures continue TCP quickly becomes conservative; once ACKs return it resumes normal RTT-based timing
choices.2: It ignores round-trip time entirely
choices.3: It never recovers normal timing once it backs off
choices.4: It always waits the maximum timeout from the first retry

question: What is the difference between TCP flow control and congestion control?

choices.1: Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed (via the receive window, rwnd); congestion control protects the network path from being overloaded (via the congestion window, cwnd)
choices.2: Flow control applies to UDP; congestion control to TCP
choices.3: They are the same mechanism with two names
choices.4: Flow control protects the network; congestion control protects the receiver

question: What governs how much data a TCP sender may have outstanding?

choices.1: Only the congestion window (cwnd)
choices.2: The effective window is min(cwnd, rwnd) — the sender must respect both the receiver's advertised window and the congestion window
choices.3: Only the receiver's window (rwnd)
choices.4: The sum cwnd + rwnd

question: What is a zero-window situation and how does TCP handle it?

choices.1: The receiver retransmits all buffered data to the sender
choices.2: The sender keeps sending at full rate and hopes the buffer clears
choices.3: The connection is immediately closed
choices.4: When the receiver's buffer is full it advertises rwnd = 0, so the sender pauses new data but periodically sends tiny zero-window probes to discover when the window reopens

question: Why does TCP need the Window Scale option?

choices.1: The 16-bit window field caps the advertised window at 65,535 bytes, which is too small for high-bandwidth, high-latency paths; window scaling expands the effective window (up to ~1 GiB) to cover a large bandwidth-delay product
choices.2: Because the window field was removed in modern TCP
choices.3: To compress the window field into fewer bits
choices.4: To let the receiver advertise a negative window

question: How does slow start grow the congestion window?

choices.1: It keeps cwnd fixed at one segment forever
choices.2: It linearly adds one segment per RTT from the start
choices.3: It immediately sends a huge burst to probe capacity
choices.4: It begins with a small initial cwnd and roughly doubles cwnd each RTT (exponential growth) until cwnd reaches ssthresh, loss/congestion is detected, or rwnd becomes the limit

question: What happens once cwnd crosses ssthresh into congestion avoidance?

choices.1: TCP switches from exponential growth to additive increase — roughly +1 MSS per RTT — a much gentler probing of capacity
choices.2: TCP resets cwnd to one segment
choices.3: TCP doubles cwnd faster than in slow start
choices.4: TCP stops sending entirely

question: Why does TCP react less harshly to 3 duplicate ACKs (fast retransmit/recovery) than to a timeout?

choices.1: Duplicate ACKs prove later segments are still reaching the receiver (the ACK clock is alive), so TCP halves ssthresh and retransmits without collapsing cwnd to 1 — whereas a timeout suggests a stalled path, dropping cwnd to ~1 MSS and re-entering slow start
choices.2: Both reactions collapse cwnd to one segment identically
choices.3: A timeout is less serious than duplicate ACKs
choices.4: Duplicate ACKs mean the connection is dead, so TCP gives up

question: Why does TCP use a four-way handshake to close a connection?

choices.1: Because the network always duplicates the final packet
choices.2: TCP is full-duplex, so data flows independently in each direction and each side must close its own sending direction separately
choices.3: Because closing requires re-authenticating both peers
choices.4: Because TCP needs four packets to flush its buffers

question: What is the sequence of the four termination steps?

choices.1: Server FIN, Client FIN, Server ACK, Client ACK
choices.2: Client SYN, Server SYN-ACK, Client ACK, Server FIN
choices.3: Client FIN, Server ACK, Server FIN, Client ACK
choices.4: Client ACK, Server FIN, Client FIN, Server ACK

question: What is a half-closed TCP connection?

choices.1: After the first FIN and its ACK, one side (e.g. the client) can no longer send data, but the other side can keep sending until it sends its own FIN
choices.2: A connection that lost its handshake and never fully opened
choices.3: A connection where data flows but ACKs are disabled
choices.4: A connection where both sides have sent FIN but neither has ACKed

question: Why does the actively-closing side enter TIME_WAIT?

choices.1: To negotiate a new sequence number space
choices.2: To re-run the three-way handshake
choices.3: To handle a possibly-lost final ACK (the peer may retransmit its FIN, so this side must remain to resend the ACK) and to let old duplicate packets expire (2MSL) before the same 4-tuple is reused
choices.4: To free the socket immediately with no delay

question: Why is graceful close sometimes only three segments instead of four?

choices.1: Because the handshake reuses the SYN packet
choices.2: Because the client skips its final ACK
choices.3: Because TCP drops the server's FIN when idle
choices.4: The server can combine its ACK and FIN into one segment (ACK+FIN), so it is three segments but still performs the same four logical actions

question: Why does establishment use three packets but termination often uses four?

choices.1: Termination needs an extra packet for encryption teardown
choices.2: Establishment is unreliable so it needs fewer packets
choices.3: There is no difference; both always use three packets
choices.4: Opening is a shared agreement (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), but closing must independently shut down two separate data directions

question: Why does TCP need a handshake before sending data?

choices.1: To assign IP addresses to both endpoints
choices.2: TCP is connection-oriented: both sides must agree they are ready to communicate and synchronize initial sequence numbers, enabling reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery
choices.3: To measure the network bandwidth
choices.4: To encrypt the connection before data flows

question: What are the three messages of the TCP handshake?

choices.1: SYN, ACK, FIN
choices.2: HELLO, READY, GO
choices.3: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
choices.4: FIN, ACK, FIN

question: What does the client's SYN packet contain and mean?

choices.1: It signals the client has finished sending data
choices.2: It requests a connection and carries the client's initial sequence number ('I want to start a TCP connection')
choices.3: It resets a broken connection
choices.4: It acknowledges the server's sequence number

question: What does the server's SYN-ACK accomplish?

choices.1: It only acknowledges the client's SYN with no sequence number
choices.2: It retransmits the client's SYN
choices.3: It both synchronizes (sends the server's own initial sequence number) and acknowledges the client's SYN
choices.4: It closes the connection

question: What is the role of sequence numbers during the handshake?

choices.1: They assign port numbers to the connection
choices.2: They measure the round-trip time only
choices.3: They encrypt the payload during setup
choices.4: Each side sends its own initial sequence number and acknowledges the other's, which lets TCP later detect missing, duplicated, or out-of-order data

question: What does TCP keepalive do?

choices.1: It speeds up the initial handshake
choices.2: It lets one side check whether the peer is still reachable when a connection has been idle for a long time, detecting half-open connections where the peer crashed or dropped without sending FIN/RST
choices.3: It reuses one TCP connection for multiple HTTP requests
choices.4: It compresses idle connections to save memory

question: How does TCP keepalive work mechanically?

choices.1: After a configured idle period it sends a small keepalive probe (a segment with no useful data); if the peer is alive it replies with an ACK, and after a configured number of failed probes the connection is declared dead
choices.2: It sends a FIN to test the peer
choices.3: It floods the peer with data until it responds
choices.4: It closes the connection immediately after any idle period

question: What three settings govern TCP keepalive?

choices.1: Source port, destination port, and sequence number
choices.2: Slow-start threshold, congestion window, and RTO
choices.3: Window size, MSS, and TTL
choices.4: Idle time (how long idle before the first probe), probe interval (wait between failed probes), and probe count (failures before declaring the connection dead)

question: How does TCP keepalive differ from HTTP keep-alive?

choices.1: They are the same mechanism at different names
choices.2: TCP keepalive (transport layer) detects dead idle connections; HTTP keep-alive (application layer) reuses one TCP connection for multiple HTTP requests
choices.3: Both operate at the transport layer
choices.4: TCP keepalive reuses connections; HTTP keep-alive detects dead ones

question: Why is TCP keepalive often too slow for application-level failure detection?

choices.1: Because it sends probes every millisecond, overwhelming the app
choices.2: It is a conservative safety mechanism with long default idle times (often hours), so applications usually implement their own faster heartbeats (ping/pong)
choices.3: Because it only works on local networks
choices.4: Because it cannot be configured at all

question: How does TCP keepalive interact with NATs and firewalls?

choices.1: The interval must be longer than the middlebox timeout
choices.2: Keepalive traffic can prevent middleboxes from deleting idle connection state, but the keepalive interval must be shorter than the middlebox's idle timeout to be useful
choices.3: Keepalive bypasses NATs entirely
choices.4: Keepalive always triggers a firewall reset

question: How does TCP detect that a segment was lost?

choices.1: The router rewrites the lost segment automatically
choices.2: There is usually no explicit 'packet lost' signal; the sender infers loss from missing acknowledgments (a timeout, or duplicate ACKs)
choices.3: The receiver emails the sender a NACK for each lost byte
choices.4: The network sends an explicit loss notification to the sender

question: What does a cumulative ACK like 'ACK 1500' mean?

choices.1: The receiver is missing 1500 bytes
choices.2: The receiver has received everything up to (but not including) byte 1500 and wants byte 1500 next
choices.3: The sender should resend bytes starting at 1500 regardless
choices.4: The receiver has received exactly 1500 bytes total across all connections

question: How does loss detection by retransmission timeout (RTO) work?

choices.1: The receiver starts a timer and resends the request
choices.2: The sender starts a timer when it sends data; if no ACK arrives before the timer expires, it assumes the segment or its ACK was lost and retransmits
choices.3: The connection is closed when the timer expires
choices.4: The router retransmits on the sender's behalf after a timeout

question: How do duplicate ACKs lead to fast retransmit?

choices.1: Duplicate ACKs mean the connection is dead and must be reopened
choices.2: The receiver sends an explicit 'retransmit now' packet
choices.3: After 1 duplicate ACK the whole window is resent
choices.4: Out-of-order arrival makes the receiver repeat the same ACK (still asking for the missing byte); after ~3 duplicate ACKs the sender assumes the segment was lost and retransmits it quickly without waiting for the timer

question: What does Selective Acknowledgment (SACK) add over cumulative ACKs?

choices.1: The receiver can report exactly which non-contiguous blocks arrived (e.g. ACK 1500, SACK 2000-3000), so the sender retransmits only the missing parts
choices.2: It replaces sequence numbers with timestamps
choices.3: It lets the receiver acknowledge data it has not received
choices.4: It encrypts the acknowledgments

question: How does TCP detect and handle a duplicate segment?

choices.1: It resets the connection on any duplicate
choices.2: It uses sequence numbers: if the receiver already has a byte range and receives it again, it discards the duplicate and re-sends the same ACK, so the application never sees the data twice
choices.3: It cannot detect duplicates at all
choices.4: It delivers the duplicate to the application a second time

question: What does TCP multiplexing mean at the transport layer?

choices.1: One application gets exclusive use of the network interface
choices.2: Multiple physical links are bonded into one connection
choices.3: Many application sockets share the network at once, and TCP keeps their data separated using sockets, ports, IP addresses, and connection state
choices.4: Data from many connections is merged into one stream

question: What uniquely identifies a single TCP connection?

choices.1: The 4-tuple: (source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port)
choices.2: Just the destination port
choices.3: The sequence number alone
choices.4: The source IP address only

question: How can two connections from the same client to the same server:port be distinguished?

choices.1: They use different destination ports
choices.2: They are separated by sequence number ranges only
choices.3: They use different source ports, so the full 4-tuples differ even though destination IP and port are identical
choices.4: They cannot be distinguished and must be merged

question: How does the OS demultiplex an incoming TCP segment to the right socket?

choices.1: It delivers to whichever socket reads first
choices.2: It inspects the application payload to guess the socket
choices.3: It matches the segment's 4-tuple (source IP/port, destination IP/port) to the established connection's socket
choices.4: It uses only the destination port

question: How does a listening socket differ from a connected socket?

choices.1: A connected socket accepts new connections; a listening socket carries data
choices.2: Listening and connected sockets are the same object reused
choices.3: A listening socket can only handle one connection ever
choices.4: A server starts with a listening socket on a port that accepts new connections; after the handshake it creates a separate connected socket for each client connection

question: Why must applications define their own message boundaries over TCP?

choices.1: TCP rejects writes that aren't message-aligned
choices.2: TCP delivers each write as a separate datagram
choices.3: TCP preserves exact message boundaries, so framing is unnecessary
choices.4: TCP provides a reliable, ordered byte stream and does not preserve application message boundaries, so two writes may be read as one chunk or split arbitrarily; the app must frame messages (length prefix, delimiter, HTTP/TLS framing)

question: What problem does Nagle's algorithm address?

choices.1: Out-of-order delivery of large segments
choices.2: Applications doing many tiny writes would send many small packets, each with TCP/IP header overhead, wasting bandwidth and increasing congestion
choices.3: Applications sending one huge packet that exceeds the MTU
choices.4: Connections that never close

question: What is the core rule of Nagle's algorithm?

choices.1: Only send data once the receive window is full
choices.2: Wait a fixed 200 ms before sending any data
choices.3: Always send every byte in its own packet immediately
choices.4: If there is unacknowledged data in flight, buffer small new writes until the previous data is acknowledged or enough accumulates for a full-sized segment; otherwise send immediately

question: With Nagle enabled, how might single-byte writes A, B, C, D be sent?

choices.1: A is sent immediately; B, C, D are buffered while waiting for A's ACK, then sent together (e.g. packet 1: A, packet 2: BCD)
choices.2: D is sent first, then A, B, C
choices.3: All four are dropped until the window opens
choices.4: Each of A, B, C, D is sent in its own packet immediately

question: What is the main tradeoff of Nagle's algorithm?

choices.1: It improves throughput/efficiency (fewer tiny packets) but can hurt latency for interactive applications due to buffering delay
choices.2: It only works on UDP
choices.3: It improves latency but wastes bandwidth
choices.4: It has no downsides

question: What is the bad interaction between Nagle's algorithm and delayed ACKs?

choices.1: Delayed ACKs disable Nagle automatically with no effect
choices.2: The sender waits to send more data until an ACK arrives, while the receiver delays its ACK hoping to piggyback it on outgoing data — together causing small but painful latency spikes
choices.3: They double every packet, wasting bandwidth
choices.4: They cause the connection to reset

question: When should an application disable Nagle's algorithm (TCP_NODELAY)?

choices.1: Never; Nagle should always remain on
choices.2: When the network is highly congested
choices.3: When transferring large bulk files
choices.4: When low latency matters more than packet efficiency — online games, remote desktop, SSH-like interactive sessions, low-latency trading, and real-time messaging

question: How does TCP set its retransmission timeout (RTO)?

choices.1: It is a fixed 1 second for every connection
choices.2: It is set by the receiver in each ACK
choices.3: Adaptively from measured round-trip time: RTO = SRTT + 4 x RTTVAR, so it grows when the network is slow/variable and shrinks when it is stable and fast
choices.4: It equals exactly the last measured RTT with no margin

question: What does TCP do to the RTO after a retransmission timeout?

choices.1: It retransmits and usually doubles the RTO (exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s) to avoid flooding a possibly-congested network with retransmissions
choices.2: It resets the connection
choices.3: It keeps the RTO fixed regardless of timeouts
choices.4: It halves the RTO to retry faster

question: What is fast retransmit?

choices.1: Sending data before the handshake completes
choices.2: Doubling the send rate after an ACK
choices.3: When the sender receives about three duplicate ACKs, it assumes the segment was lost and retransmits it immediately without waiting for the retransmission timer to expire
choices.4: Retransmitting the entire window on the first timeout

question: What is Karn's algorithm, and what problem does it prevent?

choices.1: It disables retransmission for slow connections
choices.2: It doubles the RTO on every ACK to be safe
choices.3: It uses only retransmitted segments for RTT samples
choices.4: TCP avoids using RTT samples from retransmitted segments (since an ACK can't be matched to the original vs the retransmission), preventing bad RTT measurements from corrupting the RTO estimate

question: What does SACK let the sender do during recovery?

choices.1: Acknowledge data before it arrives
choices.2: Send data without sequence numbers
choices.3: Retransmit only the missing byte ranges, because the receiver reports exactly which blocks arrived instead of just the next expected byte
choices.4: Skip retransmission entirely

question: Why is a timeout treated as a stronger congestion signal than duplicate ACKs?

choices.1: Duplicate ACKs always collapse the window to one segment, but timeouts do not
choices.2: They are treated identically by congestion control
choices.3: A timeout is a weaker signal and is ignored
choices.4: A timeout means nothing is getting through, so TCP retransmits, sharply reduces its congestion window, and re-enters slow start; duplicate ACKs prove some packets still arrive, so fast retransmit/recovery reacts more gently

question: What is TLS's role, and what varies between HTTPS-over-TCP and HTTP/3?

choices.1: TLS is a transport protocol that replaces TCP entirely
choices.2: TLS only encrypts; it never authenticates the server
choices.3: TLS is the cryptographic handshake (negotiates keys, authenticates the server, encrypts data); what varies is the transport underneath — over TCP, TLS runs on top of TCP, whereas in HTTP/3 TLS 1.3 is built into QUIC
choices.4: TLS changes between versions but the transport is always TCP

question: Why does a fresh TCP+TLS connection cost about 2 RTTs while QUIC costs about 1?

choices.1: Both always cost exactly the same; the difference is a myth
choices.2: QUIC skips authentication, saving a round trip
choices.3: TCP+TLS uses 0 RTTs and QUIC uses 2
choices.4: TCP and TLS are separate sequential handshakes (~1 RTT each), whereas QUIC combines transport setup and TLS key negotiation into one handshake

question: Why don't you run ordinary TLS directly over raw UDP?

choices.1: Classic TLS expects a reliable, ordered byte stream (which TCP provides), but raw UDP is unreliable and unordered — so DTLS or QUIC is used instead
choices.2: TLS is illegal over UDP by specification
choices.3: UDP is too slow for encryption
choices.4: UDP already encrypts everything, making TLS redundant

question: What are QUIC's Initial keys for?

choices.1: The first QUIC packets are protected with Initial keys derived from public information — not truly secret, but they prevent casual middlebox interference and make packet parsing consistent before stronger keys are derived
choices.2: They permanently encrypt all application data
choices.3: They are the final 1-RTT application-data keys
choices.4: They authenticate the server's certificate

question: In classic TCP+TLS, what does TLS NOT protect?

choices.1: The HTTP request body
choices.2: The TLS Finished messages
choices.3: Cookies and form submissions
choices.4: IP and TCP headers, source/destination IP and port, and some timing/packet-size metadata — TLS encrypts the payload, not the lower-layer headers

question: How does QUIC's metadata protection compare with TCP+TLS?

choices.1: QUIC exposes strictly more metadata than TCP+TLS
choices.2: QUIC makes traffic fully anonymous
choices.3: QUIC encrypts more of the transport metadata (most frame contents, handshake after Initial) than TCP+TLS, though IP/UDP headers, connection IDs, and some size/timing info still remain visible
choices.4: QUIC and TCP+TLS expose exactly the same metadata

question: What three security goals does TLS provide?

choices.1: Compression, caching, and routing
choices.2: Speed, simplicity, and statelessness
choices.3: Confidentiality (attackers can't read the data), authentication (verify the server is real), and integrity (attackers can't secretly modify the data)
choices.4: Availability, partition tolerance, and consistency

question: Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric encryption?

choices.1: Both are used identically for redundancy
choices.2: Asymmetric cryptography securely sets up the connection (but is slow), then symmetric encryption — which is fast — protects the actual data transfer
choices.3: Asymmetric for the data and symmetric for the handshake
choices.4: Symmetric encryption authenticates the server; asymmetric encrypts data

question: What does the browser check when verifying a server's certificate?

choices.1: That the server's IP matches a hardcoded list
choices.2: That it was issued by a trusted Certificate Authority, is still valid (not expired), matches the website's domain name, and has not been tampered with
choices.3: That the certificate uses symmetric encryption
choices.4: Only that the certificate exists

question: How does the certificate chain of trust work?

choices.1: The browser contacts the website to ask if its certificate is valid
choices.2: A trusted root CA signs an intermediate CA, which signs the website's certificate; the browser already trusts a set of root CAs, so it can verify the chain up to a trusted root
choices.3: Every website signs its own certificate with no authority
choices.4: Certificates are validated by the ISP, not the browser

question: How does ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (ECDHE) establish a shared secret?

choices.1: The server encrypts a random secret with its private key and sends it
choices.2: The client picks the secret and sends it in plaintext
choices.3: Both sides exchange public key values and combine them with their own private material to independently compute the same shared secret — which is never sent across the network
choices.4: The shared secret is transmitted in the ServerHello

question: What is the role of the TLS record layer after the handshake?

choices.1: It re-runs the handshake for every message
choices.2: It sends data unencrypted but signed
choices.3: Application data is split into TLS records, each encrypted and integrity-protected separately, so tampering with any record is detectable
choices.4: It compresses records without encrypting them

question: What is QUIC?

choices.1: An application-layer replacement for HTTP
choices.2: A faster version of TCP that runs directly over IP
choices.3: A UDP-based protocol with no reliability or encryption
choices.4: A modern, connection-oriented, secure transport protocol running over UDP that provides reliability, ordered delivery, encryption, ACKs, retransmission, congestion control, flow control, and connection migration

question: Why does QUIC run over UDP rather than directly over IP?

choices.1: Because UDP guarantees ordered delivery
choices.2: For deployability — middleboxes, NATs, firewalls, and operating systems already understand UDP, so QUIC deploys without kernel/middlebox changes; QUIC implements the transport logic itself rather than relying on UDP
choices.3: Because IP cannot carry encrypted data
choices.4: Because UDP provides the reliability QUIC needs

question: How does QUIC handle retransmission differently from TCP regarding packet numbers?

choices.1: QUIC retransmits the identical packet with the same packet number
choices.2: QUIC never reuses a packet number; lost data is resent in a NEW packet with a new packet number (QUIC tracks stream offsets to place bytes correctly), avoiding ambiguity between an original transmission and its retransmission
choices.3: QUIC reuses packet numbers but renumbers the streams
choices.4: QUIC cannot retransmit lost data at all

question: How do QUIC's multiplexed streams affect head-of-line blocking?

choices.1: QUIC streams must all be delivered in a strict global order
choices.2: QUIC has one ordered stream like TCP, so loss blocks everything
choices.3: QUIC eliminates head-of-line blocking entirely, even within a stream
choices.4: Data is ordered within a stream but not across streams, so packet loss affecting one stream doesn't block others — limiting head-of-line blocking to the affected stream rather than the whole connection

question: What is QUIC connection migration, and why can't TCP do it?

choices.1: QUIC identifies connections by connection IDs (not just the IP/port tuple), so a connection survives a network path change such as Wi-Fi to cellular; TCP ties the connection to the IP/port tuple, so such a change usually breaks it
choices.2: QUIC migrates connections by reopening a new TCP socket
choices.3: TCP supports migration natively; QUIC does not
choices.4: Migration means moving the connection to a different server

question: How does QUIC handle a corrupted or tampered packet?

choices.1: It immediately closes the connection on any corruption
choices.2: QUIC packets use authenticated encryption, so a corrupted/forged packet fails authentication/decryption and is discarded (treated like loss) rather than delivered as bad data
choices.3: It silently repairs the corrupted bits
choices.4: It delivers the corrupted bytes and lets the app decide

question: How does QUIC detect loss, including lost tail packets?

choices.1: It never detects loss because UDP has no acknowledgments
choices.2: It waits for the application to report missing data
choices.3: It relies solely on the receiver sending explicit NACKs
choices.4: It uses ACK-based detection (gaps in acknowledged packet numbers imply loss) plus a Probe Timeout (PTO) that triggers probe datagrams to recover from lost tail packets or lost acknowledgments

question: What is UDP?

choices.1: A protocol that encrypts all traffic by default
choices.2: A minimal, connectionless transport protocol that sends independent datagrams between ports without guaranteeing delivery, ordering, retransmission, or congestion control (best-effort)
choices.3: An application-layer protocol for web pages
choices.4: A reliable, ordered byte-stream protocol with a handshake

question: What does the UDP header contain?

choices.1: A 20-byte header with sequence and acknowledgment numbers
choices.2: Source/destination IP addresses and a TTL
choices.3: An 8-byte header with source port, destination port, length, and checksum
choices.4: A variable-length header with window size and flags

question: How does UDP's data model differ from TCP's?

choices.1: UDP is a byte stream; TCP is message-oriented
choices.2: UDP guarantees ordering while TCP does not
choices.3: Both are byte streams; they differ only in speed
choices.4: UDP preserves message boundaries — each datagram is one message — whereas TCP provides a continuous byte stream with no inherent message boundaries

question: What is UDP's reliability model?

choices.1: Reliable delivery but unordered
choices.2: Exactly-once delivery with deduplication
choices.3: Best-effort: datagrams may be lost, duplicated, reordered, corrupted-and-dropped, or delivered late, and UDP itself fixes none of this — the application must add reliability if it needs it
choices.4: Guaranteed in-order delivery with automatic retransmission

question: Why do protocols like QUIC keep UDP datagrams around 1200 bytes despite a theoretical max of 65507?

choices.1: Because the OS rejects datagrams larger than 1200 bytes
choices.2: Because larger datagrams are always encrypted and slower
choices.3: Large datagrams risk IP fragmentation (most networks have ~1500-byte MTU), and if any one fragment is lost the entire datagram is lost — so staying small avoids fragile fragmentation
choices.4: Because UDP's length field can't exceed 1200

question: When is UDP a good fit?

choices.1: Email and SSH sessions
choices.2: Anything needing guaranteed delivery and built-in congestion control
choices.3: When latency matters more than perfect reliability or late data is useless — DNS, online games, VoIP/video, NTP, DHCP, QUIC, and multicast/broadcast
choices.4: Reliable file transfer and ordered byte streams

question: What does two-phase locking (2PL) guarantee?

choices.1: Deadlock freedom
choices.2: Maximum concurrency with no blocking
choices.3: Serializability — the resulting schedule behaves exactly like some serial (one-at-a-time) ordering of transactions
choices.4: Eventual consistency across replicas

question: What are the two phases of 2PL?

choices.1: A commit phase and an abort phase
choices.2: A growing phase where the transaction acquires locks but releases none, and a shrinking phase where, after releasing its first lock, it can acquire no new locks
choices.3: A phase to acquire all locks and a phase to re-acquire them
choices.4: A read phase and a write phase that can interleave freely

question: Why does the two-phase rule guarantee serializability?

choices.1: Because it makes every transaction commit instantly
choices.2: Because it never uses exclusive locks
choices.3: Because it releases all locks immediately after acquiring them
choices.4: Forbidding new lock acquisitions after the first release prevents cyclic lock-ordering dependencies across transactions, so the schedule is equivalent to a serial order

question: Which lock types do reads and writes acquire in 2PL?

choices.1: Reads need no locks; only writes lock
choices.2: Reads acquire shared (S) locks; writes acquire exclusive (X) locks
choices.3: Both acquire the same exclusive lock
choices.4: Reads acquire exclusive locks; writes acquire shared locks

question: How do Strict 2PL and Rigorous 2PL strengthen basic 2PL?

choices.1: Strict 2PL holds all exclusive locks until commit/abort (preventing dirty reads and cascading aborts); Rigorous 2PL holds even shared locks until commit
choices.2: Strict 2PL releases all locks during the growing phase
choices.3: Rigorous 2PL holds no locks at all
choices.4: They both abandon the two-phase rule

question: What is a key downside of 2PL?

choices.1: It cannot guarantee serializability
choices.2: It offers higher concurrency than any other scheme
choices.3: It eliminates deadlocks but allows dirty reads
choices.4: Deadlocks can occur (e.g. T1 holds A wants B, T2 holds B wants A), handled by wait-for graphs, timeouts, or victim selection; it also gives lower concurrency than MVCC/optimistic schemes

question: What precisely is a data race?

choices.1: Any situation where two threads read the same variable
choices.2: Two threads access the same memory location concurrently, at least one access is a write, and the access is not synchronized — causing torn reads, undefined behavior in C/C++, and reordering surprises
choices.3: A thread that runs slower than expected
choices.4: Two threads running on the same core at different times

question: What is an atomicity violation?

choices.1: A sequence of operations intended to be atomic actually interleaves with another thread (e.g. an unprotected check-then-set), producing inconsistent shared state
choices.2: A thread that never releases a lock
choices.3: Using atomic variables when locks would suffice
choices.4: Two threads deadlocking on the same mutex

question: What is an order violation?

choices.1: Two threads acquiring locks in the same order
choices.2: A queue that processes items out of priority order
choices.3: Correctness requires operations to happen A then B, but concurrent execution allows B before A — the root of use-before-init, broken lazy initialization, and publication-safety bugs
choices.4: A compiler reordering that never affects correctness

question: What is the difference between a missed wakeup and a spurious wakeup, and the common fix?

choices.1: A missed wakeup is fixed by removing the mutex
choices.2: A spurious wakeup means the CPU faulted and must reboot
choices.3: They are the same bug with two names
choices.4: A missed wakeup is when a signal happens before the thread sleeps (often from using if instead of while); a spurious wakeup is being woken without the condition being true — both are handled by re-checking the predicate in a while loop (Mesa semantics)

question: What is the ABA problem in lock-free structures?

choices.1: A value oscillating between two threads forever with no progress
choices.2: Two threads both reading value A simultaneously
choices.3: A thread reads value A, another thread changes it A -> B -> A, so a compare-and-swap sees it as 'unchanged' though it actually changed — causing pointer-reuse and memory-corruption bugs
choices.4: A lock acquired twice by the same thread

question: What is the memory-reclamation (use-after-free) class of concurrency bugs?

choices.1: A thread reading uninitialized stack memory
choices.2: A thread allocating too much memory and crashing
choices.3: A thread accesses an object already freed by another thread — the biggest class of lock-free bugs — mitigated by hazard pointers, epochs, or RCU rather than freeing nodes prematurely
choices.4: A memory leak from never freeing nodes

question: What does a condition variable let a thread do?

choices.1: Run a thread at higher priority
choices.2: Sleep until some condition becomes true while atomically releasing a mutex and reacquiring it on wakeup, avoiding busy-waiting on shared state
choices.3: Store the condition value internally so no shared state is needed
choices.4: Acquire two mutexes at once safely

question: Why is it said that condition variables don't store conditions?

choices.1: A CV only coordinates sleeping and waking; the actual condition must be encoded in the shared state protected by the mutex
choices.2: Because the CV stores the condition but hides it from the thread
choices.3: Because conditions are stored in the CPU registers
choices.4: Because the condition is passed as the return value of wait()

question: Why must wait() atomically release the mutex AND sleep?

choices.1: To avoid the lost-wakeup race where, between checking the condition and going to sleep, another thread sets the condition and sends a notification the sleeper would miss
choices.2: Because the mutex must stay locked while sleeping
choices.3: To allow two threads to sleep on the same address
choices.4: To make the thread run faster

question: Why must the predicate be checked in a while loop rather than an if?

choices.1: Because notify_all requires a loop to function
choices.2: Because the mutex can only be checked in a loop
choices.3: Because if-statements are slower than while-loops
choices.4: Spurious wakeups and notifications meant for other conditions mean a woken thread must re-verify the condition; a while loop re-checks and sleeps again if it's still false

question: Why is a mutex required alongside a condition variable?

choices.1: Because the CV cannot wake threads without a mutex
choices.2: A CV provides no mutual exclusion on its own; the mutex makes the condition check and shared-state modifications atomic with respect to other threads
choices.3: The mutex is only needed for performance, not correctness
choices.4: The mutex stores the waiting threads' identifiers

question: How are condition variables typically implemented on Linux?

choices.1: By disabling interrupts during the entire wait
choices.2: Via futexes with a sequence counter: wait() does unlock(mutex) + futex_wait(seq, old_seq); notify increments the sequence counter and calls futex_wake (one waiter for signal, all for broadcast) — there is no hardware CV instruction
choices.3: Via a dedicated CPU condition-variable instruction
choices.4: By busy-spinning on a shared flag with no kernel involvement

question: What are the four Coffman conditions, all of which must hold for a deadlock?

choices.1: Atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability
choices.2: Race condition, reordering, contention, and blocking
choices.3: Mutual exclusion, starvation, livelock, and priority inversion
choices.4: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait

question: What follows from the fact that all four Coffman conditions are necessary?

choices.1: You must break all four simultaneously to prevent deadlock
choices.2: Breaking them increases the chance of deadlock
choices.3: The conditions only matter for processes, not threads
choices.4: Breaking any one of the four conditions makes deadlock impossible

question: What is the most common practical cause of deadlock?

choices.1: A lock ordering violation — e.g. Thread A locks L1 then L2 while Thread B locks L2 then L1, creating a circular wait
choices.2: Using too few locks
choices.3: Running on a single-core CPU
choices.4: Releasing locks too early

question: What is the most effective and widely used deadlock-prevention technique?

choices.1: Impose a global lock ordering so all threads acquire multiple locks in a predetermined total order, eliminating circular wait
choices.2: Disable preemption entirely
choices.3: Acquire locks in a random order to avoid patterns
choices.4: Give every thread its own copy of every lock

question: How does runtime deadlock avoidance via a wait-for graph work?

choices.1: It preallocates all resources at program start
choices.2: It prevents any thread from ever waiting
choices.3: The system tracks which threads wait on which resources, detects a cycle, and aborts a victim thread/transaction (used by PostgreSQL, SQL Server)
choices.4: It uses the Banker's algorithm, which is fast and standard for threads

question: Which is a recommended practical rule for avoiding deadlock?

choices.1: Acquire locks in a different order in each thread for fairness
choices.2: Always hold all locks for the entire program lifetime
choices.3: Never hold a mutex while performing blocking I/O, always use while-loops with condition variables, and prefer timeouts (try_lock) or lock-free constructs
choices.4: Perform disk and network I/O while holding the mutex to keep it simple

question: What is the core idea of event-based concurrency?

choices.1: Each task gets its own OS thread that blocks while waiting
choices.2: A small number of threads react to events via callbacks instead of one blocking thread per task; an event loop waits for events and dispatches handlers, letting a single thread handle thousands of connections
choices.3: Events are processed by busy-polling every resource in a tight loop
choices.4: The kernel runs the application logic directly

question: Why must event handlers be non-blocking?

choices.1: Because blocking handlers crash the kernel
choices.2: Because non-blocking handlers use more memory
choices.3: If one handler blocks, the entire event loop stalls and all other tasks stop making progress
choices.4: Blocking is fine; the loop spawns a new thread per handler

question: Why does the event-driven model scale better than thread-per-connection at high connection counts?

choices.1: Threads are always faster, so event loops are only simpler to write
choices.2: The event model avoids the kernel entirely
choices.3: Event loops use one thread per connection but smaller stacks
choices.4: Thread-per-connection incurs large per-thread stack memory and frequent context-switch overhead; the event-driven model uses few threads plus non-blocking I/O to handle many connections cheaply

question: What is the difference between the readiness and completion I/O models?

choices.1: Both are the same; the names differ by OS only
choices.2: Readiness ('the socket is ready for read/write') uses select/poll/epoll/kqueue; completion ('the operation finished') uses Windows IOCP or Linux io_uring
choices.3: Readiness is for disk and completion is for network exclusively
choices.4: Readiness means the operation finished; completion means it can start

question: How do select() and poll() differ?

choices.1: poll() is limited to 1024 FDs; select() has no limit
choices.2: select() uses a bitset (fd_set) usually limited to ~1024 FDs and needs the max FD; poll() uses an array of pollfd with no fixed limit; both are O(N) scans, with epoll/kqueue as modern replacements
choices.3: select() is O(1) while poll() is O(N)
choices.4: Only poll() is readiness-based; select() is completion-based

question: What is a notable downside of event-based concurrency?

choices.1: It always requires more threads than the thread-per-connection model
choices.2: Callback complexity ('callback hell') fragments control flow, single-threaded loops struggle to use multiple cores, and state must be passed through callbacks rather than living on a call stack
choices.3: It makes blocking I/O mandatory
choices.4: It cannot handle more than one connection at a time

question: What is a futex?

choices.1: A user-space spinlock that never sleeps
choices.2: A hardware instruction that implements a full mutex
choices.3: A kernel-only lock that always requires a syscall
choices.4: A 'Fast Userspace muTEX' — a Linux wait/wake mechanism tied to a user-space integer that does almost all mutex operations in user space and only enters the kernel when necessary

question: Why does the futex design improve performance?

choices.1: Because it disables interrupts during locking
choices.2: Because it avoids atomic instructions entirely
choices.3: Because it runs every lock operation in the kernel for safety
choices.4: An uncontended lock/unlock is just an atomic CAS/store with no syscall; the kernel is only involved on the slow path — to sleep on a contended lock or wake a waiter

question: What do FUTEX_WAIT and FUTEX_WAKE do?

choices.1: FUTEX_WAIT increments the integer; FUTEX_WAKE decrements it
choices.2: FUTEX_WAIT spins busily; FUTEX_WAKE resets the integer
choices.3: FUTEX_WAIT puts the thread to sleep only if the user-space integer still equals an expected value; FUTEX_WAKE wakes up to n threads waiting on that address
choices.4: Both immediately return without touching the kernel

question: How is lock state commonly encoded in a futex integer?

choices.1: It stores the thread ID of every waiter
choices.2: 0 = unlocked, 1 = locked with no waiters, 2 = locked with waiters — so only the value-2 case needs a FUTEX_WAKE on unlock
choices.3: It only ever holds 0 or 1; waiters aren't tracked
choices.4: 0 = locked, 1 = unlocked, 2 = error

question: Why is it said that 'a futex is not a mutex'?

choices.1: Because a futex cannot put a thread to sleep
choices.2: Because a futex provides stronger guarantees than a mutex
choices.3: A futex is a low-level wait/wake primitive; mutexes, condition variables, semaphores, and barriers are all layered on top of it
choices.4: Because a futex can only be used by the kernel

question: What does the kernel store to support futexes?

choices.1: Nothing; futexes are entirely user-space
choices.2: A hash table mapping (user-space address, process) to a list of waiters, so threads blocking on the same lock share the same per-address wait queue
choices.3: A copy of every thread's full register state per lock
choices.4: The actual lock value in kernel memory, mirrored from user space

question: What does Hoare (classic monitor) signaling guarantee?

choices.1: The waiter is woken but must reacquire the lock and recheck
choices.2: The signal is only a hint that may be stale
choices.3: On signal, the waiting thread runs immediately — before the signaler continues — in an environment where the condition is guaranteed true, so no while-loop recheck is needed and there are no spurious wakeups
choices.4: The signaler keeps running and the waiter rechecks later

question: How does Mesa signaling behave?

choices.1: Spurious wakeups are impossible
choices.2: The waiter runs immediately and the signaler suspends
choices.3: The condition is guaranteed true at wake time
choices.4: signal() only notifies; the signaling thread keeps running, the woken thread becomes runnable but must reacquire the lock, and the condition may no longer be true when it runs — so signaling is just a hint

question: What is the required usage pattern under Mesa semantics?

choices.1: Hold two mutexes while waiting
choices.2: Never check the condition; the signal guarantees it
choices.3: Use an if statement to check the condition once
choices.4: Re-check the predicate in a while loop (while (!condition) cv.wait(lock)), because the condition may be false on wakeup and spurious wakeups are allowed

question: Why are Hoare semantics hard to implement efficiently?

choices.1: They forbid the use of mutexes entirely
choices.2: They require disabling the cache coherence protocol
choices.3: They need every thread to poll a shared flag
choices.4: They require an immediate, atomic transfer of lock ownership and a forced context switch on every signal, plus the scheduler guaranteeing the waiter runs before anything else modifies shared state

question: Which systems use Mesa semantics?

choices.1: Only single-threaded runtimes
choices.2: Only hard real-time operating systems
choices.3: POSIX, Java, C#, and Go — because Mesa is easy to implement efficiently and scales well
choices.4: Only theoretical textbooks; no real system uses Mesa

question: What is the core tradeoff between Hoare and Mesa semantics?

choices.1: Mesa gives stronger guarantees but is slower than Hoare
choices.2: Hoare is more efficient on multicore systems than Mesa
choices.3: Hoare gives clean reasoning (condition true at wake, no loops) but is hard to implement efficiently due to forced context switches; Mesa is efficient and scalable but requires while-loop rechecks and allows spurious wakeups
choices.4: They are identical in behavior and performance

question: What is an interrupt?

choices.1: A type of memory cache
choices.2: A way to permanently stop a running program
choices.3: A software function that runs on a schedule
choices.4: A signal that makes the CPU pause and save its current state, jump to an interrupt handler (ISR) to handle the event, then restore the state and resume the original program

question: What problem do interrupts solve compared with polling?

choices.1: They guarantee data is never lost on any device
choices.2: They enable efficient event handling: the CPU does useful work and reacts only when hardware signals, instead of wasting cycles repeatedly checking each device (polling)
choices.3: They eliminate the need for an operating system
choices.4: They make the CPU run at a higher clock speed

question: What is the difference between hardware and software interrupts?

choices.1: Hardware interrupts come from external devices (keyboard, disk, NIC, timer); software interrupts are generated by instructions (system calls via int 0x80/syscall, debugging, exceptions)
choices.2: Both are generated only by the timer
choices.3: Software interrupts are non-maskable; hardware ones are always maskable
choices.4: Hardware interrupts come from instructions; software interrupts come from devices

question: What distinguishes maskable from non-maskable interrupts?

choices.1: Maskable interrupts (IRQ) can be enabled/disabled by the CPU (e.g. disabled during critical sections); non-maskable interrupts (NMI) cannot be disabled and are used for critical events like hardware failure or watchdog timers
choices.2: Maskable interrupts are for software; NMIs are for the timer
choices.3: Maskable interrupts cannot be disabled; NMIs can
choices.4: Both can always be disabled by user programs

question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous interrupts?

choices.1: Both occur only at fixed clock intervals
choices.2: Synchronous interrupts come from devices; asynchronous ones from instructions
choices.3: Asynchronous interrupts are external and occur at unpredictable times (disk I/O completion, timer, network); synchronous interrupts (exceptions/traps/faults) are caused by the currently executing instruction (divide by zero, page fault, illegal instruction)
choices.4: Asynchronous interrupts are always non-recoverable

question: How do faults, traps, and aborts (CPU exceptions) differ?

choices.1: A fault is unrecoverable; an abort is retried; a trap crashes the system
choices.2: A fault is correctable and the instruction can be retried (e.g. page fault); a trap occurs after the instruction completes and is used for debugging/syscalls (e.g. breakpoint); an abort is a severe, unrecoverable error (e.g. hardware failure)
choices.3: All three are identical and recoverable
choices.4: A trap is a hardware failure; a fault is a syscall

question: How do interrupts enable preemptive multitasking?

choices.1: Timer interrupts let the OS stop the current process, switch to another, and enforce scheduling policies — without interrupts, multitasking would not exist
choices.2: By running all processes on separate physical CPUs only
choices.3: By polling every process in a round-robin loop
choices.4: By letting each process voluntarily yield with no hardware help

question: What are the properties of the SPSC lock-free queue?

choices.1: It is a mutex-protected queue safe for any number of threads
choices.2: It supports many producers and consumers with no extra care
choices.3: It is lock-free but requires a global lock for the head pointer
choices.4: It is a lock-free ring buffer, wait-free for both producer and consumer, and correct only when there is exactly one producer thread and one consumer thread

question: Why does the SPSC queue not need CAS, only atomic loads/stores?

choices.1: Each index (head and tail) is written by exactly one thread, so there is no write-write contention on either index — plain atomic loads/stores with proper memory ordering suffice
choices.2: Because both threads write both indices, making CAS redundant
choices.3: Because the ring buffer is immutable
choices.4: Because CAS is illegal on power-of-two buffers

question: What is the role of release/acquire ordering in the SPSC queue?

choices.1: It is decorative; relaxed ordering would be equally correct
choices.2: It prevents the buffer from being a power of two
choices.3: It makes every operation sequentially consistent and slow
choices.4: The producer writes the data then stores the new tail with release; the consumer loads the tail with acquire before reading — the release/acquire pair ensures the written value is visible before the index advance is observed

question: What makes the SPSC queue non-blocking?

choices.1: It uses a condition variable to wait for space
choices.2: If the queue is full or empty, the operation simply returns false instead of blocking, and each side can always advance independently when the queue isn't full/empty
choices.3: It spins forever until the operation succeeds
choices.4: It blocks the producer until the consumer drains the queue

question: How does the Michael & Scott MPMC queue achieve lock-freedom?

choices.1: It is a singly-linked list with a dummy node and atomic head/tail pointers; enqueue uses CAS on tail->next then swings tail forward, and dequeue CASes head forward — all via lock-free CAS loops so multiple producers/consumers progress without mutexes
choices.2: It uses one lock per node
choices.3: It protects the linked list with a single global mutex
choices.4: It is an array-based queue with no pointers

question: What is the key caveat of the simple Michael & Scott implementation?

choices.1: It ignores safe memory reclamation and leaks nodes deliberately; a real system must add hazard pointers or epoch-based reclamation to avoid use-after-free on freed nodes
choices.2: It is wait-free and needs no atomics
choices.3: It requires the buffer capacity to be a power of two
choices.4: It cannot enqueue more than one element

question: How does single-queue multiprocessor scheduling work?

choices.1: The kernel polls each CPU in turn for work
choices.2: All runnable tasks go into one shared global run queue; when a CPU becomes idle it locks the queue, dequeues the next task, and runs it
choices.3: Each CPU has its own private run queue with no sharing
choices.4: Tasks are statically assigned to CPUs at creation and never move

question: What is a key advantage of single-queue scheduling?

choices.1: Zero lock contention on many-core systems
choices.2: Perfect load balancing — idle CPUs instantly grab ready tasks from the shared queue, with no separate migration code needed
choices.3: Excellent cache locality because tasks stay on one CPU
choices.4: It is NUMA-optimal by design

question: Why is single-queue scheduling rare on modern many-core systems?

choices.1: It cannot run more than one task at a time
choices.2: It requires special hardware not present in modern CPUs
choices.3: It provides no fairness guarantees at all
choices.4: One queue means one lock, causing heavy contention as many cores update it; it also causes cache invalidation traffic and poor cache locality as tasks bounce between CPUs

question: What is multiqueue (per-CPU) scheduling?

choices.1: A scheme where one CPU schedules all the others
choices.2: A model where tasks never run in parallel
choices.3: A single global queue protected by a finer-grained lock
choices.4: Each CPU maintains its own run queue and makes local scheduling decisions, with periodic load balancing to redistribute tasks — the model used by Linux CFS, FreeBSD, and macOS

question: What are the main advantages of multiqueue scheduling?

choices.1: Elimination of all task migration
choices.2: Reduced lock contention (per-CPU locks instead of one global lock), better cache locality (tasks stay on their home CPU), and NUMA-friendliness (work stays in the same memory domain)
choices.3: Guaranteed identical load on every CPU at all times
choices.4: Perfect global fairness with no balancing needed

question: Why is periodic load balancing needed in multiqueue scheduling, and how does Linux structure it?

choices.1: Linux balances by moving every task to CPU 0 periodically
choices.2: Per-CPU queues drift apart (some CPUs get more tasks), so load balancing migrates tasks when a CPU is idle or on a timer tick; Linux organizes CPUs into scheduler domains (cores -> NUMA node -> cluster -> system) and balances locally first
choices.3: Balancing happens only when a CPU is hot-unplugged
choices.4: Balancing is unnecessary because queues never become uneven

question: What does a mutex's user-space fast path do?

choices.1: An uncontended acquire is just an atomic instruction (CAS/exchange changing the lock 0 -> 1) in user space, with no system call
choices.2: It disables interrupts to take the lock
choices.3: It spins forever until the lock is free
choices.4: It always calls into the kernel to acquire the lock

question: What hardware guarantee makes a mutex prevent parallel access?

choices.1: The compiler serializes all lock operations
choices.2: Only one core is allowed to execute at a time
choices.3: The CPU pauses all other cores during a lock attempt
choices.4: Atomic CAS/exchange backed by the cache-coherence protocol (MESI/MOESI) ensures at most one thread can transition the lock from 0 (unlocked) to 1 (locked)

question: What is the typical spin-then-sleep strategy of real mutexes?

choices.1: Yield the CPU once and then give up
choices.2: Try the atomic acquisition; if it fails, spin briefly in case the lock frees up; if still locked, enter the kernel and sleep (e.g. futex FUTEX_WAIT) rather than spinning forever
choices.3: Immediately sleep in the kernel on every acquire
choices.4: Spin forever to avoid syscall overhead

question: How does unlocking a futex-based mutex work?

choices.1: An atomic store sets the lock value to 0, then a futex_wake is issued only if a waiter is sleeping
choices.2: It spins until another thread acquires the lock
choices.3: It always issues a kernel wake on every unlock
choices.4: It increments a counter and never wakes anyone

question: Why do interrupts not break mutual exclusion for a user-space mutex?

choices.1: The interrupt handler acquires the same mutex to coordinate
choices.2: User-space mutexes are immune because they run in the kernel
choices.3: An interrupt pauses your thread but runs in kernel mode and never tries to acquire your user-space mutex; no other thread can take the mutex while your thread holds it, so the critical section stays safe
choices.4: Interrupts are disabled whenever a user-space mutex is held

question: Why do kernel spinlocks need variants like spin_lock_irqsave?

choices.1: Because kernel locks never spin
choices.2: An interrupt handler can run on the same CPU as interrupted kernel code; if it tries to take the same lock, deadlock occurs — so spin_lock_irqsave disables interrupts on the local CPU while holding the lock (and sleeping mutexes can't be used in interrupt context since handlers can't sleep)
choices.3: Because user threads might take the kernel lock
choices.4: To make the lock faster by skipping atomics

question: What is priority inversion?

choices.1: A high-priority thread always running before lower ones
choices.2: Two threads of equal priority deadlocking
choices.3: A low-priority thread holds a mutex that a high-priority thread needs, while a medium-priority thread preempts the low-priority holder — so the high-priority thread is indirectly blocked by the medium-priority one
choices.4: The scheduler running threads in reverse priority order by design

question: Why is priority inversion dangerous?

choices.1: The high-priority thread misses its deadlines and system responsiveness collapses — it caused the Mars Pathfinder spacecraft to repeatedly reboot
choices.2: It only matters in single-threaded programs
choices.3: It wastes memory but never affects timing
choices.4: It speeds up low-priority threads unfairly

question: How does priority inheritance solve priority inversion?

choices.1: It aborts the low-priority thread holding the lock
choices.2: When a high-priority thread blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread, the OS temporarily boosts the holder's priority to the waiter's, so a medium-priority thread can't preempt it; the holder runs, releases the lock, then reverts
choices.3: It permanently raises the low-priority thread's priority
choices.4: It lowers the high-priority thread to match the holder

question: What is chained priority inheritance?

choices.1: It chains threads into a deadlock
choices.2: It boosts every thread in the system simultaneously
choices.3: If H waits on L1, which in turn waits on L2, all the threads in the chain get boosted, ensuring progress all the way down
choices.4: Only the directly-blocking thread is ever boosted

question: How is priority inheritance supported in practice on Linux/POSIX?

choices.1: Every Linux mutex uses priority inheritance by default
choices.2: Linux supports it only for PI mutexes (PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT, using the kernel rtmutex); POSIX defines priority inheritance and priority protection protocols but you must opt in
choices.3: POSIX forbids priority inheritance entirely
choices.4: It is implemented purely in user space with no kernel support

question: What did the Mars Pathfinder incident demonstrate?

choices.1: That deadlocks are impossible in real-time systems
choices.2: That priority inheritance causes spacecraft failures
choices.3: A low-priority thread holding a lock was preempted by a medium-priority thread while a high-priority thread timed out, causing repeated reboots — NASA fixed it by enabling priority inheritance
choices.4: That removing all locks solved the problem

question: What does pthread_create's third and fourth parameter specify?

choices.1: The thread's stack size and priority
choices.2: The detach state and scheduling policy
choices.3: The start_routine (a function with signature void* func(void*)) the new thread runs, and arg, the argument passed to it
choices.4: The mutex and condition variable to use

question: What does pthread_join do?

choices.1: It detaches the thread so its resources free automatically
choices.2: It forcibly terminates the target thread
choices.3: It signals a condition variable the thread waits on
choices.4: It waits for the specified thread to finish and, via retval, receives the pointer the thread returned (through return or pthread_exit)

question: What is the effect of pthread_detach?

choices.1: It lowers the thread's scheduling priority
choices.2: It detaches the thread so its resources are freed automatically when it finishes, with no need to join it
choices.3: It blocks until the thread completes
choices.4: It pauses the thread until it is re-attached

question: What does pthread_mutex_trylock do differently from pthread_mutex_lock?

choices.1: It blocks forever until the lock is available
choices.2: It locks the mutex and disables interrupts
choices.3: It attempts to lock the mutex without blocking, returning immediately whether or not it acquired the lock
choices.4: It unlocks the mutex if already held

question: What is required about the mutex when calling pthread_cond_wait?

choices.1: The mutex must be unlocked before the call
choices.2: Two mutexes must be held simultaneously
choices.3: No mutex is involved with condition variables
choices.4: The mutex must be locked before the call; pthread_cond_wait atomically releases it during the wait and reacquires it before returning

question: What does the pshared parameter of sem_init control?

choices.1: Whether the semaphore is shared only between threads of one process (0) or between processes (nonzero); value sets the initial count
choices.2: Whether the semaphore uses priority inheritance
choices.3: Whether the semaphore is binary or counting
choices.4: The maximum number of waiters allowed

question: What concurrency do reader-writer locks allow?

choices.1: Only one reader or one writer at any time, like a plain mutex
choices.2: Multiple writers concurrently but only one reader
choices.3: Any number of readers and writers simultaneously
choices.4: Multiple readers can hold the lock concurrently, but writers require exclusive access — no reader and writer (and no two writers) may hold it at once

question: Why use a reader-writer lock instead of a plain mutex?

choices.1: Because RW-locks are always faster than mutexes for every workload
choices.2: Because mutexes cannot protect shared data
choices.3: For read-heavy workloads (e.g. 99% reads), a plain mutex serializes everything, wasting parallelism; an RW-lock lets many readers proceed in parallel while writers get exclusive access
choices.4: Because RW-locks never block any thread

question: What are the core invariants an RW-lock must maintain?

choices.1: Writers and readers may always proceed together
choices.2: Readers are allowed only if no writer holds the lock; a writer is allowed only if reader_count == 0 AND writer_count == 0
choices.3: Only one reader is ever allowed at a time
choices.4: A writer may proceed while readers are still active

question: What is writer starvation and how is it addressed?

choices.1: Writers starve readers; the fix is to block writers
choices.2: It is unavoidable and has no mitigation
choices.3: A continuous stream of arriving readers can prevent a writer from ever acquiring the lock; the fix is to give writers priority — once a writer is waiting, block new readers
choices.4: It happens only with a single reader

question: Why does POSIX not support upgrading a read lock to a write lock?

choices.1: Because downgrades are the ones that deadlock
choices.2: Because upgrades require kernel support POSIX lacks
choices.3: Because upgrades are slower than re-locking
choices.4: A reader-to-writer upgrade easily deadlocks (e.g. two readers both trying to upgrade), whereas downgrade (writer-to-reader) is easier; so POSIX disallows upgrades

question: How are efficient RW-locks typically implemented?

choices.1: Entirely in the kernel with a syscall per operation
choices.2: A user-space fast path using atomics (reader increment, writer CAS) plus a kernel slow path for blocking (futexes on Linux, WaitOnAddress on Windows); Windows SRWLOCK packs state into a single atomic word
choices.3: By busy-spinning with no blocking primitive at all
choices.4: Purely with a single global mutex and no atomics

question: How do turnaround, waiting, and response time differ?

choices.1: Response time is always equal to turnaround time
choices.2: They are three names for the same measurement
choices.3: Turnaround = completion - arrival (total time to finish); waiting = time spent in the ready queue; response = time from submission to the first output (critical for interactive systems)
choices.4: Turnaround is time in the ready queue; waiting is total completion time

question: What is the convoy effect in First-Come First-Served scheduling?

choices.1: Jobs are run in random order, hurting fairness
choices.2: Because FCFS is non-preemptive, one long-running job blocks all the jobs behind it, giving a terrible average waiting time
choices.3: Short jobs preempt long ones, starving the long jobs
choices.4: The CPU idles even when jobs are ready

question: What is the key strength and weakness of Shortest Job First (SJF)?

choices.1: It guarantees no starvation and needs no predictions
choices.2: It maximizes throughput but worsens average waiting time
choices.3: It gives the optimal average waiting time, but it requires predicting CPU burst times (often inaccurate) and can starve long jobs; its preemptive variant is Shortest Remaining Time First
choices.4: It is preemptive by default and ignores burst time

question: What is the tradeoff in choosing the Round Robin time quantum?

choices.1: The quantum has no effect on performance
choices.2: A larger quantum always improves responsiveness
choices.3: A smaller quantum eliminates context switches
choices.4: Too small a quantum causes excessive context-switch overhead; too large a quantum makes Round Robin degenerate into FCFS

question: How is starvation of low-priority tasks addressed in priority scheduling?

choices.1: By using FCFS instead of priorities
choices.2: By never preempting any task
choices.3: By aging — a task's priority increases the longer it waits, so it eventually runs
choices.4: By permanently lowering high-priority tasks

question: What are the core rules of the Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)?

choices.1: All jobs share one queue with a fixed quantum
choices.2: New jobs start at the highest priority; a job that uses its entire quantum is demoted (treated as CPU-bound); a job that blocks for I/O early stays/gets promoted (interactive); and periodic priority boosting moves all jobs to the top to prevent starvation
choices.3: Jobs start at the lowest priority and are promoted on quantum use
choices.4: Jobs never move between queues once assigned

question: How does the Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) decide what to run?

choices.1: It picks the task with the smallest virtual runtime (vruntime); vruntime += dt * (1024 / weight), so high-weight tasks accrue vruntime slowly, and it stores runnable tasks in a red-black tree (O(log n), leftmost = next to run)
choices.2: It always runs the highest static-priority task to completion
choices.3: It picks the task with the largest vruntime
choices.4: It uses a fixed round-robin order with equal time slices

question: What do the P (wait) and V (signal) operations of a semaphore do?

choices.1: P decrements the counter and blocks the caller if the count would go negative / is <= 0; V increments the counter and wakes one blocked thread if any are waiting
choices.2: P wakes a thread and V puts one to sleep
choices.3: Both P and V block until the counter is zero
choices.4: P increments and V decrements the counter

question: What is the difference between counting and binary semaphores?

choices.1: A counting semaphore ranges over 0..N and allows up to N threads to access a resource (pools/throttling); a binary semaphore is 0 or 1, used for signaling, similar to a mutex but without ownership
choices.2: They are identical except for their names
choices.3: A counting semaphore can only be used by one thread
choices.4: A binary semaphore allows N threads; a counting one allows one

question: How does a binary semaphore differ from a mutex?
