choices.1: External: free space is split into many small holes so a large request can't be satisfied despite sufficient total free bytes; internal: you get more memory than requested (rounding to size classes/page alignment), so the slack is wasted
choices.2: External occurs only in the kernel; internal only in user space
choices.3: They are the same thing measured differently
choices.4: External is wasted slack inside allocations; internal is scattered holes

question: Why does fragmentation hurt performance?

choices.1: It raises RSS (causing memory pressure/paging), prevents allocating large blocks like 2 MiB/1 GiB huge pages, and increases TLB pressure (many 4 KiB pages instead of few large pages)
choices.2: It only affects disk I/O, never memory
choices.3: It has no real performance impact, only aesthetic
choices.4: It always speeds up allocation by spreading data out

question: Which is a common cause of memory fragmentation?

choices.1: Mixing long-lived and short-lived objects in the same pool, many sizes with low reuse, per-thread caches holding onto pages, and frequent small mmap/munmap regions
choices.2: Using a single object size for everything
choices.3: Allocating all memory at program start and never freeing
choices.4: Running on a 64-bit instead of 32-bit system

question: How does the Linux kernel proactively avoid fragmentation?

choices.1: It disables huge pages entirely
choices.2: The buddy allocator merges adjacent free blocks, and pages are classified by migratetype (MOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE/UNMOVABLE) on separate free lists so unmovable pages don't pepper movable areas
choices.3: It compacts memory on every single allocation
choices.4: It refuses any allocation larger than one page

question: What is memory compaction, and how does it work?

choices.1: Deleting unused files to free disk space
choices.2: Compressing page contents to fit more in RAM
choices.3: Swapping all pages to disk and reloading them contiguously
choices.4: A reactive technique where a compactor moves movable pages out of scattered areas into a target region, updates their PTEs, and performs a TLB shootdown, so the freed holes coalesce into larger blocks (run by kcompactd in the background or as direct compaction on the allocation path)

question: Why can compaction sometimes fail to defragment memory?

choices.1: Because movable pages cannot have their PTEs updated
choices.2: Because compaction only works on 32-bit systems
choices.3: Because the buddy allocator forbids any page movement
choices.4: Pinned/unmovable pages (RDMA pinning, get_user_pages, mlock, device MMIO) act as immovable rocks that block coalescing, and aggressive compaction can cause latency stalls

question: What core problem does free-space management solve across kernels, malloc, and filesystems?

choices.1: Compressing allocated data to save space
choices.2: Deciding the scheduling order of processes
choices.3: Tracking which regions are free, finding a region that fits a request, splitting/coalescing regions, and avoiding fragmentation — all efficiently; the underlying strategies are the same across domains
choices.4: Encrypting free memory so it can't be read

question: What are segregated free lists (bins), and why are they fast?

choices.1: A tree keyed by allocation timestamp
choices.2: Multiple free lists, one per size class, giving very fast O(1) allocation for common sizes and low fragmentation (used by ptmalloc/jemalloc/tcmalloc and slab allocators); the tradeoff is harder coalescing
choices.3: A single sorted list searched linearly for each allocation
choices.4: A bitmap with one bit per byte of memory

question: What is the slab allocator used for?

choices.1: Tracking free disk blocks in a filesystem
choices.2: Managing swap space on disk
choices.3: Allocating arbitrary variable-sized user-space buffers
choices.4: Allocating fixed-size kernel objects (inodes, dentries, file descriptors) from caches of object types divided into slabs, giving no fragmentation for fixed-size objects, very fast allocation, and object reuse with hot caches

question: What is the Two-Level Segregated Fit (TLSF) allocator designed for?

choices.1: Real-time and embedded systems — it provides guaranteed constant-time O(1) allocation, low fragmentation, and deterministic latency using two-level size indexes
choices.2: Maximizing throughput on batch workloads with no latency concern
choices.3: Allocating only powers of two like the buddy system
choices.4: Eliminating the need for any metadata

question: How do the first-fit and best-fit allocation strategies differ?

choices.1: First fit takes the largest block; best fit takes the first block
choices.2: First fit takes the first block large enough (fast, but fragments the start of memory); best fit takes the smallest block that fits (minimizes leftover fragments but is slow unless using a size-ordered tree)
choices.3: They are identical except in name
choices.4: Best fit is always faster than first fit

question: Which is a key challenge in free-space management?

choices.1: Ensuring every allocation is exactly one page
choices.2: Preventing the CPU from caching free blocks
choices.3: Guaranteeing allocations never need to be freed
choices.4: Balancing coalescing (which reduces fragmentation) against speed, plus handling external/internal fragmentation and multi-threaded contention (mitigated by per-thread arenas)

question: How is the 64-bit Linux virtual address space split?

choices.1: Kernel and user space share one flat region with no protection
choices.2: The top half is user space and the bottom half is kernel space
choices.3: The top half is kernel space (the same mapping shared by all processes), the bottom half is per-process user space; kernel pages are marked supervisor-only so user code can't access them
choices.4: Each process gets a completely separate kernel mapping

question: What is the direct physical map (physmap) in the Linux kernel address space?

choices.1: A region where all physical RAM is mapped 1:1 into kernel space (virt = phys + PAGE_OFFSET), giving fast physical-to-virtual translation without page-table walks; used by the buddy allocator, slab allocator, and page cache
choices.2: A per-process mapping unique to each task
choices.3: The region holding loadable kernel modules
choices.4: A mapping of non-contiguous physical memory into a contiguous virtual range

question: What is the vmalloc region used for?

choices.1: Holding the kernel's executable code and rodata
choices.2: Mapping non-contiguous physical memory into a contiguous virtual range — used for loadable kernel modules, large kernel buffers, and ioremap (MMIO); it is slower than the direct map
choices.3: Mapping all physical RAM 1:1 for fast translation
choices.4: Per-process user heap allocations

question: What does the kernel heap (slab/slub) allocate?

choices.1: The page cache for file data
choices.2: Swap space on disk
choices.3: Small kernel objects like task_struct, vm_area_struct, dentry, and inode, backed by physical pages from the buddy allocator
choices.4: User-space malloc allocations

question: Why do all processes share the same kernel address-space layout?

choices.1: Because user space and kernel space use the same page table entries with the same permissions
choices.2: Switching kernel mappings on every context switch would be far too slow; instead the kernel mapping is identical and stable, and access is blocked by supervisor-only page protection bits — enabling fast syscalls/interrupts and shared kernel text/data
choices.3: Because the kernel has no memory protection
choices.4: Because each process needs its own copy of the kernel code

question: What is the fixmap region?

choices.1: A cache of recently freed kernel objects
choices.2: A region that maps all of physical RAM 1:1
choices.3: A region with compile-time fixed virtual addresses whose underlying mappings change at runtime, used for APIC/LAPIC, early-boot temporary mappings, IO devices, and CPU GDT/TSS structures
choices.4: The user-space stack region

question: Why does page replacement exist?

choices.1: To decide which process to schedule next
choices.2: To compress pages before writing them to disk
choices.3: On a page fault when physical memory is full, the OS must choose a victim page to evict before bringing in the needed page; the policy that picks the victim is the page replacement algorithm
choices.4: To choose which file to cache in RAM

question: What is the Optimal (Bélády's OPT) algorithm and why isn't it used?

choices.1: It evicts the page that will be used farthest in the future, giving the lowest possible faults; it's unimplementable because it needs perfect knowledge of the future, so it's only a benchmark
choices.2: It evicts the oldest page and is widely used for simplicity
choices.3: It evicts a random page and is optimal on average
choices.4: It evicts the most recently used page

question: What is Bélády's anomaly, and which algorithm exhibits it?

choices.1: It is when increasing the number of frames can increase the number of page faults; it occurs in FIFO (a non-stack algorithm) but never in stack algorithms like LRU or OPT
choices.2: It is a hardware bug in the TLB
choices.3: It is when LRU performs worse than FIFO with more frames
choices.4: It is when more frames always reduce faults, as expected

question: How does the Clock (second-chance) algorithm work?

choices.1: It evicts the page with the highest access count
choices.2: It maintains a perfectly ordered list of every memory access
choices.3: It evicts pages strictly in arrival order with no recency info
choices.4: It approximates LRU using a per-page reference bit: a clock hand sweeps the pages, evicting one with R=0, and clearing the R bit of pages with R=1 (giving them a second chance) — all at O(1) overhead

question: Why isn't true LRU used directly in real operating systems?

choices.1: Because LRU requires future knowledge
choices.2: True LRU requires a timestamp on every memory access or a fully ordered list of pages, which hardware can't track cheaply for every access, so OSes approximate it (NRU, aging, clock)
choices.3: Because LRU performs worse than FIFO in practice
choices.4: Because LRU suffers from Bélády's anomaly

question: How does Linux's real page-replacement approach work?

choices.1: It maintains two LRU lists — an active list (hot pages) and an inactive list (cold pages); recently used pages stay/promote to active, untouched pages demote to inactive, and eviction comes from the inactive list, with the reference bit simulating LRU
choices.2: It uses true per-access-timestamp LRU
choices.3: It uses pure FIFO across all pages
choices.4: It evicts random pages with no lists

question: Why do page-replacement algorithms prefer evicting clean pages over dirty ones?

choices.1: Clean pages take longer to reload, so evicting them is cheaper
choices.2: There is no cost difference; the choice is arbitrary
choices.3: Dirty pages are always more recently used, so they're kept
choices.4: A clean page can just be discarded (its disk copy is identical), while a dirty page must first be written back to disk (≈100,000× slower than RAM); the enhanced clock uses the (R, D) bits, evicting R=0,D=0 pages first

question: What is a page swap?

choices.1: Moving a memory page between RAM and secondary storage (swap space): page-out is RAM -> swap, page-in is swap -> RAM, as part of demand paging
choices.2: Copying a page from one process to another
choices.3: Swapping the order of two pages in RAM
choices.4: Compressing a page to fit more in RAM

question: Why does swapping exist?

choices.1: Physical memory is limited but each process has a large virtual address space, so the OS keeps active pages in RAM, moves inactive ones to disk, and brings them back on demand
choices.2: To encrypt pages before storing them
choices.3: To make every page accessible to every process
choices.4: To eliminate the need for page tables

question: What does the page table entry contain for a page that has been swapped out?

choices.1: A swap slot index instead of a physical frame number, so a fault on that page triggers a swap-in
choices.2: The disk's full file path for the page
choices.3: The physical frame number, since the page is still mapped
choices.4: Nothing — the PTE is deleted when a page is swapped

question: What is thrashing?

choices.1: When the combined working set of processes doesn't fit in RAM, so processes continuously page-fault and the CPU spends most of its time waiting for disk I/O, collapsing throughput
choices.2: When the TLB is flushed too often
choices.3: When pages are never evicted from RAM
choices.4: When the CPU runs too many threads at once

question: What does the kswapd background reclaim daemon do?

choices.1: It only runs when the system is shutting down
choices.2: It compacts the page tables to save memory
choices.3: It synchronously evicts a page on every single page fault
choices.4: It asynchronously scans the inactive LRU lists when free memory drops below a watermark, evicting pages and writing dirty ones to disk, so that page faults don't block on disk I/O

question: What is page prefetching (readahead), and when does it fail?

choices.1: Writing dirty pages to disk early, which fails on SSDs
choices.2: Evicting pages before they're used, which always helps
choices.3: Loading every page of a file into RAM at open time, which never fails
choices.4: Loading pages into RAM before they're referenced based on predicted access (e.g. sequential readahead grows a per-file window; swap readahead loads neighboring swapped-out pages); it fails for random or pointer-chasing access with no locality

question: Why is a single-level (linear) page table impractical for 64-bit address spaces?

choices.1: It's an array indexed by VPN; for a 64-bit space with 4 KB pages you'd need 2^52 entries (tens of petabytes), most of them unused — wasteful for sparse address spaces
choices.2: It only works for physical addresses
choices.3: It's too fast for the TLB to keep up with
choices.4: It cannot store protection bits

question: How do hierarchical (multi-level) page tables save memory, and what's the cost?

choices.1: They store the whole table in the TLB, eliminating walks
choices.2: The virtual address is split into indices into a tree of page-table pages, so only the branches for actually-used regions are allocated (sparse) — but a TLB miss then requires multiple memory accesses (4-5 levels to walk)
choices.3: They use one global table for all processes
choices.4: They remove the need for an offset field

question: How is a virtual address translated in the x86-64 4-level page table scheme?

choices.1: The VA is hashed to find the frame in one step
choices.2: The 48-bit VA is split into four 9-bit indices (PML4 -> PDPT -> PD -> PT) plus a 12-bit offset, walking each level to reach the final PTE and physical frame
choices.3: A single 48-bit index directly selects the frame
choices.4: The top 16 bits select the frame and the rest is the offset

question: What is an inverted page table, and what is its main drawback?

choices.1: One table per process indexed by VPN, which uses the most memory
choices.2: One global table indexed by physical frame (each entry holds PID, VPN, flags), with translation via hash(PID, VPN); it's very small (size = number of frames) but translation needs a slower hash lookup and it can't easily support shared pages
choices.3: A table that maps physical to physical addresses
choices.4: A table stored entirely in the TLB with no memory cost

question: What are nested/extended page tables (EPT/NPT) used for?

choices.1: Speeding up single-process translation by caching walks
choices.2: Replacing the TLB entirely
choices.3: Virtualization — they add a second translation layer (guest VA -> guest PA via guest tables, then guest PA -> host PA via host tables), enabling hardware-assisted VMs at the cost of two-dimensional page walks
choices.4: Mapping files into memory without the page cache

question: What is the difference between a minor (soft) and major (hard) page fault?

choices.1: A minor fault is for reads and a major fault is for writes
choices.2: A minor fault needs no disk I/O — the data is already in RAM (first-touch zero page, copy-on-write, or a file page already in the page cache); a major fault requires disk/swap I/O and has orders-of-magnitude higher latency
choices.3: They are the same; the names differ by architecture
choices.4: A minor fault crashes the process; a major fault is recoverable

question: What is paging?

choices.1: Storing all of a process's memory in the TLB
choices.2: Swapping the entire process to disk at once
choices.3: Dividing memory into variable-sized logical segments
choices.4: Dividing the virtual address space into fixed-size pages and physical memory into equal-size frames, mapped by page tables — any page can map to any frame (not necessarily contiguous), which eliminates external fragmentation

question: How does the MMU translate a virtual address under paging?

choices.1: It searches RAM linearly for the matching page
choices.2: It splits the VA into a virtual page number (VPN) and an offset, looks up the VPN in the TLB, and on a hit forms the physical address; on a TLB miss it performs a page-table walk
choices.3: It hashes the entire address to find a frame directly
choices.4: It adds a fixed base register to the VA with no lookup

question: What is the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)?

choices.1: A CPU cache of recently used page-table entries that avoids a full page-table walk on every memory access
choices.2: A buffer holding pages about to be swapped to disk
choices.3: A queue of pending page faults
choices.4: A table mapping filenames to inodes

question: Which fields does a page table entry (PTE) typically contain?

choices.1: A frame number (or 'not present'), a present bit, protection bits (read/write/execute), a dirty bit, and an accessed/reference bit
choices.2: Only the frame number and nothing else
choices.3: The filename and offset of the backing file
choices.4: The process ID and thread ID

question: What is the purpose of the NX/XD (execute-disable) bit in a PTE?

choices.1: It makes the page read-only
choices.2: When set, the page cannot be executed — critical for W^X (write-xor-execute) and preventing stack/heap code-execution attacks (DEP)
choices.3: It marks the page as dirty
choices.4: It disables caching for the page

question: What is the tradeoff of using large/huge pages (2 MiB or 1 GiB)?

choices.1: They increase the number of TLB entries needed
choices.2: They always make every workload faster with no downside
choices.3: They reduce TLB pressure and page-table overhead (fewer entries/levels), lowering TLB misses and page faults, but cause more internal fragmentation and are harder to allocate as large contiguous physical regions
choices.4: They eliminate internal fragmentation entirely

question: What is a segmentation fault?

choices.1: When a program runs out of stack space and pauses
choices.2: A SIGSEGV — when a program accesses memory it's not allowed to (an unmapped address or a permission violation); the hardware faults and the OS typically terminates the process
choices.3: When the CPU executes an invalid opcode
choices.4: When two segments of a program overlap in memory

question: How does the hardware first detect a segfault-causing access?

choices.1: The disk controller reports the bad access
choices.2: The OS scans all memory accesses in software
choices.3: On a memory access the MMU translates the VA via the TLB/page table; if the address is unmapped or the access violates permissions (e.g. writing to read-only memory), it raises a #PF page-fault exception — before the OS is involved
choices.4: The compiler inserts a runtime bounds check

question: After a page fault, how does control reach the kernel?

choices.1: The CPU switches to kernel mode, saves the registers and execution state, and jumps to the OS's page-fault handler via the interrupt descriptor table (IDT)
choices.2: The process is immediately killed with no handler
choices.3: The MMU fixes the access itself with no kernel involvement
choices.4: The faulting instruction directly calls a user-space handler

question: How does the kernel's page-fault handler decide whether a fault is recoverable?

choices.1: It always retries the instruction until it succeeds
choices.2: It checks the faulting address and error code: a swapped-out page is loaded (soft fault), a copy-on-write first write makes a private copy, a stack-growth fault allocates a page — but an invalid address or wrong permission is unrecoverable and leads to a segfault
choices.3: It picks recoverability at random
choices.4: Every page fault is unrecoverable and ends in a segfault

question: How is a segmentation violation delivered to the process?

choices.1: The kernel sends the SIGSEGV signal; if the process registered a handler it runs, otherwise the default action is to terminate the process (with an optional core dump)
choices.2: The CPU retries the faulting instruction automatically
choices.3: The kernel rolls back the process to its last checkpoint
choices.4: The kernel pauses the process indefinitely

question: Why is the NULL page left unmapped, and is the faulting instruction retried?

choices.1: The lowest page (around 0x0) is intentionally unmapped so a NULL dereference faults instead of corrupting memory; the OS does not retry the faulting instruction for an invalid access (it's invalid by definition) — control only returns to run a signal handler
choices.2: The faulting instruction is always retried after the handler
choices.3: The NULL page is mapped read-write for safety, and the instruction is always retried
choices.4: The NULL page holds the interrupt vector table, and faults are ignored

question: What is segmentation as a memory-management scheme?

choices.1: Dividing memory into fixed-size pages mapped to frames
choices.2: Splitting a file into blocks for storage
choices.3: Dividing the address space into logically meaningful segments (code, data, heap, stack, shared libraries), each contiguous with its own base, limit, and permissions; memory is addressed as (segment number, offset)
choices.4: Storing the whole process on disk and loading it on demand

question: How does segmentation translate a logical address mechanically?

choices.1: It hashes (segment, offset) to find the physical frame
choices.2: It adds the offset to the program counter
choices.3: Physical address = base[segment] + offset, with a hardware check that offset <= limit[segment]; if it exceeds the limit, a segmentation fault is raised
choices.4: It walks a multi-level page table

question: What is a key motivation for segmentation?

choices.1: Different segments need different protection (code read-only/executable; heap and stack read-write/non-executable) and independent growth (stack grows down, heap grows up), plus easy sharing of code/libraries across processes
choices.2: To eliminate the need for a base register
choices.3: To force all memory to be the same fixed size
choices.4: To use one giant flat region with uniform permissions

question: Why did pure segmentation decline in favor of paging?

choices.1: Segmentation could not support more than one process
choices.2: Segmentation provided no memory protection at all
choices.3: Variable-sized segments cause external fragmentation, are hard to compact, and large sparse segments waste memory; paging uses fixed-size pages that avoid external fragmentation
choices.4: Paging is simpler to implement than segmentation in every way

question: What role does segmentation play on x86-64 today?

choices.1: It is mostly disabled — only the FS and GS segments remain meaningful (used for thread-local storage) — and paging does all the address translation
choices.2: It has been removed entirely with no remaining use
choices.3: It is used for every memory access alongside paging
choices.4: It fully replaces paging for all translation

question: What is the typical layout of a process's virtual address space (high to low)?

choices.1: Heap at the very top growing downward into the stack
choices.2: A single flat region with no distinct segments
choices.3: Text at the top, then stack, heap, and kernel at the bottom
choices.4: Kernel space, then the stack (grows down), the mmap region (shared libraries/thread stacks), the heap (grows up), BSS (zero-initialized globals), the data segment (initialized globals), the text segment (read-only code), and the unmapped NULL page

question: What are the core motivations for virtual memory?

choices.1: Forcing programs to know their exact physical load address
choices.2: Eliminating the need for any disk storage
choices.3: Making every process share one physical address space directly
choices.4: Giving each process a private isolated address space, allowing programs to use more memory than physical RAM (paging to disk), simplifying relocation/linking, enabling efficient sharing, and enforcing protection/security

question: How does the base-and-bounds mechanism work?

choices.1: It walks a multi-level page table for each access
choices.2: It hashes the virtual address to a physical frame
choices.3: A base register holds the physical start of the process's region and a bounds/limit register its size; on each access the hardware checks VA < bounds (else trap) and computes PA = base + VA
choices.4: It maps each page independently to any frame

question: Why was base-and-bounds replaced by paging?

choices.1: It provided no memory protection whatsoever
choices.2: It allows only one contiguous region per process (causing fragmentation), no sharing between processes, no lazy allocation, no swapping/overcommit, and only all-or-nothing protection
choices.3: It was too slow because every access required a page-table walk
choices.4: It could not relocate programs in memory

question: Why does x86-64 use only 48 of the 64 address bits for virtual addressing?

choices.1: Its 4-level page tables use four 9-bit indices plus a 12-bit offset = 48 bits; using all 64 bits would require roughly 36 PB of page tables per process, so 48 bits (256 TB per process) was chosen as already far more than needed
choices.2: Because the TLB can only hold 48 entries
choices.3: Because the CPU registers are only 48 bits wide
choices.4: Because RAM chips physically can't address more than 48 bits

question: What are canonical addresses on x86-64?

choices.1: Addresses that are aligned to a 4 KB page boundary
choices.2: Addresses whose top 16 bits are always zero
choices.3: Bits 63-48 must be sign-extended copies of bit 47, creating two canonical halves (low: 0x0000... and high: 0xFFFF8...); non-canonical addresses cause a general protection fault, which simplifies pointer arithmetic and leaves room for future expansion (LA57 -> 57 bits)
choices.4: Addresses that fall entirely within the heap

question: How is the base-and-bounds region enforced across a context switch?

choices.1: On a switch the OS saves the outgoing process's base and bounds and loads the incoming process's values; these registers are privileged (only the kernel can change them), so each process's region is enforced entirely by hardware
choices.2: Base and bounds are recomputed from the page table each time
choices.3: User code reloads the base and bounds registers itself
choices.4: The bounds register is shared by all processes

question: What is consistent hashing, and what problem does it solve?

choices.1: It encrypts keys before distributing them across nodes
choices.2: It maps both keys and nodes onto the same circular keyspace (a ring) so that adding or removing a node moves only ~K/N keys instead of nearly all of them — solving the resharding problem of naive hash(key) % N
choices.3: It eliminates the need for any hashing
choices.4: It guarantees every node holds exactly the same keys

question: Why is hash(key) % N problematic when the number of nodes changes?

choices.1: It only works for fewer than 4 nodes
choices.2: It leaves most keys unassigned
choices.3: It requires a central coordinator to compute every lookup
choices.4: Changing N remaps almost every key (e.g. going from 4 to 5 nodes reshuffles ~80%), causing a cache-miss storm or a massive data migration

question: On the consistent-hashing ring, which node owns a given key?

choices.1: The node whose hash is numerically closest to the key's hash
choices.2: The node with the most free capacity
choices.3: The first node found going clockwise from the key's position on the ring
choices.4: A randomly chosen node, rebalanced periodically

question: What happens to key assignments when a node is added or removed?

choices.1: All keys are redistributed evenly across every node
choices.2: Keys are unaffected; only metadata changes
choices.3: Half of all keys move to the new node
choices.4: Adding the Nth node steals only the keys in one arc (on average ~K/N) from its clockwise successor; removing a node reassigns only its keys to the next clockwise node — not a global reshuffle

question: What problem do virtual nodes (vnodes) solve in consistent hashing?

choices.1: They make hot keys distribute across nodes automatically
choices.2: They remove the ring's clockwise assignment rule
choices.3: Placing each physical node at many ring points averages out the arcs (even load) and, on removal, spreads its load across many successors instead of dumping it on one neighbor; it also enables weighting for heterogeneous capacity
choices.4: They eliminate the need for replication

question: Does consistent hashing (even with vnodes) solve the hot-key problem?

choices.1: Yes, vnodes spread a single hot key across all nodes
choices.2: No — the ring always sends one key to one node; hot keys need separate mitigations like key salting/splitting, replicating/caching the hot key, or bounded loads
choices.3: Yes, the clockwise rule balances hot keys automatically
choices.4: Hot keys cannot occur under consistent hashing

question: Roughly how do these latencies order from fastest to slowest?

choices.1: All of these are within the same order of magnitude
choices.2: SSD ~1 ns, RAM ~100 us, L1 ~10 ms
choices.3: L1 cache ~1 ns, main memory ~100 ns, SSD random read ~tens of microseconds, same-datacenter round trip ~0.5 ms, HDD seek ~5-10 ms, cross-continent round trip ~150 ms
choices.4: Main memory ~1 ns, L1 cache ~100 ns, HDD seek ~1 us, network ~1 ms

question: What is the key ratio that explains why caching exists?

choices.1: Memory is about 2x faster than a network call
choices.2: A network call is faster than an L1 cache hit
choices.3: Disk is faster than memory for random access
choices.4: Memory is roughly 100,000x faster than a cross-country network call

question: How do successive CPU cache levels relate in size and speed?

choices.1: Each level is 10x smaller and 10x faster than the one above
choices.2: All cache levels have the same size and latency
choices.3: Each level is roughly 10x larger and 10x slower than the one above it (a full RAM access is ~100x an L1 hit)
choices.4: L3 is faster than L1 because it is larger

question: What is the handy 'seconds per day' constant for QPS estimation?

choices.1: About 3,600, so daily requests / 3,600 ~ average QPS
choices.2: About 86,400 ~ 10^5, so daily requests / 10^5 ~ average QPS
choices.3: About 1,000,000, so daily requests / 10^6 ~ average QPS
choices.4: About 1,440, the number of minutes per day

question: How should you relate peak QPS to average QPS when provisioning?

choices.1: Peak QPS is always lower than average QPS
choices.2: Peak QPS equals average QPS, so they are interchangeable
choices.3: Peak QPS is roughly 2-3x average QPS, so provision for the peak rather than the mean
choices.4: Peak QPS is about 100x average QPS

question: How much faster is a cache hit than the database query it replaces?

choices.1: Roughly 100-1000x faster — the whole reason to cache
choices.2: Slower, but more consistent
choices.3: About 2x faster
choices.4: Roughly the same speed, just cheaper

question: How do REST, GraphQL, and gRPC model operations?

choices.1: REST uses binary RPC; gRPC uses JSON resources; GraphQL uses HTTP verbs
choices.2: All three model resources with HTTP verbs
choices.3: REST models resources acted on with HTTP verbs; GraphQL is a query language over a typed graph where clients fetch exactly the fields they need; gRPC models functions/methods (RPC) called over a fast binary protocol
choices.4: They are three names for the same protocol

question: What are REST's two classic fetching weaknesses?

choices.1: It can only return binary data and cannot stream
choices.2: It cannot be cached by CDNs
choices.3: It requires HTTP/2 and a proxy for browsers
choices.4: Over-fetching (you get the whole resource even if you need one field) and under-fetching / N+1 (one screen needs several round trips)

question: What is GraphQL's main strength, and its main weakness?

choices.1: Strength: the client asks for exactly the fields it needs in one round trip (killing over/under-fetching); weakness: caching is hard (a single dynamic POST endpoint), and naive resolvers cause N+1 queries unless batched
choices.2: Strength: trivial HTTP caching; weakness: it can't express nested queries
choices.3: Strength: binary compactness; weakness: no typed schema
choices.4: Strength: browser-only support; weakness: no field selection

question: What characterizes gRPC?

choices.1: Fast, compact binary Protobuf serialization over HTTP/2 with multiplexing and bidirectional streaming, plus a strict typed contract with codegen — but it isn't human-readable and has limited browser support (needs grpc-web/a proxy)
choices.2: A query language letting clients pick exact fields
choices.3: Human-readable JSON over HTTP/1.1 with easy curl debugging
choices.4: A resource-oriented model using HTTP status codes

question: How do the three paradigms compare on caching?

choices.1: All three are equally easy to cache via HTTP
choices.2: GraphQL caches for free; REST cannot be cached
choices.3: gRPC is the easiest to cache; REST the hardest
choices.4: REST is easy to cache (HTTP caching/CDNs); GraphQL and gRPC are both hard to cache

question: What is the rule of thumb for choosing a paradigm?

choices.1: Public/cacheable/broad-reach -> REST; client-driven field selection or aggregating many backends or varied frontends -> GraphQL; internal service-to-service with low latency and streaming -> gRPC
choices.2: Always use REST for internal low-latency service calls
choices.3: Always use gRPC for public web APIs
choices.4: GraphQL for everything; the others are obsolete

question: How does the cache-aside (lazy loading) strategy work?

choices.1: The app writes only to the cache, which flushes to the DB later
choices.2: The app talks to both cache and DB: on a read it checks the cache, and on a miss reads the DB, writes the value into the cache, then returns it; on a write it updates the DB and invalidates the cache entry
choices.3: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously
choices.4: The cache loads from the DB itself on every miss

question: What distinguishes cache-aside from read-through caching?

choices.1: Cache-aside writes synchronously to the DB; read-through writes asynchronously
choices.2: In cache-aside the application repopulates the cache on a miss; in read-through the cache itself loads from the DB via a provider/loader, so the app only ever talks to the cache
choices.3: Read-through caches all data eagerly; cache-aside never caches
choices.4: They are identical strategies

question: What are the trade-offs of write-through caching?

choices.1: It risks losing writes if the cache crashes
choices.2: It gives the lowest possible write latency
choices.3: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously, giving strong consistency and no stale data for cached keys, but at the cost of higher write latency and caching data that may never be read
choices.4: It leaves the cache and DB eventually consistent

question: What are the benefits and the core risk of write-behind (write-back) caching?

choices.1: The app writes only to the cache and returns immediately, with the cache flushing to the DB asynchronously (batched/coalesced) — giving very low write latency and high throughput, but risking data loss if the cache crashes before flushing
choices.2: It is the simplest strategy with synchronous DB writes
choices.3: It always reads stale data from the DB
choices.4: It gives strong consistency with no risk of data loss

question: What is the cache miss penalty shared by cache-aside and read-through?

choices.1: The cache must be fully warmed before any read succeeds
choices.2: Every read, hit or miss, goes to the database
choices.3: Writes are slow because they hit cache and DB synchronously
choices.4: The first read of any key is slow because a miss requires fetching from the DB and populating the cache (three trips for cache-aside)

question: Which is a sensible common strategy combination?

choices.1: Cache-aside with no invalidation for strong consistency
choices.2: Write-behind + write-through together for guaranteed durability
choices.3: Read-through alone guarantees the cache never goes stale
choices.4: Cache-aside + TTL is the simplest, most common general-purpose setup; read-through + write-through gives a fully cache-fronted, strongly consistent system at the cost of slower writes

question: What is the difference between eviction and expiration?

choices.1: Eviction is triggered by capacity (the cache is full, so make room); expiration (TTL) is triggered by time (the entry is too old) regardless of how full the cache is — they are orthogonal and usually combined
choices.2: Eviction is time-based; expiration is capacity-based
choices.3: They are the same mechanism with different names
choices.4: Eviction applies only to reads; expiration only to writes

question: How does the FIFO eviction policy behave, and what is its main drawback?

choices.1: It never evicts; it only expires entries by TTL
choices.2: It evicts the earliest-inserted entry regardless of usage (a simple queue); it's cheap but ignores access patterns, so a frequently used hot entry gets evicted just because it's old, and it can suffer Belady's anomaly
choices.3: It evicts the least recently accessed entry, exploiting locality
choices.4: It evicts the most frequently used entry

question: What is the main vulnerability of the LRU eviction policy?

choices.1: It is vulnerable to scans/flooding — a one-time sweep over many cold items (e.g. a full table scan) evicts the genuinely hot working set, tanking the hit rate afterward
choices.2: It always keeps the oldest items forever
choices.3: It cannot be implemented in O(1) time
choices.4: It ignores recency entirely

question: What are the drawbacks of the LFU eviction policy?

choices.1: It requires no per-entry state at all
choices.2: It evicts the most recently used item, which hurts locality
choices.3: It is identical to FIFO in behavior
choices.4: It suffers stale popularity (an item popular long ago keeps a high count and lingers even after demand disappears) and punishes new items (which start with a low count and get evicted quickly) — so it needs aging/decay to adapt

question: What does TTL (time-to-live) provide, and what does it NOT do?

choices.1: It bounds staleness (data is never older than the TTL) and is self-cleaning, but it is not size-aware — the cache can still fill up before anything expires, so you still need a capacity-driven eviction policy
choices.2: It guarantees the cache never serves stale data and never fills up
choices.3: It evicts the least recently used entry when full
choices.4: It caps memory usage, removing the need for eviction

question: Which production-grade or specialized eviction policies are worth knowing?

choices.1: Only FIFO and LRU exist in real systems
choices.2: MRU (evicts the most recently used, good for sequential scans), ARC (self-tunes between recency and frequency), and W-TinyLFU (used by Caffeine, pairs a frequency sketch with an LRU window)
choices.3: MRU is the default for locality-heavy workloads
choices.4: Random replacement is never used in practice

question: What is a cache stampede (thundering herd), and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: When one key overloads a single node; fixed by replication
choices.2: When the cache returns stale data after a write; fixed by versioning
choices.3: When requests hit keys absent from cache and DB; fixed by Bloom filters
choices.4: When a hot key expires or is missing, many concurrent requests miss at once and all rush to recompute the same expensive value, flooding the DB; mitigations include request coalescing/single-flight, early/probabilistic refresh, TTL jitter, and stale-while-revalidate

question: Where does cache inconsistency (stale reads) come from?

choices.1: The cache and DB are separate copies that diverge — e.g. the DB is updated without invalidating the cache, a slow repopulating read races with a concurrent write, or a write-behind cache lags the DB; mitigations include TTLs, invalidate-on-write, write-through, and versioned keys
choices.2: The cache always perfectly mirrors the DB; staleness is impossible
choices.3: It is caused solely by network packet loss
choices.4: Stale reads only happen when the cache is empty

question: What is the hot-key problem and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: A key that doesn't exist anywhere; fixed by negative caching
choices.2: A key that expires too quickly; fixed by raising its TTL only
choices.3: A single key gets a hugely disproportionate share of traffic, concentrating load on one cache node/shard and defeating horizontal scaling; mitigations include local/client-side caching, key replication/fan-out, random suffixes, and hotspot detection
choices.4: A key that is too large; fixed by compression

question: What is cache penetration and how is it addressed?

choices.1: One key overloading a node; fixed by replication
choices.2: The cache lagging the DB; fixed by write-through
choices.3: Requests for keys that exist in neither the cache nor the DB (often malicious or buggy) always miss and hit the DB pointlessly; fixes include negative caching (caching the 'not found' result with a short TTL) or a Bloom filter to reject keys that definitely don't exist
choices.4: Many keys expiring at once; fixed by TTL jitter

question: What is a cache avalanche?

choices.1: A request for a nonexistent key
choices.2: A large fraction of the cache expires or fails at the same time (or the whole cache tier goes down), dumping massive load on the DB; fixes include TTL jitter, layered caches, circuit breakers, and a highly-available cache tier
choices.3: A single hot key expiring under load
choices.4: The cache serving slightly stale data

question: What is the cold-start cache problem?

choices.1: An empty cache (after a deploy, restart, or flush) has a 0% hit rate, so all traffic hits the DB until it warms up; the fix is to pre-warm critical keys on startup
choices.2: A cache that is too cold to fit in RAM
choices.3: A key that is accessed only once and never again
choices.4: A cache that never expires any entries

question: What is the typical ordering of caching layers from client to origin?

choices.1: Browser/client cache -> CDN/edge -> reverse-proxy/gateway -> app in-process -> distributed cache (Redis) -> database buffer pool -> disk, with each layer absorbing traffic so the next sees less
choices.2: There is only one cache layer between the app and the DB
choices.3: Disk -> database -> Redis -> app -> CDN -> browser, in that request order
choices.4: All caches sit at the same layer with equal latency

question: What is the key benefit and limitation of a client-side/browser cache?

choices.1: It is shared across all users and easy to invalidate centrally
choices.2: It gives the fastest possible hit (zero network, governed by HTTP headers like Cache-Control/ETag), but you can't invalidate it directly once shipped — you wait for the TTL or use versioned URLs (app.v3.js)
choices.3: It requires a network round trip for every hit
choices.4: It survives across all servers in the fleet

question: What is the main risk of an application in-process (local) cache?

choices.1: It is shared across instances and never drifts
choices.2: It requires a network hop for every access
choices.3: It is always slower than a distributed cache
choices.4: It is per-instance, so N app servers hold N copies that can drift out of sync, and it doesn't survive process restarts — though its ns-µs access with no network makes it a good hot-key shield

question: What are the strengths and the risk of a distributed cache (Redis/Memcached)?

choices.1: It lives inside each app process with no network cost
choices.2: It is a shared sub-ms source of truth for the whole fleet — consistent across instances, surviving app restarts, scaling independently, and sharded via consistent hashing — but it adds a network hop and the cache tier itself needs HA
choices.3: It is per-instance and cannot be shared
choices.4: It cannot be sharded across nodes

question: What are database/storage caches, and their limitation?

choices.1: Caches inside the data tier — the buffer pool/page cache (hot pages in RAM skip disk), query/result caches, and materialized views; they're 'free' but per-database and don't offload the DB the way an external cache does
choices.2: Browser caches governed by HTTP headers
choices.3: CDN edge caches near the user
choices.4: External Redis clusters shared by all services

question: What is the two-tier (L1 in-process + L2 distributed) caching pattern, and its cost?

choices.1: It uses two distributed caches to double throughput, with no invalidation cost
choices.2: L1 (per-instance, ns) absorbs hot keys with no network; on a miss L2 (shared Redis, sub-ms) absorbs the rest fleet-wide; on an L2 miss the DB is hit — mirroring the CPU's L1/L2/L3 hierarchy — at the cost of harder invalidation (two caches to keep coherent)
choices.3: It replaces the database entirely with two cache tiers
choices.4: It is slower than a single cache because of the extra layer

question: What does two-phase locking (2PL) guarantee, and what should it not be confused with?

choices.1: It guarantees durability by flushing the write-ahead log
choices.2: It guarantees atomic commit across nodes, the same as 2PC
choices.3: It guarantees serializability (the isolation I in ACID) within a database; it should not be confused with two-phase commit (2PC), which is an atomic-commit protocol across multiple nodes
choices.4: It guarantees availability during network partitions

question: What is the defining rule of two-phase locking?

choices.1: A transaction cannot acquire any lock after it has released one — it has a growing phase (acquire only) and a shrinking phase (release only), separated by the 'lock point'
choices.2: A transaction may acquire locks only after releasing all previous ones
choices.3: A transaction acquires and releases locks in any order it likes
choices.4: A transaction must release each lock immediately after using it

question: What is the difference between shared (S) and exclusive (X) locks?

choices.1: A shared lock is for writing; an exclusive lock is for reading
choices.2: Both can be held by multiple transactions simultaneously
choices.3: An exclusive lock allows concurrent shared locks
choices.4: A shared lock is for reading and multiple transactions can hold it on the same item; an exclusive lock is for writing, only one transaction can hold it, and it excludes shared locks too

question: How does Strict 2PL improve on Basic 2PL?

choices.1: It releases all locks as early as possible to maximize concurrency
choices.2: It acquires all locks before the transaction starts
choices.3: It holds all exclusive (write) locks until the transaction commits or aborts, preventing other transactions from reading uncommitted writes and thus eliminating cascading aborts — the most common variant
choices.4: It removes the two-phase rule entirely

question: What is the property and cost of Conservative (Static) 2PL?

choices.1: It is the same as Strict 2PL with shared locks released early
choices.2: It guarantees no isolation but high availability
choices.3: A transaction acquires all its locks up front before it begins, which makes it deadlock-free, but it requires knowing the full lock set in advance and reduces concurrency
choices.4: It releases locks during the growing phase, improving throughput

question: How does 2PL compare with MVCC?

choices.1: They are identical approaches to concurrency control
choices.2: MVCC blocks readers against writers while 2PL never blocks
choices.3: 2PL keeps multiple versions while MVCC uses locks
choices.4: 2PL is pessimistic (blocks on conflict, so readers and writers block each other); MVCC is optimistic and keeps multiple versions so readers never block (they see a snapshot) — many databases combine MVCC for reads with 2PL-style locking for writes

question: What is the Saga pattern?

choices.1: A locking protocol that holds locks across all services until commit
choices.2: A single atomic transaction spanning all the services at once
choices.3: A caching strategy for distributed reads
choices.4: A way to maintain data consistency across multiple services/shards without a distributed transaction: a sequence of local transactions, each committing independently, where a failure triggers compensating transactions that semantically undo prior work — trading atomicity for availability and eventual consistency

question: What is a compensating transaction in a saga?

choices.1: A new transaction that semantically undoes a previously committed step (not a database rollback, since the original commit is already durable and visible); it must be idempotent and safe to retry
choices.2: A lock held until the entire saga completes
choices.3: A database rollback that erases the original committed step
choices.4: A read that re-fetches the latest value

question: What is the difference between choreography and orchestration in sagas?

choices.1: Both require a two-phase commit coordinator
choices.2: They differ only in the programming language used
choices.3: Choreography has each service publish events that others subscribe and react to, with no central controller (loose coupling but hard to trace); orchestration has a central coordinator explicitly tell each service what to do and invoke compensations (explicit and monitorable, but another component to operate)
choices.4: Choreography uses a central coordinator; orchestration uses events

question: What is the pivot transaction in a saga?

choices.1: The point of no return: once it commits the saga will complete — everything before it is compensatable, and everything after it is retryable (must eventually succeed)
choices.2: The compensating transaction for the final step
choices.3: A lock acquired across all participating services
choices.4: The first step of the saga, which can always be undone

question: Which ACID property do sagas NOT provide, and what problem does that cause?

choices.1: They provide full ACID including isolation
choices.2: They lack durability, so committed steps can vanish
choices.3: Sagas provide atomicity (via compensation), consistency, and durability but NOT isolation — because each step commits immediately, intermediate states are visible, causing anomalies like dirty reads and lost updates (mitigated by semantic locks marking records as PENDING)
choices.4: They lack consistency, so data is always corrupt

question: When should you use a saga versus two-phase commit?

choices.1: They are interchangeable in all situations
choices.2: Use a saga for long-running, microservice, or cross-shard operations where you can tolerate brief inconsistency reconciled by compensation; reach for 2PC only within a single database/trust boundary needing strict isolation
choices.3: Sagas are for short transactions; 2PC for long-running ones
choices.4: Always use 2PC for microservices and sagas for single databases

question: What does a circuit breaker do?

choices.1: It caches responses from a slow dependency
choices.2: It load-balances requests across healthy replicas
choices.3: It wraps calls to an external dependency and stops sending traffic to it when it's unhealthy, instead of endlessly retrying or waiting on a broken dependency
choices.4: It encrypts traffic between two services

question: What are the three states of a circuit breaker?

choices.1: On, Off, and Standby
choices.2: Closed (normal operation, requests allowed through), Open (the dependency is unhealthy, requests are blocked or fail fast), and Half-open (a few test requests check whether it has recovered)
choices.3: Read, Write, and Idle
choices.4: Healthy, Degraded, and Failed, with no recovery testing

question: Why is 'failing fast' a key benefit of circuit breakers?

choices.1: It makes the failing service respond faster
choices.2: It retries the request more aggressively to force success
choices.3: Instead of waiting seconds for each request to time out, the breaker immediately returns an error or fallback, preserving resources like request threads, DB connections, memory, CPU, and sockets — since many outages come from resource exhaustion while waiting on a failed service
choices.4: It hides the error from monitoring so alerts don't fire

question: How does a circuit breaker help an already-overloaded service recover?

choices.1: By sending it more requests to keep it warm
choices.2: By restarting the service automatically
choices.3: By increasing the timeout so requests wait longer
choices.4: By opening the circuit it reduces the request load, giving the struggling service breathing room to recover instead of hammering it until it fully collapses

question: How does a circuit breaker isolate failure and degrade gracefully?

choices.1: It shuts down the entire application to contain the failure
choices.2: It creates a boundary around the failure, so e.g. a down recommendation service doesn't hang the whole product page — the page still loads without recommendations (degraded, not catastrophic)
choices.3: It retries until the recommendation service comes back
choices.4: It forces every dependent service to fail together

question: What fallback behaviors can a system use while a circuit is open?

choices.1: Permanently disable the affected feature
choices.2: Block all requests until the dependency recovers, with no response
choices.3: Crash the caller to free its resources
choices.4: Return cached data, show partial results, use default values, skip non-critical features, queue work for later, or return a clear error quickly

question: What does it mean for an operation to be idempotent?

choices.1: It always returns a different result each time
choices.2: Doing it once has the same final effect as doing it multiple times, so repeated attempts don't create extra side effects
choices.3: It must be executed inside a transaction
choices.4: It can only ever be executed once

question: Why does idempotency matter during failures?

choices.1: It makes operations run faster
choices.2: Failures are ambiguous (timeouts, dropped connections, lost responses, redeliveries), so the client often can't tell if the operation succeeded; idempotency makes retrying safe
choices.3: It guarantees the operation never fails
choices.4: It encrypts the request so it can't be tampered with

question: Why is retrying a non-idempotent POST /payments dangerous?

choices.1: If the first request succeeded but the response was lost, retrying may charge the customer twice
choices.2: It will always be rejected by the server
choices.3: It corrupts the database schema
choices.4: It is impossible to retry a POST request

question: How does an idempotency key make a payment retry safe?

choices.1: The key forces the server to process the payment again
choices.2: The key tells the server to skip the payment entirely
choices.3: The client sends a unique key (e.g. Idempotency-Key: abc-123); if it retries with the same key, the server recognizes the already-processed operation and returns the original result instead of creating a second payment
choices.4: The key encrypts the payment amount so it can't change

question: Why is idempotency especially important with message queues and microservices?

choices.1: Microservices never retry messages
choices.2: It makes messages deliver faster
choices.3: Queues guarantee exactly-once delivery, so idempotency is unnecessary
choices.4: Many systems provide at-least-once delivery, so a message may be delivered more than once (e.g. a consumer crashes before acknowledging); an idempotent handler detects the already-processed event and avoids duplicating the action

question: What does a timeout do, and why is it needed?

choices.1: It guarantees the operation eventually succeeds
choices.2: It encrypts the request to prevent tampering
choices.3: It controls how long to wait before retrying after a failure
choices.4: It sets a maximum time you're willing to wait for an operation; without it a program could wait forever on a slow call, tying up threads, memory, and sockets — a timeout turns 'maybe it'll respond eventually' into 'it didn't respond in time'

question: What does a backoff control, and what is exponential backoff?

choices.1: A backoff is the act of giving up after the first failure
choices.2: A backoff caches the failed response
choices.3: A backoff controls how long you wait before retrying after a failure; exponential backoff waits increasing delays (e.g. 1s, 2s, 4s) instead of retrying instantly
choices.4: A backoff sets the maximum time to wait for a single operation

question: Why does exponential backoff help a struggling system?

choices.1: It makes each retry faster than the last
choices.2: It removes the need for timeouts
choices.3: It guarantees the request succeeds on the second attempt
choices.4: It avoids retry storms where many clients hammer the service, gives the failing system time to recover, reduces wasted work, and improves success rates for transient failures

question: How do timeouts and backoffs solve different parts of the same problem?

choices.1: They are two names for the same mechanism
choices.2: A timeout stops you from waiting too long; a backoff stops you from retrying too quickly — together (with a max retry count) they form a safe retry pattern
choices.3: Both control how long to wait before retrying
choices.4: Both set the maximum time to wait for one operation

question: Why should jitter be added to backoff delays?

choices.1: In large systems many clients may fail at the same time; if they all retry after exactly 1s, 2s, 4s they overload the service again in synchronized waves — jitter adds randomness to spread retries out and reduce traffic spikes
choices.2: Jitter makes the retries happen faster on average
choices.3: Jitter encrypts the retry timing for security
choices.4: Jitter guarantees all clients retry at the same instant

question: What is client-side load balancing?

choices.1: A load balancer that runs on the client's operating system kernel
choices.2: Balancing load between a client's CPU cores
choices.3: A technique where the client chooses which server instance to call directly, instead of sending every request through a central load balancer
choices.4: A technique where the server decides which client to serve next

question: How does a client discover available server instances in client-side load balancing?

choices.1: It asks the central load balancer for the list
choices.2: It hardcodes a single fixed server address
choices.3: It uses a service registry (e.g. Consul, Eureka, etcd, Kubernetes service discovery) to get the list of healthy instances, then picks one with a strategy like round robin
choices.4: It broadcasts to every IP on the network

question: What is a key benefit of client-side load balancing?

choices.1: Simpler clients that need no extra logic
choices.2: Centralized control that's easy to update everywhere at once
choices.3: Lower latency (the client calls the backend directly, avoiding an extra hop through a load balancer) and no single central load-balancer bottleneck, plus better service-discovery integration and more routing control
choices.4: Guaranteed perfectly even traffic distribution

question: What is a key drawback of client-side load balancing?

choices.1: It requires a dedicated hardware appliance
choices.2: Clients become more complex — each must do service discovery, health tracking, server selection, retries, and timeouts — and updating the load-balancing behavior may require updating many clients, risking inconsistent behavior
choices.3: It always adds an extra network hop
choices.4: It cannot integrate with service discovery

question: How does client-side load balancing differ from server-side?

choices.1: In client-side the client makes the routing decision and knows the backend instances (usually no extra hop, but higher client complexity); in server-side the load balancer decides and the client only knows the LB (usually an extra hop, lower client complexity)
choices.2: In client-side the load balancer decides; in server-side the client decides
choices.3: Both always add an extra network hop
choices.4: Server-side requires the client to know all backends

question: How can a sidecar proxy relate to client-side load balancing?

choices.1: A sidecar proxy is a hardware appliance in the data center
choices.2: A sidecar proxy forces all traffic through a central load balancer
choices.3: A sidecar proxy replaces the need for any backend servers
choices.4: A sidecar proxy (e.g. Envoy) handles the load-balancing logic on behalf of the application, giving many client-side benefits without putting all the logic directly into application code

question: What information does a Layer 4 load balancer use to route traffic?

choices.1: Transport-layer information — source/destination IP, source/destination port, and protocol (TCP/UDP) — without inspecting application content like HTTP paths, headers, or cookies
choices.2: The request body and API version
choices.3: The user's session and authentication token
choices.4: HTTP URL paths, cookies, and headers

question: What can a Layer 7 load balancer do that Layer 4 cannot?

choices.1: Operate at the application layer to route by URL path, hostname, cookies, or headers (e.g. send /api to API servers), and support TLS termination, web-application-firewall integration, and rate limiting
choices.2: Route purely by IP and port with minimal overhead
choices.3: Handle raw TCP and UDP with the least processing
choices.4: Balance load without understanding any protocol

question: When is a Layer 4 load balancer the better choice?

choices.1: When you need a web application firewall and TLS termination
choices.2: When routing HTTP requests to microservices by header
choices.3: When you need URL-path-based and cookie-based routing
choices.4: For very high throughput and low latency with TCP/UDP or non-HTTP protocols (databases, DNS, VoIP), where minimal application awareness and simple connection distribution are needed

question: What is an example of Layer 7 routing?

choices.1: Forwarding a TCP connection on port 443 to a server with no path awareness
choices.2: Distributing UDP DNS packets across resolvers
choices.3: Sending /api requests to API servers, /static requests to static-asset servers, and admin.example.com to admin servers, or keeping a user on the same backend via a cookie
choices.4: Balancing PostgreSQL connections across replicas by IP

question: How do hardware and software load balancers compare?

choices.1: They are identical except for their names
choices.2: Hardware load balancers are cheaper and more flexible than software ones
choices.3: A hardware load balancer is a dedicated physical appliance (e.g. F5, Citrix) with high, predictable performance but is expensive, less flexible, and prone to vendor lock-in; a software load balancer runs on general-purpose servers/VMs/containers (e.g. NGINX, HAProxy, Envoy), is flexible, scalable, and cloud-native but consumes server resources
choices.4: Software load balancers can only operate at Layer 4

question: What is the purpose of load-balancer health checks?

choices.1: They balance load across the load balancer's own CPU cores
choices.2: They cache responses from healthy backends
choices.3: The load balancer periodically checks backends (Layer 4: can the server accept a TCP connection? Layer 7: does /health return HTTP 200?) and stops sending traffic to any that fail
choices.4: They encrypt traffic to each backend server

question: How does the round robin load-balancing algorithm work, and what does it ignore?

choices.1: It picks the fastest-responding server each time
choices.2: It sends each request to the server with the fewest active connections
choices.3: It routes by hashing the client IP
choices.4: It sends requests to servers one after another in a fixed rotating order; it assumes all servers are equal and ignores current load, active connections, CPU, and response time

question: What does weighted round robin add over plain round robin?

choices.1: It always sends traffic to the fastest server
choices.2: It routes based on the requested URL
choices.3: Stronger servers receive proportionally more traffic according to a configured weight, useful when servers have different capacities
choices.4: It picks two random servers and routes to the less loaded one

question: When is the least-connections algorithm a better fit than round robin?

choices.1: When routing cached objects to the same server
choices.2: For long-lived connections or requests with uneven durations (WebSockets, database connections), because it sends new traffic to the server with the fewest active connections rather than assuming equal load
choices.3: When you need sticky sessions without cookies
choices.4: For short identical requests where simplicity matters most

question: What does the least-response-time algorithm optimize for?

choices.1: It keeps each client on the same server
choices.2: It rotates through servers equally
choices.3: It routes traffic to the backend that is currently responding the fastest (lowest latency), making it good for latency-sensitive, user-facing applications
choices.4: It routes to the server using the least network bandwidth

question: How does the 'power of two choices' algorithm work, and why is it useful?

choices.1: It randomly selects two servers, compares their load, and sends the request to the less loaded one — avoiding heavily loaded servers without checking every backend, making it very scalable for large systems
choices.2: It picks the two fastest servers and alternates between them
choices.3: It hashes the request across exactly two shards
choices.4: It always checks all servers and picks the best one

question: What is the IP-hash algorithm good for, and what is its weakness?

choices.1: It hashes the client IP to a server so the same client consistently reaches the same backend (basic session persistence without cookies); but it becomes uneven when many users share an IP (NAT/VPN/proxy) and remaps many clients when servers are added or removed
choices.2: It distributes load perfectly evenly regardless of client IPs
choices.3: It routes based on the requested URL for caching
choices.4: It picks a random server each request, ignoring the client

question: What is kernel bypass?

choices.1: Moving network I/O out of the OS kernel into user space, so packets travel between the NIC and the application without the kernel's TCP/IP stack, system calls, copies, and interrupts on the path
choices.2: Disabling the network card to reduce interrupts
choices.3: Caching network packets in the CPU's L1 cache
choices.4: Running the application inside the kernel for speed

question: Why is the normal kernel network path slow and jittery for latency-critical work?

choices.1: Because the NIC cannot do DMA in the normal path
choices.2: Every packet crosses the user/kernel boundary (a syscall), is delivered via interrupts and softirqs, is copied from kernel to user buffers, and is processed by a generic stack — adding latency and, worse, variance that wrecks the tail (p99.9)
choices.3: Because the kernel compresses every packet
choices.4: Because user space is always slower than kernel space

question: How does DPDK achieve kernel bypass?

choices.1: It runs the standard kernel TCP stack but at higher priority
choices.2: It intercepts the BSD sockets API and keeps the kernel stack
choices.3: A Poll-Mode Driver busy-polls the NIC ring buffers directly from user space (no interrupts, burning a CPU core), with zero-copy packets DMA'd into hugepage-backed user buffers, and the application provides its own UDP/TCP stack
choices.4: It writes packets directly into remote machine memory

question: What is distinctive about Solarflare/Onload compared to DPDK?

choices.1: It writes directly into the remote peer's memory
choices.2: It requires rewriting the application around a custom stack
choices.3: It runs entirely inside the kernel with no user-space component
choices.4: Onload intercepts the standard BSD sockets API and runs the stack in user space, so the application keeps recv()/send() semantics while bypassing the kernel — a lower integration cost than DPDK (no rewrite)

question: How does RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) work?

choices.1: The NIC writes directly into remote application memory, bypassing both CPUs' kernels (and often the remote CPU entirely) for ultra-low latency and near-zero-copy, via the verbs API over transports like InfiniBand, RoCE, or iWARP
choices.2: It batches syscalls using shared submission/completion rings
choices.3: It runs an eBPF program at the earliest point in the driver
choices.4: It polls the local NIC from user space with its own TCP stack

question: What do you give up by using kernel bypass?

choices.1: The ability to use DMA at all
choices.2: Nothing; it is strictly better in every way
choices.3: All networking — the machine can no longer reach the network
choices.4: A burned CPU core per polling thread (busy-poll is 100% CPU by design), kernel features (firewall, tcpdump, routing, the mature TCP stack), debuggability, plus hardware lock-in and operational complexity — so it's only worth it for genuinely latency-critical paths

question: What is the governing idea of low-latency design?

choices.1: Maximize average throughput at any cost to variance
choices.2: Prioritize correctness over predictability on the hot path
choices.3: Always scale horizontally by adding more threads to the hot path
choices.4: Mechanical sympathy plus low variance — latency isn't just being fast on average but having low variance, so you optimize the tail (p99.9), not the mean

question: How should the hot path and cold path be separated?

choices.1: Both paths should share the same threads and code for simplicity
choices.2: The hot path (the per-event critical path) should be allocation-free, branch-light, lock-free, and cache-hot; the cold path (startup, logging, recovery, stats) prioritizes correctness and is kept out of the hot instruction-cache footprint and off the critical path
choices.3: The cold path should be made as fast as the hot path
choices.4: Logging and stats should run inline on the hot path

question: What is the recommended threading model for low latency?

choices.1: One thread handling all stages sequentially with blocking I/O
choices.2: Thread-per-core: one busy thread per critical stage pinned to an isolated core (isolated from the scheduler/IRQs), avoiding locks via single-writer designs and lock-free handoff (e.g. an SPSC ring buffer) between stages
choices.3: A thread pool that migrates threads across cores freely
choices.4: Many threads sharing mutable state behind a global mutex

question: Why do latency-critical loops busy-poll instead of blocking?

choices.1: Blocking is impossible on modern hardware
choices.2: Spin-polling avoids the need for the NIC entirely
choices.3: Busy-polling uses less CPU than blocking
choices.4: Blocking on an interrupt or syscall adds a wakeup plus a context switch — microseconds of jitter — so spin-polling the NIC/queue trades a burned CPU core for predictable, low, jitter-free latency (throughput systems do the opposite to save CPU)

question: Why eliminate allocation and syscalls on the hot path?

choices.1: Allocation is free, so this is only about code style
choices.2: Object pools are slower than calling new/delete repeatedly
choices.3: Syscalls are faster than function calls on the hot path
choices.4: Allocation means a lock, maybe a syscall, cache-cold memory, and tail spikes, so you pre-allocate and reuse buffers via object pools/arenas; and each syscall is a user/kernel transition, which for networking motivates kernel bypass

question: Why warm everything up before the critical period (e.g. market open)?

choices.1: It permanently lowers the CPU's clock to reduce variance
choices.2: Cold caches, cold branch predictors, un-faulted pages, and first-call costs hit the very first event hardest — so warming up (touching memory, running the hot path with dummy data, pre-faulting pages, priming caches) makes event #1 as fast as event #1000
choices.3: It reduces the total number of events processed
choices.4: Warming up is only needed for managed runtimes with a JIT

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: What are tail latency and jitter?

choices.1: Tail latency is network bandwidth; jitter is packet loss
choices.2: Tail latency is the average response time; jitter is the median
choices.3: They both refer to the fastest 1% of requests
choices.4: Tail latency is how slow your slowest requests are (the p99, p99.9, p99.99 of the distribution, not the average); jitter is the variability itself — the spread between the fast common case and the slow outliers

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: Why does the mean latency 'lie'?

choices.1: Because the mean is always larger than the maximum
choices.2: Because the median always equals the mean for latency
choices.3: Latency distributions are right-skewed with a long tail: most requests are fast and a few are very slow, so the rare slow ones drag the mean while hiding in it — you should report a distribution (p50/p90/p99/p99.9/max)
choices.4: Because averages are impossible to compute over latency

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: How does fan-out amplify tail latency?

choices.1: Fan-out averages out the tails, making them disappear
choices.2: Fan-out reduces the tail because requests run in parallel
choices.3: When one request fans out to many services and waits for all of them, the slowest response sets the overall latency; e.g. 100 services each at p99=10ms means ~63% of top-level requests hit at least one slow service — so a service's p99 becomes its caller's median
choices.4: Fan-out only affects throughput, never latency

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: What is coordinated omission in latency measurement?

choices.1: When two threads coordinate to skip the same request
choices.2: When the load generator sends too many requests at once
choices.3: A trap where naive benchmarks under-measure the tail: when the system stalls they stop sending requests during the stall, so the requests that would have been slow are never recorded; the fix is to measure against the intended schedule (e.g. wrk2 or HdrHistogram's CO correction)
choices.4: When percentiles are averaged instead of merged

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: Which are common sources of jitter (latency variance)?

choices.1: Only the programming language's syntax
choices.2: Only the number of CPU cores available
choices.3: GC pauses, page faults/swapping/TLB misses/NUMA-remote access, scheduling preemption and context switches, CPU frequency scaling, interrupts and syscalls, lock contention/false sharing, network retransmits/head-of-line blocking, and cold starts
choices.4: Only network bandwidth limits

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: How should tail latency be measured properly?

choices.1: Use a low-resolution clock to reduce overhead
choices.2: Record a full histogram (e.g. HdrHistogram) rather than a running mean, merge histograms across shards rather than averaging percentiles, use the highest-resolution clock (hardware NIC timestamps for tick-to-trade), and watch p99.9/p99.99 and max
choices.3: Average the p99 values reported by each shard
choices.4: Track only the average and the median

question: What is the distinction between ordering events and measuring real time across machines?

choices.1: Both always require GPS-disciplined hardware clocks
choices.2: Ordering needs physical sync; measuring needs only logical clocks
choices.3: Ordering events across machines is solved with logical/vector clocks (no physical sync needed); measuring real latency, timestamping for audit/regulation, and correlating logs need physical clocks agreeing to a tight bound
choices.4: Neither requires any form of clock

question: What two sub-problems arise because machine clocks drift?

choices.1: Cardinality and sampling rate
choices.2: Latency and bandwidth
choices.3: Offset (how far a clock is from the reference, corrected by stepping/slewing) and drift/skew (the rate difference, corrected by continuously disciplining the frequency); measuring the offset requires estimating uncertain one-way network delay
choices.4: Jitter and packet loss

question: How do NTP and PTP differ in accuracy and mechanism?

choices.1: PTP works only over the internet while NTP works only on LANs
choices.2: NTP is more accurate than PTP because it uses hardware timestamps
choices.3: NTP uses software timestamps in the OS and achieves ~1-10 ms over the internet (sub-ms on a good LAN); PTP (IEEE 1588) uses hardware assistance on LANs to reach sub-microsecond to nanosecond accuracy with a GPS-disciplined grandmaster clock
choices.4: Both achieve identical nanosecond accuracy

question: Where does the big accuracy jump from NTP to PTP actually come from?

choices.1: From PTP using TCP instead of UDP
choices.2: From PTP using a faster cryptographic algorithm
choices.3: From PTP polling the time server more frequently
choices.4: From where the timestamp is taken: hardware timestamping has the NIC stamp the packet at the wire (PHY), removing host-side software jitter (interrupts, scheduling, kernel-stack time) and giving nanosecond-grade determinism — not from the protocol math itself

question: How is a high-precision local clock kept aligned to a reference, and why?

choices.1: A GPS/GNSS receiver provides UTC plus a PPS (pulse-per-second) edge as the grandmaster reference, and the local clock is continuously disciplined (frequency-adjusted) toward it rather than stepped — to avoid time going backwards, which would break ordering and measurement
choices.2: The reference is taken from the application's wall-clock reads
choices.3: The clock is stepped abruptly to the reference on every update
choices.4: The local clock is left free-running and never corrected

question: Why does high-frequency trading care about precise time synchronization?

choices.1: It does not; HFT ignores clock synchronization
