choices.2: For latency measurement (tick-to-trade needs clocks agreeing to nanoseconds), sequencing/audit (reconstructing who saw what when), regulation (MiFID II RTS 25 mandates clock sync to UTC within tight bounds like 100 us), and fairness/forensics
choices.3: Only to display the correct local time to traders
choices.4: Only to schedule batch jobs at night

question: What are the three pillars of observability?

choices.1: Dashboards, alerts, and runbooks
choices.2: CPU, memory, and disk
choices.3: Metrics (aggregate numbers over time), logs (discrete event records), and traces (the path of one request across services)
choices.4: Latency, throughput, and availability

question: How does observability differ from monitoring?

choices.1: Monitoring watches known dashboards/alerts for known problems ('is the thing I expected to break, broken?'); observability is about debugging the unknown-unknowns ('why is this request slow, given I never anticipated this failure?')
choices.2: Monitoring uses traces while observability uses only metrics
choices.3: They are identical; observability is just a newer word
choices.4: Observability watches known problems; monitoring finds unknown ones

question: Why is cardinality 'the enemy' for metrics?

choices.1: Because cardinality only affects logs, never metrics
choices.2: Because low cardinality makes dashboards slow
choices.3: Because metrics cannot store any labels at all
choices.4: Each unique label combination is a new time series, so high-cardinality labels (user_id, order_id) explode storage — those high-cardinality details belong in logs/traces, not metric labels

question: What is a trace, and what does it make debuggable?

choices.1: A trace follows one request end-to-end as a tree of timed spans (one per operation/service) linked by a propagated trace id; it localizes which hop is slow, making distributed latency (tail amplification) debuggable
choices.2: A trace is a single aggregated latency number per service
choices.3: A trace is a counter of total requests
choices.4: A trace is a log line with a timestamp

question: What do the RED and USE methods measure?

choices.1: Both measure only error rates
choices.2: RED is for databases and USE is for caches
choices.3: RED measures resources; USE measures requests
choices.4: RED (per service, request-driven) measures Rate, Errors, and Duration — the user-facing golden signals; USE (per resource) measures Utilization, Saturation, and Errors — to find a bottleneck resource like a CPU, disk, or connection pool

question: What is the relationship between SLI, SLO, SLA, and the error budget?

choices.1: The error budget equals the SLO itself
choices.2: SLIs are contracts with customers and SLAs are measured numbers
choices.3: SLA is the internal target and SLO is the external contract
choices.4: An SLI is a measured number (e.g. % of requests < 200ms), an SLO is the internal target for it, and an SLA is the external contract with consequences if breached; the error budget = 100% - SLO, and you alert on SLO burn / symptoms rather than every cause

question: Why doesn't a multi-GB file fit the normal request/response model?

choices.1: Because HTTP cannot transfer binary data
choices.2: Because the database row limit is exceeded
choices.3: It won't fit in memory on the app server, a single HTTP upload request times out or fails partway, streaming it through your servers wastes their bandwidth and CPU, and one network blip wastes the whole multi-GB transfer
choices.4: Because blob stores cannot hold files larger than 1 GB

question: What is the central insight for handling large blobs?

choices.1: Stream every byte through the app server so it can validate them
choices.2: Don't move blob bytes through your application servers — move them directly between the client and a blob store (S3/GCS/Azure), and let your servers handle only small metadata and permissions
choices.3: Store the entire blob inside the application database
choices.4: Compress the blob so it fits in a single request

question: How does a presigned (pre-authorized) URL work for blob access?

choices.1: It is a permanent public URL anyone can use forever
choices.2: The app server grants the client temporary, scoped permission to talk to the blob store directly (pinned to a key, content-type, max size, and a short expiry), so the bytes never touch your servers while the app still controls who can upload/download what — the URL is a capability that expires
choices.3: It routes the upload through the app server for validation
choices.4: It encrypts the blob so only the app can read it

question: What does multipart upload provide for large files?

choices.1: Splitting the blob into parts uploaded independently (in parallel, any order, each acknowledged with an ETag, then stitched on 'complete') gives higher throughput via parallelism, resumability (retry a failed part alone, not the whole 5 GB), and pausability across sessions
choices.2: It stores each part in a different database
choices.3: It uploads the whole file in one atomic request
choices.4: It compresses the file into a single part

question: How are large blob downloads accelerated and made resumable?

choices.1: By disabling resumption to keep transfers atomic
choices.2: HTTP Range requests let a client fetch specific byte ranges, enabling parallel/segmented downloads and resume-after-disconnect, and a CDN caches blobs at edge locations near users so most downloads come from nearby rather than one central region
choices.3: By converting the blob to text before sending
choices.4: By streaming the whole blob through the app server with no caching

question: How is the metadata/blob split handled, and what is the two-phase consistency fix?

choices.1: The metadata is stored inside the blob itself, so they can't diverge
choices.2: The two systems are kept strongly consistent with a distributed transaction
choices.3: The blob bytes live in the blob store (keyed by a blob key) while queryable metadata lives in your DB; since they are two separate systems, a status field ('pending' -> 'available') with a finalize step handles divergence — a sweeper cleans up pending rows whose upload never completed, and a reconciliation job GCs orphaned blobs
choices.4: The blob store is queried directly for all metadata, with no database

question: What is the fundamental move for handling long-running tasks?

choices.1: Running the work inside the request thread with a longer timeout
choices.2: Storing the task in memory and processing it on the next request
choices.3: Decoupling acceptance from execution: the server enqueues a job and immediately returns 202 Accepted with a job id, while background workers drain the queue asynchronously
choices.4: Making the user wait synchronously for the work to finish

question: How does a visibility timeout keep job processing reliable?

choices.1: It locks the entire queue until the worker finishes
choices.2: It guarantees exactly-once delivery with no redelivery
choices.3: When a worker pulls a job, the broker makes it invisible to others for the visibility timeout (so two workers don't grab it); on success the worker acks and the job is deleted, but if it fails or the timeout expires before the ack, the job becomes visible again and is redelivered
choices.4: It deletes the job immediately on pull so it can never be reprocessed

question: What is the purpose of a dead-letter queue (DLQ)?

choices.1: It permanently deletes failed jobs to save space
choices.2: It is the main queue workers pull from
choices.3: After N failed attempts, a poison job (e.g. bad input) is moved aside to a DLQ so it doesn't retry infinitely and block the pipeline, while being preserved for inspection, manual replay, and alerting
choices.4: It stores all successfully completed jobs for auditing

question: Why do retries demand idempotency?

choices.1: Because idempotency makes retries faster
choices.2: Because queues guarantee exactly-once delivery, so retries never duplicate
choices.3: A retried job may be one that actually succeeded but failed to ack, so it runs again; retries are only safe if the work is idempotent (running it twice equals running it once)
choices.4: Because retries always run on a different worker that can't see prior state

question: What is the crucial caveat about queue backpressure?

choices.1: Queues eliminate the need to ever scale workers
choices.2: Backpressure means the producers automatically slow down with no configuration
choices.3: A large enough queue can absorb any sustained overload indefinitely
choices.4: A queue absorbs temporary bursts, not a permanently higher load — if average arrival rate exceeds average drain rate, no queue saves you; you need more capacity (workers/shards) or less load (shedding/rate limiting)

question: How do you prevent slow bulk jobs from starving fast urgent ones?

choices.1: Increase the visibility timeout for bulk jobs
choices.2: Make all jobs the same priority to be fair
choices.3: Use separate queues per priority/class plus dedicated worker pools, so a flood of slow bulk jobs can't block fast urgent ones (job-level head-of-line blocking)
choices.4: Use a single queue and process strictly in arrival order

question: What is the dual-write problem?

choices.1: Two services writing to the same database row concurrently
choices.2: Writing the same row twice in one transaction
choices.3: Writing to a primary and a replica that disagree
choices.4: Updating your database and publishing an event to a broker are two separate systems with no shared transaction — writing the DB then publishing can lose the event on a crash, while publishing then writing can emit a phantom event for a change that rolled back; no ordering of two independent writes is safe

question: How does the transactional outbox pattern solve the dual-write problem?

choices.1: It stores events only in the broker, never the database
choices.2: It writes the event into an outbox table in the same database, in the same local transaction as the business change, so one atomic commit means both the change and the event row land or neither; a separate relay process then publishes the unsent rows to the broker
choices.3: It publishes the event first and rolls it back if the DB write fails
choices.4: It uses a distributed two-phase commit across the DB and broker

question: What delivery guarantee does the outbox relay provide, and what does it require of consumers?

choices.1: Ordered delivery with no possibility of duplicates
choices.2: Exactly-once delivery, so consumers need no deduplication
choices.3: At-most-once delivery, so some events are silently dropped
choices.4: At-least-once delivery (the relay can publish a row then crash before marking it sent, republishing it), so consumers must be idempotent and dedupe by event id

question: How does Change Data Capture (CDC) read changes compared to a polling relay?

choices.1: CDC is identical to polling but runs more frequently
choices.2: CDC queries the broker for missing events
choices.3: CDC writes events directly from the application with no database involvement
choices.4: Instead of the relay periodically SELECTing unsent outbox rows (which adds DB load and polling latency), CDC tails the database's own replication log (the WAL/binlog) and turns committed row-changes into a stream (e.g. Debezium to Kafka) — low latency, no app-side query load

question: What is the key insight behind log-based CDC?

choices.1: The broker's log is the source of truth, not the database
choices.2: CDC only works with NoSQL databases
choices.3: The database's write-ahead log IS the event stream — log-based CDC can capture changes to business tables directly (sometimes removing the need for an explicit outbox) and syncs the DB to a search index, cache, or warehouse without dual writes
choices.4: CDC requires rewriting all application queries

question: Why not just use a distributed transaction (2PC) across the database and broker?

choices.1: Because brokers always support 2PC but databases do not
choices.2: Because 2PC cannot guarantee atomicity
choices.3: A distributed transaction / two-phase commit across the DB and broker is slow, blocking, and usually unsupported by message brokers
choices.4: Because 2PC across a DB and broker is the recommended standard approach

question: What does a durable execution engine let you do?

choices.1: Run stateless request/response handlers faster
choices.2: Write a long-running, multi-step process as ordinary code (loops, conditionals, function calls) while the engine guarantees it survives crashes, restarts, and failures and resumes exactly where it left off (e.g. Temporal, Azure Durable Functions)
choices.3: Define a process as a declarative JSON state machine run by a managed service
choices.4: Store domain events that you manually fold into state

question: How does a durable execution engine resume a process after a crash?

choices.1: It re-runs the workflow function from the top on another worker, but instead of really re-executing each step it feeds the recorded results back from a durable event history (memoization), fast-forwarding through completed steps and continuing at the first step with no recorded result
choices.2: It checkpoints the entire process memory to disk and reloads it
choices.3: It keeps the process in memory and never needs to resume
choices.4: It restarts the whole process from scratch, redoing all work

question: Why must durable-execution workflow code be deterministic?

choices.1: Because non-deterministic code cannot be compiled
choices.2: Because the engine re-runs the workflow code to rebuild state from history, the code must make the same decisions given the same history — so you can't call now()/random()/uuid() or do I/O directly inside workflow code; non-determinism is pushed into activities (whose results are recorded) or engine-provided deterministic APIs
choices.3: Because deterministic code runs faster
choices.4: Because the engine only supports pure functions for performance

question: Which capability is a hallmark of durable execution engines?

choices.1: Eliminating the need for any datastore
choices.2: Guaranteed sub-microsecond latency on every step
choices.3: Running arbitrary non-deterministic code in the workflow body
choices.4: Durable timers — 'sleep for 30 days' is a single line that the engine persists and wakes even after many restarts/deploys, holding no thread while it waits — plus automatic retries, signals/queries, and Saga-style compensation

question: What is a notable drawback of durable execution engines?

choices.1: They require no infrastructure at all
choices.2: They cannot run processes longer than a few seconds
choices.3: Versioning is hard — changing workflow code while executions are mid-flight can break replay because the new code produces a different history — plus the determinism constraint trips up newcomers and self-hosting means running a stateful cluster
choices.4: They cannot retry failed steps

question: Where does a durable execution engine sit among the alternatives?

choices.1: It is a simple cron job with retries
choices.2: It is the 'workflow as code' point on the spectrum — more power and expressiveness than a declarative workflow DSL, but more structure and reliability guarantees than rolling your own with event sourcing
choices.3: It is the lowest-code, fully-managed option with the least flexibility
choices.4: It is a pattern, not a runtime, where you fold events yourself

question: What is the core idea of event sourcing?

choices.1: Caching the current state in memory for fast reads
choices.2: Storing only the latest value and overwriting it on each change
choices.3: Instead of storing the current state, you store the full sequence of immutable events that led to it; the events are the source of truth and current state is a left-fold (replay) of those events — append-only, never updating or deleting in place
choices.4: Storing events in a broker and discarding them after delivery

question: How do events differ from commands in event sourcing?

choices.1: Commands are stored append-only; events are mutable
choices.2: They are the same thing with different names
choices.3: Events are requests that may fail; commands are immutable facts
choices.4: An event records something that already happened and is immutable, named in past tense (OrderPlaced); a command is an intent/request that may still fail (PlaceOrder)

question: What are snapshots used for in event sourcing?

choices.1: Snapshots replace the event log as the new source of truth
choices.2: Snapshots store commands rather than state
choices.3: Snapshots are immutable events that can never be rebuilt
choices.4: Replaying thousands of events on every read is slow, so you periodically persist the folded state and only replay events since the last snapshot; the events remain the source of truth and snapshots are a disposable performance optimization

question: Why does event sourcing pair with CQRS and projections?

choices.1: Because querying a fold-over-events is awkward, you separate the write model (append events) from read models and build projections — subscribers consume the event stream and maintain read-optimized views (SQL tables, search indexes, caches); new projections can be built later by replaying history
choices.2: Because CQRS stores events while event sourcing stores current state
choices.3: Because projections replace the need for an event store
choices.4: Because CQRS makes reads strongly consistent with writes

question: What is a key benefit of event sourcing?

choices.1: A complete audit trail — every change is a stored fact, so 'how did we get here?' is answerable by construction — plus time travel (reconstruct any past state by replaying events) and the ability to build new read models by replaying history
choices.2: Strong read-after-write consistency everywhere
choices.3: Easy deletion of data to satisfy GDPR erasure
choices.4: Simpler querying than a traditional CRUD database

question: What is a significant drawback of event sourcing?

choices.1: It makes simple CRUD apps simpler
choices.2: Event schema evolution is painful because events are immutable and live forever, so changing their shape needs versioning/upcasting of old events; querying is also hard (needs projections, introducing eventual consistency between write and read sides)
choices.3: It loses historical information on every write
choices.4: It cannot support audit trails

question: What is a managed workflow system?

choices.1: A pattern for storing domain events and folding them into state
choices.2: A library you embed to run workflows as ordinary code in your process
choices.3: A service where you define a multi-step process as a declarative workflow definition (a graph/state machine in JSON/YAML/DSL) and a fully managed service executes it, handling retries, state, and progress visibility (e.g. AWS Step Functions, Airflow, Argo)
choices.4: A message queue with workers

question: What is the defining split in a managed workflow system?

choices.1: The split between read models and write models
choices.2: The split between the database and the message broker
choices.3: The split between definition (what you author: the steps, order, branching, retry/timeout policy, error handling) and execution (the managed service owns durable state between steps, dispatching, retries, scheduling, and a visual run history) — the process is data, not code
choices.4: The split between the client and the server

question: What are the two common flavors of managed workflow systems?

choices.1: Push-based and pull-based message queues
choices.2: State-machine/orchestration systems (Step Functions, Cloud Workflows) for event-driven per-request business processes, and DAG schedulers (Airflow, Argo, Dagster) for scheduled data pipelines and batch jobs
choices.3: Synchronous and asynchronous request handlers
choices.4: Relational and NoSQL databases

question: What is a key benefit of a managed workflow system?

choices.1: The lowest possible cost at very high volume
choices.2: Low operational burden — it's managed, with no stateful cluster to run, scale, or patch — plus fast development via declarative definitions and prebuilt connectors, built-in reliability, and an excellent visual run history
choices.3: Maximum expressiveness for complex dynamic logic
choices.4: No vendor lock-in, since definitions are fully portable

question: What is the main drawback of managed workflow systems?

choices.1: They have no visibility into running executions
choices.2: They require you to run and scale a stateful cluster yourself
choices.3: Limited expressiveness — a JSON/DSL state machine is clumsy for complex logic, loops, and dynamic behavior compared to real code — plus vendor lock-in and per-transition cost at scale (this is exactly where durable-execution 'workflow as code' wins)
choices.4: They cannot retry failed steps or persist state

question: Where does a managed workflow system sit relative to durable execution engines and event sourcing?

choices.1: It is the 'low-code, fully-managed' end — least operational burden and least code, but least flexible and most lock-in; when the logic outgrows the declarative DSL, you move to a durable execution engine (workflow as code)
choices.2: It is identical to event sourcing
choices.3: It offers the most control and expressiveness of the three
choices.4: It is a pattern with no runtime, where you fold events yourself

question: How does the fixed window counter rate-limiting algorithm work, and what is its main flaw?

choices.1: It counts requests in a fixed interval (e.g. 100/minute), resetting the counter at each boundary; it's simple, fast, and low-memory but allows boundary bursts — a client can send 100 at 12:00:59 and another 100 at 12:01:00, i.e. 200 in ~2 seconds
choices.2: It leaks requests at a constant rate to smooth traffic
choices.3: It fills a bucket with tokens at a steady rate
choices.4: It keeps a timestamp of every request and is perfectly precise

question: What are the trade-offs of the sliding window log algorithm?

choices.1: It is the lowest-memory algorithm but allows boundary bursts
choices.2: It approximates the window by weighting the previous window's count
choices.3: It allows controlled bursts via accumulated tokens
choices.4: It keeps a timestamp of every request, drops those older than the window, and counts what remains — very accurate and free of boundary bursts, but high memory (one timestamp per request) and more expensive for high-traffic users

question: How does the sliding window counter approximate a true sliding window?

choices.1: It combines the current fixed-window count with a weighted portion of the previous window's count (weighted by the fraction of the window not yet elapsed), avoiding storing every timestamp — much less memory than the log and smoother than the fixed window, but only approximate
choices.2: It stores every request timestamp for perfect accuracy
choices.3: It resets the counter abruptly at each window boundary
choices.4: It leaks tokens at a constant rate

question: How does the token bucket algorithm work?

choices.1: It stores a timestamp per request and counts the recent ones
choices.2: A bucket fills with tokens at a steady rate up to a maximum capacity; each request consumes a token, so accumulated tokens allow controlled bursts while enforcing a smooth long-term average rate
choices.3: Requests enter a queue and are processed at a fixed leak rate
choices.4: It counts requests per fixed interval and resets at boundaries

question: What does the leaky bucket algorithm achieve?

choices.1: It resets a counter at fixed window boundaries
choices.2: It allows large bursts by accumulating tokens
choices.3: Requests enter a bucket that leaks (or is processed) at a constant rate, smoothing outgoing traffic to a steady pace and protecting downstream systems from bursts — but queueing introduces latency
choices.4: It stores every request timestamp for precision

question: What is the key difference between token bucket and leaky bucket?

choices.1: They are identical in behavior
choices.2: Token bucket smooths output; leaky bucket allows bursts
choices.3: Token bucket stores every timestamp; leaky bucket uses a counter
choices.4: Token bucket controls the average rate while allowing bursts (save up tokens, spend them quickly later); leaky bucket controls the output rate by smoothing requests so they leave at a steady pace — token bucket suits APIs tolerating bursts, leaky bucket suits downstreams needing steady flow

question: What core problem does a CDN solve?

choices.1: The internet is physically large and distance causes delay; without a CDN every user fetches from a distant origin, giving higher latency, heavy origin load during spikes, higher bandwidth cost, and more DDoS exposure
choices.2: Databases are too slow for read-heavy workloads
choices.3: Encryption is too slow over long distances
choices.4: Servers run out of memory under load

question: What is the basic idea of a CDN?

choices.1: A faster protocol that replaces HTTP
choices.2: A single powerful origin server that handles all global traffic
choices.3: A network of edge servers distributed around the world near users acts as a geographically distributed cache, so users are routed to a nearby edge server instead of always reaching the origin
choices.4: A database replicated to every user's device

question: What is the difference between a CDN cache hit and a cache miss?

choices.1: A miss permanently disables caching for that file
choices.2: On a hit the edge contacts the origin; on a miss it serves from cache
choices.3: On a hit the edge already has the content and serves it without contacting the origin (fast); on a miss the edge fetches the content from the origin, returns it to the user, and usually stores a copy so the next request for it is a hit
choices.4: Both hit and miss always contact the origin

question: How are users routed to a nearby CDN edge server?

choices.1: Via DNS, Anycast, or CDN-specific routing logic that picks an edge that is geographically and network-close, healthy, and able to serve the requested content
choices.2: By the origin redirecting every request after processing it
choices.3: By the user manually selecting the closest server
choices.4: By broadcasting the request to all edges and taking the first reply

question: What content are CDNs especially good at caching, and how do they help dynamic content?

choices.1: They excel at static content (images, video, CSS, JS, fonts, downloads); for dynamic content they may not fully cache the response but still help via faster routing, TLS termination, compression, HTTP/2 or HTTP/3, and edge compute
choices.2: They only cache database query results
choices.3: They cannot serve any dynamic content at all
choices.4: They cache everything including per-user dynamic pages perfectly

question: How does a CDN act as a shield for the origin?

choices.1: It encrypts the origin's disk to prevent data theft
choices.2: Traffic flows through the CDN before reaching the origin, so the CDN can block or absorb DDoS attacks, bad bots, suspicious traffic, excessive request rates, and some web exploits (via WAF features), reducing unwanted load on the origin
choices.3: It exposes the origin's IP directly to all clients for speed
choices.4: It replaces the origin entirely so it can never be attacked

question: How does regional partitioning differ from plain replication?

choices.1: Replication gives every region a copy of ALL the data (for read scaling/failover); regional partitioning has each region OWN a slice of the data (the users who belong to it) and run the full application near them
choices.2: Replication splits data by region while partitioning copies everything
choices.3: Regional partitioning caches static content; replication serves dynamic content
choices.4: They are the same; both copy all data everywhere

question: What is a 'home region' in regional partitioning?

choices.1: A backup region used only during failover
choices.2: The region where the CDN edge is located
choices.3: The region responsible for a given piece of data, so a European user's data lives in Europe and most requests stay entirely within one region (short paths, low latency, the local DB is the source of truth)
choices.4: The region with the most servers, which owns all data

question: How are users routed to the correct region?

choices.1: The database decides the region after processing the query
choices.2: Via GeoDNS (DNS returns a different IP by the resolver's location), Anycast (the same IP announced from many regions, with BGP delivering to the nearest), or an edge routing layer that forwards to a user's home region
choices.3: All requests go to one central region that forwards them
choices.4: Users manually pick a region at login

question: What is the usual pragmatic consistency design across regions?

choices.1: All regions accept writes independently with no reconciliation
choices.2: Reads always go cross-region to the home region for every request
choices.3: Writes stay in the home region (fast, strongly consistent locally) and other regions get asynchronous, eventually-consistent copies for reads, with each user pinned to their home region so they read their own writes from one authoritative place
choices.4: Every write uses synchronous multi-region consensus for strong consistency everywhere

question: Besides latency, what is a major driver of regional partitioning?

choices.1: Reducing the number of servers needed
choices.2: Eliminating the need for a CDN
choices.3: Making all data globally readable with no restrictions
choices.4: Data residency / compliance — regulations like GDPR require citizens' data to physically reside within geographic borders, which regional partitioning makes natural since the EU region owns EU users' data

question: How do CDN, replication, and regional partitioning relate?

choices.1: They are three names for the same technique
choices.2: A CDN handles writes while regional partitioning handles static assets
choices.3: A CDN caches static content near users (no app logic or writes); replication gives every node all data for read-scaling/failover; regional partitioning has each region own a slice of user data and run the full app — the real fix for dynamic, write-heavy workloads near users, and they stack together
choices.4: Regional partitioning replaces the need for both CDN and replication

question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

choices.1: They are the same thing with different names
choices.2: Authentication enforces permissions; authorization proves identity
choices.3: Authentication (authN) is 'who are you?' — proving identity; authorization (authZ) is 'what are you allowed to do?' — enforcing permissions on a proven identity; authN comes first and authZ acts on its result (401 = not authenticated, 403 = not authorized)
choices.4: Authorization always comes before authentication

question: What is the trade-off between stateful sessions and stateless JWT tokens?

choices.1: Sessions store a record server-side and return a random id in a cookie — easy to revoke but requiring a lookup every request and a shared store; JWTs are signed, self-contained tokens any service verifies locally without a lookup (scaling horizontally) but are hard to revoke before expiry (mitigated by short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens)
choices.2: They are functionally identical
choices.3: JWTs are easy to revoke while sessions cannot be revoked
choices.4: Sessions are stateless and JWTs are stateful

question: What is a key caveat about JWT payloads?

choices.1: Don't put secrets in the payload — it is base64-encoded and only signed, not encrypted, so anyone can read it; the signature only proves integrity, and the token should always travel over TLS
choices.2: The payload is encrypted, so secrets are safe inside it
choices.3: The signature encrypts the payload for confidentiality
choices.4: JWTs cannot carry any claims like roles or expiry

question: What is the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC)?

choices.1: OIDC is an older version of OAuth2
choices.2: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework for delegated access (letting a user grant App A limited access to their data on Service B without sharing their password), issuing an access token — it is NOT about identity; OIDC is an identity layer on top of OAuth2 that adds an ID token (a JWT) saying who the user is — what 'Log in with Google' uses
choices.3: They are competing, unrelated protocols
choices.4: OAuth2 proves identity while OIDC grants delegated access

question: How do RBAC and ABAC authorization models differ?

choices.1: RBAC (role-based) attaches permissions to roles and gives users roles (admin/editor/viewer) — simple and coarse; ABAC (attribute-based) evaluates policies over attributes (user department, resource owner, time, location) — fine-grained and flexible but more complex
choices.2: ABAC is simpler and coarser than RBAC
choices.3: RBAC is attribute-based; ABAC is role-based
choices.4: Both grant every user full access by default

question: What are the roles of access, refresh, and ID tokens?

choices.1: An access token is for authorization (sent to APIs, short-lived), a refresh token is used to mint new access tokens, and an ID token (OIDC) carries the user's identity for the client
choices.2: All three are identity tokens used interchangeably
choices.3: The refresh token is short-lived and the access token is long-lived
choices.4: The access token mints refresh tokens and the ID token is sent to APIs

question: Why are cross-shard queries (scatter-gather) problematic?

choices.1: They are impossible, so sharded systems forbid all queries
choices.2: They only affect writes, never reads
choices.3: A query whose results span multiple shards must fan out to every relevant shard and merge the results, so latency is bound by the slowest shard and one slow/failed shard degrades the whole query — JOINs and aggregations across shards are especially costly
choices.4: They are faster than single-shard queries because they parallelize

question: Why do distributed transactions across shards lose single-database ACID guarantees, and what's the common alternative?

choices.1: Sharded systems automatically provide full ACID across shards for free
choices.2: The fix is to never write to a sharded database
choices.3: A transaction writing rows on different shards can't rely on one database's ACID; maintaining atomicity needs two-phase commit (slow, blocking, coordinator-dependent), so many sharded systems instead use the Saga pattern — local transactions with compensating actions — accepting eventual consistency
choices.4: Cross-shard transactions are faster than single-shard ones

question: Why is rebalancing/resharding hard, and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: With naive hash % N, adding a shard remaps nearly all keys (a huge, risky migration), and rebalancing must happen online without downtime; mitigations include consistent hashing (only ~1/N keys move), directory-based mapping (move data then update the map), and over-provisioning logical shards up front
choices.2: There is no way to rebalance a sharded system
choices.3: Rebalancing is instant and requires no data movement
choices.4: Resharding only moves a single key at a time

question: Why are global constraints like uniqueness hard in a sharded system?

choices.1: Uniqueness is checked globally for free by every shard
choices.2: Sharding makes constraints easier, not harder
choices.3: Uniqueness and auto-increment can only be checked within a shard, so global uniqueness needs a distributed ID scheme (UUIDs, Snowflake IDs, or a central allocator); foreign keys across shards aren't enforced by the database (integrity moves to the app); and secondary indexes must be local (scatter-gather to query) or global (a separate sharded structure)
choices.4: The database enforces all global constraints transparently across shards

question: What causes hotspots even with many shards?

choices.1: Hotspots are caused by too many distinct key values
choices.2: Hotspots only occur with exactly one shard
choices.3: Even data distribution always prevents any hotspot
choices.4: Data skew (one range or tenant holds far more rows than others), access skew (a 'celebrity' key gets disproportionate traffic regardless of even data spread), and a sequential shard key sending all new writes to one shard

question: What is the golden rule of sharding?

choices.1: Always shard on a sequential/auto-increment key
choices.2: Spread every entity's data across all shards for balance
choices.3: Pick a shard key that keeps the data a single request needs on a single shard — most sharding pain comes from operations that have to cross shard boundaries
choices.4: Use as many shards as possible regardless of the key

question: What is the difference between native and application-level sharding?

choices.1: Native sharding requires the app to pick the shard manually
choices.2: They are identical approaches
choices.3: Application-level (manual) sharding has the app or a middleware proxy (Vitess for MySQL, Citus for Postgres) decide the shard — maximum control and complexity; native (automatic) sharding has the database manage partitioning, routing, and rebalancing internally so the app talks to a logical endpoint
choices.4: Application-level sharding hides the shards from the app

question: Why does sharding terminology vary across systems?

choices.1: Because only relational databases use the word 'shard'
choices.2: What one system calls a shard, another calls a partition, vnode, region, tablet, or chunk — but the underlying idea (split data by a key across nodes) is the same
choices.3: Because each term means a fundamentally different mechanism
choices.4: Because the terms refer to replication, not sharding

question: How do Cassandra and ScyllaDB approach sharding?

choices.1: They use a central coordinator that assigns each row to a shard
choices.2: They are built around consistent hashing: the partition key is hashed onto a token ring, each node owns ranges of tokens subdivided into virtual nodes for even distribution, and they are masterless/peer-to-peer so adding a node automatically reassigns a fraction of the ring — sharding and replication are unified
choices.3: They store all data on one master node with read replicas
choices.4: They require manual range-boundary configuration per shard

question: How do NewSQL systems (Spanner, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB) shard?

choices.1: They avoid sharding entirely and run on a single node
choices.2: They split data into ranges/tablets by primary-key ranges and automatically split and move them as they grow or get hot, replicate each range via consensus (Paxos/Raft) for fault tolerance, and provide first-class distributed ACID transactions (Spanner uses TrueTime; CockroachDB uses hybrid logical clocks)
choices.3: They forbid cross-shard transactions like NoSQL stores
choices.4: They shard manually via application code only

question: How does Redis Cluster shard data?

choices.1: It hashes keys onto a token ring with virtual nodes
choices.2: It stores all keys on one node with no partitioning
choices.3: It uses a fixed set of 16384 hash slots, mapping each key to a slot via CRC16(key) % 16384 and distributing slots across nodes; resharding moves slots (and their keys) between nodes live, and hash tags {...} force related keys into the same slot for multi-key operations
choices.4: It splits tables by primary-key ranges into tablets

question: What is the industry trend in database sharding?

choices.1: Toward eliminating sharding in favor of single huge servers
choices.2: Toward transparent, automatic sharding — pushing partitioning, rebalancing, and even distributed transactions into the database so applications can scale without re-implementing the hard parts by hand
choices.3: Toward fully manual application-level sharding everywhere
choices.4: Toward exposing all physical shard details to the application

question: How does sharding differ from replication?

choices.1: Replication copies the whole dataset to multiple nodes (for availability/read scaling); sharding splits different data onto different nodes (for write/storage scaling) — they are complementary and usually combined, with each shard itself replicated
choices.2: Sharding copies all data everywhere; replication splits it
choices.3: Replication is for writes and sharding is for reads
choices.4: They are the same technique

question: What are the trade-offs of range-based sharding?

choices.1: It distributes keys perfectly evenly and never has hotspots
choices.2: It makes range queries expensive and scatter across shards
choices.3: It requires no choice of shard key
choices.4: Rows are assigned to shards by contiguous ranges of the shard key, giving excellent range queries and easy splitting of a hot range — but sequential keys (timestamps, auto-increment IDs) send all new writes to the last shard (hotspots) and ranges can be skewed/unbalanced

question: What are the strengths and weaknesses of hash-based (mod N) sharding?

choices.1: Hashing the key and taking it modulo N gives even distribution avoiding hotspots even with sequential keys, with fast deterministic routing — but range queries scatter across all shards and resharding is painful because changing N remaps almost every key (forcing a massive migration)
choices.2: It excels at range queries but distributes unevenly
choices.3: It keeps logically adjacent data physically together
choices.4: It makes resharding trivial with no key movement

question: What is the trade-off of directory-based (lookup) sharding?

choices.1: It requires no lookup and routes by pure hashing
choices.2: A lookup service maps each shard key to a shard, giving maximum flexibility (any key can map to any shard, rebalancing is easy — move data then update the map) — but the directory is a single point of failure and bottleneck that must be replicated/cached, and it adds a lookup hop per request
choices.3: It cannot be rebalanced once set up
choices.4: It eliminates all latency from routing

question: What are the benefits and risks of geo/entity-based sharding?

choices.1: It scatters related data across all shards
choices.2: It is the same as hash-based sharding
choices.3: Partitioning along a business dimension (region, tenant, customer) gives data locality (related rows co-located, avoiding cross-shard joins) and supports data residency/compliance — but a single large tenant or popular region can overwhelm one shard (the noisy-neighbor problem)
choices.4: It guarantees perfectly even distribution across shards

question: What makes a good shard key?

choices.1: High cardinality (many distinct values so data spreads finely), even access distribution (to avoid hotspots), and a match to query patterns (most queries answerable from one shard) — while avoiding monotonic values like timestamps for hash routing unless combined with another field; it's the single most important, hard-to-change decision
choices.2: A sequential auto-increment value for predictable ordering
choices.3: Any column at random, since it can easily be changed later
choices.4: Low cardinality so keys group together

question: What is the FIX protocol, and what are its two layers?

choices.1: A proprietary multicast market-data feed format
choices.2: The de-facto standard messaging protocol for order entry and trade communication between market participants — a session layer (a reliable, ordered, recoverable conversation over TCP: sequence numbers, heartbeats, resend) plus an application layer (business messages: new order, cancel, execution report)
choices.3: A consensus protocol for replicating trades across exchanges
choices.4: A binary RPC framework for microservices

question: What is the FIX wire format, and what is its downside?

choices.1: A JSON document over HTTP/2
choices.2: A flat list of tag=value pairs separated by the SOH control character; it is self-describing (every field tagged), human-readable, and flexible — but verbose on the wire and slow to parse (string scanning, integer parsing, no fixed layout)
choices.3: A fixed-layout binary struct parsed by overlay, very fast
choices.4: A compressed columnar format optimized for analytics

question: How does the FIX session layer provide reliable delivery over TCP?

choices.1: It uses UDP multicast with no recovery
choices.2: It relies purely on TCP with no extra reliability
choices.3: It re-sends every message continuously regardless of acknowledgment
choices.4: Every message is numbered with a MsgSeqNum per direction, heartbeats prove liveness when idle, and a ResendRequest replays missed messages — so after a drop both sides reconcile by sequence number and no order is lost or duplicated (persisting sent messages is mandatory to answer a resend)

question: What is the role of the ExecutionReport (35=8) in the FIX order lifecycle?

choices.1: It is the workhorse message reporting every state transition of an order (New/acked, partially filled, filled, canceled, rejected, replaced), with ClOrdID (tag 11) tying acks and fills back to the originating order as the client's idempotency key
choices.2: It carries only market-data price updates
choices.3: It is the message a client sends to place a new order
choices.4: It authenticates the session at logon

question: Why do HFT firms often move off classic FIX for latency-critical order entry?

choices.1: Because FIX text is faster than binary on the hot path
choices.2: Because binary protocols are easier to debug with curl
choices.3: Classic tag=value text is ubiquitous and self-describing (great for integration) but verbose and slow to parse, which is bad on the hot path; for latency-critical order entry exchanges offer binary protocols (FIX SBE/FAST or native binary) with fixed layouts parsed by struct overlay — orders of magnitude faster to encode/decode
choices.4: Because FIX cannot represent orders at all

question: When HFT swaps FIX text for a binary encoding, what typically survives?

choices.1: Everything changes, including the session model
choices.2: The session concepts (sequence numbers, heartbeats, resend) usually survive — only the encoding changes from self-describing text to packed binary; HFT order entry uses binary while back-office, allocations, and cross-firm integration stay on FIX
choices.3: Binary encodings remove the need for any session layer
choices.4: The text encoding survives but sequence numbers are dropped

question: Why are market data feeds pushed over UDP multicast rather than TCP?

choices.1: The exchange sends one stream to a multicast group and the network fabric fans it out to all subscribers simultaneously (minimal latency, scales to thousands); TCP would serialize the same data per subscriber and its retransmit/ordering adds head-of-line blocking — but the app must then handle UDP's drops/reorders itself
choices.2: UDP guarantees ordered, reliable delivery that TCP lacks
choices.3: TCP cannot carry binary market data
choices.4: UDP multicast retransmits lost packets automatically on the hot path

question: How does a feed handler detect packet loss?

choices.1: It compares checksums on every packet
choices.2: Every message carries a monotonically increasing sequence number; the handler tracks the next expected value, and a gap (e.g. receiving 1004 when expecting 1002) means dropped packets — it must NOT apply the ahead-of-gap message (the book would be wrong), so it buffers and triggers recovery
choices.3: It waits for the exchange to send a 'loss' notification
choices.4: It assumes no loss occurs over multicast

question: What is A/B line arbitration?

choices.1: Exchanges publish the same sequenced stream twice over two independent network paths (feed A and feed B); the handler listens to both and takes whichever packet arrives first per sequence number, so a drop on one line is covered by the other with zero recovery latency
choices.2: It encrypts the feed twice for security
choices.3: It compresses the stream into two halves
choices.4: It load-balances orders across two matching engines

question: How does a feed handler recover when a real gap occurs on both lines?

choices.1: It switches to a TCP connection for all future data
choices.2: It ignores the gap and continues applying messages
choices.3: It keeps buffering live incrementals, obtains a snapshot (a periodic full book image tagged with the sequence number it's valid as of) or replayed messages up to seq S, discards buffered incrementals <= S, applies those > S, and resumes the live feed in sync
choices.4: It restarts the entire trading system from scratch

question: What is conflation in a market data feed, and when is it acceptable?

choices.1: When a slow downstream consumer (a GUI or slow strategy) can't keep up, conflation collapses multiple updates to the same price level into the latest value (sending state, not history) — acceptable only for slow/visual consumers, never on the trading hot path
choices.2: Conflation is applied on the hot path to speed up matching
choices.3: Conflation merges two exchanges' feeds into one and is always safe
choices.4: Conflation deduplicates the A and B lines

question: Which techniques minimize a feed handler's latency?

choices.1: Allocating new book structures on every parse
choices.2: Kernel bypass (Solarflare/Onload, DPDK) to receive multicast straight into user space, busy-polling the NIC, hardware NIC timestamps on ingress, pre-allocated cache-aligned book structures, and measuring with percentiles rather than means
choices.3: Routing all packets through the kernel TCP stack
choices.4: Blocking on interrupts to save CPU on the hot path

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What is an order book and a matching engine?

choices.1: An order book is a log of completed trades; the matching engine compresses it
choices.2: An order book is the data structure holding all resting buy orders (bids) and sell orders (asks) for an instrument, organized by price; the matching engine takes incoming orders, matches them against the book, generates trades, and publishes results — a single-threaded, in-memory, deterministic state machine on the hot path
choices.3: An order book is a relational database table; the matching engine runs SQL queries
choices.4: They are two names for the market data feed

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What are the best bid, best ask, and spread?

choices.1: The mid is the most recently traded price
choices.2: The best bid is the highest buy price and the best ask the lowest sell price (top of book); the gap between them is the spread and the midpoint is the mid — a marketable order crosses the spread and trades immediately, while a passive/resting order provides liquidity
choices.3: The best bid is the lowest buy and the best ask the highest sell
choices.4: The spread is the total quantity available in the book

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: How does price-time (FIFO) priority determine matching?

choices.1: The most recently placed order trades first
choices.2: Price priority comes first (the best price trades first), then a time tiebreak within a level (the first order at that price trades first); it rewards being early, which drives the latency race — whereas pro-rata instead splits fills proportionally to order size
choices.3: The largest order at any price always trades first
choices.4: Orders trade in random order to be fair

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: Why must the cancel operation be O(1) in a matching engine?

choices.1: The three hot-path operations are add, cancel, and match, and cancel dominates in real markets (HFT cancels the vast majority of orders), so cancel must be O(1) — achieved via an order_id -> Order* hash map plus intrusive FIFO lists for O(1) unlink
choices.2: Because the exchange charges per cancel
choices.3: Because cancels must be slower than adds for fairness
choices.4: Because cancels are rare and only happen at end of day

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What is the price-ladder (price-indexed array) data structure trick?

choices.1: Keeping levels in a balanced binary search tree for O(log n) access
choices.2: Storing the book on disk and memory-mapping it
choices.3: For instruments with a bounded tick range, levels are indexed directly by (price / tick_size) in a flat array, giving O(1) access, branch-light, cache-hot performance with best-bid/ask pointers scanned to the next non-empty level on depletion — the classic HFT approach
choices.4: Storing all price levels in a hash table keyed by order id

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: How is a matching engine made crash-recoverable and scalable?

choices.1: By using a relational database with ACID transactions for each match
choices.2: By multithreading a single book across many cores with locks
choices.3: By writing the full book state to disk after every order
choices.4: The book lives in RAM and persistence is achieved by logging the input event stream as an append-only journal (not by writing book state) so replaying the journal rebuilds the book after a crash; you scale OUT by sharding instruments across engines and replicate the deterministic state machine for HA

question: What is Memcached, and what is it deliberately NOT?

choices.1: A relational database with SQL support
choices.2: A message broker with durable queues
choices.3: A persistent database with rich data structures and replication
choices.4: A high-performance, distributed, multi-threaded in-memory cache — and only a cache: a pure volatile key-to-opaque-bytes store with no persistence, no replication, and no data structures beyond blobs

question: How does Memcached's threading model differ from Redis's?

choices.1: Both are single-threaded for command execution
choices.2: Memcached is single-threaded while Redis is multi-threaded
choices.3: Memcached uses one thread per key
choices.4: Memcached is multi-threaded, scaling across CPU cores within one node for high throughput on big multi-core boxes, unlike Redis whose command execution is single-threaded

question: What is Memcached's slab allocator, and its downside?

choices.1: Memory is pre-carved into fixed-size 'slab classes' to avoid fragmentation, with values placed in the smallest slab that fits (predictable memory, no fragmentation); the downside is slab calcification / internal waste — a value slightly larger than a slab class wastes the remainder, and memory assigned to one slab class is hard to reassign
choices.2: It compresses values to fit any size, with no waste
choices.3: It allocates exact-size blocks per value, causing fragmentation
choices.4: It stores values on disk to avoid memory limits

question: How is data distributed across Memcached nodes?

choices.1: A master node routes every request to the right shard
choices.2: Nodes gossip to agree on key ownership
choices.3: Memcached nodes don't know about each other; the client library shards keys across nodes, ideally using consistent hashing so adding/removing a node only remaps ~1/N of keys instead of flushing the whole cache
choices.4: All nodes hold a full replica of every key

question: What happens to data in Memcached on a restart?

choices.1: Data is restored from an append-only log
choices.2: Data is replicated from a peer node
choices.3: Data is restored from a periodic snapshot
choices.4: Everything is lost — Memcached has no persistence; it is purely a volatile cache, with LRU eviction per slab class when memory is full

question: When should you pick Memcached over Redis?

choices.1: When you need persistence and replication out of the box
choices.2: When you need a built-in clustering protocol
choices.3: When you need sorted sets and streams
choices.4: When you need only a fast volatile blob cache with multi-core throughput and dead-simple operations; reach for Redis the moment you need data structures, persistence, pub/sub, locks, or built-in HA

question: Why is Redis being single-threaded a source of speed rather than slowness?

choices.1: Single-threaded command execution means no lock contention, no context switches, and every command is atomic by construction (it runs to completion before the next); the bottleneck is network/memory not CPU, and you scale past one core by sharding
choices.2: Single-threading is slow, which is why Redis needs persistence
choices.3: Single-threading requires locks around every command
choices.4: Single-threading lets Redis use all CPU cores in parallel

question: What makes Redis far more than a key-value cache?

choices.1: It stores data exclusively on disk for durability
choices.2: It only stores opaque blobs like Memcached
choices.3: It is a full relational database with JOINs
choices.4: Its rich data structures — strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets (great for leaderboards/rate limiters), streams (a Kafka-like log with consumer groups), bitmaps, and HyperLogLogs — so you pick the structure to fit the job

question: How do Redis's RDB and AOF persistence options differ?

choices.1: Neither persists data; Redis is always purely volatile
choices.2: RDB logs every command; AOF takes periodic snapshots
choices.3: RDB is a periodic point-in-time snapshot (compact, fast restart, but can lose the last few minutes on a crash); AOF (Append-Only File) logs every write command and replays it on restart (far less data loss, but larger files and slower restart)
choices.4: Both guarantee zero data loss on any crash

question: How does Redis Cluster shard the keyspace?

choices.1: It assigns each key to a random node with no fixed mapping
choices.2: It splits the keyspace into 16384 hash slots (slot = CRC16(key) % 16384) distributed across primary nodes (each with replicas); hash tags {...} force related keys into the same slot so multi-key operations can span them
choices.3: It hashes keys onto a token ring with virtual nodes like Cassandra
choices.4: It replicates every key to all nodes

question: What provides Redis high availability for a single-primary (non-sharded) setup?

choices.1: Sentinel monitors primaries and performs automatic failover plus client discovery, while replication asynchronously copies the primary to one or more replicas (read scaling + a failover copy that can lag)
choices.2: A master election via an external ZooKeeper cluster
choices.3: Redis Cluster is the only HA option for a single primary
choices.4: Synchronous replication to every replica before acknowledging writes

question: What is the caveat with using Redis as a distributed lock?

choices.1: Redis locks are always perfectly safe with no caveats
choices.2: Redis cannot implement locks at all
choices.3: SET key val NX PX ttl gives a lock, but it is not safe for correctness-critical mutual exclusion without fencing tokens — a GC-paused (or otherwise stalled) holder can think it still owns the lock after it has expired and been granted to someone else
choices.4: Locks require the AOF to be disabled

question: What is Cassandra's 'tunable consistency'?

choices.1: Cassandra is fundamentally an AP system (eventually consistent by default) but lets you choose per query how many replicas must respond, trading off consistency, latency, and availability
choices.2: Consistency is fixed cluster-wide and cannot be changed
choices.3: It is a CP system that rejects writes during any partition
choices.4: It always enforces strong consistency on every operation

question: What does the QUORUM consistency level require?

choices.1: A majority of all replicas to respond — floor(RF/2) + 1 (e.g. 2 of 3 with RF=3) — while LOCAL_QUORUM requires a quorum only within the local datacenter to avoid cross-DC latency
choices.2: A minority of replicas to respond
choices.3: Exactly one replica to respond
choices.4: Every single replica to respond

question: What is the R + W > RF rule in Cassandra?

choices.1: It only applies to writes, not reads
choices.2: R + W > RF guarantees the lowest possible latency
choices.3: R + W must be less than RF for strong consistency
choices.4: If the read consistency level (R) plus the write consistency level (W) exceeds the replication factor (RF), the read and write replica sets must overlap on at least one node, guaranteeing the read sees the latest acknowledged write (strong consistency); e.g. RF=3 with W=2 and R=2 gives 4 > 3

question: How does Cassandra resolve write conflicts, and what is the consequence?

choices.1: It uses two-phase commit to serialize all conflicting writes
choices.2: Last-Write-Wins: every column value carries a timestamp and the highest timestamp wins, requiring no locks or coordination so writes are fast and never block — but there is no read-modify-write safety by default, so two concurrent increment-style updates can lose data
choices.3: It merges conflicting values by summing them
choices.4: It rejects any write that conflicts with another

question: What is hinted handoff in Cassandra?

choices.1: It routes reads to the fastest replica
choices.2: It rejects writes until all replicas are up
choices.3: If a replica is down during a write, the coordinator stores a hint (a pending write) and replays it when the replica recovers, so writes succeed even with a replica temporarily offline (hints have a time limit, after which repair fixes the gap)
choices.4: It hands off the entire partition to a new node permanently

question: When and why would you use a Cassandra Lightweight Transaction (LWT)?

choices.1: To make reads faster by skipping replicas
choices.2: For operations that need conditional/linearizable semantics like 'insert only if not exists' or 'update only if unchanged', LWT runs a Paxos consensus round giving compare-and-set — but it's expensive (multiple round trips) so it should be used sparingly for the few operations that truly need it
choices.3: For every write, to guarantee strong consistency by default
choices.4: To resolve Last-Write-Wins conflicts automatically

question: How does Cassandra data modeling differ fundamentally from relational modeling?

choices.1: In Cassandra you model the QUERIES first and design tables to serve them, denormalizing and duplicating data freely (one table per query pattern) — the opposite of relational normalization where you model the data then query it however you like
choices.2: You normalize the data fully and rely on JOINs like a relational DB
choices.3: You model entities first and let Cassandra optimize the queries
choices.4: There is no difference; CQL is identical to SQL

question: Which capabilities does Cassandra's wide-column model NOT provide?

choices.1: It allows arbitrary WHERE clauses on any column efficiently
choices.2: No JOINs, no foreign keys/referential integrity, no arbitrary WHERE clauses (you query mostly by primary key), and only limited aggregations
choices.3: It supports full JOINs and foreign keys like a relational database
choices.4: It provides full SQL with subqueries and window functions

question: What are the two parts of a Cassandra primary key and their roles?

choices.1: PRIMARY KEY ((partition key), clustering columns): the partition key decides WHERE the row lives (which node/partition), and the clustering columns decide HOW rows are sorted WITHIN that partition
choices.2: Both parts just uniquely identify the row with no effect on layout
choices.3: The first part is the column name and the second is its value
choices.4: The partition key sorts rows and the clustering columns decide the node

question: What does the partition key determine?

choices.1: It is only a uniqueness constraint with no effect on storage location
choices.2: It selects which datacenter handles the query
choices.3: It determines the sort order of rows within a partition
choices.4: It is hashed to decide which node(s) store the row, and all rows with the same partition key live together on the same node in one partition

question: What do clustering columns control?

choices.1: They enforce foreign-key relationships between tables
choices.2: They decide which node stores the row
choices.3: They have no effect on how data is stored
choices.4: Within a partition, rows are physically stored sorted by the clustering columns, which makes range scans and ordered reads inside that partition extremely cheap

question: What are the two competing goals (and cardinal sins) when designing a partition key?

choices.1: You need even distribution (high cardinality so load spreads, avoiding hot partitions) AND bounded partition size (e.g. add a time bucket so partitions don't grow unbounded); the two cardinal sins are hot partitions and unbounded partitions
choices.2: You need low cardinality and unbounded partitions for efficiency
choices.3: Partition key choice doesn't affect performance
choices.4: You need a sequential key and a single global partition

question: How does Cassandra propagate cluster state without a master?

choices.1: A master node broadcasts state to all others
choices.2: Each node polls a central registry on every request
choices.3: A ZooKeeper cluster keeps the authoritative membership
choices.4: Via gossip — a peer-to-peer epidemic protocol where roughly every second each node picks a few random peers and exchanges its view of every node's state; information spreads exponentially until the whole cluster converges, with no central coordinator

question: What information does gossip carry about each node?

choices.1: The SQL query plans the node has executed
choices.2: Only whether the node is up or down, nothing else
choices.3: The full contents of every partition the node stores
choices.4: Status (up/down/joining/leaving/normal), a heartbeat/generation version counter so newer info wins over stale info, the token ranges owned, schema version, datacenter/rack, and load

question: What are seed nodes in Cassandra?

choices.1: The nodes that hold the only writable copy of the data
choices.2: Coordinator nodes that route every query
choices.3: Well-known addresses a joining node first gossips with to learn about the cluster; they are bootstrap contacts, not special masters
choices.4: The nodes that run the failure detector for everyone else

question: How does Cassandra's Phi Accrual failure detector work?

choices.1: Instead of a fixed 'missed N heartbeats = dead' rule, it tracks the history of heartbeat inter-arrival times per node and computes Phi — a continuously rising suspicion value relative to that node's normal timing — marking the node down when Phi exceeds a threshold; this adapts to network conditions and avoids false positives on slow links
choices.2: It marks a node dead after exactly three missed heartbeats
choices.3: It relies on the node self-reporting that it has failed
choices.4: It pings every node from a central monitor every second

question: How does Cassandra keep serving when a node holding a replica goes down?

choices.1: A leader election is triggered to pick a replacement before any request is served
choices.2: Because every partition has RF replicas and there's no master, the other RF-1 replicas still serve reads and accept writes (RF=3 with QUORUM tolerates one node down), while hinted handoff covers the down replica's writes and read/anti-entropy repair reconcile divergence later
choices.3: The whole cluster halts until the node recovers
choices.4: All writes are rejected until the node returns

question: Why does Cassandra have no single point of failure?

choices.1: There is no master (no leader election to stall on, nothing whose loss stops writes), any node can coordinate any request, gossip provides decentralized membership, RF replicas plus hinted handoff and repair let data survive and self-heal, and multi-DC replication survives an entire datacenter outage
choices.2: Because a ZooKeeper quorum guarantees a coordinator is always available
choices.3: Because every node holds a full copy of all data
choices.4: Because one master node is replicated three times

question: In Cassandra, what do partitioning and replication each decide?

choices.1: Partitioning encrypts data; replication compresses it
choices.2: Partitioning decides which node OWNS a given row (via consistent hashing on a token ring); replication decides how many COPIES exist and where (via replication factor and strategy)
choices.3: Both decide only the sort order of rows
choices.4: Partitioning decides the number of copies; replication decides the owning node

question: How does Cassandra place a row on the token ring?

choices.1: The row is stored on a randomly chosen node
choices.2: A master node assigns each row to a partition
choices.3: The row goes to whichever node has the most free space
choices.4: The partition key is hashed (default Murmur3) to produce a token on the ring; each node owns a contiguous range of tokens, and the row is stored on the node whose range contains its token (then replicated clockwise)

question: What benefit do virtual nodes (vnodes) provide?

choices.1: They eliminate the need for replication
choices.2: They let one node own the entire ring for simplicity
choices.3: Each node owns many small token ranges scattered around the ring (rather than one big range), so load is balanced evenly and, when a node fails or joins, many nodes share the rebalancing (faster, smoother) — and bigger nodes can be given more vnodes
choices.4: They store the data in memory instead of on disk

question: How are replicas placed for a partition with RF=3, and is there a master replica?

choices.1: The replicas are the next RF-1 (here 2) nodes clockwise from the primary token owner, and crucially all RF copies are EQUAL masterless peers — any of them can serve a read or accept a write (unlike Kafka/ZooKeeper which have a leader per partition)
choices.2: One replica is the master that all reads and writes must go through
choices.3: All replicas are placed on the same node for locality
choices.4: Replicas are chosen at random across the cluster

question: What does NetworkTopologyStrategy provide over SimpleStrategy?

choices.1: It removes the need for a replication factor
choices.2: It stores all replicas in a single rack for speed
choices.3: It is topology-aware: you set the replication factor PER datacenter and it places replicas across DIFFERENT racks within each DC so a rack failure doesn't destroy all copies, enabling multi-region clusters
choices.4: It just walks the ring clockwise ignoring topology

question: What is the role of the snitch in Cassandra?

choices.1: It tells Cassandra the rack/datacenter topology so replicas are spread across racks for fault isolation and reads are routed to the nearest/healthiest replica
choices.2: It elects the coordinator node for each request
choices.3: It hashes partition keys to tokens
choices.4: It compacts SSTables in the background

question: What is the coordinator in a Cassandra request?

choices.1: A ZooKeeper process that assigns requests to nodes
choices.2: The single master that owns all the data
choices.3: A dedicated router node every request must pass through
choices.4: Because Cassandra is masterless, a client connects to any node, which becomes the coordinator for that request — it hashes the partition key to a token, finds the RF replicas (knowing the ring via gossip), contacts as many as the consistency level requires, and assembles the reply, even if it doesn't own the data

question: What is token-aware routing, and what does it require?

choices.1: Modern drivers know the ring and send the request directly to a node that owns the data, eliminating the extra coordinator-to-replica hop (lower latency, evenly spread coordinator load) — which requires the client to supply the partition key
choices.2: It routes by hashing the entire row contents
choices.3: The driver picks a random node and lets it forward the request
choices.4: The coordinator always adds an extra hop to reach the replicas

question: How does Cassandra's read path resolve replicas at consistency level > ONE?

choices.1: The coordinator sends a full DATA read to the fastest/closest replica and DIGEST reads (just a checksum) to the others needed for CL; if the digests match it returns the data, but if they disagree it fetches full data, returns the newest by timestamp, and triggers read repair to fix the stale replicas
choices.2: It reads from every replica fully and returns the first response
choices.3: It reads from a single master replica only
choices.4: It returns whichever value the most replicas agree on, ignoring timestamps

question: How does a write propagate in Cassandra relative to the consistency level?

choices.1: The write is buffered until all replicas are confirmed up
choices.2: The write is sent only to the number of replicas equal to the consistency level
choices.3: The write goes to one master replica that forwards it later
choices.4: The write is sent to ALL RF replicas in parallel (each writing to its commit log + memtable and ACKing); the consistency level only governs how many ACKs the coordinator waits for before reporting success, and a down replica gets a hinted handoff

question: What is the production-standard driver load-balancing policy for Cassandra?

choices.1: Random selection of any node in any datacenter
choices.2: Always route to a single fixed coordinator node
choices.3: TokenAwarePolicy wrapping DCAwareRoundRobinPolicy: prefer a replica that owns the data (no extra hop), prefer the local datacenter (avoid cross-region latency), and round-robin among local candidates — pairing naturally with LOCAL_QUORUM
choices.4: Always route to the node with the lowest IP address

question: Why can't Cassandra efficiently answer a query without the partition key?

choices.1: Because non-key columns are encrypted and unreadable
choices.2: The coordinator can only locate data via the token of the partition key; without it there's nowhere to route, so it would have to scan every node (a full cluster scan), and secondary indexes are local to each node so querying one still fans out to all nodes — which is why you model one table per query
choices.3: Because the coordinator can query any column with equal efficiency
choices.4: Because Cassandra forbids reading more than one row at a time

question: What storage engine does Cassandra use, and what is its core trick?

choices.1: A B-tree that updates rows in place for read optimization
choices.2: A log-structured merge-tree (LSM-tree): it never updates data in place, only appends, turning writes into fast sequential disk operations and deferring cost to background compaction (versus a B-tree's in-place updates and random I/O)
choices.3: A hash table stored entirely in memory with no disk
choices.4: A relational engine with row-level locking

question: What is the Cassandra write path on a single node?

choices.1: The write updates a B-tree index and locks the row
choices.2: The write directly modifies the relevant SSTable in place
choices.3: The write is appended to the commit log on disk (durability, replayed on crash) and applied to an in-memory sorted memtable; when the memtable fills it is flushed to disk as an immutable SSTable
choices.4: The write goes only to memory and is never persisted

question: What are the consequences of SSTables being immutable?

choices.1: Each row always lives in exactly one SSTable
choices.2: A DELETE immediately erases the data from disk
choices.3: An UPDATE doesn't edit anything — it writes a new value (newer timestamp) in a new SSTable; a DELETE writes a tombstone marker; so a row's latest state may be spread across several SSTables plus the memtable
choices.4: An UPDATE rewrites the existing SSTable in place

question: What is the purpose of a bloom filter on an SSTable?

choices.1: A probabilistic 'is this key maybe in this SSTable?' check that lets a read skip SSTables that definitely don't contain the key, speeding up reads
choices.2: It compresses the SSTable to save disk space
choices.3: It guarantees a key is present without reading the SSTable
choices.4: It sorts the rows within the SSTable

question: Why do reads in Cassandra cost more than writes?

choices.1: Reads must contact every node in the cluster
choices.2: A row's data may be scattered across the memtable and multiple SSTables (plus tombstones), so a read must check the memtable, use bloom filters to skip SSTables, read candidates, and merge all fragments by timestamp (newest column wins, applying tombstones) to reconstruct the current row
choices.3: Reads always rewrite the data they touch
choices.4: Reads acquire a global lock that writes don't

question: What is a tombstone, and why are delete-heavy workloads an anti-pattern?

choices.1: Since SSTables are immutable, a DELETE writes a tombstone (a marker + timestamp) that shadows older values during read merges; the data and tombstone are only physically removed during compaction after gc_grace_seconds — so delete-heavy or queue-like workloads accumulate tombstones, slowing reads and even causing failures
choices.2: A tombstone is a backup copy of a deleted row
choices.3: Tombstones speed up reads by marking hot rows
choices.4: Deletes erase data instantly, so they never affect reads

question: What is Cassandra's sweet spot?

choices.1: Small datasets with ad-hoc analytical queries and strong consistency
choices.2: Huge volumes of writes, across many nodes/regions, with known query patterns, where you need always-on availability and can live with eventual consistency
choices.3: Single-node transactional workloads with complex JOINs
choices.4: Read-heavy workloads with unpredictable access patterns

question: Which workload is a good fit for Cassandra?

choices.1: A banking ledger requiring strict multi-row ACID invariants
choices.2: Ad-hoc analytical queries slicing data many unforeseen ways
choices.3: A small app where a single Postgres would suffice
choices.4: Write-heavy workloads at scale such as time-series, logging, metrics, and event/IoT ingestion, where the LSM storage engine's high write throughput shines

question: Why is Cassandra a poor choice when you need ACID transactions and JOINs?

choices.1: It supports JOINs but only across two tables
choices.2: It has no multi-partition transactions, no real JOINs, and no foreign keys; for banking ledgers or strict invariants you should use a relational DB or NewSQL (Spanner/CockroachDB) — Cassandra's Lightweight Transactions are slow and single-partition only
choices.3: It enforces foreign keys automatically
choices.4: It provides full multi-row ACID transactions out of the box

question: Why are queue/job-queue workloads the classic Cassandra anti-pattern?

choices.1: Queues require JOINs that Cassandra forbids
choices.2: Cassandra processes queue items out of order
choices.3: Cassandra cannot insert and delete in the same table
choices.4: Constant insert+delete generates masses of tombstones, so read performance collapses; you should use a real queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka) instead

question: Why are update-heavy or delete-heavy workloads problematic in Cassandra?

choices.1: Updates and deletes are blocked entirely
choices.2: They force a full cluster scan on every operation
choices.3: Last-Write-Wins means concurrent updates silently lose data (no read-modify-write safety), and deletes create tombstones that slow reads — so mutable, contended state is an anti-pattern
