choices.4: They require switching to strong consistency, which Cassandra lacks

question: How does Cassandra compare to DynamoDB and ScyllaDB?

choices.1: ScyllaDB is a managed cloud service unrelated to Cassandra
choices.2: DynamoDB is open-source and self-managed while Cassandra is proprietary
choices.3: They are all identical with the same operational model
choices.4: DynamoDB is the managed/serverless equivalent of the same Dynamo lineage (no ops, pay-per-use, vendor lock-in) versus Cassandra's self-managed open-source flexibility; ScyllaDB is a C++ Cassandra-compatible rewrite for higher per-node performance

question: What is ClickHouse?

choices.1: A managed key-value store like DynamoDB
choices.2: An in-memory cache like Redis
choices.3: A row-oriented OLTP database for transactional workloads
choices.4: An open-source columnar OLAP database built for blazing-fast analytical queries over huge datasets (billions of rows scanned per second) — an analytics/aggregation engine, not a transactional store

question: Why is ClickHouse fast?

choices.1: It keeps every row fully in memory and scans row-by-row
choices.2: It is columnar (a query touches only referenced columns), uses vectorized execution (processing columns in SIMD batches), applies heavy per-column compression, and uses a sparse primary index that marks every N-th row (granule) so range scans skip irrelevant granules — all massively parallel
choices.3: It avoids compression to reduce CPU cost
choices.4: It uses a B-tree with a per-row index for point lookups

question: How does the ClickHouse MergeTree storage engine work?

choices.1: Every insert immediately rewrites the entire table
choices.2: Data is stored unsorted and indexed by a hash table
choices.3: Inserts are written as immutable sorted 'parts' (insert in bulk, not row-by-row) and background merges combine them into bigger sorted parts (an LSM-style append-and-merge design); data is sorted by the ORDER BY key and partitioning prunes whole partitions
choices.4: Each row is updated in place in a B-tree

question: How do ClickHouse materialized views maintain rollups efficiently?

choices.1: They run on insert: new rows flow through a query into a target (often Aggregating)MergeTree table, maintaining pre-aggregated rollups incrementally — so you compute at write time and read cheap, serving dashboards over billions of rows in milliseconds
choices.2: They store the raw rows and aggregate at read time only
choices.3: They are static snapshots refreshed manually once a day
choices.4: They are recomputed from scratch on every query

question: What are ClickHouse's limitations as an OLAP (not OLTP) engine?

choices.1: No efficient single-row lookups, no transactions across statements, and UPDATE/DELETE are heavy 'mutations' (rewriting parts) rather than cheap point edits — data is meant to be append-mostly and inserted in batches
choices.2: It supports full cross-statement ACID transactions
choices.3: It performs best with row-at-a-time inserts
choices.4: It excels at single-row transactional updates

question: When should you use ClickHouse versus alternatives?

choices.1: Use it for single-row key-value lookups at low latency
choices.2: Use it as the primary transactional database for an app
choices.3: Use it for analytics/aggregations over huge append-mostly data (event analytics, observability/metrics, clickstream, tick/market-data analytics, real-time dashboards); avoid it for transactional app data, frequent updates/deletes, or single-row lookups — and note kdb+ is finance-specialized and Snowflake/BigQuery are fully-managed warehouses
choices.4: Use it only when you need strong cross-row consistency

question: What is Amazon DynamoDB?

choices.1: A fully managed, serverless NoSQL key-value/document database offering single-digit-millisecond latency at any scale; the commercial heir to the Dynamo paper, where AWS handles partitioning, replication, and scaling so you run nothing
choices.2: An in-memory cache you deploy on your own servers
choices.3: A columnar OLAP warehouse for analytics
choices.4: A self-managed open-source relational database

question: What are the DynamoDB primary-key options and their effects?

choices.1: A single composite key with no effect on data placement
choices.2: A partition key only (pure key-value lookup) or a partition key + sort key (wide items per partition sorted by the sort key, enabling range queries within a partition); the partition key is hashed to decide which partition stores the item, and the sort key orders items within it
choices.3: A foreign key referencing another table
choices.4: Only an auto-increment integer primary key

question: What is DynamoDB single-table design?

choices.1: Storing multiple entity types in one table using generic PK/SK attributes and overloaded GSIs so a single query can fetch a whole object graph (e.g. a user + their orders) without JOINs — maximizing performance and minimizing round trips at the cost of a non-obvious schema
choices.2: Storing all data in a single column as JSON
choices.3: Splitting each entity type into its own normalized table with JOINs
choices.4: Using one table per query like Cassandra recommends

question: What consistency and transaction options does DynamoDB offer?

choices.1: Reads are eventually consistent by default or strongly consistent (opt-in), internally replicated across 3 availability zones with quorum-style writes; TransactWriteItems/TransactGetItems provide ACID transactions across multiple items — a capability most Dynamo-style stores lack
choices.2: Writes are asynchronous with no replication
choices.3: All reads are always strongly consistent with no choice
choices.4: It offers no transactions of any kind

question: What causes a 'hot partition' (throttling) in DynamoDB?

choices.1: Using a high-cardinality partition key with even traffic
choices.2: A low-cardinality or skewed partition key concentrates traffic on one partition; adaptive capacity and automatic partition splitting absorb mild hotspots, but a poorly chosen partition key still throttles — the cardinal sin, same as in Cassandra
choices.3: Enabling on-demand capacity mode
choices.4: Adding too many global secondary indexes

question: How does DynamoDB compare with Cassandra?

choices.1: Same Dynamo lineage and partition-key model, but DynamoDB is managed and serverless (no nodes to run, pay-per-use, AWS lock-in, built-in transactions, Streams, Global Tables) while Cassandra is self-managed open source with tunable consistency (R+W>RF) that you must run, repair, and compact yourself
choices.2: They share no design heritage
choices.3: DynamoDB is self-managed open source; Cassandra is the managed service
choices.4: Cassandra is serverless while DynamoDB requires running nodes

question: What is kdb+?

choices.1: A managed serverless key-value store
choices.2: A row-oriented relational database for general OLTP
choices.3: A columnar, in-memory-first time-series database with an integrated array-programming language (q), dominant in quantitative finance and HFT — ingesting millions of market-data events per second and querying billions of ticks at extreme speed
choices.4: A full-text search engine

question: Why is kdb+ fast?

choices.1: It interprets each value individually with no vectorization
choices.2: It relies on a large disk-based B-tree index
choices.3: It stores each row as a separate object and scans row-by-row
choices.4: Its data model and execution model are both columnar and vectorized end-to-end: columns are contiguous arrays (minimal I/O, cache-friendly sequential scans), q operates on whole columns at once (SIMD-amenable, no per-row interpreter overhead), the hot data lives in RAM, and the engine has a tiny footprint

question: What is q in kdb+?

choices.1: An array/vector language (a descendant of APL/k) where operations apply to whole columns, with a SQL-like 'q-sql' layer plus terse functional vector primitives; queries, stored procedures, and the database all live in one process — famously terse with a steep learning curve
choices.2: A graphical query builder with no scripting
choices.3: Standard ANSI SQL with JOINs and subqueries
choices.4: A Python library for data analysis

question: How does kdb+ lay out tables on disk?

choices.1: In-memory tables hold live/recent data, splayed tables save each column as its own file (read only the columns you need), and partitioned tables split by a column (usually DATE) into directories so queries prune to the relevant partitions — 'column = file, partition = directory'
choices.2: All tables are stored as a single row-oriented file
choices.3: Tables are stored only in memory and never persisted
choices.4: Each row is saved as a separate file on disk

question: What is the canonical kdb+ tickerplant architecture?

choices.1: A single monolithic database handling ingest and history with locks
choices.2: A feed handler sends to the tickerplant (an ultra-low-latency process that logs every incoming message for durability and fans it out to subscribers), which feeds the RDB (real-time DB holding today's data in RAM) and, at end of day, the HDB (historical DB on disk, partitioned by date)
choices.3: A relational database with triggers writing to a warehouse
choices.4: A message queue that discards data after delivery

question: What is an asof join (aj) in kdb+, and why does it matter for market data?

choices.1: It joins two tables only on exactly equal timestamps
choices.2: It aggregates rows into time buckets
choices.3: It is a standard inner join on a key column
choices.4: For each row in one table, it finds the most recent matching row in another table as of that row's timestamp (e.g. 'for each trade, what was the prevailing quote at that instant?') — a time-aligned join that is awkward and slow in SQL but native and fast in kdb+, exactly what market-data analytics needs constantly

question: What is InnoDB's clustered index, and why does it matter?

choices.1: It is an in-memory cache of the most recently accessed rows
choices.2: It stores rows in insertion order regardless of the primary key
choices.3: The primary key is a B+tree whose leaves hold the full rows, so the table itself is stored physically in primary-key order ('index-organized') — making PK lookups and PK range scans very fast
choices.4: It is a separate index that duplicates every column for fast scans

question: Why does a secondary-index lookup in InnoDB take an extra step?

choices.1: Secondary indexes are only kept in memory and must be rebuilt per query
choices.2: Secondary indexes must scan the whole table
choices.3: A secondary index stores (indexed columns -> primary key), so a secondary lookup does two steps: secondary index -> PK -> clustered index (a 'bookmark lookup')
choices.4: Secondary indexes store full rows, so there is no extra step

question: Why should an InnoDB primary key be small and monotonic?

choices.1: A random PK improves cache locality on inserts
choices.2: The PK size has no effect on secondary indexes
choices.3: A large PK speeds up every secondary lookup
choices.4: A large or random PK (e.g. a random UUID) bloats every secondary index (which stores the PK) and causes page splits/fragmentation on insert, whereas a small, sequential PK avoids both

question: What is the difference between MySQL's redo log and binlog?

choices.1: The redo log is a write-ahead log for crash recovery (durability); the binlog is a separate logical record of all changes that drives replication and CDC (Postgres unifies these in its WAL, but MySQL has both)
choices.2: The binlog handles crash recovery and the redo log handles replication
choices.3: Neither is used for replication
choices.4: They are the same log under two names

question: What is MySQL's default transaction isolation level, and why is it a gotcha?

choices.1: SERIALIZABLE by default
choices.2: READ UNCOMMITTED by default
choices.3: REPEATABLE READ — which is a classic gotcha because PostgreSQL defaults to READ COMMITTED, so the same code can behave differently across the two databases
choices.4: READ COMMITTED, the same as PostgreSQL

question: How does InnoDB table storage differ from PostgreSQL's?

choices.1: PostgreSQL is index-organized while MySQL uses a heap
choices.2: MySQL/InnoDB stores the table as a clustered index (index-organized by the primary key), while PostgreSQL uses a heap with separate indexes; MySQL is also thread-per-connection versus Postgres's process-per-connection
choices.3: Both are index-organized by the primary key
choices.4: Both store tables as a heap with separate indexes

question: What is PostgreSQL known for?

choices.1: Being an in-memory cache like Redis
choices.2: Being a columnar OLAP warehouse
choices.3: Being an object-relational database renowned for correctness, standards compliance, and extensibility — the modern default relational choice with ACID, strong SQL, MVCC, a rich set of index types, and an extension ecosystem (PostGIS, Citus, pgvector, TimescaleDB)
choices.4: Being a schemaless document store with no transactions

question: What is the implication of PostgreSQL's process-per-connection model?

choices.1: Each connection is a lightweight thread, so pooling is never needed
choices.2: Each client connection is a backend OS process (not a thread), which is robust and isolated but makes connections heavy — so you use a connection pooler (PgBouncer) for high connection counts
choices.3: Connections share one process, so a crash takes them all down
choices.4: It limits Postgres to a single connection at a time

question: How does PostgreSQL's MVCC work, and what is VACUUM for?

choices.1: An UPDATE writes a new row version and marks the old one dead (so readers never block writers and see a consistent snapshot without locks); the dead row versions ('bloat') accumulate, and VACUUM/AUTOVACUUM reclaims them — the classic Postgres operational concern
choices.2: VACUUM compresses the WAL to save disk
choices.3: MVCC locks readers against writers for consistency
choices.4: It updates rows in place and never creates dead versions

question: Which index types make PostgreSQL able to absorb search, geo, and JSON workloads?

choices.1: Only B-tree indexes, like every relational database
choices.2: Beyond the default B-tree: Hash (equality), GIN (inverted index for full-text/JSONB/arrays), GiST (geometric/nearest-neighbor, the basis of PostGIS), and BRIN (block-range, for huge naturally-ordered tables), plus partial/expression/covering indexes
choices.3: It has no specialized index types
choices.4: LSM-tree indexes for write-heavy workloads

question: What does PostgreSQL's Serializable isolation level use, and what does it catch?

choices.1: No serializable level exists in PostgreSQL
choices.2: Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI), which detects write-skew anomalies that plain snapshot isolation allows; SELECT ... FOR UPDATE also gives explicit row locks for read-modify-write
choices.3: Plain snapshot isolation with no anomaly detection
choices.4: Two-phase locking that blocks all readers

question: What is the role of PostgreSQL's WAL (write-ahead log)?

choices.1: It stores dead row versions until VACUUM runs
choices.2: Every change is logged before pages are written, providing crash recovery, and it is the basis of streaming replication and logical replication/decoding (CDC)
choices.3: It is only used for full-text search indexes
choices.4: It caches query results to speed up reads

question: What is etcd?

choices.1: An in-memory cache for bulk data
choices.2: A high-throughput message queue
choices.3: A distributed, strongly-consistent key-value store for coordination metadata, built on Raft with a gRPC/HTTP API — best known as the backing store for Kubernetes — that chooses strong consistency over availability (CP)
choices.4: A general-purpose relational database

question: How does etcd's data model differ from ZooKeeper's?

choices.1: Both store large binary blobs
choices.2: etcd has no versioning of keys
choices.3: etcd uses a hierarchical tree while ZooKeeper is flat
choices.4: etcd uses a flat, sorted key-value space with MVCC revisions (small values), whereas ZooKeeper uses a hierarchical znode tree; etcd's global revision number lets clients read/watch from a specific revision with no missed events

question: What are etcd leases used for?

choices.1: They batch multiple writes into one transaction
choices.2: They compress old key revisions
choices.3: You attach a TTL to keys and the client keeps the lease alive with heartbeats; when the lease expires its keys are deleted, giving automatic liveness — the role ZooKeeper's ephemeral nodes play, and the basis of service registration and locks
choices.4: They permanently pin keys so they can never be deleted

question: How do etcd watches improve on ZooKeeper's?

choices.1: etcd has no watch mechanism
choices.2: etcd watches are one-shot and must be re-registered
choices.3: etcd watches stream changes to a key or prefix from a given revision — event-driven and gap-free (no missed events between reads) — unlike ZooKeeper's one-shot watches that must be re-registered each time and can miss intermediate changes
choices.4: etcd watches poll the server on a fixed interval

question: How does etcd achieve consistency, and what happens during a partition?

choices.1: Every member accepts writes independently and merges later
choices.2: It uses gossip with eventual consistency
choices.3: A cluster of (usually 3 or 5) members replicates the key-value state via Raft — an elected leader orders all writes and a write commits once a quorum persists it (2f+1 tolerates f failures) — and during a partition only the majority side keeps a leader and accepts writes, so there's no split brain (CP)
choices.4: Both sides of a partition keep accepting writes

question: What should etcd NOT be used for?

choices.1: For storing configuration and feature flags
choices.2: As the control plane of a distributed system
choices.3: For service discovery and leader election
choices.4: As a general database, a queue, or for large/bulk data — the whole dataset must fit comfortably in memory and writes go through one leader (limited throughput); it's for small, critical coordination metadata

question: What is Apache ZooKeeper?

choices.1: A distributed coordination service providing a strongly consistent, highly available place to store small amounts of coordination metadata (config, leader election, locks, membership, naming) — not a general-purpose database
choices.2: A distributed cache for bulk data
choices.3: A relational database with full SQL support
choices.4: A high-throughput event-streaming log

question: What are the three types of ZooKeeper znodes, and which combination powers locks/election?

choices.1: Leader, follower, and observer znodes
choices.2: Read-only, write-only, and append-only znodes
choices.3: Hot, warm, and cold znodes
choices.4: Persistent (stays until deleted), ephemeral (auto-deleted when the creating client's session ends — key for liveness), and sequential (appends a monotonic counter for global ordering); ephemeral + sequential is the workhorse for locks and leader election

question: What is the role of an observer in a ZooKeeper ensemble?

choices.1: It stores backups but never serves clients
choices.2: It votes in elections but cannot serve reads
choices.3: It is the single server that orders all writes
choices.4: Like a follower but it does NOT vote — it receives committed state and serves reads, letting you scale read capacity without enlarging the voting quorum (which would slow writes)

question: How does ZooKeeper keep every server's data identical?

choices.1: Via ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast), a leader-based consensus protocol where a single leader orders all writes, every server applies them in the same order, and each write gets a globally ordered transaction id (zxid = epoch, counter)
choices.2: Each server independently decides write order
choices.3: Via gossip with eventual convergence
choices.4: Via the Raft protocol with no leader

question: What is the read/write asymmetry in ZooKeeper?

choices.1: Both reads and writes require a quorum
choices.2: Reads go through the leader and writes are served locally
choices.3: Both reads and writes are served locally with no quorum
choices.4: Writes go through the leader plus a quorum round trip (slower but linearizable), while reads are served locally from a server's in-memory copy (fast but possibly stale) — call sync() before a read to force a fresh, linearizable read

question: Why does ZooKeeper use an odd number of servers, and what does it sacrifice under a partition?

choices.1: The count is irrelevant; it is an AP system
choices.2: Even numbers tolerate more failures; it sacrifices consistency
choices.3: Odd numbers are required by the JVM; it stays available on both sides
choices.4: With 2f+1 servers it tolerates f failures (a 4-node cluster tolerates only 1, same as 3, so odd is strictly better); during a partition only the side with a quorum stays writable — it chooses consistency over availability (CP)

question: What is Apache Kafka, and what is its 'dumb broker, smart consumer' design?

choices.1: A smart broker that routes each message and deletes it after delivery
choices.2: An in-memory cache that evicts messages under pressure
choices.3: A relational database optimized for event tables
choices.4: A distributed, append-only commit log built for high-throughput event streaming: it retains a durable, ordered log that any number of consumers can read and re-read at their own pace, with the broker just appending bytes and consumers tracking their own offset

question: What is a Kafka partition, and what ordering does it guarantee?

choices.1: A replica of the whole topic on another broker
choices.2: A time window after which records are deleted
choices.3: A consumer group sharing the work of a topic
choices.4: A subdivision of a topic that is an independent append-only log — the unit of ordering and parallelism; ordering is guaranteed within a partition (not across), and partitioning by key (hash(key) % partitions) routes a key's records to the same partition

question: How does Kafka achieve durable writes with the ISR?

choices.1: It writes only to the leader and never replicates
choices.2: It waits for every replica in the cluster, not just the ISR
choices.3: It acknowledges writes before any replica persists them
choices.4: Each partition is replicated with a leader and followers; the ISR (In-Sync Replica set) are replicas caught up to the leader, and with acks=all plus min.insync.replicas, a write is only acknowledged once it's on that many replicas — so RF=3 + min.insync.replicas=2 tolerates one broker down with zero data loss

question: How do Kafka consumer groups and offsets work?

choices.1: Offsets are shared across all groups, so one group's progress affects others
choices.2: A consumer group shares a topic's partitions (each partition consumed by exactly one consumer in the group), while different groups read the same topic independently (pub-sub) at their own pace; Kafka doesn't track reads — consumers commit offsets, which enables replay
choices.3: Every consumer in a group reads every partition
choices.4: Kafka tracks each consumer's read position centrally and deletes consumed records

question: What is the difference between Kafka's delete retention and log compaction?

choices.1: Compaction stores every version of every key forever
choices.2: Delete retention drops segments older than a time or beyond a size (a log with a retention window); log compaction instead retains the latest value per key forever (a changelog/snapshot of current state by key), used for CDC, Kafka Streams state stores, and __consumer_offsets
choices.3: Both delete all records after they are consumed
choices.4: Delete retention keeps the latest value per key; compaction deletes by age

question: Why is Kafka fast?

choices.1: Sequential disk appends (O(1), no fragmentation), the OS page cache serving hot log pages from RAM, zero-copy (sendfile) sending Disk -> Page Cache -> NIC with no user-space copy, batching + compression, partitioning for parallelism, and pull-based consumers giving natural backpressure
choices.2: It keeps the entire log in user-space memory and never touches disk
choices.3: It pushes every message individually with per-message acknowledgment
choices.4: It uses random-access writes optimized by an index

question: What is RabbitMQ, and how does its 'smart broker' model differ from Kafka?

choices.1: A relational database with message tables
choices.2: An in-memory key-value cache
choices.3: A traditional message broker built around smart routing: it actively routes each message to the right queue(s) and pushes to consumers, tracking per-message acknowledgements — a transient mailbox where a message is delivered, ACKed, and deleted, versus Kafka's durable replayable log
choices.4: A durable, replayable log where consumers track their own offsets

question: How do producers publish in RabbitMQ?

choices.1: Producers broadcast to every consumer simultaneously
choices.2: Producers write directly to a specific queue by name
choices.3: Producers always publish to an exchange (never to a queue directly) with a routing key, and bindings decide which queue(s) the message lands in — so producers don't need to know about queues
choices.4: Producers append to a partitioned log by key

question: What are RabbitMQ's four exchange types?

choices.1: Direct (route to queues whose binding key exactly matches the routing key), Topic (wildcard pattern match), Fanout (broadcast to all bound queues, ignoring the routing key), and Headers (route on message header attributes)
choices.2: Range, Hash, List, and Consistent
choices.3: Primary, Replica, Leader, and Follower
choices.4: Persistent, Transient, Durable, and Ephemeral

question: How does RabbitMQ handle a message when a consumer dies before acknowledging it?

choices.1: The broker blocks all other consumers until that one recovers
choices.2: The message is permanently deleted with no redelivery
choices.3: The message is replayed from a retained log by offset
choices.4: The broker pushes a message and waits for an ACK before deleting it; if the consumer dies before ACK (or sends a NACK), the message is requeued and redelivered to another consumer — giving at-least-once delivery

question: What does prefetch=1 (QoS) achieve with competing consumers?

choices.1: It sends all messages to one consumer for maximum throughput
choices.2: The broker sends one message and waits for its ACK before sending the next, ensuring fair dispatch so a slow consumer isn't flooded and work goes to whoever is free — the classic competing-consumers task queue, where multiple consumers on one queue each get a message exactly once
choices.3: It replays every message to every consumer
choices.4: It partitions the queue across consumers like Kafka

question: What is a Dead Letter Exchange (DLX) in RabbitMQ?

choices.1: A replica exchange used only during failover
choices.2: An exchange that permanently deletes failed messages
choices.3: Where a message goes when it is rejected/NACKed with requeue=false, expires (TTL), or exceeds the queue's max length — routed to a dead-letter queue for inspection/retry/alerting; a common pattern dead-letters to a retry queue with a TTL that dead-letters back to the main queue for delayed retries
choices.4: The main exchange every producer publishes to

question: What is Elasticsearch?

choices.1: A message broker for event streaming
choices.2: An in-memory key-value cache
choices.3: A relational database with full ACID transactions
choices.4: A distributed search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene whose core capability is full-text search (finding documents by relevance over unstructured text in milliseconds) plus fast aggregations — the 'E' in the ELK stack for log search and observability

question: What is an inverted index, the foundation of Elasticsearch?

choices.1: It stores rows sorted by primary key in a B-tree
choices.2: It is a cache of the most recent search results
choices.3: It maps each document to the list of words it contains
choices.4: It maps each word to the documents containing it (with positions/frequencies), so 'find all docs containing X' is a direct lookup rather than a scan — the opposite of mapping a document to its words

question: What does Elasticsearch's analysis step do, and why does it matter?

choices.1: It encrypts the text before storing it
choices.2: It compresses documents to save disk space
choices.3: Before indexing, text runs through an analyzer (tokenization plus filters like lowercasing, stemming 'running'->'run', stop-word removal, synonyms), and query text is analyzed the same way so they match — which is why ES matches meaning rather than exact substrings
choices.4: It sorts documents by their creation time

question: How does Elasticsearch rank matching documents?

choices.1: By the document's creation timestamp, newest first
choices.2: By the number of fields each document has
choices.3: By the alphabetical order of the document id
choices.4: By a relevance score (BM25 by default, a refined TF-IDF): rarer query terms and higher term frequency (length-normalized) score higher, so the best matches are returned first

question: How is an Elasticsearch index distributed, and what is fixed at creation?

choices.1: An index is split into primary shards (each a self-contained Lucene index) with replica shards on other nodes for HA and read scaling; the primary shard count is FIXED at index creation (too few caps scale, too many wastes overhead), and a coordinating node scatter-gathers a query across shards and merges/ranks results, near-real-time after a ~1s refresh
choices.2: The number of primary shards can be changed at any time with no cost
choices.3: Queries hit only one shard, never scatter-gathering
choices.4: Each index lives on a single node with no sharding

question: What is Elasticsearch's role relative to a primary database?

choices.1: It is a write-only log that never serves reads
choices.2: It replaces the primary database entirely with full ACID
choices.3: It is the authoritative transactional source of truth
choices.4: It is NOT a system of record — near-real-time, eventually consistent, with no multi-document ACID — so it should be treated as a derived index fed from a primary DB (often via CDC); if Elasticsearch and the DB disagree, the DB wins and you reindex

question: What is the Empty Base Optimisation (EBO)?

choices.1: A layout rule allowing an empty base class subobject to occupy zero bytes in a derived class
choices.2: A compiler pass that removes base classes with no virtual functions from the vtable
choices.3: An optimisation that merges identical empty classes into a single shared definition
choices.4: A linker optimisation that strips unused base class code from the final binary

question: Why must an empty class have sizeof >= 1 as a standalone or member object?

choices.1: Because the vtable pointer always requires at least one byte of storage
choices.2: Because malloc cannot return allocations of zero bytes
choices.3: Because distinct objects of the same type must have distinct addresses — zero-size objects would break array element identity and pointer arithmetic
choices.4: Because alignment requirements round every object size up to at least one byte
choices.5: Because the standard requires every class to have at least one implicit member

question: Why is a base subobject allowed to occupy zero bytes when a member of the same empty type is not?

choices.1: Base subobjects are constructed before members, so their storage can be reclaimed afterwards
choices.2: Members must be addressable via offsetof, but base classes are exempt from offsetof
choices.3: The compiler inlines base class data into the derived class only when it is empty
choices.4: A base subobject is not a complete object, so it is exempt from the distinct-address rule as long as no two subobjects of the same type share an address

question: Given `struct Empty {};`, `struct WithMember { Empty e; int x; };` and `struct WithBase : Empty { int x; };`, what are the typical sizes on a 64-bit system?

choices.1: WithMember is 5 bytes, WithBase is 4 bytes
choices.2: Both are 8 bytes because the empty type always contributes padding
choices.3: WithMember is 8 bytes (1 byte for e plus 3 padding plus 4 for x); WithBase is 4 bytes because EBO gives the base zero bytes
choices.4: Both are 4 bytes because empty classes never take storage
choices.5: WithMember is 8 bytes, WithBase is 5 bytes rounded up to 8 by alignment

question: How do library implementations exploit EBO so that sizeof(std::unique_ptr<T>) == sizeof(T*) for a stateless deleter?

choices.1: They store the deleter in a global registry indexed by the pointer value
choices.2: They specialise unique_ptr to drop the deleter entirely when it is trivially destructible
choices.3: They allocate the deleter lazily on the heap only when reset() is called
choices.4: They use the compressed pair idiom: privately inherit from the potentially-empty deleter type instead of storing it as a member

question: In which of these situations does EBO fail to make the empty base free?

choices.1: The derived class has a member whose type is the same as the empty base, forcing the two same-type subobjects to distinct addresses
choices.2: The derived class declares a virtual destructor
choices.3: The empty base declares a static data member
choices.4: The derived class is used as an element type in a std::vector
choices.5: The empty base has a user-declared default constructor

question: Is a compiler required to perform EBO?

choices.1: Yes, it has been mandatory for all class types since C++11
choices.2: No, and in practice most ABIs skip it because it complicates debugging
choices.3: It is optional in general, but mandatory for standard-layout classes, which must place empty bases at offset 0
choices.4: It is only permitted when the base is declared final
choices.5: It is required only when the derived class is trivially copyable

question: What advantage does C++20's `[[no_unique_address]]` attribute have over classic inheritance-based EBO?

choices.1: It guarantees the annotated member is zero bytes on every compiler, including MSVC by default
choices.2: It works on members of any object type — including final classes and lambdas — without restructuring the class to inherit from the policy type
choices.3: It also compresses non-empty members by packing them into padding bits
choices.4: It makes the member immune to the same-type-collision rule that limits EBO

question: What is the MSVC-specific caveat around empty base optimisation?

choices.1: MSVC never performs EBO because its ABI predates the C++98 standard
choices.2: MSVC applies EBO only to classes compiled with /std:c++20 or later
choices.3: MSVC applies EBO to virtual bases only, not to non-virtual ones
choices.4: MSVC does not apply EBO to multiple empty bases by default for ABI-compatibility reasons; it needs __declspec(empty_bases), and plain [[no_unique_address]] is ignored without the [[msvc::no_unique_address]] spelling

question: What is the core mechanism of a seqlock?

choices.1: Readers take a shared lock and writers take an exclusive lock on the sequence word
choices.2: A sequence counter is odd during writes and even when stable; readers retry if the counter was odd or changed across their read
choices.3: Writers copy the data, publish a new pointer, and defer freeing the old version
choices.4: Readers and writers alternate access using a ticket-based fairness counter

question: How does a seqlock invert the priorities of a reader-writer lock?

choices.1: It gives readers exclusive access and makes writers speculative
choices.2: It blocks both sides but for shorter critical sections
choices.3: It makes the writer wait-free and readers speculative — readers detect torn reads and retry instead of blocking the writer
choices.4: It lets writers proceed in parallel while readers serialise on a mutex

question: Why do seqlock readers avoid cache-line ping-pong between cores?

choices.1: The payload is replicated per core so readers touch local copies
choices.2: Readers batch their accesses to amortise coherency traffic
choices.3: The sequence counter is stored in non-cacheable memory
choices.4: Readers perform no writes to shared cache lines at all — no reader count, no lock word — so lines stay in shared state across reader cores

question: What is the formal problem with a straightforward C++ seqlock implementation, and a standard mitigation?

choices.1: The payload reads race with payload writes — formally UB even though torn values are discarded; mitigate with relaxed atomic members or byte-wise atomic memcpy
choices.2: The sequence counter can overflow, which is UB for unsigned types; mitigate with a 128-bit counter
choices.3: The release fence is not allowed before a relaxed store; mitigate by making every store seq_cst
choices.4: Readers may deadlock with the writer; mitigate with a back-off protocol

question: For which data is a seqlock the right choice?

choices.1: A large linked structure traversed in place by readers
choices.2: Small, trivially copyable, read-mostly values — like a market-data snapshot shared with strategy processes
choices.3: A write-heavy queue with many producers
choices.4: Any data structure that must guarantee wait-free reads

question: What forward-progress guarantees does a seqlock give?

choices.1: Both readers and the writer are wait-free
choices.2: Readers are wait-free; the writer may retry indefinitely
choices.3: The writer is wait-free; readers are only lock-free and can starve under a high write rate
choices.4: Neither side has any progress guarantee; it is a blocking algorithm

question: Why is simply CASing the tail forward insufficient for a multi-producer queue?

choices.1: CAS is too slow to keep up with multiple producers
choices.2: A consumer can observe the advanced tail before the producer has written the element — claiming a slot and publishing its contents are separate steps
choices.3: CAS cannot operate on 64-bit counters on most platforms
choices.4: The tail pointer would suffer ABA problems that CAS cannot detect

question: In Vyukov's bounded MPMC queue, what role do the per-cell sequence numbers play?

choices.1: They provide a global total order of all enqueue operations for linearizability proofs
choices.2: They detect queue-full conditions without reading the head pointer
choices.3: They encode each cell's state per lap — a producer publishes by storing seq = pos + 1, and a consumer knows the slot's data is ready only when seq matches; this also avoids ABA via lap counting
choices.4: They act as ticket locks so only one thread can touch a cell at a time

question: Why is Vyukov's MPMC queue not lock-free in the strict sense?

choices.1: It uses a spinlock to protect the position counters
choices.2: Its CAS loop can theoretically livelock, which disqualifies lock-freedom
choices.3: Consumers block on an OS futex when the queue is empty
choices.4: A producer suspended between claiming a slot and publishing it stalls the consumer waiting on that specific slot

question: What makes Vyukov's intrusive MPSC push wait-free for producers?

choices.1: Producers perform one unconditional atomic exchange on the head — no CAS retry loop
choices.2: Producers write to thread-local buffers that the consumer merges
choices.3: A lock-free allocator guarantees bounded allocation time
choices.4: The consumer yields its timeslice whenever a producer is active

question: Why do HFT systems often prefer N per-producer SPSC rings over one MPMC queue?

choices.1: SPSC rings can hold larger elements than MPMC queues
choices.2: It eliminates the shared claim counter whose cache line ping-pongs between producers, at the cost of polling N rings and losing global FIFO order
choices.3: MPMC queues cannot be placed in shared memory
choices.4: SPSC rings provide stronger ordering guarantees across producers

question: Why do bounded array-based queues dominate in low-latency code over unbounded linked designs?

choices.1: Linked lists cannot be made thread-safe at all
choices.2: Arrays allow priority ordering of messages
choices.3: Bounded arrays sidestep safe memory reclamation (hazard pointers/epochs) that unbounded node-based designs require, and avoid allocation on the hot path
choices.4: Unbounded queues violate the C++ memory model

question: How do Disruptor consumers "remove" events from the ring?

choices.1: They CAS the event slot to a tombstone value
choices.2: They don't — each consumer just advances its own sequence counter; entries are only overwritten once all gating sequences have passed them
choices.3: They move events into a private dequeue buffer
choices.4: The producer removes events after the slowest consumer acknowledges via callback

question: How does the Disruptor get batching "for free"?

choices.1: The producer accumulates events and publishes them in fixed-size groups
choices.2: Events are compressed together before being written to the ring
choices.3: A background thread coalesces adjacent events between polls
choices.4: A consumer that wakes to find the cursor N ahead processes all N events in one sweep, amortising one fence over the batch with prefetch-friendly sequential access

question: How are consumer dependency graphs (e.g. journal before business logic) expressed in the Disruptor?

choices.1: A dependent consumer gates on the upstream consumer's sequence instead of the producer cursor
choices.2: Each stage gets its own ring buffer connected by handoff queues
choices.3: Consumers acquire stage-ordered locks before processing
choices.4: The producer writes each event once per stage in dependency order

question: Why does the Disruptor pre-allocate all event objects up front?

choices.1: To guarantee events are laid out in priority order
choices.2: Because the JVM cannot allocate inside callbacks
choices.3: So publishing mutates a recycled slot rather than allocating — the hot path touches only the ring and counters
choices.4: To allow events to be shared between multiple ring buffers

question: What trade-off does choosing a busy-spin wait strategy make?

choices.1: Lowest latency in exchange for burning 100% of a core — appropriate for dedicated pinned cores
choices.2: Higher throughput in exchange for unordered delivery
choices.3: Lower memory usage in exchange for occasional missed events
choices.4: Fairness between consumers in exchange for higher median latency

question: When is the Disruptor's shape preferable to a set of point-to-point queues?

choices.1: When events must be load-balanced so each consumer sees a disjoint subset
choices.2: When producers vastly outnumber consumers
choices.3: When events must be persisted to disk before processing
choices.4: When every consumer must see every event (multicast) or stages form a pipeline — one ring serves all consumers with per-consumer progress, where queues would need one copy per consumer

question: What is the fundamental problem RCU's grace period solves?

choices.1: Preventing two writers from publishing conflicting versions
choices.2: Knowing when no reader can still be dereferencing the old version, so it can be safely freed
choices.3: Ensuring readers see updates in the order they were published
choices.4: Bounding the memory used by concurrent readers

question: What does an RCU writer do to update a structure?

choices.1: Locks out readers, updates in place, then unlocks
choices.2: Increments a sequence counter, writes in place, increments again
choices.3: Copies the structure, applies the change, publishes the new version with one atomic pointer store, then defers freeing the old version until a grace period elapses
choices.4: Sends the update to a dedicated mutation thread that owns the data

question: In epoch-based reclamation, why do three epochs suffice?

choices.1: Three epochs allow two writers and one reader to proceed concurrently
choices.2: The epoch counter must be a multiple of the cache line size
choices.3: Two epochs are for reads and one is reserved for writes
choices.4: A reader is always within one epoch of the global value, so two full advances strictly separate threads that could have seen a retired object from those that cannot have

question: What is the key trade-off between EBR/RCU and hazard pointers?

choices.1: EBR reads are nearly free but a stalled reader blocks all reclamation (unbounded garbage); hazard pointers bound garbage but cost a store+fence per protected pointer
choices.2: Hazard pointers are faster to read but use unbounded memory; EBR bounds memory at the cost of locking
choices.3: EBR only works in the kernel while hazard pointers only work in userspace
choices.4: Hazard pointers protect whole regions while EBR protects individual objects

question: Which workload is the sweet spot for RCU?

choices.1: Write-heavy queues with many producers
choices.2: Read-mostly hot-path state like symbol tables or risk limits — read millions of times a second, updated a few times a minute
choices.3: Large structures where every field changes on each update
choices.4: Data shared between exactly two threads in a pipeline

question: What obligation does RCU place on readers?

choices.1: They must acquire a shared lock before each traversal
choices.2: They must copy the entire structure before reading it
choices.3: They must retry their read if a version counter changes
choices.4: They must not block or sleep inside the read-side critical region (or must announce quiescence promptly in QSBR), or they stall reclamation for everyone

question: Why is shared memory the fastest IPC channel in steady state?

choices.1: The kernel prioritises shared-memory pages in the scheduler
choices.2: It compresses data between processes automatically
choices.3: Data moves via plain loads and stores with no syscalls and no copies — the kernel is out of the data path entirely
choices.4: It uses DMA hardware to move data between address spaces

question: Why must you store offsets rather than pointers inside a shared memory region?

choices.1: Pointers are larger than offsets and waste shared memory
choices.2: Each process may map the region at a different virtual address (ASLR), so raw pointers into the region don't translate across processes
choices.3: The kernel rewrites pointers during page migration
choices.4: Atomic operations are only defined on integer offsets

question: When is std::atomic<T> safe to use between processes in shared memory?

choices.1: Always — atomics are process-agnostic by definition
choices.2: Only when protected by a process-shared mutex
choices.3: Only for types smaller than the cache line size
choices.4: When the type is lock-free: lock-free atomics are required to be address-free, operating on the bytes themselves, whereas a non-lock-free atomic's hidden mutex is per-process

question: What do robust mutexes (PTHREAD_MUTEX_ROBUST) add for cross-process use?

choices.1: If the holding process dies, the next locker gets EOWNERDEAD instead of deadlocking forever
choices.2: They automatically retry the lock with exponential backoff
choices.3: They guarantee FIFO fairness between processes
choices.4: They encrypt the mutex state against tampering by other processes

question: Why do trading systems back shared memory regions with hugepages and pre-fault them?

choices.1: Hugepages are the only page size that supports MAP_SHARED
choices.2: Hugepages make shm_unlink cleanup automatic on crash
choices.3: Fewer TLB misses on large regions, and touching every page up front means the hot path never takes a page fault
choices.4: The kernel refuses to swap hugepages to disk, unlike mlock'd small pages

question: Why do HFT systems run feed handler and strategies as separate processes over shm rather than threads in one process?

choices.1: Threads cannot busy-poll shared data structures
choices.2: Fault isolation and deployment isolation: a strategy crash doesn't take down the feed handler, and processes can be restarted or upgraded independently — while decode still happens once, shared zero-copy
choices.3: The scheduler gives processes strictly higher priority than threads
choices.4: Shared memory between processes is faster than shared memory between threads

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: What is the difference between IOC and FOK time-in-force?

choices.1: IOC rests in the book briefly; FOK never enters the book
choices.2: IOC is for market orders only; FOK is for limit orders only
choices.3: IOC fills whatever is fillable immediately (partials OK) and cancels the rest; FOK fills entirely immediately or cancels entirely — neither ever rests
choices.4: IOC cancels at end of day; FOK cancels after the first partial fill

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: What does a post-only order guarantee, and why do makers use it?

choices.1: It guarantees execution at the posted price within one second
choices.2: It is rejected or repriced if it would cross the spread, guaranteeing you add liquidity — so a quote sent as the market moves can't accidentally take and pay taker fees
choices.3: It guarantees the order is displayed at the front of the queue
choices.4: It prevents the order from being matched against hidden liquidity

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: How does an iceberg order behave when its visible tip is filled?

choices.1: The entire hidden reserve becomes visible at once
choices.2: The order converts to a market order for the remainder
choices.3: The hidden reserve cancels automatically
choices.4: The tip is replenished from hidden reserve, and the new tip goes to the back of the queue at that price

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: Under price-time priority, which amendment typically preserves queue position?

choices.1: Decreasing the order's size only
choices.2: Increasing the order's size only
choices.3: Improving the price by one tick
choices.4: Any cancel/replace, since the order id is preserved

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: Why is HFT order flow dominated by IOC and post-only orders?

choices.1: Exchanges charge lower fees for these two types than for plain limit orders
choices.2: They are the only types binary protocols like OUCH support
choices.3: IOC takes without leaving resting risk in the book, and post-only makes passive quoting safe from accidental crossing — covering both sides of the take/make split
choices.4: Regulators require automated traders to use immediate or passive-only orders

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Types & Time In Force.md
question: What is a stop-limit order?

choices.1: A limit order that converts to a market order at a size threshold
choices.2: Inactive until the last trade touches the stop price, then it becomes a limit order — bounded price, but it may not fill
choices.3: A limit order that cancels when the price moves away by a stop distance
choices.4: Two linked orders where filling one cancels the other

question: What is adverse selection for a market maker?

choices.1: The exchange selecting which maker's quote to fill randomly rather than by priority
choices.2: Regulators auditing a disproportionate share of maker trades
choices.3: Losing queue position after amending an order's price
choices.4: The counterparties who choose to hit your quotes are disproportionately informed or faster — your fills skew toward the trades you least want

question: How does a market maker typically respond to accumulating a long inventory position?

choices.1: Skew both quotes downward (or widen/hedge) to sell more and buy less
choices.2: Cancel all quotes until the position decays
choices.3: Raise both quotes to increase the value of the held inventory
choices.4: Switch from making to taking on the same side

question: Why does queue position matter so much in wide-tick instruments?

choices.1: Exchanges pay larger rebates to earlier queue positions
choices.2: When the spread is pinned at one tick, price competition is impossible — being early at the best price collects the uninformed flow, while joining a long queue collects mostly what everyone else declined
choices.3: Wide-tick instruments use pro-rata allocation, which favours early orders
choices.4: Queue position determines which participant receives market data first

question: Why do market makers invest heavily in latency even when not doing latency arbitrage?

choices.1: Exchanges route fills preferentially to the fastest members
choices.2: Latency determines the maker rebate tier
choices.3: Defensively: being fast to cancel your own stale quotes is protection against being sniped when news or correlated instruments move
choices.4: Slower firms pay higher clearing fees per trade

question: How is the price of an opening or closing auction determined?

choices.1: The last trade price before the auction begins
choices.2: The midpoint of the final bid-ask spread
choices.3: A volume-weighted average of all orders submitted
choices.4: A single uncross price that maximises executable volume across the batched orders

question: Decompose a market maker's realised PnL as in the note.

choices.1: Spread + rebate capture, minus adverse-selection losses, plus/minus inventory mark-to-market
choices.2: Volume times spread, minus exchange fees, minus infrastructure cost
choices.3: Taker fees collected minus maker rebates paid, plus hedging gains
choices.4: Gross trading profit minus the cost of cancelled orders

question: What does the sequencer pattern buy a modern exchange architecture?

choices.1: Parallel matching across symbols without coordination
choices.2: Every input gets a global sequence number, so downstream components are deterministic state machines replaying one stream — giving replayability, audit, fairness, and trivial hot-standby failover
choices.3: Encryption of order flow between gateway and matching engine
choices.4: Automatic load balancing of orders across matching engines

question: Why must a matching engine avoid clocks, randomness, and I/O in its hot path?

choices.1: They are too slow to fit the latency budget
choices.2: Regulators prohibit non-deterministic components at venues
choices.3: Determinism is the design contract: the engine must be a pure function of (book state, next sequenced message) so replay and standby failover reproduce identical state
choices.4: Clock reads require kernel mode, which the engine cannot enter

question: Which of these is a standard pre-trade risk check?

choices.1: Post-trade settlement netting
choices.2: End-of-day position reconciliation
choices.3: Backtesting the strategy that generated the order
choices.4: A price collar rejecting limits too far from the reference price (fat-finger protection)

question: What is cancel-on-disconnect and why is it essential for market makers?

choices.1: On session drop the venue mass-cancels your resting orders — a crashed maker must not leave stale quotes in the book to be picked off
choices.2: A client-side timer that cancels orders older than a threshold
choices.3: The venue cancelling both sides of a self-trade
choices.4: Automatic cancellation of orders when a symbol is halted

question: Why can't 15c3-5 pre-trade risk checks be skipped for latency, and what follows for engineering?

choices.1: They are cryptographically enforced by the exchange gateway hardware
choices.2: The market access rule makes them mandatory and non-bypassable, so the engineering problem is making them fast — in-memory limits, lock-free counters, per-symbol tables — not optional
choices.3: Skipping them voids the firm's colocation contract
choices.4: They run asynchronously after the order is sent, so they add no latency anyway

question: How do exchange matching engines achieve hot-standby failover?

choices.1: Synchronous state snapshots copied to the standby every millisecond
choices.2: Distributed consensus (Raft) on every book mutation
choices.3: A primary and standby replay the same sequenced input stream, so the standby deterministically holds identical state and can take over — consensus on input order replaces consensus on state
choices.4: The standby rebuilds state from the public market data feed on takeover

question: What are ITCH and OUCH respectively?

choices.1: Nasdaq's outbound order-level market data feed (ITCH) and its inbound binary order entry protocol (OUCH)
choices.2: Two versions of the FIX session layer
choices.3: An order entry protocol (ITCH) and a market data protocol (OUCH)
choices.4: The multicast transport (ITCH) and TCP transport (OUCH) used by all US venues

question: Why is handling an ITCH feed so much cheaper than parsing FIX?

choices.1: ITCH messages are compressed with a hardware-accelerated codec
choices.2: ITCH sends only top-of-book changes while FIX sends full snapshots
choices.3: ITCH runs over UDP, which has lower per-byte cost than TCP
choices.4: ITCH messages are fixed-size, fixed-offset binary structs — decoding is a type byte plus loads at known offsets, versus FIX's ASCII tag=value scanning, splitting and atoi per field

question: How does a subscriber build the order book from ITCH?

choices.1: By requesting book snapshots at a fixed interval
choices.2: By replaying every order-level event (add, execute, cancel, delete, replace) — the feed is the input log and your book is the derived state, reproducible exactly on replay
choices.3: By aggregating the per-level depth updates the feed provides
choices.4: By subscribing only to trade messages and inferring the book statistically

question: What does MoldUDP64 add on top of raw UDP multicast for ITCH?

choices.1: Encryption and authentication of the market data stream
choices.2: Forward error correction so lost packets are reconstructed
choices.3: Session id and sequence numbers for gap detection, with recovery via A/B feed arbitration, a unicast re-request server, or snapshot channels
choices.4: Congestion control that slows the feed when subscribers fall behind

question: Why does an order-level (L3) feed let you estimate your queue position?

choices.1: The exchange includes your queue rank in every acknowledgement
choices.2: L3 feeds are delivered in queue-priority order rather than time order
choices.3: Queue position can be computed from the aggregated depth alone
choices.4: You see every individual add and cancel at your price level, so you can count the resting quantity that arrived before your own order

question: Where does FIX remain the right tool despite binary protocols owning the fast path?

choices.1: Broker order routing, allocations and back-office flows, where interoperability and flexibility beat parse speed
choices.2: Market data distribution to colocated subscribers
choices.3: Intra-exchange communication between gateway and matching engine
choices.4: Nowhere — FIX is fully deprecated at modern venues

question: Why shouldn't order prices be stored in a double?

choices.1: Doubles are slower than integers on modern CPUs by an order of magnitude
choices.2: Doubles cannot represent values above 2^53 at all
choices.3: Most decimal fractions like 0.1 have no exact binary representation — literals are already wrong, sums drift, and equality comparisons break
choices.4: The C++ standard forbids doubles as map keys

question: What is the fixed-point representation of a price?

choices.1: A pair of floats holding the integer and fractional parts
choices.2: An integer count of a fixed smallest unit — e.g. $123.45 stored as int64 1234500 at scale 1e-4 — so all arithmetic is exact integer arithmetic
choices.3: A decimal string parsed on demand
choices.4: An IEEE 754 decimal128 value

question: What must you handle when multiplying two fixed-point values?

choices.1: Nothing — integer multiplication is closed under the scale
choices.2: Only the sign, since scales cancel automatically
choices.3: Converting both operands to double first to avoid truncation
choices.4: The scales multiply ((a·10^4)·(b·10^4) = ab·10^8): renormalise by dividing by the scale with explicit rounding, using a wider intermediate like __int128 against overflow

question: Why does a fixed-point internal representation mean zero conversion on the hot path?

choices.1: Exchange binary protocols already send prices as scaled integers (e.g. ITCH price(4)), so wire format and internal format coincide
choices.2: The compiler constant-folds all scale conversions
choices.3: Fixed-point values are cached in registers across messages
choices.4: The NIC converts prices to host format in hardware

question: Why wrap the raw int64 price in a strong type?

choices.1: Strong types are required for atomic operations on prices
choices.2: It enables the empty base optimisation on price-holding classes
choices.3: So prices, quantities and notionals can't silently mix — scale mismatches become compile errors instead of silent ×10^4 bugs
choices.4: It makes the value immutable after construction

question: Where do doubles remain appropriate in a trading system?

choices.1: Position and PnL accounting, since errors average out
choices.2: Signals, statistics and model math where tiny relative error is irrelevant — exact at the edges (orders, money), approximate in the middle
choices.3: Order prices, provided they are rounded before sending
choices.4: Nowhere — all numeric code must be integer-based

question: What is catastrophic cancellation?

choices.1: Overflow when two large values are added
choices.2: The compiler cancelling terms algebraically under -ffast-math
choices.3: Loss of precision when a result underflows to a subnormal
choices.4: Subtracting nearly equal numbers cancels their leading digits, leaving previously-negligible rounding error as the answer's leading digits

question: Why is variance via E[X²] − E[X]² numerically terrible, and what replaces it?

choices.1: It subtracts two huge nearly-equal terms, destroying significant digits (even yielding negative variance); Welford's single-pass algorithm computes deviations from a running mean instead
choices.2: It requires two passes over the data; replace with a one-pass sum of squares
choices.3: It overflows for large n; replace with pairwise summation
choices.4: Division by n loses precision; replace with multiplication by 1/n

question: How does Kahan summation keep error O(ε) independent of n?

choices.1: It sorts the inputs by magnitude before summing
choices.2: It accumulates in extended 80-bit precision
choices.3: A compensation variable recovers exactly what each addition's rounding lost — ((t − sum) − y) — and feeds it back into the next term, at ~2× the flops
choices.4: It splits each value into high and low halves and sums them separately

question: Why does -ffast-math break Kahan summation?

choices.1: It switches to single precision internally
choices.2: It licenses the compiler to assume floating-point associativity, so it algebraically simplifies the compensation term ((t − sum) − y) to zero and deletes the correction entirely
choices.3: It disables the FPU's rounding modes
choices.4: It vectorises the loop, which reorders the additions randomly

question: What is the robust way to compare two computed doubles for near-equality?

choices.1: Absolute epsilon alone: |a − b| < 1e-9
choices.2: Relative epsilon alone: |a − b| < rel · |a|
choices.3: Exact equality after rounding both to 12 decimal places
choices.4: Combined tolerance — |a − b| ≤ max(abs_eps, rel · max(|a|, |b|)) — or a ULP-distance bound; absolute alone fails for big numbers, relative alone fails near zero

question: Why do parallel reductions give run-to-run different results, and what fixes it when reproducibility matters?

choices.1: FP addition is not associative, so different reduction orders round differently; fix the reduction order, control FMA contraction, or accumulate in integers
choices.2: Threads race on the accumulator; fix with a mutex around each addition
choices.3: The OS scheduler perturbs the FPU state between threads; pin threads to cores
choices.4: Denormals flush differently per core; disable flush-to-zero

question: Why is std::unordered_map slow by specification rather than by implementation?

choices.1: The standard requires cryptographic hashing of all keys
choices.2: The standard mandates stable element addresses and bucket iteration, forcing node-based chaining — one allocation per element and a pointer chase per lookup
choices.3: The standard caps its load factor at 0.5, wasting memory
choices.4: The standard requires locking for thread safety on every access

question: Why can't open-addressing deletion simply mark a slot empty?

choices.1: The slot's destructor cannot run while probes are in flight
choices.2: Empty slots would be reused out of insertion order
choices.3: It would break probe chains — a later element that probed past this slot would become unreachable; hence tombstones or backward-shift deletion
choices.4: The load factor accounting would drift and prevent resizing

question: What is the Robin Hood insertion rule and its main effect?

choices.1: New elements always displace the resident so recent keys are fastest
choices.2: Elements are stolen from overfull buckets and moved to underfull ones during rehash
choices.3: The hash function is re-seeded whenever a probe exceeds a threshold
choices.4: On collision the element further from its home slot keeps the slot ('the poorer wins'); probe-length variance collapses, enabling early termination and high load factors

question: How does SwissTable (absl::flat_hash_map) use SIMD in lookups?

choices.1: A 7-bit H2 hash fragment is stored in a parallel control byte array; one SSE2 compare against a 16-byte group filters 16 slots per instruction, touching only metadata before any key comparison
choices.2: Keys are compared 16 at a time with vectorised memcmp
choices.3: The hash function itself is computed with AVX-512 for all candidate keys
choices.4: SIMD gathers prefetch all 16 candidate values in parallel

question: What is the key behavioural difference of flat maps that breaks naive migrations from std::unordered_map?

choices.1: Flat maps cannot store move-only value types
choices.2: Rehash invalidates all pointers and iterators to elements — code holding a V* across inserts breaks; use the node variant or map to unique_ptr when addresses must be stable
choices.3: Flat maps iterate in key order rather than insertion order
choices.4: Erase is O(n) in flat maps, so erase-heavy code times out

question: Why does hash quality matter more for linear probing than for chaining?

choices.1: Linear probing hashes each key twice, doubling any bias
choices.2: Chaining rehashes automatically while linear probing cannot
choices.3: Weak hashes (e.g. identity on sequential ids) create primary clustering — runs of full slots that merge and explode probe lengths, whereas chaining just gets slightly longer chains
choices.4: Linear probing requires cryptographic hashes for correctness

question: What distinguishes an intrusive container from std::list<T>?

choices.1: The linkage hooks live inside the element itself; the container links existing objects without allocating nodes or copying values
choices.2: It stores elements contiguously like a vector
choices.3: It performs reference counting on its elements
choices.4: It is lock-free by construction

question: Why are intrusive containers the canonical order-book representation?

choices.1: They sort orders by price automatically
choices.2: They compress orders to fit more levels in cache
choices.3: They allow lock-free concurrent matching across threads
choices.4: One pool-allocated order can sit in a price level's FIFO and be found via an id map simultaneously, giving O(1) allocation-free cancels and matches

question: How does cancel-by-id work in O(1) with an intrusive price-level FIFO?

choices.1: Each level keeps a secondary hash from id to queue index
choices.2: Hash lookup gives the Order*, and the object's own embedded hooks let it unlink from its level's list directly — no walking, no stored iterators
choices.3: The FIFO is actually a ring buffer indexed by order id
choices.4: Cancels are deferred and batched at the end of each matching cycle

question: What lifetime responsibility do intrusive containers impose?

choices.1: Elements must be heap-allocated with new
choices.2: The container frees elements only when explicitly told to
choices.3: The container doesn't own elements — you must keep objects alive while linked and unlink before destroying them (or use auto_unlink hooks), else dangling links are UB
choices.4: Elements must be trivially destructible

question: What is the cost of an object belonging to several intrusive containers at once?

choices.1: Each membership requires its own hook decided at compile time — N containers means N sets of hook pointers embedded in the object
choices.2: Each additional container doubles insertion time
choices.3: The object must be copied once per container
choices.4: Only two simultaneous memberships are supported by boost::intrusive

question: What does combining intrusive containers with a pre-allocated object pool achieve?

choices.1: Automatic defragmentation of the pool as objects unlink
choices.2: Thread-safe insertion without atomics
choices.3: Stable iteration order across container types
choices.4: Zero allocations on the hot path — objects come from the pool and linking/unlinking never allocates — plus the pool controls memory layout for locality

question: What is the safe and fast idiom for reading a big-endian u32 at an arbitrary buffer offset?

choices.1: reinterpret_cast the buffer pointer to const uint32_t* and dereference
choices.2: memcpy into a local uint32_t (compiles to a single load) then std::byteswap — avoiding the alignment and strict-aliasing UB of pointer casting
choices.3: Read the four bytes individually and shift-or them together, which compilers cannot optimise
choices.4: Use a packed struct overlay and take a reference to the member

question: Why do fixed-offset binary formats beat flexible formats on hot paths?

choices.1: They compress better on the wire
choices.2: They are easier to version than schemaful formats
choices.3: The fastest parse is no parse: fields are read with a handful of loads at known offsets — no scanning, no branches on delimiters, no allocation
choices.4: They are the only formats NICs can checksum in hardware

question: Why are protobuf varints a poor fit for latency-critical decoding?

choices.1: They inflate small integers to five bytes
choices.2: They require two passes over the message
choices.3: They are patented and require licensed decoders
choices.4: Decoding is a data-dependent loop — the length isn't known until you read each byte — which branch-mispredicts, unlike fixed-width fields at fixed offsets

question: How does SBE (Simple Binary Encoding) handle schema evolution?

choices.1: Append-only fields plus a schema version field — old readers simply ignore the appended tail
choices.2: Self-describing tags on every field like FIX
choices.3: A negotiation handshake that downgrades both sides to the older schema
choices.4: It doesn't — any schema change requires simultaneous upgrades

question: Why do binary protocols over TCP add a length prefix to each message?

choices.1: TCP requires application-level framing hints for Nagle's algorithm
choices.2: TCP is a byte stream with no message boundaries — a length prefix lets the reader split messages without scanning, handling frames that arrive split across packets
choices.3: The length field doubles as a checksum
choices.4: Routers use the prefix to prioritise small control messages

question: Why must a wire format be specified explicitly rather than inferred from a C++ struct's layout?

choices.1: C++ structs cannot contain fixed-width integer types
choices.2: Struct layout is always randomised for security
choices.3: The compiler may pad and align members differently across platforms/compilers — the wire format is an ABI and endianness, offsets and padding must be pinned down exactly
choices.4: The standard forbids memcpy from structs with private members

question: How is a binary heap stored and where are node i's children?

choices.1: As a linked binary tree with parent pointers; children are dereferenced
choices.2: As a sorted array; children are at i-1 and i+1
choices.3: As a hash table keyed by priority
choices.4: Implicitly in an array as a complete tree — children of i at 2i+1 and 2i+2, so navigation is arithmetic, not pointer chasing

question: Why is building a heap from n elements O(n) rather than O(n log n)?

choices.1: Sifting down from the last internal node to the root: most nodes are near the leaves and sift only a little, and the total work telescopes to O(n)
choices.2: Because comparisons are constant-time, unlike sorting
choices.3: It isn't — build-heap is Θ(n log n) in the worst case
choices.4: The array is already a valid heap half the time on random input

question: Which is a real limitation of std::priority_queue?

choices.1: It cannot hold move-only types
choices.2: Its top() operation is O(log n)
choices.3: No decrease-key and no element handles — Dijkstra either pushes duplicates and skips stale pops, or needs an indexed heap; equal priorities also pop in unspecified order
choices.4: It heap-allocates a node per push

question: How do you get FIFO tie-breaking for equal priorities with std::priority_queue?

choices.1: Enable the stable flag in the comparator
choices.2: Push (priority, monotonically increasing sequence number) pairs so ties break on insertion order
choices.3: Use std::stable_sort on the underlying container after each push
choices.4: Switch the underlying container from vector to deque

question: Why do 4-ary heaps often beat binary heaps in practice, and Fibonacci heaps lose despite better asymptotics?

choices.1: 4-ary heaps have O(1) push; Fibonacci heaps have O(n) top
choices.2: 4-ary heaps avoid the comparator entirely on half the levels
choices.3: Fibonacci heaps are not available in the standard library
choices.4: A shallower tree with children sharing cache lines means fewer cache misses per sift; Fibonacci's O(1) decrease-key drowns in constants and pointer-chasing locality costs

question: Why is a heap the wrong structure for an order book?

choices.1: A book needs ordered iteration over price levels, FIFO within a level, and O(1) cancel by id — a heap exposes only the extreme element and gives none of these cheaply
choices.2: Heaps cannot store prices as integer keys
choices.3: Heap memory cannot be shared between processes
choices.4: Heaps are too slow for the required O(log n) inserts

question: In SwissTable, how is the 64-bit hash split and what does each part do?

choices.1: Two independent 32-bit hashes are computed; one for the group, one for collision resolution
choices.2: H1 (top 57 bits) selects WHERE to look — the starting 16-slot group via H1 % num_groups — while H2 (low 7 bits) is the fingerprint stored in the control bytes saying WHAT to expect there
choices.3: The low 32 bits index the slot array directly; the high 32 bits are stored as a checksum
choices.4: H1 (low 7 bits) picks the slot within a group; H2 (top 57 bits) picks the group

question: How does a SwissTable lookup know it can stop probing, and why don't tombstones stop it?

choices.1: It stops after a fixed maximum of four groups, the bound tombstones are designed to maintain
choices.2: It stops when the probe returns to its starting group after wrapping the table
choices.3: Finding any EMPTY control byte in the group proves the key was never displaced past that point; DELETED bytes mark slots that were occupied at insertion time, so the sought key may have probed past them — which is exactly the chain information tombstones preserve
choices.4: It stops when the H2 match mask is zero for the current group, since the key would have matched there

question: What is the copy-and-swap implementation of assignment?

choices.1: operator=(T other) takes its parameter BY VALUE (the copy/move constructor does the copying), swaps *this with the copy, and the copy's destructor releases the old state
choices.2: operator=(const T& rhs) locks both objects, copies field by field, then unlocks
choices.3: operator=(T&& rhs) moves from rhs and leaves it empty
choices.4: A free function assign(T&, const T&) that clears the target then rebuilds it from the source

question: Why does copy-and-swap provide the strong exception guarantee where naive assignment fails?

choices.1: The swap operation catches and re-throws any exceptions from the copy
choices.2: It performs the copy inside a try block and rolls back on failure
choices.3: The compiler enforces noexcept on the whole operator when the parameter is by value
choices.4: All fallible work (allocation) happens while constructing the by-value parameter, BEFORE *this is touched; naive assignment deletes the old state first, so a throwing allocation leaves *this dangling

question: How does copy-and-swap handle self-assignment (x = x)?

choices.1: It requires the usual this == &rhs guard at the top of the operator
choices.2: Correctly with no check at all: x is copied into the parameter, swapped, and the copy destroyed — and skipping the explicit check also avoids paying a branch in the common non-self case
choices.3: It is undefined behaviour and must be prevented by the caller
choices.4: The swap detects aliasing and becomes a no-op

question: Why does the single operator=(T other) also serve as move assignment in C++11?

choices.1: The compiler synthesises a separate move overload from the copy one
choices.2: std::swap moves its arguments internally
choices.3: The by-value parameter binds an rvalue argument via the MOVE constructor, so x = std::move(y) constructs the parameter by move and never allocates; writing a separate operator=(T&&) alongside would be ambiguous
choices.4: Move assignment is unnecessary once swap is defined, because the compiler rewrites moves as swaps

question: What requirement does the idiom place on swap, and how is it typically provided?

choices.1: A noexcept swap that exchanges only raw members (pointers, sizes), usually as a hidden friend found by ADL — if swap can throw, the exception-safety story collapses
choices.2: A virtual swap member so derived classes can override it
choices.3: A swap specialisation in namespace std for the class
choices.4: swap must allocate a temporary buffer to guarantee atomicity

question: Why does std::vector::operator= NOT use copy-and-swap?

choices.1: Copy-and-swap would invalidate iterators, which vector assignment must preserve
choices.2: vector predates the idiom and is frozen by ABI compatibility
choices.3: swap on vectors is O(n), making the idiom quadratic
choices.4: Copy-and-swap always allocates a new buffer, but when the destination's existing capacity suffices, element-wise copy into it does zero allocations — vector chooses that reuse, trading the strong guarantee for efficiency
